# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marking scheme used (KTU 2024):** Part A = 3 marks each (no choice, 2 questions to be answered out of given); Part B = 14 marks each (one full question with internal choice, sub-parts a & b of 7 marks each).

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define Burgers vector. How is it used to differentiate between edge and screw dislocations?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Burgers vector $\vec{b}$ is the crystallographic vector that represents the magnitude and direction of lattice displacement caused by a dislocation. It is found by drawing a closed atom-to-atom Burgers circuit around the dislocation in the real lattice and comparing it with the corresponding circuit in a perfect reference lattice; the closure failure in the perfect lattice equals $\vec{b}$ (1 mark).

It differentiates dislocations geometrically:
- If $\vec{b}$ is **perpendicular** to the dislocation line, it is an **edge dislocation** (1 mark).
- If $\vec{b}$ is **parallel** to the dislocation line, it is a **screw dislocation** (1 mark).
- For all other angles, the dislocation is **mixed**.

> [!TIP]
> Examiner reward: A labelled diagram (edge + screw) earns full marks even if the words are short. Always include a sketch.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**What is the role of surface defects in crack initiation in crystalline materials?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Surface defects (grain boundaries, free surfaces, twin boundaries, stacking faults) act as **preferential sites for crack nucleation** because of three reasons (1 mark each):

1. **Stress concentration:** broken atomic bonds and elastic misfit at the defect create localised stress raisers described by the Griffith–Orowan relation $\sigma_f = \sqrt{2E(\gamma_s+\gamma_p)/(\pi a)}$, where the defect effectively behaves as a crack of half-length $a$.
2. **Dislocation pile-ups:** slip dislocations moving under applied stress pile up at grain boundaries, multiplying the local stress by the number of dislocations in the pile-up, eventually fracturing the boundary.
3. **Environmental segregation and embrittlement:** solutes like H, S, P, Bi segregate to grain boundaries, reducing cohesive strength and promoting **intergranular fracture**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write "crack initiates at GBs because GBs are weak." This is too vague. Examiner wants the **mechanism** (stress concentration, pile-up, segregation).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a) Derive the expression for the critical resolved shear stress to operate a Frank–Read source. Explain how this mechanism explains precipitation hardening in Al–Cu alloys. (7 marks)**

**(b) Define dislocation density. Derive the Taylor equation relating flow stress to dislocation density. Discuss the validity of this relation in nanocrystalline materials. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (a) (7 marks)

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Consider a dislocation segment of length $L$ pinned at both ends under an applied shear stress $\tau$. Force per unit length on the dislocation (Peach–Koehler) is $F/L = \tau b$ (1 mark).

When the segment bows out into a circular arc of radius $R$ subtending angle $2\phi$ at the centre, line tension $T$ acts to straighten it. The equilibrium condition is:

$$\tau b L = 2 T \sin \phi$$

Using $T = G b^2 / 2$ and $L = 2 R \sin \phi$ (1 mark), we have:

$$\tau b (2 R \sin \phi) = 2 \cdot \dfrac{G b^2}{2} \cdot \sin \phi$$

$$\tau b R = \dfrac{G b^2}{2}$$

$$\tau = \dfrac{G b}{2 R}$$

The stress is **maximum** when $R$ is **minimum**, which occurs when the segment becomes a semicircle ($R = L/2$, $\phi = 90°$) (1 mark). Substituting:

$$\boxed{\tau_c = \dfrac{G b}{L}}$$

(1 mark for the final boxed expression with units and statement of the physical meaning.)

**Application to Al–Cu precipitation hardening (2 marks):**

In Al–Cu alloys, Guinier–Preston (GP) zones and $\theta'$ precipitates pin dislocation segments (1 mark). The effective pinned length $L$ decreases as the precipitate density increases. Since $\tau_c = Gb/L$, the activation stress for slip rises sharply, producing the **age-hardening** response characteristic of duralumin (Al-4%Cu) (1 mark).

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (b) (7 marks)

**Definition of dislocation density (1 mark):**
Dislocation density $\rho$ is defined as the total length of dislocation lines per unit volume of crystal:

$$\rho = \dfrac{L_{total}}{V} \quad [\text{m}^{-2}]$$

Typical values: $10^{10} \text{ to } 10^{12} \text{ m}^{-2}$ for annealed metals; up to $10^{16} \text{ m}^{-2}$ for heavily cold-worked metals.

**Derivation of Taylor equation (4 marks):**
Consider a gliding dislocation on its slip plane, encountering a forest of dislocations piercing the plane with mean spacing $\ell$. From geometry:

$$\rho \ell^2 \approx 1 \implies \ell \approx \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\rho}}$$

(1 mark)

The gliding segment bows between forest obstacles like a miniature FRS. The stress required to bypass is:

$$\Delta \tau = \alpha \dfrac{G b}{\ell} = \alpha G b \sqrt{\rho}$$

(2 marks)

Adding the lattice friction stress $\tau_0$ (Peierls–Nabarro stress):

$$\boxed{\tau = \tau_0 + \alpha G b \sqrt{\rho}}$$

(1 mark for final expression)

**Validity in nanocrystalline materials (2 marks):**

The Taylor equation assumes a forest of well-defined, mobile dislocations (1 mark). In nanocrystalline metals ($d < 10$ nm), grains are too small to sustain a pile-up or even a single mobile dislocation — the lattice concept of "line defect" breaks down at the grain scale. Plasticity shifts to **grain boundary sliding, diffusional creep (Coble creep), and grain rotation**. The Taylor equation is no longer valid in this regime, and the **inverse Hall–Petch effect** is observed: yield strength decreases as grain size decreases below the critical value (1 mark).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Q.A):**
> - Forgetting the **factor of 2** in $L = 2 R \sin \phi$ → lose 1 mark.
> - Writing $T = Gb^2$ instead of $T = Gb^2 / 2$ → lose 0.5 mark.
> - In the nano-discussion, omitting the **critical grain size (~10 nm)** loses 1 mark.
> - Failing to **state assumptions** of the Taylor model (mobile dislocations, forest) loses 1 mark.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a) What are grain boundaries? Differentiate between low-angle and high-angle grain boundaries. Derive the Read–Shockley equation for the energy of a low-angle tilt boundary. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the driving force for grain growth. Derive the isothermal grain growth law. Discuss two industrial applications where grain growth control is critical. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (a) (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
A grain boundary is a surface defect that separates two crystals (grains) of the same phase but different crystallographic orientation. Grain boundaries are 2–3 atomic diameters wide, with disrupted bonding and excess free energy.

**Comparison table (2 marks):**

| Property | LAGB ($\theta < 15°$) | HAGB ($\theta > 15°$) |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | Array of edge dislocations | Disordered transition zone |
| Energy | $\gamma = \gamma_0 \theta (A - \ln \theta)$ | Saturated, ~0.5–1 J/m² |
| Mobility | Lower | Higher |
| Resistance to slip | Low | High — pile-ups form |

**Derivation of Read–Shockley equation (4 marks):**

A low-angle tilt boundary is modelled as a vertical array of edge dislocations spaced $D$ apart (1 mark). Each dislocation has elastic strain energy per unit length:

$$\dfrac{E}{L} = \dfrac{G b^2}{4 \pi (1-\nu)} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{R}{r_0}\right)$$

(1 mark)

For a square array of dislocations, the effective outer cut-off radius is proportional to $D$. The energy per unit area of boundary is then the energy per dislocation divided by $D$:

$$\gamma_{GB} = \dfrac{1}{D} \cdot \dfrac{G b^2}{4 \pi (1-\nu)} \left[ A - \ln \theta \right]$$

Using $D = b / \theta$ (small-angle approximation) (1 mark):

$$\gamma_0 = \dfrac{G b}{4 \pi (1-\nu)}$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_{GB} = \gamma_0 \, \theta \, (A - \ln \theta)}$$

(1 mark for the final boxed expression.)

The equation shows $\gamma_{GB}$ rises with $\theta$, peaks near $\theta \approx 15° - 20°$, and saturates for HAGBs.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (b) (7 marks)

**Driving force (2 marks):**
Grain growth is driven by the reduction in total grain boundary energy. Atoms at a convex boundary have a higher chemical potential than those at a concave boundary by an amount:

$$\Delta \mu = \dfrac{2 \gamma_{GB} \Omega}{r}$$

where $\Omega$ is atomic volume and $r$ is the radius of curvature (1 mark). This causes atoms to diffuse from convex to concave sides, **boundaries migrate toward their centre of curvature**, larger grains grow, smaller ones shrink (1 mark).

**Derivation of growth law (3 marks):**
The boundary velocity is $v = M \cdot \Delta G$, where $M$ is GB mobility. The rate of grain-size change is proportional to $v$ and inversely proportional to $r$ (1 mark):

$$\dfrac{dD}{dt} = \dfrac{K'}{D}$$

(For ideal 2D grain growth, the mean radius of curvature scales as $D$.) Rearranging:

$$D \, dD = K' \, dt$$

Integrating from $D_0$ to $D$ over time $0$ to $t$ (1 mark):

$$\dfrac{D^2 - D_0^2}{2} = K' t$$

$$\boxed{D^2 - D_0^2 = K t, \quad K = K_0 \exp(-Q/RT)}$$

(1 mark for final expression.)

For real materials, the exponent $n$ is 2 to 4 depending on solute drag and Zener pinning.

**Industrial applications (2 marks):**

1. **Grain-oriented electrical steel (transformer cores):** The steel is processed with MnS inhibitors and a final high-temperature anneal that promotes **abnormal grain growth** of Goss-textured grains ($\{110\}\langle 001 \rangle$) up to cm size. This reduces magnetic hysteresis loss by an order of magnitude (1 mark).
2. **Tungsten filaments (incandescent lamps):** A small amount of K (potassium bubbles) is added. During recrystallisation, K bubbles pin GBs, producing an extremely elongated, interlocking grain structure that resists **creep and sag** at operating temperatures > 2500 °C (1 mark).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Q.B):**
> - In the Read–Shockley derivation, omitting $D = b / \theta$ and the small-angle approximation loses 2 marks.
> - In grain growth, mixing up **driving force** ($\Delta G = 2 \gamma V_m / r$) with **mobility** loses marks.
> - Vague industrial applications (e.g., "grain size affects strength") fetch no credit. Examiner wants **specific alloy / product / property**.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### 🔹 Surface Defects
- 2-D lattice discontinuities: GBs, free surfaces, twins, stacking faults, antiphase boundaries.
- GB energy is typically **2–3 J/m²** in metals; free surface energy **2–3× higher**.
- Crack initiation follows Griffith–Orowan: $\sigma_f = \sqrt{2 E (\gamma_s + \gamma_p) / (\pi a)}$.
- Three mechanisms: stress concentration, dislocation pile-up, environmental segregation.

### 🔹 Burgers Vector
- Closure failure of a Burgers circuit in the perfect lattice.
- **Edge:** $\vec{b} \perp$ line; **Screw:** $\vec{b} \parallel$ line; **Mixed:** in between.
- For cubic: $b = a\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2} / n$.
- Dislocation energy $\propto b^2$ → system picks smallest $b$ → close-packed directions.
- Conservation at nodes: $\sum \vec{b} = 0$ (Kirchhoff's law for dislocations).

### 🔹 Frank–Read Source
- Pinned segment of length $L$; critical stress $\tau_c = G b / L$.
- FRS = multiplication engine → explains how a few dislocations become millions during plastic deformation.
- Foundation of **precipitation hardening**: short pinned segments = high $\tau_c$ = strong alloy.

### 🔹 Dislocation Density & Strength
- $\rho$ ranges from $10^{10}$ (annealed) to $10^{16}$ m$^{-2}$ (heavily cold-worked).
- **Taylor equation:** $\tau = \tau_0 + \alpha G b \sqrt{\rho}$.
- **Hall–Petch:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y / \sqrt{d}$.
- **Nano-crossover:** below ~10 nm, inverse Hall–Petch; mechanism shifts to GB sliding, Coble creep.

### 🔹 Grain Boundaries
- LAGB ($\theta < 15°$): dislocation array, spacing $D = b/\theta$.
- HAGB ($\theta > 15°$): disordered, energy saturated.
- **Read–Shockley:** $\gamma_{GB} = \gamma_0 \theta (A - \ln \theta)$.
- LAGB → low resistance to slip; HAGB → strong barrier, pile-up site.

### 🔹 Grain Growth
- Driving force: $\Delta G = 2 \gamma_{GB} V_m / r$ — boundary curvature.
- **Kinetics:** $D^n - D_0^n = K_0 t \exp(-Q / RT)$; ideal $n = 2$, real $n = 2 - 4$.
- **Arrest mechanisms:** solute drag, **Zener pinning** $P_Z = 3 f \gamma_{GB} / (2 r_p)$.
- Applications: electrical steel (abnormal growth of Goss grains), W filaments (K-bubble pinning), sintering of ceramics.

### 🔹 Polishing & Etching
- Stages: sectioning → mounting → grinding (SiC 240–2000) → diamond polish (6→1 µm) → chemo-mechanical final polish (colloidal silica 0.04 µm).
- Etching preferentially attacks high-energy sites (GBs, defects, certain phases).
- Etchants: **Nital** (steels), **Keller's** (Al alloys), **Kalling's** (martensite).
- Reveals grain size, phases, porosity, cracks — quantitative image analysis gives ASTM grain-size number.

### 🔹 X-ray Diffraction
- **Bragg's law:** $n \lambda = 2 d \sin \theta$.
- Cubic: $1/d^2 = (h^2+k^2+l^2)/a^2$.
- Peak position → phase ID (ICDD), lattice parameter.
- Peak intensity → structure factor, texture.
- Peak width (FWHM $\beta$) → **Scherrer** crystallite size $D = K \lambda / (\beta \cos \theta)$.
- Peak shift → residual stress, solid-solution lattice distortion.
- **FCC selection rule:** $h,k,l$ all even or all odd; **BCC:** $h+k+l$ = even.

### 🔹 Master Formula Quick-Fire
| Quantity | Formula |
|---|---|
| Bragg | $n\lambda = 2 d \sin\theta$ |
| Critical FRS stress | $\tau_c = G b / L$ |
| Taylor strengthening | $\tau = \tau_0 + \alpha G b \sqrt{\rho}$ |
| Hall–Petch | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ |
| Read–Shockley | $\gamma_{GB} = \gamma_0 \theta (A - \ln\theta)$ |
| GB driving force | $\Delta G = 2 \gamma_{GB} V_m / r$ |
| Grain growth | $D^n - D_0^n = K_0 t \exp(-Q/RT)$ |
| Zener pinning | $P_Z = 3 f \gamma_{GB} / (2 r_p)$ |
| Scherrer | $D = K \lambda / (\beta \cos\theta)$ |
| LAGB spacing | $D = b / \theta$ |
| Griffith–Orowan | $\sigma_f = \sqrt{2 E (\gamma_s + \gamma_p) / (\pi a)}$ |
| Burgers (cubic) | $b = a\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2} / n$ |
| Dislocation energy | $E/L = [G b^2 / (4\pi K)] \ln(R/r_0)$ |
| Peach–Koehler force | $F/L = \tau b$ |
| Line tension | $T \approx G b^2 / 2$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Strategy (KTU 2024):**
> 1. Memorise the 16 master formulas above — they cover 95% of numericals.
> 2. Be able to draw, in 2 minutes, an edge dislocation with Burgers circuit, a Frank–Read source, and a Bragg diffraction setup.
> 3. Know the difference between LAGB and HAGB in three crisp sentences.
> 4. Be ready to derive **one** of: Bragg, Frank–Read critical stress, Taylor, Hall–Petch.
> 5. Recall the nano-crossover (inverse Hall–Petch at $d < 10$ nm) — favourite 7-marker.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT526 - METALLURGY AND MATERIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Simple Problems - SEM and TEM - Diffusion in Solids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) — Formal Definition

A **Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM)** is a high-resolution electron-optical instrument that produces topographical, morphological, and compositional images of a specimen by scanning its surface with a focused beam of **accelerated electrons** (typically 0.5 keV to 30 keV) in a raster scan pattern and detecting the **secondary electrons (SE)** and **backscattered electrons (BSE)** emitted from the sample surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** SEM resolution is governed by **Abbe's diffraction limit equation**:
> $$d = \frac{0.61 \, \lambda}{\mu \sin \beta}$$
> where $\lambda$ is the electron wavelength, $\mu$ is the refractive index of the medium (≈ 1 for vacuum), and $\beta$ is the semi-angle of collection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Flashlight on a Wall"**
> Imagine sweeping a fine flashlight beam across a darkened wall. Each illuminated spot glows back at you. An SEM does the same, but with an *electron* "flashlight" — as the beam scans line-by-line (raster pattern), secondary electrons knocked out of the surface are collected and converted pixel-by-pixel into a magnified image. Smaller beam diameter → sharper image.

## 1.2 Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) — Formal Definition

A **Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM)** is an electron-optical instrument that transmits a high-energy (60–300 keV) electron beam through an ultra-thin specimen (typically **< 200 nm**) and forms an image from the **electrons that pass through**. The image contrast arises from **diffraction, scattering, and phase** interactions of the transmitted electrons with the atomic lattice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** TEM offers the highest spatial resolution of all microscopy techniques (sub-Ångström in modern aberration-corrected instruments) because it uses very short **de Broglie wavelengths** of high-energy electrons.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "X-ray Vision Slide Projector"**
> Think of an old slide projector: light passes *through* a transparent slide, and a magnified image of the slide appears on the screen. A TEM works the same way, except:
> 1. The "light" is a beam of high-energy electrons (because their wavelength is ~100,000× smaller than visible light).
> 2. The "slide" is a sample sliced so thin that electrons can punch through.
> 3. The "screen" is a fluorescent phosphor or a CCD camera.

## 1.3 Diffusion in Solids — Formal Definition

**Diffusion in solids** is the **thermally activated, time-dependent migration of atoms, ions, vacancies, or interstitial species through a crystalline lattice** driven by a chemical potential gradient (concentration gradient, electrical potential, or stress gradient).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Diffusion is mathematically described by **Fick's First Law** (steady-state) and **Fick's Second Law** (non-steady-state). The diffusion coefficient follows the **Arrhenius equation**:
> $$D = D_0 \, \exp\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Ink Drop in a Still Pond"**
> Drop a single drop of ink into a glass of still water. The ink slowly spreads outward — from high concentration (center) to low concentration (edges) — until uniform. The same thing happens when carbon diffuses from a high-carbon steel surface into a low-carbon core during **carburization**. The "speed of spread" is the **diffusion coefficient $D$**, and the "driving force" is the **concentration gradient**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Note:** KTU questions on this topic will test three skills simultaneously:
> 1. Calculating **electron wavelength** from accelerating voltage.
> 2. Calculating **microscope resolution** using Abbe's equation.
> 3. Solving **diffusion problems** (depth of penetration, time required, concentration at a point).
> Master all three before the exam.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Electron Wavelength — The Foundation of EM Resolution

Because resolution in an electron microscope depends on the **de Broglie wavelength** of the electrons, we must first be able to compute $\lambda$ from the accelerating voltage $V$.

### 2.1.1 Non-Relativistic Form (valid for $V < \approx 30$ kV)

From de Broglie's relation $\lambda = h/p$ and the kinetic energy $eV = \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2$:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}}$$

Substituting $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $m_e = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C:

$$\boxed{\lambda \text{ (nm)} = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{V \text{ (volts)}}}}$$

### 2.1.2 Relativistic Form (required for $V > 30$ kV — typical TEM)

When the electron velocity approaches a significant fraction of $c$, relativistic mass correction is necessary:

$$\boxed{\lambda \text{ (Å)} = \frac{12.27}{\sqrt{V \left(1 + 0.978 \times 10^{-6} \, V\right)}}}$$

At 100 kV → $\lambda \approx 0.0370$ Å = **0.00370 nm**
At 200 kV → $\lambda \approx 0.0251$ Å = **0.00251 nm**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why so small?** Visible light has $\lambda \approx 550$ nm — about **150,000× longer** than a 100 kV electron wavelength. That is why electron microscopes can resolve atomic features, while light microscopes are limited to ~200 nm.

## 2.2 SEM — Operating Principles (Step-by-Step)

1. **Electron source (Gun):** Tungsten filament, LaB₆, or Field Emission Gun (FEG) emits electrons via thermionic emission or field emission.
2. **Acceleration:** Electrons are accelerated through 0.5 – 30 kV potential difference.
3. **Condenser & Objective Lenses:** Electromagnetic lenses focus the divergent beam into a **fine probe (≈ 1–10 nm diameter)** at the sample surface.
4. **Scanning Coils:** Deflect the probe in a raster pattern (line-by-line) across the specimen.
5. **Signal Generation:** Primary electrons interact with the sample, producing:
   - **Secondary electrons (SE):** Low energy (< 50 eV) — topographical contrast.
   - **Backscattered electrons (BSE):** High energy — compositional (Z) contrast.
   - **Characteristic X-rays:** For Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS).
6. **Detection & Display:** Detectors convert electron signals into electrical pulses, mapped 1:1 to pixels on a CRT/monitor.

## 2.3 TEM — Operating Principles (Step-by-Step)

1. **Electron source:** High-brightness FEG or LaB₆; accelerated at **60–300 kV**.
2. **Condenser Lens System:** Demagnifies the source into a small, parallel, or convergent beam.
3. **Specimen Stage:** Ultra-thin sample (< 200 nm) inserted into the beam path.
4. **Objective Lens:** The most critical lens — forms the first **intermediate image** and the **diffraction pattern** in the back focal plane.
5. **Intermediate & Projector Lenses:** Magnify the image stepwise (up to **1,000,000×**).
6. **Screen / CCD:** Electrons strike a phosphor screen or CCD, producing a visible image.

> [!NOTE]
> **Imaging Modes:**
> - **Bright Field (BF):** Only the direct (unscattered) beam forms the image — mass-thickness contrast.
> - **Dark Field (DF):** Only a diffracted beam is used — enhances defects, second phases.
> - **High-Resolution TEM (HRTEM):** Uses multiple beams — lattice fringes and atomic columns directly visible.

## 2.4 Fick's First Law (Steady-State Diffusion)

When the concentration at every point in the solid is **constant with time** ($\partial C / \partial t = 0$), diffusion is in **steady state**.

$$J = -D \, \frac{dC}{dx}$$

- $J$ = flux [atoms/(m²·s) or mol/(m²·s)]
- $D$ = diffusion coefficient [m²/s]
- $dC/dx$ = concentration gradient [atoms/m⁴]
- The **negative sign** indicates that atoms diffuse **from high → low** concentration (down the gradient).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering example:** Hydrogen permeation through a Pd membrane. Once steady state is reached, the flux through the membrane is constant — used in hydrogen purification plants.

## 2.5 Fick's Second Law (Non-Steady-State Diffusion)

For most practical heat-treatment processes (carburization, nitriding, homogenization), concentration changes with time. Combining Fick's First Law with the **continuity equation** $\partial C / \partial t = -\partial J / \partial x$:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x} \left( D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} \right)}$$

For **constant D** (the most common KTU assumption):

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \, \frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}}$$

## 2.6 Solution for a Semi-Infinite Solid (Most Tested in KTU)

Boundary conditions: $C(x, 0) = C_0$ for all $x \geq 0$; $C(0, t) = C_s$ for all $t \geq 0$; $C(\infty, t) = C_0$.

$$\boxed{\frac{C_x - C_0}{C_s - C_0} = 1 - \mathrm{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)}$$

This is the **Gauss error-function solution**. The argument $\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}$ is dimensionless and is often denoted $z$.

## 2.7 Arrhenius Temperature Dependence of D

$$D = D_0 \, \exp\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

$$ \ln D = \ln D_0 - \frac{Q}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} $$

For two temperatures:

$$\ln\left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right) = -\frac{Q}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

- $D_0$ = pre-exponential factor [m²/s]
- $Q$ = activation energy [J/mol or eV/atom]
- $R$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature [K]

## 2.8 Maximum Useful Magnification

The smallest detail the human eye can resolve at a normal viewing distance (25 cm) is about **0.2 mm**. Therefore, the maximum useful magnification is:

$$M_{\max} = \frac{0.2 \text{ mm}}{d_{\min}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{5} \text{ nm}}{d_{\min} \text{ (nm)}}$$

## 2.9 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Form | Variables & Units |
|---|----------|------|---------------------|
| 1 | $\lambda \text{ (nm)} = 1.227 / \sqrt{V}$ | Non-relativistic $\lambda$ | $V$ in volts |
| 2 | $\lambda \text{ (Å)} = 12.27 / \sqrt{V(1+0.978 \times 10^{-6} V)}$ | Relativistic $\lambda$ | $V$ in volts |
| 3 | $d = 0.61 \, \lambda / (\mu \sin \beta)$ | Abbe resolution | $d, \lambda$ in same units |
| 4 | $M_{\max} = (2 \times 10^{5} \text{ nm}) / d$ | Max useful magnification | $d$ in nm |
| 5 | $J = -D (dC / dx)$ | Fick's First Law | $J$: mol/m²·s; $D$: m²/s |
| 6 | $\partial C / \partial t = D \, \partial^{2} C / \partial x^{2}$ | Fick's Second Law (constant D) | $D$ in m²/s |
| 7 | $(C_x - C_0)/(C_s - C_0) = 1 - \mathrm{erf}(z)$ | Semi-infinite solution | $z = x / 2\sqrt{Dt}$ |
| 8 | $D = D_0 \exp(-Q / RT)$ | Arrhenius | $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K |
| 9 | $\ln(D_2 / D_1) = -(Q/R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$ | Two-temperature form | $T$ in K |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) and the conversion **1 eV/atom = 96,485 J/mol = 96.485 kJ/mol**. KTU problems often give $Q$ in eV/atom and $R$ in J/(mol·K), forcing unit conversion.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Abbe's Resolution Limit (Why 0.61?)

When a parallel beam of wavelength $\lambda$ passes through a circular aperture of semi-angle $\beta$, the first diffraction minimum forms a ring of radius $r$ given by the condition $r \sin \theta = 1.22 \lambda$ (Airy's criterion for a circular aperture). For a lens focusing at a distance $f$, the radius of the Airy disk in the image plane is:

$$r_{\text{Airy}} = \frac{1.22 \, \lambda \, f}{2 n \sin \beta} = \frac{0.61 \, \lambda}{n \sin \beta}$$

For two adjacent points to be just resolved, the distance between them must equal $2 r_{\text{Airy}}$, but the standard convention defines resolution as the *first-order* separation $d$ (centre-to-centre distance = $1.22 \lambda / \mathrm{NA}$). In most KTU problems the simplified form $d = 0.61 \lambda / \mathrm{NA}$ is used.

## 3.2 Derivation of Fick's First Law (Phenomenological)

Fick postulated (1855) that the diffusive flux is *proportional* to the concentration gradient, analogous to Fourier's law of heat conduction and Ohm's law of electrical conduction.

**Step 1 — Statement of proportionality:**
$$J \propto -\frac{dC}{dx}$$

**Step 2 — Introduce proportionality constant D (the diffusion coefficient):**
$$J = -D \, \frac{dC}{dx}$$

**Step 3 — In 3-D, generalize to vector form:**
$$\vec{J} = -D \, \nabla C$$

**Step 4 — Physical meaning of D:**
- High $D$ → fast diffusion (e.g., H in Pd: $D \sim 10^{-8}$ m²/s at room temperature).
- Low $D$ → slow diffusion (e.g., substitutional Ni in Cu: $D \sim 10^{-30}$ m²/s at room temperature).

## 3.3 Derivation of Fick's Second Law

**Step 1 — Fick's First Law:**
$$J = -D \, \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$

**Step 2 — Conservation of mass (continuity equation):**
Consider a slab of thickness $dx$ and unit cross-section. The net rate of accumulation of solute in the slab must equal the difference between the flux entering at $x$ and the flux leaving at $x + dx$:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} \, dx = J_x - J_{x+dx} = -\frac{\partial J}{\partial x} \, dx$$

**Step 3 — Combine Step 1 and Step 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} &= -\frac{\partial J}{\partial x} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-D \, \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}\right) = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(D \, \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — For constant D:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} &= D \, \frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **Fick's Second Law**. It is a **parabolic partial differential equation** — analogous to the heat equation, with $D$ playing the role of thermal diffusivity.

## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Problems

### Problem 1 — Electron Wavelength (Non-Relativistic)

> **Q:** An SEM is operated at an accelerating voltage of 20 kV. Calculate the de Broglie wavelength of the electrons. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda \text{ (nm)} &= \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{V}} \\
&= \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{20{,}000}} \\
&= \frac{1.227}{141.42} \\
&= 8.68 \times 10^{-3} \text{ nm} \\
&= 0.00868 \text{ nm} \approx 8.68 \text{ pm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification using full de Broglie equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}} \\
&= \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 20{,}000}} \\
&= \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{5.838 \times 10^{-45}}} \\
&= \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{7.641 \times 10^{-23}} \\
&= 8.67 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m} \\
&= 0.00867 \text{ nm} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The wavelength (8.68 pm) is ~60,000× smaller than green light (550 nm), which is *why* SEM can resolve nanoscale features.

### Problem 2 — SEM Resolution and Maximum Useful Magnification

> **Q:** A tungsten filament SEM operates at 30 kV. The objective lens has a semi-collection angle of $\beta = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ rad and the medium is vacuum ($\mu = 1$). Calculate (a) the electron wavelength, (b) the Abbe resolution, and (c) the maximum useful magnification. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Solution:**

**(a) Electron wavelength:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{30{,}000}} = \frac{1.227}{173.21} = 7.08 \times 10^{-3} \text{ nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Abbe resolution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \frac{0.61 \, \lambda}{\mu \sin \beta} \\
  &= \frac{0.61 \times 7.08 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times \sin(5 \times 10^{-3})} \\
  &= \frac{0.61 \times 7.08 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} \quad (\text{since } \sin\theta \approx \theta \text{ for small } \theta) \\
  &= \frac{4.32 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} \\
  &= 0.864 \text{ nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Maximum useful magnification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\max} &= \frac{0.2 \text{ mm}}{d} = \frac{2 \times 10^{5} \text{ nm}}{0.864 \text{ nm}} \\
        &\approx 2.31 \times 10^{5} \; (\approx 231{,}000\times)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> In real SEMs, practical resolution is worse than the Abbe limit because of **spherical aberration, chromatic aberration, and astigmatism**. Real SEM resolution is typically 1–10 nm. But for KTU calculations, use the Abbe value.

### Problem 3 — TEM Resolution (Relativistic)

> **Q:** A TEM is operated at 200 kV. Calculate the wavelength using the relativistic formula and find the maximum resolution if the objective lens has $\beta = 10^{-2}$ rad. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda \text{ (Å)} &= \frac{12.27}{\sqrt{V\left(1 + 0.978 \times 10^{-6} V\right)}} \\
  &= \frac{12.27}{\sqrt{200{,}000 \, (1 + 0.978 \times 10^{-6} \times 200{,}000)}} \\
  &= \frac{12.27}{\sqrt{200{,}000 \, (1 + 0.1956)}} \\
  &= \frac{12.27}{\sqrt{200{,}000 \times 1.1956}} \\
  &= \frac{12.27}{\sqrt{239{,}120}} \\
  &= \frac{12.27}{488.99} \\
  &= 0.02509 \text{ Å} = 2.509 \times 10^{-3} \text{ nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \frac{0.61 \, \lambda}{\sin \beta} = \frac{0.61 \times 2.509 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-2}} \\
  &= 0.153 \text{ nm} = 1.53 \text{ Å}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is sufficient to resolve **interatomic spacings** (typical d-spacings: 2–4 Å in metals).

### Problem 4 — Diffusion Coefficient from Arrhenius Data

> **Q:** For carbon diffusion in $\alpha$-iron, $D_0 = 6.7 \times 10^{-7}$ m²/s and $Q = 80{,}000$ J/mol. Calculate $D$ at 500°C. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert temperature to Kelvin:**
$$T = 500 + 273 = 773 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into Arrhenius equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= D_0 \exp\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right) \\
  &= 6.7 \times 10^{-7} \times \exp\left(-\frac{80{,}000}{8.314 \times 773}\right) \\
  &= 6.7 \times 10^{-7} \times \exp(-12.45) \\
  &= 6.7 \times 10^{-7} \times 3.92 \times 10^{-6} \\
  &= 2.63 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** At 500 °C, carbon diffuses about **10 billion times faster** in iron than at room temperature (where $D \sim 10^{-21}$ m²/s). This is why **carburization is done at high T**.

### Problem 5 — Steady-State Diffusion Through a Membrane

> **Q:** A 1 mm thick iron membrane separates a carburizing atmosphere (1.2 wt% C) from a decarburizing side (0.1 wt% C) at 1000 °C. If $D = 3 \times 10^{-11}$ m²/s, calculate the steady-state carbon flux. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify values:**
- $C_1 = 1.2$ wt% C, $C_2 = 0.1$ wt% C
- $x_1 = 0$, $x_2 = 1$ mm = $10^{-3}$ m
- $D = 3 \times 10^{-11}$ m²/s

**Step 2 — Apply Fick's First Law for linear gradient:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= -D \frac{C_2 - C_1}{x_2 - x_1} = -D \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta x} \\
  &= -3 \times 10^{-11} \times \frac{(0.1 - 1.2) \times 10^{-2}}{10^{-3}} \quad (\text{converting wt\% to fraction}) \\
  &= -3 \times 10^{-11} \times \frac{-1.1 \times 10^{-2}}{10^{-3}} \\
  &= -3 \times 10^{-11} \times (-11) \\
  &= 3.3 \times 10^{-10} \text{ kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)} \quad \text{or in wt% units} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Or, in more common KTU notation (treating C values as raw wt%):

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= -D \frac{dC}{dx} = -3 \times 10^{-11} \times \frac{0.1 - 1.2}{10^{-3}} \\
  &= -3 \times 10^{-11} \times (-1100) \\
  &= 3.3 \times 10^{-8} \text{ wt\%} \cdot \text{m/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The flux is **positive**, confirming that carbon diffuses from the high-concentration side to the low-concentration side.

### Problem 6 — Non-Steady-State (Error-Function) Problem

> **Q:** A steel with 0.2 wt% C is carburized at 927 °C in an atmosphere that maintains surface carbon at 1.0 wt%. For carbon in $\gamma$-iron, $D_0 = 2.3 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s and $Q = 148{,}000$ J/mol. Calculate the carbon concentration at a depth of 0.5 mm after 10 hours. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate D at 927 °C (1200 K):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 2.3 \times 10^{-5} \times \exp\left(-\frac{148{,}000}{8.314 \times 1200}\right) \\
  &= 2.3 \times 10^{-5} \times \exp(-14.835) \\
  &= 2.3 \times 10^{-5} \times 3.61 \times 10^{-7} \\
  &= 8.3 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the dimensionless argument $z$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
z &= \frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \sqrt{8.3 \times 10^{-12} \times 10 \times 3600}} \\
  &= \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \sqrt{2.988 \times 10^{-7}}} \\
  &= \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \times 5.466 \times 10^{-4}} \\
  &= \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{1.093 \times 10^{-3}} \\
  &= 0.457
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Look up or compute erf(0.457):**
From standard error-function tables: $\mathrm{erf}(0.457) \approx 0.476$

**Step 4 — Compute $C_x$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{C_x - C_0}{C_s - C_0} &= 1 - \mathrm{erf}(z) = 1 - 0.476 = 0.524 \\
C_x &= C_0 + 0.524 (C_s - C_0) \\
    &= 0.2 + 0.524 \times (1.0 - 0.2) \\
    &= 0.2 + 0.524 \times 0.8 \\
    &= 0.2 + 0.419 \\
    &= 0.619 \text{ wt\% C} \quad \approx 0.62 \text{ wt\% C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The carbon concentration at 0.5 mm depth after 10 hours of carburization is **0.62 wt% C**.

### Problem 7 — Time Required for a Given Penetration

> **Q:** Using the data from Problem 6, how long will it take to achieve a carbon concentration of 0.6 wt% at a depth of 0.75 mm? [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Solve for $\mathrm{erf}(z)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{0.6 - 0.2}{1.0 - 0.2} &= 1 - \mathrm{erf}(z) \\
\frac{0.4}{0.8} &= 1 - \mathrm{erf}(z) \\
0.5 &= 1 - \mathrm{erf}(z) \\
\mathrm{erf}(z) &= 0.5 \implies z \approx 0.477
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $t$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{x^2}{4 D z^2} = \frac{(7.5 \times 10^{-4})^2}{4 \times 8.3 \times 10^{-12} \times (0.477)^2} \\
  &= \frac{5.625 \times 10^{-7}}{4 \times 8.3 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.2275} \\
  &= \frac{5.625 \times 10^{-7}}{7.554 \times 10^{-12}} \\
  &= 7.45 \times 10^{4} \text{ s} \\
  &\approx 20.7 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick sanity check:** Doubling the depth (0.5 → 0.75 mm — a factor of 1.5) should require about 1.5² = 2.25× longer. The actual ratio is 20.7 / 10 ≈ 2.07, slightly less because $z$ changed from 0.457 to 0.477 (1/erf curve is mildly nonlinear). This confirms consistency.

### Problem 8 — Activation Energy from Two-Temperature Data

> **Q:** The diffusion coefficient of Cu in Al is $3.5 \times 10^{-14}$ m²/s at 600 K and $5.1 \times 10^{-13}$ m²/s at 700 K. Find the activation energy. [KTU University Exam — June 2022]

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the two-temperature form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln\left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right) &= -\frac{Q}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Plug in values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln\left(\frac{5.1 \times 10^{-13}}{3.5 \times 10^{-14}}\right) &= -\frac{Q}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{700} - \frac{1}{600}\right) \\
\ln(14.57) &= -\frac{Q}{8.314}\left(-2.381 \times 10^{-4}\right) \\
2.678 &= \frac{Q}{8.314} \times 2.381 \times 10^{-4} \\
Q &= \frac{2.678 \times 8.314}{2.381 \times 10^{-4}} \\
Q &= \frac{22.27}{2.381 \times 10^{-4}} \\
Q &= 93{,}540 \text{ J/mol} \approx 93.5 \text{ kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reality check:** Activation energy for substitutional diffusion in FCC metals is typically 150–200 kJ/mol. Our 93.5 kJ/mol value is somewhat low, suggesting the data may represent grain-boundary diffusion (lower Q than lattice diffusion) or an interstitial-like mechanism.

## 4.1 SEM — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Electron Source<br/>Tungsten Filament or FEG] --> A2[Anode Plate<br/>Accelerating Voltage 0.5 to 30 kV]
    A2 --> A3[Condenser Lens 1<br/>Initial Demagnification]
    A3 --> A4[Condenser Lens 2<br/>Final Demagnification]
    A4 --> A5[Objective Aperture<br/>Controls Beam Diameter]
    A5 --> A6[Objective Lens<br/>Final Focus to 1 to 10 nm Probe]
    A6 --> A7[Scanning Coils<br/>Raster X Y Deflection]
    A7 --> A8[Specimen Surface<br/>SE BSE X ray Generation]
    A8 --> A9[Secondary Electron Detector<br/>Everhart Thornley]
    A8 --> A10[Backscatter Detector<br/>Solid State Diode]
    A8 --> A11[EDS Detector<br/>Si Li Drift]
    A9 --> A12[Amplifier and ADC]
    A10 --> A12
    A11 --> A12
    A12 --> A13[Image Processor<br/>Pixel by Pixel Mapping]
    A13 --> A14[Display Monitor<br/>Magnified SEM Image]
```

## 4.2 TEM — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Electron Source<br/>FEG or LaB6 Cathode] --> B2[Acceleration Tube<br/>60 to 300 kV]
    B2 --> B3[Condenser Lens 1<br/>Beam Size Control]
    B3 --> B4[Condenser Lens 2<br/>Convergence Control]
    B4 --> B5[Specimen Stage<br/>Ultra Thin Foil less than 200 nm]
    B5 --> B6[Objective Lens<br/>Primary Imaging Lens]
    B6 --> B7[Selected Area Aperture<br/>SAD Diffraction]
    B6 --> B8[Intermediate Lens 1<br/>First Magnification]
    B8 --> B9[Intermediate Lens 2<br/>Second Magnification]
    B9 --> B10[Projector Lens<br/>Final Magnification]
    B10 --> B11[Phosphor Screen<br/>Direct Visual Image]
    B10 --> B12[CCD Camera<br/>Digital Image Capture]
    B11 --> B13[Final Image<br/>up to 1 Million x]
    B12 --> B13
```

## 4.3 Diffusion Analysis — Decision Tree for Solving KTU Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Given: Diffusion Problem] --> C2{Steady State or<br/>Time Dependent}
    C2 -- Steady State --> C3[Use Fick First Law<br/>J equals minus D dC by dx]
    C3 --> C4[Identify C1 C2 x1 x2 D]
    C4 --> C5[Substitute and Solve for J or D]
    C2 -- Time Dependent --> C6[Use Fick Second Law<br/>Error Function Solution]
    C6 --> C7[Compute z equals x over 2 sqrt Dt]
    C7 --> C8[Use Error Function Tables<br/>Find erf of z]
    C8 --> C9[Solve for C x or t or x]
    C1 --> D1{Temperature<br/>Dependence Needed}
    D1 -- Yes --> D2[Apply Arrhenius<br/>D equals D0 exp minus Q by RT]
    D2 --> D3[Convert T to Kelvin]
    D3 --> D4[Check Q units<br/>J per mol or eV per atom]
    D4 --> D5[Solve for D or Q or T]
```

## 4.4 Comparison: SEM vs TEM (Visual Summary)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[SEM] --> P2[Scans Surface<br/>with Electron Beam]
    P2 --> P3[Detects SE and BSE<br/>from Surface]
    P3 --> P4[Topography and<br/>Composition Image]
    P5[TEM] --> P6[Transmits Electrons<br/>through Thin Foil]
    P6 --> P7[Detects Transmitted<br/>and Diffracted Electrons]
    P7 --> P8[Internal Structure<br/>Atomic Resolution Image]
```

## 4.5 Diffusion Mechanisms in Solids (Schematic of Solute Pathways)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Diffusion Mechanisms in Crystalline Solids] --> M2[Vacancy Mechanism<br/>Substitutional Atoms]
    M1 --> M3[Interstitial Mechanism<br/>Small Atoms in Gaps]
    M1 --> M4[Interstitialcy Mechanism<br/>Displaced Interstitials]
    M1 --> M5[Grain Boundary Diffusion<br/>Fast Path along GBs]
    M1 --> M6[Surface Diffusion<br/>Fastest Path on Free Surfaces]
    M2 --> R1[Requires Vacancy Formation<br/>Activation Energy Q equals Qf plus Qm]
    M3 --> R2[Small Distortion of Lattice<br/>Lower Activation Energy]
    M4 --> R3[Pushes Neighbour into<br/>Adjacent Interstitial Site]
    M5 --> R4[DB approximately 0.6 to 0.8 times DL<br/>at moderate Temperatures]
    M6 --> R5[DS greater than DGB greater than DL<br/>DS fastest at Low T]
```
