# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between SEM and TEM based on the mode of image formation. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | SEM | TEM |
|---|---|---|
| Beam-sample interaction | Scans the *surface* | Transmits *through* the specimen |
| Detected signal | Secondary electrons, backscattered electrons, X-rays | Transmitted and diffracted electrons |
| Specimen form | Bulk (with conductive coating if non-conductive) | Ultra-thin foil (< 200 nm) |
| Image contrast | Topographical and compositional (Z) | Mass-thickness and diffraction |
| Typical resolution | 1–10 nm | 0.05–0.2 nm (atomic) |
| Typical magnification | 10× to 500,000× | 100× to 1,000,000× |

### Q2. What is steady-state diffusion? State Fick's First Law. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Model Answer:**

**Steady-state diffusion** is the condition under which the diffusing flux $J$ and the concentration profile $C(x)$ are *independent of time* at every point in the medium; i.e., $\partial C / \partial t = 0$. A practical example is hydrogen gas permeating steadily through a palladium membrane at constant upstream and downstream pressures.

**Fick's First Law:**
$$J = -D \frac{dC}{dx}$$

where $J$ is the diffusion flux (mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹), $D$ is the diffusion coefficient (m²/s), and $dC/dx$ is the concentration gradient (mol·m⁻⁴). The negative sign indicates that net atomic transport occurs in the direction of decreasing concentration.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

> **(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working principle of a Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM). Derive the expression for the maximum resolving power of the SEM. **(7 marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]
>
> **(b)** An iron membrane 5 mm thick is used to separate a carburizing gas (1.5 wt% C) from a decarburizing gas (0.05 wt% C) at 1100 °C. If the diffusion coefficient of carbon in iron at this temperature is $4.5 \times 10^{-10}$ m²/s, calculate the steady-state flux of carbon through the membrane. Also find the carbon concentration at a depth of 2 mm from the high-concentration surface. **(7 marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Working Principle (Block Diagram):**

```
Electron Source → Accelerating Anode → Condenser Lens 1 → Condenser Lens 2
                 → Objective Aperture → Objective Lens → Scanning Coils
                 → Specimen → SE / BSE / X-ray Detectors → Amplifier → Display
```

**Step-by-step explanation:**

1. **Electron source:** Thermionic emission from a tungsten filament, or field emission from a FEG cathode, generates electrons.
2. **Acceleration:** A potential difference of 0.5–30 kV accelerates electrons toward the anode.
3. **Condenser lenses (1 & 2):** Electromagnetic lenses demagnify the electron source into a fine probe (1–10 nm diameter) at the specimen surface.
4. **Scanning coils:** Deflect the beam in a raster pattern (line-by-line) across the surface.
5. **Signal generation:** Primary electron–atom collisions in the specimen generate:
   - **Secondary electrons** (topographic contrast)
   - **Backscattered electrons** (compositional contrast)
   - **Characteristic X-rays** (EDS analysis)
6. **Detection & display:** Detectors convert the signals into electrical pulses, synchronously mapped to pixels on a CRT/monitor.

**Derivation of Resolving Power:**

The minimum resolvable distance $d$ between two features is given by Abbe's criterion for a circular aperture:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \frac{0.61 \, \lambda}{\mu \sin \beta}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\lambda = h / \sqrt{2 m_e e V} = 1.227 / \sqrt{V}$ nm (non-relativistic).

The de Broglie wavelength of accelerated electrons is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{V \text{ (volts)}}} \text{ nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting into Abbe's equation, the maximum resolving power is:

$$
\boxed{d_{\min} = \frac{0.61 \times 1.227}{\sqrt{V} \cdot \mu \sin \beta} = \frac{0.748}{\sqrt{V} \cdot \mu \sin \beta} \text{ nm}}
$$

For $V = 30$ kV and $\beta = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ rad in vacuum ($\mu = 1$):

$$
d_{\min} = \frac{0.748}{\sqrt{30{,}000} \times 5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.748}{173.2 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.748}{0.866} \approx 0.864 \text{ nm}
$$

**Maximum useful magnification:**

$$
M_{\max} = \frac{0.2 \text{ mm}}{d_{\min}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{5} \text{ nm}}{0.864 \text{ nm}} \approx 2.31 \times 10^{5}
$$

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**
- Thickness $L = 5$ mm = $5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $C_{\text{high}} = 1.5$ wt% C, $C_{\text{low}} = 0.05$ wt% C
- $D = 4.5 \times 10^{-10}$ m²/s
- $T = 1100 + 273 = 1373$ K

**Step 1 — Steady-state flux (Fick's First Law):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= -D \frac{dC}{dx} = -D \frac{C_{\text{low}} - C_{\text{high}}}{L} \\
  &= -4.5 \times 10^{-10} \times \frac{0.05 - 1.5}{5 \times 10^{-3}} \quad \text{(wt\% units)} \\
  &= -4.5 \times 10^{-10} \times \frac{-1.45}{5 \times 10^{-3}} \\
  &= -4.5 \times 10^{-10} \times (-290) \\
  &= 1.305 \times 10^{-7} \text{ wt\%} \cdot \text{m/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)} \\
  &= 1.305 \times 10^{-7} \text{ kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)} \quad \text{(since 1 wt% = 10 kg/m³ approx.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Concentration at $x = 2$ mm (linear profile under steady state):**

Under steady state, the concentration varies linearly from $C_{\text{high}}$ at $x = 0$ to $C_{\text{low}}$ at $x = 5$ mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(x) &= C_{\text{high}} + \frac{(C_{\text{low}} - C_{\text{high}})}{L} \cdot x \\
C(2 \text{ mm}) &= 1.5 + \frac{(0.05 - 1.5)}{5} \times 2 \\
                &= 1.5 + (-1.45) \times 0.4 \\
                &= 1.5 - 0.58 \\
                &= 0.92 \text{ wt\% C}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Part (b) tests whether students understand that **linear concentration profile only exists under steady state**. Many students wrongly apply the error-function solution here. Read the problem statement carefully: "**calculate the steady-state flux**" → use Fick's First Law, not the error-function solution.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the construction and working of a Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM). Compare its resolving power with that of an optical microscope. **(7 marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
>
> **(b)** A 0.1 wt% C steel is carburized at 1000 °C. The surface carbon is suddenly raised to and held at 1.2 wt% C. For carbon in $\gamma$-iron at 1000 °C, $D = 3 \times 10^{-11}$ m²/s. Calculate the time required to increase the carbon content to 0.5 wt% at a depth of 0.4 mm. **(7 marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Construction of TEM (Block Schematic):**

```
Electron Gun → Condenser Lens 1 → Condenser Aperture → Condenser Lens 2
              → Specimen (thin foil, < 200 nm) → Objective Lens
              → Objective Aperture → Intermediate Lens 1 → Intermediate Lens 2
              → Projector Lens → Phosphor Screen / CCD Camera
```

**Step-by-step working:**

1. **Electron gun:** A field-emission gun (FEG) or LaB₆ cathode emits electrons thermionically; they are accelerated to 60–300 kV.
2. **Condenser lens system:** Two condenser lenses demagnify the source into a small, parallel, or slightly convergent beam.
3. **Specimen:** Ultra-thin foil (< 200 nm) prepared by ion-beam milling or electropolishing.
4. **Objective lens:** The most important lens; forms the first image and the diffraction pattern.
5. **Intermediate & projector lenses:** Magnify the image stepwise.
6. **Imaging:** Final image is viewed on a fluorescent phosphor screen or recorded on a CCD.

**Resolving power comparison:**

For visible light (green, $\lambda \approx 550$ nm, NA $\approx 1.4$):

$$
d_{\text{optical}} = \frac{0.61 \times 550}{1.4} \approx 240 \text{ nm}
$$

For TEM at 200 kV ($\lambda \approx 0.0025$ nm, $\beta = 10^{-2}$ rad):

$$
d_{\text{TEM}} = \frac{0.61 \times 0.0025}{10^{-2}} = 0.153 \text{ nm}
$$

**Ratio of resolutions:**

$$
\frac{d_{\text{optical}}}{d_{\text{TEM}}} = \frac{240}{0.153} \approx 1570
$$

Hence the TEM can resolve features **~1500× smaller** than the best optical microscope, sufficient to image individual atomic columns.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $C_0 = 0.1$ wt% C, $C_s = 1.2$ wt% C, $C_x = 0.5$ wt% C
- $x = 0.4$ mm = $4 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- $D = 3 \times 10^{-11}$ m²/s
- $T = 1000 + 273 = 1273$ K

**Step 1 — Apply the error-function solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{C_x - C_0}{C_s - C_0} &= 1 - \mathrm{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right) \\
\frac{0.5 - 0.1}{1.2 - 0.1} &= 1 - \mathrm{erf}(z) \\
\frac{0.4}{1.1} &= 1 - \mathrm{erf}(z) \\
0.3636 &= 1 - \mathrm{erf}(z) \\
\mathrm{erf}(z) &= 0.6364
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Find $z$ from error-function tables:**

From standard tables, $\mathrm{erf}(z) = 0.6364 \implies z \approx 0.617$.

**Step 3 — Solve for $t$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{x^2}{4 D z^2} = \frac{(4 \times 10^{-4})^2}{4 \times 3 \times 10^{-11} \times (0.617)^2} \\
  &= \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-7}}{4 \times 3 \times 10^{-11} \times 0.3807} \\
  &= \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-7}}{4.568 \times 10^{-11}} \\
  &= 3502 \text{ s} \\
  &\approx 58.4 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes in Diffusion Problems:**
> 1. **Wrong temperature conversion:** Use **KELVIN**, not Celsius, in the Arrhenius equation.
> 2. **Wrong $z$ formula sign:** $z = x / 2\sqrt{Dt}$ — *never* $x / \sqrt{Dt}$ (missing the factor of 2 inside the square root).
> 3. **Confusing $C_s$, $C_0$, $C_x$:** $C_s$ = surface (boundary); $C_0$ = initial bulk; $C_x$ = unknown at depth $x$. Mixing them is the #1 error.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert units:** $D$ in m²/s but $x$ in mm — convert all to SI.
> 5. **Using non-relativistic $\lambda$ for TEM:** At 200 kV, this gives 10% error; at 1000 kV, 100% error. **Always** use the relativistic formula for TEM.

> [!WARNING]
> **Microscope Calculation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in Abbe's equation:** $\lambda$ and $d$ MUST be in the same units. If $\lambda$ is in nm, $d$ will be in nm.
> 2. **Forgetting the 0.61 factor:** Some students use just $\lambda / \mathrm{NA}$, losing 39% of the mark.
> 3. **Small-angle approximation:** For small $\beta$, $\sin \beta \approx \beta$ (in radians). Use this for SEM ($\beta \sim 10^{-3}$ rad), but **not** for optical microscopes (where $\beta$ can be much larger).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Microscopy (SEM & TEM)

- [ ] **Electron wavelength is ~10⁵× shorter than visible light** — that's *why* electron microscopes are powerful.
- [ ] **Non-relativistic formula** $\lambda = 1.227 / \sqrt{V}$ nm is valid for $V < 30$ kV (typical SEM).
- [ ] **Relativistic formula** $\lambda = 12.27 / \sqrt{V(1 + 0.978 \times 10^{-6} V)}$ Å is required for TEM ($V = 60$–$300$ kV).
- [ ] **Abbe's resolution** $d = 0.61 \lambda / (\mu \sin \beta)$ is the theoretical diffraction limit.
- [ ] **Maximum useful magnification** $M_{\max} = 0.2 \text{ mm} / d$ — the eye cannot resolve below 0.2 mm at 25 cm.
- [ ] **SEM** scans the *surface*, detects secondary/backscattered electrons → topographical + compositional image.
- [ ] **TEM** transmits electrons *through* the foil → internal structure, atomic resolution, diffraction pattern.

### Diffusion in Solids

- [ ] **Fick's First Law** $J = -D(dC/dx)$ is for **steady state** — concentration profile is linear and time-independent.
- [ ] **Fick's Second Law** $\partial C/\partial t = D \, \partial^2 C / \partial x^2$ is for **time-dependent** (transient) diffusion.
- [ ] **Error-function solution** applies to the **semi-infinite solid** with constant surface concentration — the most-tested KTU boundary condition.
- [ ] **Dimensionless argument** $z = x / (2\sqrt{Dt})$ — find from tables, then back-solve for $C_x$, $t$, or $x$.
- [ ] **Arrhenius** $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$ — higher T → higher D (exponentially).
- [ ] **Always** convert temperature to **Kelvin** before using Arrhenius.
- [ ] **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** is the universal gas constant — memorize it.
- [ ] **1 eV/atom = 96.485 kJ/mol** — needed when $Q$ is given in eV/atom instead of J/mol.
- [ ] **Vacancy mechanism** dominates for substitutional diffusion (high $Q$).
- [ ] **Interstitial mechanism** dominates for small atoms like H, C, N in metals (low $Q$).
- [ ] **Diffusion speed ordering:** $D_S > D_{GB} > D_L$ (surface fastest, lattice slowest at low T).

### Common Physical Constants to Memorize

| Constant | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Planck's constant | $h$ | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s |
| Electron mass | $m_e$ | $9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg |
| Elementary charge | $e$ | $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C |
| Gas constant | $R$ | $8.314$ J/(mol·K) |
| Speed of light | $c$ | $3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s |
| Avogadro's number | $N_A$ | $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ /mol |
| Boltzmann's constant | $k$ | $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K |

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy for KTU 2024:**
> 1. In the 3-mark Part A, focus on **defining terms and stating formulas** — these are quick marks.
> 2. In the 14-mark Part B, always include a **labeled block diagram** for SEM/TEM (1–2 marks reserved for diagram alone).
> 3. For diffusion problems, **show the substitution step-by-step** — examiners award partial credit generously.
> 4. **Box your final numerical answer** with units — a value without units loses a mark.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fick's Laws
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT526 - METALLURGY AND MATERIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fick's Laws

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Diffusion and Fick's Laws

**Diffusion** in materials science is the thermally activated, net transport of atoms, ions, vacancies, or molecules through a solid (or fluid) lattice driven by a chemical potential gradient. It is the principal mechanism behind phenomena such as solid-state phase transformations, sintering, creep, oxidation, carburization of steels, homogenization of alloys, and semiconductor doping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Fick's Laws are the constitutive phenomenological equations governing diffusion in solids. **Fick's First Law** describes **steady-state diffusion** (flux is constant with position), while **Fick's Second Law** describes **non-steady-state diffusion** (concentration evolves with time).

The two laws, formulated by Adolf Fick in 1855 by analogy with Fourier's law of heat conduction, are:

- **Fick's First Law (1855):** The diffusion flux is proportional to the concentration gradient.
- **Fick's Second Law:** The time rate of change of concentration equals the divergence of the flux, leading to a partial differential equation analogous to the heat equation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Ink Drop in a Still Pond"

Imagine a single drop of red ink falling into a perfectly still glass of clear water. The dye does not stay concentrated; it slowly spreads out so that eventually, the entire glass appears uniformly pink.

- **If you keep adding ink at a constant rate (and removing it at the same rate)**, the concentration profile along any vertical line looks *the same at every instant* — this is **steady-state diffusion**, governed by **Fick's First Law**.
- **If you add a single drop and just watch**, the concentration at any point keeps changing with time, becoming lower at the source and higher elsewhere — this is **non-steady-state diffusion**, governed by **Fick's Second Law**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:**
> - **No time dependence of profile** → use Fick's **First Law**.
> - **Concentration evolves with time** → use Fick's **Second Law**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Boltzmann Constant (k):** $k = 1.380 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$
- **Gas Constant (R):** $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- **Standard Temperature (T):** measured in **Kelvin (K)**
- **Standard Diffusion Coefficient Unit:** $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ (or more practically, $\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$)
- **Activation Energy Unit:** **J/mol** or **eV/atom**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Diffusion Matters in Metallurgy:**
> Without Fick's Laws, we could not predict the depth of carbon penetration in carburized gears, the rate of homogenization in cast alloys, the dopant profile in silicon wafers, or the creep life of turbine blades. Every heat-treatment specification in industry is essentially a solved Fick's Law problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fick's First Law — Steady-State Diffusion

**Statement:** The net flux of diffusing species per unit cross-sectional area is directly proportional to the concentration gradient measured in the direction of diffusion.

$$\boxed{J = -D \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}}$$

Where:
- $J$ = diffusion flux [atoms/(m²·s)] or [mol/(m²·s)]
- $D$ = diffusion coefficient (diffusivity) [m²/s]
- $C$ = concentration [atoms/m³] or [mol/m³]
- $x$ = position coordinate [m]
- The **negative sign** indicates that atoms diffuse **down** the concentration gradient (from high to low concentration) — a purely physical, not mathematical, choice.

#### Underlying Assumptions (Critical for KTU):

1. **Steady state:** $\partial C/\partial t = 0$, so flux is constant along $x$.
2. **Isotropic medium:** $D$ is the same in all directions.
3. **Dilute solution / ideal behavior:** $D$ is independent of $C$.
4. **No chemical reaction or phase transformation** occurring.
5. **One-dimensional diffusion** (extended forms exist for 2D/3D using $\nabla C$).

### 2.2 Fick's Second Law — Non-Steady-State Diffusion

**Statement:** The time rate of change of concentration at a point equals the second spatial derivative of concentration, scaled by the diffusivity.

$$\boxed{\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \dfrac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}}$$

This is derived directly by combining Fick's First Law with the **continuity equation** (conservation of mass):

$$\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = -\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial x}$$

Substituting $J = -D(\partial C/\partial x)$ and assuming $D$ is independent of concentration (or position):

$$\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \dfrac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}$$

### 2.3 Boundary Conditions for the Standard Semi-Infinite Case (Carburization / Decarburization)

| Boundary / Initial Condition | Physical Meaning | Mathematical Form |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Initial concentration in solid | Uniform starting solute content | $C(x,\,0) = C_0$ |
| Surface concentration | Constant gas-phase / bath concentration | $C(0,\,t) = C_s$ |
| Far-field condition | Solid is semi-infinite; far from surface, $C$ unchanged | $C(\infty,\,t) = C_0$ |

### 2.4 The Error Function Solution (Boltzmann-Matano Analysis)

The analytical solution to Fick's Second Law for the boundary conditions above is:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{C_x - C_0}{C_s - C_0} = 1 - \mathrm{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)}$$

Where $\mathrm{erf}(z)$ is the **Gaussian error function**:

$$\mathrm{erf}(z) = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \int_{0}^{z} e^{-u^{2}}\, du$$

| Argument $z$ | $\mathrm{erf}(z)$ |
| :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 0 |
| 0.5 | 0.5205 |
| 1.0 | 0.8427 |
| 1.5 | 0.9661 |
| 2.0 | 0.9953 |

> [!NOTE]
> The term $\sqrt{Dt}$ has units of length and is called the **characteristic diffusion length** — it sets the natural length scale over which concentration changes appreciably during time $t$.

### 2.5 Temperature Dependence — The Arrhenius Form

Diffusion is a **thermally activated process**; $D$ follows an Arrhenius relationship:

$$\boxed{D = D_0 \,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)}$$

Where:
- $D_0$ = pre-exponential (frequency) factor [m²/s]
- $Q$ = activation energy for diffusion [J/mol]
- $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- $T$ = absolute temperature [K]

Taking natural logarithms (useful for KTU graphical problems):

$$\ln D = \ln D_0 - \dfrac{Q}{R}\cdot\dfrac{1}{T}$$

A plot of $\ln D$ vs. $1/T$ is a straight line with slope $-Q/R$.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Common Use |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $J = -D\,\partial C/\partial x$ | Fick's First Law | Steady-state flux |
| 2 | $\partial C/\partial t = D\,\partial^{2}C/\partial x^{2}$ | Fick's Second Law | Time-dependent profile |
| 3 | $(C_x - C_0)/(C_s - C_0) = 1 - \mathrm{erf}(x/2\sqrt{Dt})$ | Error function solution | Carburization, dopant diffusion |
| 4 | $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$ | Arrhenius diffusivity | Temperature dependence |
| 5 | $\ln D = \ln D_0 - (Q/R)(1/T)$ | Linearized Arrhenius | Graphical $Q$ determination |
| 6 | $J_x = -D_x\,\partial C/\partial x$ (anisotropic) | Directional form | Single crystals, polycrystals |
| 7 | $D \propto 1/T$ for gases, $D \propto T$ for solids (qualitative) | Trend (low T) | Relative comparison |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Carburizing / Nitriding of Steels:** Surface hardening of gears, camshafts, and bearings to improve fatigue life.
2. **Semiconductor Doping:** Controlled diffusion of boron, phosphorus, arsenic into silicon wafers to form p-n junctions.
3. **Homogenization Annealing:** Eliminating segregation in as-cast ingots to ensure uniform mechanical properties.
4. **Sintering of Powder Compacts:** Atomic transport between particles densifies the compact.
5. **Creep of Turbine Blades:** Diffusion-controlled vacancy climb at high homologous temperatures.
6. **Oxidation / Corrosion:** Oxygen diffusion through growing oxide scale on metals.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Fick's Second Law from Fick's First Law (Conservation of Mass Argument)

**Step 1 — Consider an infinitesimal control volume** in the solid between $x$ and $x + dx$ with unit cross-sectional area.

**Step 2 — Mass balance (continuity equation):** The rate of accumulation of solute inside the slab must equal the net influx from the two faces.

$$\text{Rate in at }x\ -\ \text{Rate out at }(x+dx) = \text{Rate of accumulation}$$

**Step 3 — In mathematical form:**

$$J(x) \cdot A\ -\ J(x+dx) \cdot A = \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t}\cdot A \cdot dx$$

**Step 4 — Cancel the area $A$ and divide by $dx$:**

$$-\dfrac{J(x+dx) - J(x)}{dx} = \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t}$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the left side as a derivative:**

$$-\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute Fick's First Law** $J = -D\,\partial C/\partial x$ (assuming $D$ is constant):

$$\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = -\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(-D\,\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D\,\dfrac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}}$$

This is **Fick's Second Law of Diffusion** in one dimension.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Error Function Solution (Boltzmann Transformation)

We seek $C(x,t)$ satisfying:

$$\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D\,\dfrac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}$$

with:
- $C(x,\,0) = C_0$
- $C(0,\,t) = C_s$
- $C(\infty,\,t) = C_0$

**Step 1 — Introduce the similarity variable** $\eta$ (Boltzmann's ansatz). Because there is no natural length scale in the problem, we hypothesize that the profile depends on $x$ and $t$ only through the combination:

$$\eta = \dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}$$

**Step 2 — Chain-rule the partial derivatives:**

$$\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = \dfrac{dC}{d\eta}\cdot\dfrac{\partial \eta}{\partial t} = \dfrac{dC}{d\eta}\cdot\left(-\dfrac{x}{4\sqrt{D}\,t^{3/2}}\right) = -\dfrac{\eta}{2t}\cdot\dfrac{dC}{d\eta}$$

$$\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x} = \dfrac{dC}{d\eta}\cdot\dfrac{\partial \eta}{\partial x} = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\cdot\dfrac{dC}{d\eta}$$

**Step 3 — Second derivative with respect to $x$:**

$$\dfrac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\cdot\dfrac{dC}{d\eta}\right) = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\cdot\dfrac{d^{2}C}{d\eta^{2}}\cdot\dfrac{\partial \eta}{\partial x}$$

$$= \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\cdot\dfrac{d^{2}C}{d\eta^{2}}\cdot\dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{Dt}} = \dfrac{1}{4Dt}\cdot\dfrac{d^{2}C}{d\eta^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into Fick's Second Law:**

$$-\dfrac{\eta}{2t}\cdot\dfrac{dC}{d\eta} = D\cdot\dfrac{1}{4Dt}\cdot\dfrac{d^{2}C}{d\eta^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify (the $t$ cancels and $D$ cancels):**

$$\dfrac{d^{2}C}{d\eta^{2}} + 2\eta\,\dfrac{dC}{d\eta} = 0$$

**Step 6 — Let $p = dC/d\eta$:**

$$\dfrac{dp}{d\eta} + 2\eta\,p = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \dfrac{dp}{p} = -2\eta\,d\eta$$

Integrate:

$$\ln p = -\eta^{2} + A \quad\Longrightarrow\quad p = A' e^{-\eta^{2}}$$

**Step 7 — Integrate once more:**

$$C(\eta) = A'\int_{0}^{\eta} e^{-u^{2}}\,du + B$$

**Step 8 — Apply boundary conditions:**
- At $\eta = 0$ (i.e., $x = 0$): $C = C_s$, and since $\int_0^0 = 0$, we get $B = C_s$.
- At $\eta \to \infty$ (i.e., $x \to \infty$): $C = C_0$. Using the Gaussian integral $\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-u^{2}}\,du = \sqrt{\pi}/2$:

$$C_0 = A'\cdot\dfrac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} + C_s \quad\Longrightarrow\quad A' = -\dfrac{2(C_s - C_0)}{\sqrt{\pi}}$$

**Step 9 — Final Solution:**

$$C(\eta) = C_s - \dfrac{2(C_s - C_0)}{\sqrt{\pi}}\int_{0}^{\eta} e^{-u^{2}}\, du = C_0 + (C_s - C_0)\!\left[1 - \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}\int_{0}^{\eta} e^{-u^{2}}\, du\right]$$

Recognizing the **error function** definition $\mathrm{erf}(\eta) = (2/\sqrt{\pi})\int_{0}^{\eta} e^{-u^{2}}\,du$:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{C_x - C_0}{C_s - C_0} = 1 - \mathrm{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A steel component with initial carbon content of $0.20\ \text{wt\%}$ is carburized in an atmosphere that maintains a surface carbon concentration of $1.00\ \text{wt\%}$. The diffusion coefficient of carbon in austenite (γ-iron) at the treatment temperature is $D = 1.0 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$. Determine the carbon concentration at a depth of $1.0\ \text{mm}$ after $10\ \text{hours}$ of treatment. Also, estimate the time required to obtain a concentration of $0.60\ \text{wt\%}$ C at the same depth.

**Given:**
- $C_0 = 0.20\ \text{wt\%}$
- $C_s = 1.00\ \text{wt\%}$
- $D = 1.0 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$
- $x = 1.0\ \text{mm} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$
- $t = 10\ \text{h} = 36\,000\ \text{s}$

**Step 1 — Compute the similarity variable:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{2\sqrt{(1.0 \times 10^{-11})(36\,000)}}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate $Dt$:**

$$Dt = (1.0 \times 10^{-11})(36\,000) = 3.6 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the square root:**

$$\sqrt{Dt} = \sqrt{3.6 \times 10^{-7}} = 6.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Final value of $\eta$:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.833$$

**Step 5 — Read $\mathrm{erf}(0.833)$** from standard tables (linear interpolation between $\mathrm{erf}(0.80) = 0.7421$ and $\mathrm{erf}(0.85) = 0.7707$):

$$\mathrm{erf}(0.833) \approx 0.7606$$

**Step 6 — Compute $(C_x - C_0)/(C_s - C_0)$:**

$$\dfrac{C_x - 0.20}{1.00 - 0.20} = 1 - 0.7606 = 0.2394$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $C_x$:**

$$C_x = 0.20 + 0.2394 \times 0.80 = 0.20 + 0.1915 = \mathbf{0.3915\ \text{wt\%}}$$

**Step 8 — Time required for $C_x = 0.60\ \text{wt\%}$:**

$$\dfrac{0.60 - 0.20}{1.00 - 0.20} = 1 - \mathrm{erf}(\eta) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \mathrm{erf}(\eta) = 0.50$$

From tables, $\mathrm{erf}(\eta) = 0.50$ at $\eta = 0.4769$.

$$\eta = \dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}} = 0.4769 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \sqrt{Dt} = \dfrac{x}{2 \times 0.4769} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{0.9538} = 1.048 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$$

**Step 9 — Solve for $t$:**

$$t = \dfrac{(1.048 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{1.0 \times 10^{-11}} = \dfrac{1.099 \times 10^{-6}}{1.0 \times 10^{-11}} = 1.099 \times 10^{5}\ \text{s} \approx \mathbf{30.5\ \text{hours}}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Steady vs. Non-Steady-State Comparison

| Feature | Fick's First Law (Steady State) | Fick's Second Law (Non-Steady) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Time dependence | $\partial C/\partial t = 0$ | $\partial C/\partial t \neq 0$ |
| Equation | $J = -D\,\partial C/\partial x$ | $\partial C/\partial t = D\,\partial^{2}C/\partial x^{2}$ |
| Mathematical type | Ordinary differential equation | Partial differential equation |
| Typical application | Gas permeation through a membrane, hydrogen through Pd foil | Carburization, homogenization, dopant diffusion |
| Solution form | Linear $C(x)$ profile, constant $J$ | Error-function profile, time-varying $J$ |
| Boundary conditions needed | $C(0)$ and $C(L)$ only | $C(0,t)$, $C(x,0)$, $C(\infty,t)$ |
| Common KTU trap | Forgetting that $D$ varies with $T$ | Mixing up $\mathrm{erf}$ and $\mathrm{erfc}$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of Diffusion Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["DRIVING FORCE: Concentration Gradient dC/dx"] --> nodeB["FICK'S FIRST LAW: J equals -D times dC/dx"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["STEADY STATE: dC/dt equals 0, J constant"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["APPLICATION: Gas Permeation, Membrane Separation"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["CONTINUITY EQUATION: dC/dt equals -dJ/dx"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["FICK'S SECOND LAW: dC/dt equals D times d2C/dx2"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["BOUNDARY CONDITIONS: Cs at x=0, C0 initial, C0 at infinity"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["BOLTZMANN VARIABLE: eta equals x over 2 root Dt"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["ODE SOLUTION: d2C/deta2 + 2 eta dC/deta equals 0"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["ERROR FUNCTION SOLUTION: Cx equals C0 + Cs minus C0 times 1 minus erf eta"]
    nodeF --> nodeK["TEMPERATURE EFFECT: D equals D0 exp minus Q over RT"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["ARRHENIUS PLOT: ln D vs 1/T slope gives Q over R"]
    nodeJ --> nodeM["ENGINEERING USES: Carburization, Doping, Homogenization"]
    nodeL --> nodeM
```

### 4.2 Schematic Representation — Carburization Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["GAS ATMOSPHERE: CH4, CO, CO2"] --> nodeQ["SURFACE LAYER: Cs = 1.00 wt percent C"]
    nodeQ --> nodeR["DIFFUSION ZONE: C decreases from Cs to C0 along x"]
    nodeR --> nodeS["BULK MATERIAL: C0 = 0.20 wt percent C"]
    nodeR --> nodeT["PROFILE: 1 minus erf of x over 2 root Dt"]
    nodeT --> nodeU["CASE DEPTH: x at which C equals target value"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Decision Flow for Selecting the Correct Law

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["GIVEN DIFFUSION PROBLEM"] --> q1{"IS CONCENTRATION PROFILE TIME INDEPENDENT?"}
    q1 -- YES --> fs1["USE FICK'S FIRST LAW"]
    fs1 --> fs2["J equals -D times dC/dx"]
    fs2 --> fs3["FLUX IS CONSTANT WITH x"]
    q1 -- NO --> fs4["USE FICK'S SECOND LAW"]
    fs4 --> fs5["dC/dt equals D times d2C/dx2"]
    fs5 --> fs6{"BOUNDARY CONDITIONS KNOWN?"}
    fs6 -- YES --> fs7["APPLY ERROR FUNCTION SOLUTION"]
    fs6 -- NO --> fs8["USE NUMERICAL OR FINITE DIFFERENCE METHOD"]
    fs7 --> fs9["CHECK: TEMPERATURE DEPENDENCE OF D"]
    fs9 --> fs10["D equals D0 exp minus Q over RT"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** These block diagrams map the **logical flow** of how an engineer selects and applies Fick's Laws in an industrial problem. They substitute for physical sketches that cannot be rendered as Mermaid graphics.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State Fick's First and Second Laws of diffusion and write their mathematical forms.**

**Model Answer:**

> Fick's **First Law** of diffusion states that the net flux of diffusing species per unit cross-sectional area is directly proportional to the concentration gradient, and occurs in the direction of decreasing concentration.
> $$\boxed{J = -D\,\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}}$$
>
> Fick's **Second Law** describes non-steady-state diffusion, where the concentration at a point changes with time. It is derived by combining the First Law with the continuity equation:
> $$\boxed{\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D\,\dfrac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}}$$
>
> Both laws are the constitutive equations for mass transport in solids, paralleling Fourier's law of heat conduction and Ohm's law of electrical conduction. **[3 marks]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the significance of the negative sign in Fick's First Law?**

**Model Answer:**

> The negative sign in $J = -D(\partial C/\partial x)$ is a directional convention. Since diffusion naturally proceeds **from regions of high concentration to regions of low concentration**, the concentration gradient $\partial C/\partial x$ is **negative** when measured in the direction of mass flow. The negative sign in the equation ensures that the **flux $J$ is positive** (i.e., atoms physically move in the positive $x$ direction) when concentration decreases with increasing $x$.
>
> Physically, the negative sign reflects the **second law of thermodynamics**: atoms move to maximize entropy and reduce the chemical potential gradient. Mathematically, the equation is an *empirical constitutive relation*, and the sign is a convention, not a fundamental truth. **[3 marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **forget to convert $t$ into seconds** when computing $Dt$, leading to a 2–3 mark deduction.
> - Mixing up **erf(z)** and **erfc(z) = 1 − erf(z)** is a classic 2-mark error; remember that the standard solution uses **erf**, not **erfc**.
> - The **negative sign** in Fick's First Law is often dropped by students; this is a 1-mark penalty.
> - Always state the **assumptions** (steady state, constant $D$, 1-D geometry) before applying the formula — 1 mark is reserved for this.
> - In Arrhenius problems, $T$ must be in **Kelvin**, not °C.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a) Derive Fick's Second Law of diffusion starting from Fick's First Law. Clearly state the assumptions made. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Fick's First Law states:

$$J = -D\,\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x} \quad\text{...(i)}$$

Consider an infinitesimal slab of thickness $dx$ and unit cross-sectional area at position $x$. The solute accumulation rate inside this slab equals the net inward flux:

$$\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t}\,dx = J(x) - J(x+dx)$$

Dividing by $dx$ and taking the limit:

$$\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = -\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial x} \quad\text{(continuity equation)} \quad\text{...(ii)}$$

Substituting (i) into (ii):

$$\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = -\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(-D\,\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}\right) = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(D\,\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}\right)$$

**Assumption:** $D$ is independent of position and concentration (constant $D$):

$$\boxed{\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D\,\dfrac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}}$$

**Assumptions required:**
1. One-dimensional diffusion along $x$.
2. Diffusion coefficient $D$ is constant (concentration-independent).
3. Isotropic medium.
4. No source/sink term (no chemical reaction).
5. No convection or drift velocity. **[7 marks]**

---

**(b) For a semi-infinite solid with constant surface concentration $C_s$ and initial concentration $C_0$, derive the error-function solution of Fick's Second Law. State the boundary conditions. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The governing equation is:

$$\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D\,\dfrac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}} \quad\text{...(1)}$$

**Boundary / Initial conditions:**

$$C(x,\,0) = C_0, \quad C(0,\,t) = C_s, \quad C(\infty,\,t) = C_0 \quad\text{...(2)}$$

Introduce the **Boltzmann similarity variable:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}} \quad\text{...(3)}$$

Apply chain rule:

$$\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = -\dfrac{\eta}{2t}\,\dfrac{dC}{d\eta}, \quad \dfrac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}} = \dfrac{1}{4Dt}\,\dfrac{d^{2}C}{d\eta^{2}} \quad\text{...(4)}$$

Substitute (4) into (1):

$$-\dfrac{\eta}{2t}\,\dfrac{dC}{d\eta} = D \cdot \dfrac{1}{4Dt}\,\dfrac{d^{2}C}{d\eta^{2}}$$

Simplify:

$$\dfrac{d^{2}C}{d\eta^{2}} + 2\eta\,\dfrac{dC}{d\eta} = 0 \quad\text{...(5)}$$

Let $p = dC/d\eta$, then $dp/d\eta + 2\eta p = 0$, giving:

$$\ln p = -\eta^{2} + A \quad\Longrightarrow\quad p = A' e^{-\eta^{2}}$$

Integrate:

$$C(\eta) = A'\int_{0}^{\eta} e^{-u^{2}}\, du + B \quad\text{...(6)}$$

Apply boundary conditions:
- At $\eta = 0$: $C = C_s \Rightarrow B = C_s$.
- At $\eta \to \infty$: $C = C_0$ and $\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-u^{2}}\, du = \sqrt{\pi}/2$, so $A' = -2(C_s - C_0)/\sqrt{\pi}$.

Substituting back into (6):

$$\boxed{\dfrac{C_x - C_0}{C_s - C_0} = 1 - \mathrm{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)}$$ **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) Define the diffusion coefficient. Show that the diffusion coefficient depends on temperature according to the Arrhenius equation. How is the activation energy determined experimentally? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **diffusion coefficient $D$** [m²/s] is defined as the proportionality constant between the flux and the concentration gradient in Fick's First Law. It quantifies the *ease* with which atoms move through a given lattice; a larger $D$ means faster diffusion.

**Arrhenius Dependence:**
Diffusion is a thermally activated process. The jump frequency of an atom over the energy barrier $Q$ (activation energy) follows the Boltzmann factor $\exp(-Q/RT)$. Thus:

$$D = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

where $D_0$ is the pre-exponential factor (related to jump distance and attempt frequency) and $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$.

**Experimental Determination of $Q$:**
Taking the natural logarithm:

$$\ln D = \ln D_0 - \dfrac{Q}{R}\cdot\dfrac{1}{T}$$

Measure $D$ at several temperatures (e.g., from carburization depth-time data). Plot $\ln D$ vs. $1/T$ — the slope is $-Q/R$. Multiplying the slope by $-R$ gives the activation energy $Q$. For example, if slope $m = -20\,000\ \text{K}$, then $Q = -mR = 20\,000 \times 8.314 = 1.66 \times 10^{5}\ \text{J/mol}$. **[7 marks]**

---

**(b) A steel gear with $0.18\ \text{wt\%}$ C is carburized at $950\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ in an atmosphere that maintains the surface carbon at $1.10\ \text{wt\%}$. The diffusion coefficient of C in γ-Fe at this temperature is $D = 5.0 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$. Calculate the carbon concentration at a depth of $0.8\ \text{mm}$ after $5\ \text{hours}$ of treatment. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $C_0 = 0.18\ \text{wt\%}$, $C_s = 1.10\ \text{wt\%}$, $D = 5.0 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$, $x = 0.8 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$, $t = 5 \times 3600 = 18\,000\ \text{s}$.

**Step 1 — Compute $Dt$:**

$$Dt = (5.0 \times 10^{-12})(18\,000) = 9.0 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\sqrt{Dt}$:**

$$\sqrt{Dt} = \sqrt{9.0 \times 10^{-8}} = 3.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the similarity variable $\eta$:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}} = \dfrac{0.8 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 3.0 \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{0.8 \times 10^{-3}}{6.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.333$$

**Step 4 — Read $\mathrm{erf}(1.333)$** from tables. Linear interpolation between $\mathrm{erf}(1.30) = 0.9340$ and $\mathrm{erf}(1.35) = 0.9438$:

$$\mathrm{erf}(1.333) \approx 0.9340 + \dfrac{1.333 - 1.30}{1.35 - 1.30}(0.9438 - 0.9340) = 0.9340 + 0.66 \times 0.0098 \approx 0.9405$$

**Step 5 — Apply the error-function solution:**

$$\dfrac{C_x - 0.18}{1.10 - 0.18} = 1 - 0.9405 = 0.0595$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $C_x$:**

$$C_x = 0.18 + 0.0595 \times 0.92 = 0.18 + 0.0547 \approx \mathbf{0.235\ \text{wt\%}}$$

Therefore, the carbon concentration at $0.8\ \text{mm}$ depth after $5\ \text{h}$ is approximately **0.235 wt%**. **[7 marks]**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fick's Laws of Diffusion**
>
> - **Fick's First Law:** $J = -D\,\partial C/\partial x$. Used for **steady-state** problems (constant flux). Always remember the **negative sign**.
> - **Fick's Second Law:** $\partial C/\partial t = D\,\partial^{2}C/\partial x^{2}$. Used for **non-steady-state** (time-dependent) problems.
> - **Boltzmann's similarity variable** $\eta = x/(2\sqrt{Dt})$ reduces the PDE to a solvable ODE, leading to the **error function solution**.
> - The error function solution requires three conditions: **$C_s$ at surface, $C_0$ initially, $C_0$ at infinity** (semi-infinite solid).
> - $\sqrt{Dt}$ is the **characteristic diffusion length** — it sets the penetration depth in a given time.
> - Diffusion coefficient follows **Arrhenius** behavior: $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$. $T$ must be in **Kelvin**.
> - $\ln D$ vs. $1/T$ gives a straight line with slope $-Q/R$.
> - **Units of $D$:** m²/s (SI) or cm²/s (cgs). **$1\ \text{cm}^2/\text{s} = 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$**.
> - Common KTU traps: (i) forgetting the negative sign, (ii) using °C instead of K in Arrhenius, (iii) confusing erf and erfc, (iv) not converting hours to seconds.
> - **Industrial applications to remember:** carburization of steel, semiconductor doping, sintering, homogenization annealing, creep, and oxidation.
> - **Mechanism reminder:** Diffusion occurs via **vacancy, interstitial, interstitialcy, and ring** mechanisms depending on solute size and crystal structure.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
