<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Phase Diagram?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Phase Diagram** (also called an **Equilibrium Diagram** or **Constitutional Diagram**) is a graphical representation that shows the limits of stability of the various phases of a metallic alloy system under conditions of equilibrium, as a function of **temperature**, **pressure**, and **composition**.

A *phase* is defined as a physically and chemically homogeneous portion of a system that has a distinct interface with the surrounding phases. In metallurgy, phases include pure metals, solid solutions, intermediate compounds, and liquid solutions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Chef's Recipe Map"**
> Imagine a phase diagram as a **GPS map for a chef** making alloys. The axes (Temperature vs. Composition) tell the chef "if I am at this temperature and put this much of element B into element A, what will my alloy LOOK LIKE inside?" Just as a weather map shows whether it is raining or sunny, a phase diagram tells us whether the alloy is **solid, liquid, or a mixture**, and what microstructural phases will form upon cooling.

## 1.2 Why Do We Need Alloying?

Pure metals have limited engineering usefulness. Alloying is done to **enhance or tailor** specific properties:

| Desired Property | Example Alloy System |
|---|---|
| Increased strength | Fe–C (Steel) |
| Improved corrosion resistance | Fe–Cr–Ni (Stainless Steel) |
| Better castability | Al–Si (Piston alloy) |
| Higher electrical conductivity | Cu–Be |
| Lower melting point | Pb–Sn (Solder) |
| Magnetic properties | Fe–Si (Transformer steel) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight — Why Alloying?**
> 1. To **increase strength and hardness** (solid solution strengthening, precipitation hardening).
> 2. To **improve corrosion and oxidation resistance**.
> 3. To **modify physical properties** (electrical, magnetic, thermal).
> 4. To **improve machinability and castability**.
> 5. To **achieve specific mechanical properties** like fatigue resistance and toughness.

## 1.3 Classification of Alloys

Alloys are broadly classified based on the **number of constituents** and **atomic arrangement**:

### (a) Based on Number of Constituents
- **Binary Alloys** — Two elements (e.g., Cu–Zn, Fe–C)
- **Ternary Alloys** — Three elements (e.g., Fe–Cr–Ni)
- **Quaternary Alloys** — Four or more elements (e.g., high-speed steels)

### (b) Based on Atomic Arrangement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Fundamental Categories:**
> 1. **Homogeneous / Single-Phase Alloys** — *Solid Solutions* (Substitutional or Interstitial)
> 2. **Heterogeneous / Multi-Phase Alloys** — *Mixtures of two or more phases*

**Solid Solutions** are of two types:
- **Substitutional Solid Solution** — Solute atoms replace solvent atoms in the lattice (e.g., Cu in Ni).
- **Interstitial Solid Solution** — Small solute atoms fit into the voids between solvent atoms (e.g., C in α-Fe).

**Intermediate Phases / Compounds** are also possible (e.g., Fe₃C, CuZn).

## 1.4 Hume-Rothery's Rules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> **Hume-Rothery's Rules** (proposed by William Hume-Rothery, 1934) are empirical conditions that predict when two metals will form an **extensive substitutional solid solution**.

The four primary rules for substantial solid solubility are:

1. **Atomic Size Factor** — The difference in atomic radii between solute and solvent should be **less than ±15%**.
$$\text{Size Factor} = \frac{r_{solute} - r_{solvent}}{r_{solvent}} \times 100\% \leq \pm 15\%$$

2. **Crystal Structure** — Both metals should have the **same crystal structure** in the temperature range of interest (e.g., both FCC, both BCC).

3. **Valence (Electronegativity)** — Metals should have the **same valence**. A metal of higher valence dissolves more of a lower valence metal than vice versa.

4. **Electronegativity** — The closer the **electronegativities**, the greater the solubility. Large differences favor compound formation rather than solid solution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Guest-Host Compatibility Test"**
> Think of a solid solution like inviting guests (solute atoms) into a hotel (solvent lattice). For everyone to be comfortable: guests must be of similar size (Rule 1), speak the same "language" (Rule 2 — same crystal structure), have compatible "electrical signatures" (Rule 3 — valence), and not have too conflicting a "personality" (Rule 4 — electronegativity). If these conditions are met, they form a peaceful, well-mixed alloy.

## 1.5 Five Common Types of Binary Equilibrium Diagrams

The KTU syllabus mandates detailed study of these five types:

| Type | Description | Example System |
|---|---|---|
| **Type 1** | Complete Solid Solubility in Liquid and Solid States (Isomorphous) | Cu–Ni, Cu–Au, Ag–Au |
| **Type 2** | Complete Solid Solubility in Liquid, NO Solubility in Solid (Eutectic) | Bi–Cd, Pb–Sb (partial) |
| **Type 3** | Limited Solid Solubility in Solid, Eutectic System | Pb–Sn, Cu–Zn (Brass partial) |
| **Type 4** | Peritectic System | Cu–Zn (high Zn), Fe–Ni |
| **Type 5** | Compound Formation (with congruent melting) | Mg–Cu, Cu–Au (ordered phases) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** The Iron–Carbon (Fe–Fe₃C) diagram, although technically a eutectic system, is treated as a special diagram due to its immense engineering importance and will be discussed in detail in Section 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Type 1: Isomorphous Binary Phase Diagram (Cu–Ni System)

**Characteristics:**
- Two components are **completely miscible** in both liquid and solid states.
- Three distinct regions: **Liquid (L)**, **Solid + Liquid (α + L)**, and **Solid (α)**.
- The boundary between L and α+L is called the **Liquidus line**.
- The boundary between α+L and α is called the **Solidus line**.

**Cooling Sequence (e.g., 60% Ni – 40% Cu):**
1. At temperature T₁, cooling begins in the **liquid region**.
2. At T₂, the liquidus is crossed → first **solid α** crystals form (composition differs from liquid).
3. Between T₂ and T₃, solidification proceeds with **compositional gradient** in the solid (coring, see §2.6).
4. At T₃, the solidus is crossed → solidification complete; alloy is fully **α phase**.
5. Below T₃, the alloy remains a **homogeneous solid solution**.

## 2.2 Type 2: Eutectic Binary Phase Diagram (Bi–Cd)

**Characteristics:**
- Components are **completely miscible in liquid** but **completely immiscible in solid**.
- The **eutectic point (E)** is the lowest temperature at which the liquid can exist.
- At eutectic: Liquid → Solid₁ + Solid₂ (on cooling).
- **Eutectic reaction:** $L \rightarrow \alpha + \beta$

**Key features:**
- Eutectic temperature (Tₑ) and eutectic composition (Cₑ) are fixed.
- Above Tₑ: liquid exists.
- Below Tₑ: a mixture of two solid phases exists.

## 2.3 Type 3: Eutectic with Limited Solid Solubility (Pb–Sn)

This is the **most important** type for engineering.

**Key Features:**
- Each component has **limited solubility** in the other in the solid state.
- Three solid-state regions: **α, β, and (α + β)**.
- Solubility **decreases with decreasing temperature** → gives rise to **secondary phase precipitation** (e.g., β_Sn precipitates from α_Pb).
- Eutectic reaction: $L_{C_E} \xrightarrow{\text{cool}} \alpha_{C_\alpha} + \beta_{C_\beta}$ at Tₑ = **183°C** for Pb–Sn system.

**Important Points (Microstructural Constituents for Pb–Sn):**
- **Pro-eutectic α** — primary α formed above Tₑ.
- **Pro-eutectic β** — primary β formed above Tₑ.
- **Eutectic mixture (α + β)** — fine lamellar structure formed at Tₑ.
- **Secondary β (β_SS)** — precipitated from α as it cools below the solvus line.

## 2.4 Type 4: Peritectic System

**Peritectic Reaction:**
$$L + \alpha \xrightarrow{\text{cool}} \beta$$

- Occurs at a **fixed temperature**.
- A liquid phase reacts with a solid phase to form a new solid phase.
- Common in: **Cu–Zn**, **Fe–Ni**, **Ag–Pt**.

## 2.5 Type 5: Compound Formation (with Congruent Melting)

- Components form an **intermediate compound** (e.g., MgCu₂).
- The compound has its **own melting point** (congruent melting).
- The diagram can be considered as **two or more simple diagrams joined** at the compound composition.

## 2.6 Coring

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> **Coring** is a microstructural phenomenon in which the **center of a solidifying grain is richer in high-melting-point component**, while the **outer regions (last to solidify) are richer in low-melting-point component**, due to **non-equilibrium cooling**.

**Why it happens:**
- During solidification, the **first solid to form is richer in the higher-melting component**.
- **Diffusion in the solid state is too slow** to homogenize the composition during rapid cooling.
- This creates a **concentration gradient** within each grain — from core (high-melting-rich) to rim (low-melting-rich).

**Effect on Properties:**
- Reduces **ductility and toughness**.
- Creates **localized corrosion susceptibility**.
- Can be **eliminated by annealing** (homogenization heat treatment).

## 2.7 The Lever Rule (Quantitative Analysis of Phase Diagrams)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Purpose:** The Lever Rule is used to determine the **relative amounts (fractions)** of phases present in a two-phase region of a binary phase diagram at any given temperature.

**Statement of the Lever Rule:**
In a two-phase region, the **fraction of a phase** is proportional to the **length of the tie line on the opposite side** of the composition point.

**For a binary alloy of overall composition $C_0$ at temperature T**, in a region containing phases α and β:

$$\text{Fraction of } \alpha \; (W_\alpha) = \frac{C_\beta - C_0}{C_\beta - C_\alpha}$$

$$\text{Fraction of } \beta \; (W_\beta) = \frac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_\beta - C_\alpha}$$

where $C_\alpha$ and $C_\beta$ are the compositions of α and β at temperature T, read from the phase boundaries.

**Derivation** (full derivation in Section 3).

## 2.8 Gibbs Phase Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (J. Willard Gibbs, 1876):**
> The **Gibbs Phase Rule** gives the number of degrees of freedom (F) available in a system at equilibrium, in terms of the number of components (C) and the number of phases (P).

$$F = C - P + 2$$

For **condensed systems** (where pressure is constant, as in most metallurgical systems), the **Condensed Phase Rule** is used:

$$F = C - P + 1$$

**Interpretation:**
- **C** = Number of chemically independent components.
- **P** = Number of phases present at equilibrium.
- **F** = Number of independent variables (T, P, composition) that can be changed without changing the number of phases.

**Application to a Binary Eutectic at the Eutectic Point:**
- C = 2, P = 3 (L, α, β)
- $F = 2 - 3 + 1 = 0$ → **Invariant reaction** (no degrees of freedom; T and compositions are fixed).

## 2.9 Invariant Reactions in Binary Phase Diagrams

| Reaction Type | Reaction | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Eutectic** | $L \rightarrow \alpha + \beta$ | Liquid solidifies to two solids on cooling |
| **Peritectic** | $L + \alpha \rightarrow \beta$ | Liquid + solid → new solid on cooling |
| **Eutectoid** | $\gamma \rightarrow \alpha + \beta$ | One solid → two solids (e.g., Austenite → Ferrite + Cementite) |
| **Peritectoid** | $\alpha + \beta \rightarrow \gamma$ | Two solids → one solid on cooling |

> [!NOTE]
> The difference between *eutectic* and *eutectoid* is that in eutectoid, the parent phase is **solid** rather than liquid. This is **highly tested in KTU exams**.

## 2.10 Iron–Carbon (Fe–Fe₃C) Equilibrium Diagram

This is the **backbone of ferrous metallurgy** and is heavily tested in KTU.

**Key Components and Phases:**

| Phase | Crystal Structure | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **α-Ferrite (α-Fe)** | BCC | Stable below 912°C, max C solubility = 0.022 wt% at 727°C |
| **γ-Austenite (γ-Fe)** | FCC | Stable 912°C–1394°C, max C solubility = 2.14 wt% at 1147°C |
| **δ-Ferrite (δ-Fe)** | BCC | Stable 1394°C–1538°C, max C solubility = 0.09 wt% |
| **Cementite (Fe₃C)** | Orthorhombic | Intermetallic compound, 6.67 wt% C |
| **Liquid** | — | Exists above liquidus line |

**Critical Points and Temperatures:**

| Point | Temperature (°C) | Carbon (wt%) | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 1538 | 0 | Melting point of pure iron |
| **Peritectic** | 1493 | 0.16 | $L + \delta \rightarrow \gamma$ |
| **Eutectic (C)** | 1147 | 4.30 | $L \rightarrow \gamma + Fe_3C$ (Ledeburite) |
| **Eutectoid (S)** | 727 | 0.76 | $\gamma \rightarrow \alpha + Fe_3C$ (Pearlite) |

**Key Microstructures:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pearlite** is a lamellar mixture of **ferrite (α)** and **cementite (Fe₃C)**, formed by the **eutectoid decomposition of austenite** at 727°C. It has a characteristic "fingerprint" appearance under a microscope.

> **Ledeburite** is the eutectic mixture of **austenite (γ)** and **cementite (Fe₃C)**, formed at 1147°C in high-carbon (cast iron) compositions.

**Classification of Fe–C Alloys:**
- **Steels:** C content < 2.14 wt% (hypoeutectoid: < 0.76%; eutectoid: = 0.76%; hypereutectoid: 0.76% – 2.14%)
- **Cast Irons:** C content > 2.14 wt% (hypoeutectic: < 4.30%; eutectic: = 4.30%; hypereutectic: > 4.30%)

## 2.11 Heat Treatment of Steels

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Treatment** is a controlled process of heating and cooling of a metal/alloy in its solid state to obtain **desired microstructure and properties**.

**Common Heat Treatment Processes:**

| Process | Heating | Cooling | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Annealing** | Above upper critical temp. | Slow cooling (furnace) | Soft, ductile, stress-relieved |
| **Normalizing** | Above upper critical temp. | Air cooling | Refined grain, uniform structure |
| **Hardening (Quenching)** | Above upper critical temp. | Rapid cooling (water/oil) | Hard, brittle (Martensite) |
| **Tempering** | Reheat hardened steel (150–650°C) | Air cooling | Reduces brittleness, increases toughness |
| **Spheroidizing** | Prolonged heating near A₁ | Very slow cooling | Globular cementite, machinable |
| **Stress Relieving** | Below lower critical temp. | Slow cooling | Removes internal stresses |

## 2.12 TTT (Time–Temperature–Transformation) Diagram

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> A **TTT Diagram** (also called an **Isothermal Transformation Diagram** or **S-Curve**) shows the **time required for austenite to transform** into other phases (pearlite, bainite) at a constant temperature.

**Key Features:**
- **Y-axis:** Temperature (logarithmic time scale on X-axis).
- **X-axis:** Time (logarithmic).
- Two C-shaped curves indicate **start** and **finish** of transformation.
- **Above the nose:** Transformation begins after an **incubation period**.
- **At the nose (~550°C for eutectoid steel):** Transformation is **fastest**.
- **Mₛ (Martensite Start)** and **M_f (Martensite Finish)** are horizontal lines at ~200°C and ~50°C (no isothermal holding needed for martensite).

**Transformation Products (by Temperature Range):**
1. **Pearlite** (coarse) — formed just below A₁ (~650°C): soft, coarse lamellae.
2. **Pearlite** (fine) — formed closer to nose (~600°C): harder, fine lamellae.
3. **Bainite** — formed between nose and Mₛ (250–550°C): acicular (feathery/lath-like) structure; harder than pearlite, tougher than martensite.
4. **Martensite** — formed by rapid cooling (quenching) below Mₛ: body-centered tetragonal (BCT), very hard and brittle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Race Track for Atoms"**
> Think of TTT diagram as a "race track" for atoms. At the **nose**, atoms have the perfect combination of energy and mobility to transform quickly — like race cars at the fastest section. At **higher temperatures** (above nose), atoms are too energetic and stable; at **lower temperatures** (below nose), atoms are frozen in place and cannot move. The transformation "stalls" at both extremes and is fastest at the nose.

## 2.13 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Rule | Expression | Use / Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Atomic Size Factor (Hume-Rothery)** | $\% \text{Size Difference} = \frac{r_A - r_B}{r_B} \times 100$ | Predicts substitutional solubility |
| **Lever Rule – Wα** | $W_\alpha = \frac{C_\beta - C_0}{C_\beta - C_\alpha}$ | Mass fraction of α phase |
| **Lever Rule – Wβ** | $W_\beta = \frac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_\beta - C_\alpha}$ | Mass fraction of β phase |
| **Gibbs Phase Rule** | $F = C - P + 2$ | General phase rule |
| **Condensed Phase Rule** | $F = C - P + 1$ | Metallurgical systems (constant P) |
| **% Ferrite in Hypoeutectoid Steel** | $\% \alpha = \frac{0.76 - C_0}{0.76 - 0.022} \times 100$ | For $C_0 < 0.76\%$ |
| **% Pearlite in Hypoeutectoid Steel** | $\% \text{Pearlite} = \frac{C_0 - 0.022}{0.76 - 0.022} \times 100$ | For $C_0 < 0.76\%$ |
| **% Cementite in Hypereutectoid Steel** | $\% Fe_3C = \frac{C_0 - 0.76}{6.67 - 0.76} \times 100$ | For $C_0 > 0.76\%$ |
| **% Pearlite in Hypereutectoid Steel** | $\% \text{Pearlite} = \frac{6.67 - C_0}{6.67 - 0.76} \times 100$ | For $C_0 > 0.76\%$ |
| **Eutectoid Pearlite α:Fe₃C Ratio** | $\alpha : Fe_3C = 88 : 12$ (by mass) | Constant for eutectoid steel |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When using the Lever Rule, **always draw the tie line** at the given temperature first, then read the compositions. Marks are awarded for the **diagram, setup, and final fraction**.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Lever Rule

**Setup:** Consider a binary alloy of overall composition $C_0$ at temperature T, lying in a two-phase region (α + β). Let the compositions of the two phases at this temperature be $C_\alpha$ and $C_\beta$, with $C_\alpha < C_0 < C_\beta$.

**Step 1 — Mass Balance:**
Let $W_\alpha$ = mass fraction of phase α, and $W_\beta$ = mass fraction of phase β. Then:
$$W_\alpha + W_\beta = 1$$

**Step 2 — Component B (Solute) Mass Balance:**
The total mass of solute B in the alloy equals the sum of solute B in each phase:
$$C_0 \cdot (W_\alpha + W_\beta) = C_\alpha \cdot W_\alpha + C_\beta \cdot W_\beta$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Mass Balance:**
Using $W_\beta = 1 - W_\alpha$:
$$C_0 = C_\alpha \cdot W_\alpha + C_\beta \cdot (1 - W_\alpha)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $W_\alpha$:**
$$C_0 = C_\alpha W_\alpha + C_\beta - C_\beta W_\alpha$$
$$C_0 - C_\beta = W_\alpha (C_\alpha - C_\beta)$$
$$W_\alpha = \frac{C_0 - C_\beta}{C_\alpha - C_\beta} = \frac{C_\beta - C_0}{C_\beta - C_\alpha}$$

> This is the **Lever Rule expression for the α phase** (note the inversion of the negative sign).

**Step 5 — Similarly, solve for $W_\beta$:**
$$W_\beta = \frac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_\beta - C_\alpha}$$

**Analogy — The Fulcrum Principle:** The composition $C_0$ acts as the **fulcrum (pivot)**, while $C_\alpha$ and $C_\beta$ are the two ends of the lever. The heavier side (closer phase) carries more weight (mass fraction).

## 3.2 Derivation of the Gibbs Phase Rule (Thermodynamic Basis)

**Starting from First Principles:**

For a system with **C components** and **P phases** at equilibrium, the total number of variables to be specified is:

- **Temperature (T):** 1 variable
- **Pressure (P):** 1 variable
- **Composition:** For each of the P phases, we have (C − 1) independent composition variables (since the sum of mole fractions = 1).

**Total Variables (V):**
$$V = 2 + P(C - 1)$$

**Equilibrium Conditions:**
At equilibrium, the **chemical potential** of each component must be equal in all phases. For each component, we have (P − 1) independent equations. With C components:
$$\text{Number of equations} = C(P - 1)$$

**Degrees of Freedom (F):**
$$F = V - \text{Equations} = [2 + P(C - 1)] - C(P - 1)$$
$$F = 2 + PC - P - CP + C$$
$$F = 2 + C - P$$

$$\boxed{F = C - P + 2}$$

**Condensed System (Constant Pressure):** Pressure is fixed, so subtract 1:
$$\boxed{F = C - P + 1}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example: Fe–C Steel Phase Fraction Calculation

> **Problem:** A hypoeutectoid steel contains **0.40 wt% C**. Calculate:
> (a) The % of proeutectoid ferrite at room temperature.
> (b) The % of pearlite at room temperature.
> (c) The % of eutectoid ferrite within the pearlite.
> (d) The total % of cementite.

**Given:**
- $C_0 = 0.40$ wt% C
- $C_\alpha$ (room temp) = 0.022 wt% C (solubility in α-Fe at room temp)
- $C_{Fe_3C}$ = 6.67 wt% C
- Eutectoid composition $C_E$ = 0.76 wt% C

**Step (a): % Proeutectoid Ferrite**
Using Lever Rule at room temperature, between α and Fe₃C:
$$W_\alpha = \frac{6.67 - 0.40}{6.67 - 0.022} = \frac{6.27}{6.648} = 0.9432$$
$$\% \alpha_{\text{total}} = 94.32\%$$

But this includes the ferrite within pearlite. To find proeutectoid ferrite only, we use:
$$\% \text{Proeutectoid } \alpha = \frac{0.76 - 0.40}{0.76 - 0.022} = \frac{0.36}{0.738} = 0.4878$$
$$\% \text{Proeutectoid Ferrite} = 48.78\%$$

**Step (b): % Pearlite**
$$\% \text{Pearlite} = \frac{0.40 - 0.022}{0.76 - 0.022} = \frac{0.378}{0.738} = 0.5122$$
$$\% \text{Pearlite} = 51.22\%$$

**Step (c): % Eutectoid Ferrite (within Pearlite)**
Pearlite = 88% ferrite + 12% cementite (constant eutectoid ratio).
$$\% \text{Eutectoid } \alpha = 0.88 \times 51.22 = 45.07\%$$

**Step (d): % Total Cementite**
$$\% Fe_3C = 0.12 \times 51.22 = 6.15\%$$

**Verification:**
Total = % Proeutectoid α + % Eutectoid α + % Fe₃C
$$= 48.78 + 45.07 + 6.15 = 100.00\% \; ✓$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effects of Alloying Elements on Fe–C Diagram

| Alloying Element | Effect on Eutectoid Temperature | Effect on Eutectoid Composition | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ni** | Lowers | Shifts left (lower C) | Toughness, austenitic steels |
| **Mn** | Lowers | Shifts left | Hardenability, Hadfield steel |
| **Cr** | Raises | Shifts right | Corrosion resistance, tool steels |
| **Mo** | Raises | Shifts right | Creep resistance |
| **Si** | Raises | Shifts right | Spring steels, electrical steels |
| **V, Ti, Nb** | Raises | Strongly shifts right | Grain refinement, carbide formers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mn and Ni are austenite stabilizers** (lower A₁, expand γ-field). **Cr, Mo, Si, V are ferrite stabilizers** (raise A₁, contract γ-field). This is a **favorite KTU viva question**.

## 3.5 Critical Cooling Rate Derivation (Conceptual)

The **Critical Cooling Rate (CCR)** is the **minimum rate of cooling** required to avoid the pearlite/bainite nose of the TTT diagram and produce **100% martensite**.

$$CCR = \frac{T_{\text{austenitizing}} - M_s}{t_{\text{nose}}}$$

where $t_{\text{nose}}$ is the time at the nose of the TTT diagram.

**Example:** For eutectoid steel, if austenitizing temperature = 760°C, $M_s$ = 200°C, and $t_{\text{nose}}$ = 1 second:
$$CCR = \frac{760 - 200}{1} = 560°C/s$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alloying elements (especially Cr, Ni, Mo, Mn) shift the TTT curve to the right**, reducing the critical cooling rate and **increasing hardenability** without the need for drastic quenching.

## 3.6 Properties Table: Iron–Carbon Phases

| Phase | Hardness (HV) | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Ductility (%) | Crystal Structure |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ferrite (α)** | 80–100 | 250 | 35–40 | BCC |
| **Austenite (γ)** | 180–200 | 650 | 40–50 | FCC |
| **Cementite (Fe₃C)** | 1000–1100 | Very brittle | ~0 | Orthorhombic |
| **Pearlite** | 200–250 | 800 | 10–15 | Lamellar (α + Fe₃C) |
| **Bainite** | 350–500 | 1200 | 5–8 | Acicular |
| **Martensite** | 600–900 | 1500–2000 | <1 | BCT |
| **Spheroidite** | 100–150 | 500 | 25 | Globular Fe₃C in α |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State the four Hume-Rothery conditions for extensive substitutional solid solubility.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four Hume-Rothery conditions for extensive substitutional solid solubility are:
1. **Atomic Size Factor:** The atomic radii of solute and solvent must differ by less than **±15%** to avoid excessive lattice strain.
2. **Same Crystal Structure:** Both metals should possess the **same crystal structure** in the temperature range of interest to enable mutual substitution.
3. **Similar Valence:** The metals should have the **same valence**; a metal of higher valence dissolves more of a lower valence metal.
4. **Similar Electronegativity:** The closer the **electronegativity values**, the greater the solubility. Large differences favor compound formation.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define Gibbs Phase Rule. What is the degree of freedom at the eutectic point of a binary system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Definition:** The Gibbs Phase Rule is given by $F = C - P + 2$, where C = number of components, P = number of phases, and F = degrees of freedom (number of independent variables such as temperature, pressure, and composition that can be changed without altering the number of phases).

**At Eutectic Point:** For a binary eutectic (e.g., Pb–Sn):
- C = 2 (Pb, Sn)
- P = 3 (Liquid, α, β)
- Applying Condensed Phase Rule (constant pressure): $F = 2 - 3 + 1 = \mathbf{0}$

Hence, the eutectic point is an **invariant reaction** — both temperature and composition are fixed.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**(a) [7 marks]** Draw the Iron–Carbon (Fe–Fe₃C) equilibrium diagram and label all the phases, critical temperatures, and compositions.

**(b) [7 marks]** A hypoeutectoid steel contains **0.45 wt% C**. Determine:
- (i) The % of proeutectoid ferrite
- (ii) The % of pearlite
- (iii) The % of cementite
at room temperature.

### Model Solution:

**(a) [7 marks]:** The Fe–Fe₃C diagram should include:
- **Axes:** Temperature (Y) vs. Carbon wt% (X)
- **Phases Labeled:** α (ferrite, BCC), γ (austenite, FCC), δ (delta ferrite, BCC), Fe₃C (cementite, orthorhombic), L (liquid)
- **Critical Points:** A (1538°C, 0% C), Peritectic (1493°C, 0.16% C), Eutectic C (1147°C, 4.30% C), Eutectoid S (727°C, 0.76% C)
- **Phase Boundaries:** Liquidus, Solidus, Solvus, A₁, A₃, A_cm lines
- **Single-phase regions:** α, γ, δ, L, Fe₃C
- **Two-phase regions:** α + γ, γ + Fe₃C, α + Fe₃C, L + γ, L + Fe₃C
- (Drawing and labeling: 5 marks; Correct identification of features: 2 marks)

**(b) [7 marks]:**
Given: $C_0 = 0.45$ wt% C; $C_\alpha = 0.022$ wt%; $C_{Fe_3C} = 6.67$ wt%; $C_S = 0.76$ wt%.

**(i) Proeutectoid Ferrite (using tie line between α and γ at 727°C):**
$$\% \text{Proeutectoid } \alpha = \frac{0.76 - 0.45}{0.76 - 0.022} \times 100 = \frac{0.31}{0.738} \times 100 = 42.00\%$$

**(ii) Pearlite:**
$$\% \text{Pearlite} = \frac{0.45 - 0.022}{0.76 - 0.022} \times 100 = \frac{0.428}{0.738} \times 100 = 58.00\%$$

**(iii) Cementite (only within pearlite, since $C_0 < 0.76\%$):**
$$\% Fe_3C = 0.12 \times 58.00 = 6.96\%$$

**Verification:** Proeutectoid α (42.00%) + Eutectoid α (88% of 58% = 51.04%) + Cementite (6.96%) = 100.00% ✓
