### Question B (14 Marks). [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **TTT (Time–Temperature–Transformation) diagram** for eutectoid steel with a neat sketch. Discuss the various microstructural products obtained at different temperature ranges.

**(b) [7 marks]** Differentiate between **Pearlite, Bainite, and Martensite** with respect to:
- (i) Formation temperature
- (ii) Morphology
- (iii) Hardness
- (iv) Mechanism of formation

### Model Solution:

**(a) [7 marks]:**

**Definition (2 marks):** A TTT diagram (or isothermal transformation diagram) shows the time required for austenite to begin and finish transformation into other products at constant sub-critical temperatures. Time is plotted on a **logarithmic scale**.

**Sketch (3 marks):** Should show:
- Y-axis: Temperature (°C), with A₁ at 727°C marked
- X-axis: Time (log scale, seconds)
- Two C-curves: transformation **start** and **finish** lines
- "Nose" of the curve at ~550°C, where transformation is fastest
- Mₛ (~200°C) and M_f (~50°C) horizontal lines for martensite

**Microstructural Products (2 marks):**
- **Above 550°C (Pearlite range):** Coarse pearlite (high T) to fine pearlite (lower T)
- **250–550°C (Bainite range):** Upper bainite (feathery) at higher T, lower bainite (acicular) at lower T
- **Below Mₛ:** Martensite (BCT structure)

**(b) [7 marks]:**

| Property | Pearlite | Bainite | Martensite |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Formation T | 550–727°C | 250–550°C | < 200°C |
| (ii) Morphology | Lamellar (α + Fe₃C) | Acicular / feathery | Plate / needle (BCT) |
| (iii) Hardness (HV) | 200–300 | 350–550 | 600–900 |
| (iv) Mechanism | Diffusional (eutectoid) | Diffusional (intermediate) | Diffusionless (shear/athermal) |
| Cooling | Slow (furnace) | Moderate (salt bath) | Rapid (quench) |

(Each row correctly identified: ~1.5 marks; tabulation and explanation: 1 mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Eutectic vs. Eutectoid** — Eutectic involves **liquid** parent phase; eutectoid involves **solid** parent phase. Marks lost if interchanged.
> 2. **Forgetting the Condensed Phase Rule** — In metallurgical systems, pressure is constant. Use $F = C - P + 1$, not $F = C - P + 2$.
> 3. **Mixing up Proeutectoid and Eutectoid phases** — For hypoeutectoid steel, ferrite is **proeutectoid** (forms before eutectoid) AND **eutectoid** (forms within pearlite). Total ferrite ≠ proeutectoid ferrite.
> 4. **Cementite calculation in hypereutectoid steel** — Proeutectoid cementite must be **added** to eutectoid cementite (12% of pearlite). Students often forget proeutectoid Fe₃C.
> 5. **Drawing TTT diagram with linear time axis** — Always use **logarithmic** time scale; marks deducted for linear.
> 6. **Drawing Fe–C diagram without labeling compositions and temperatures** — Examiner expects **all critical points labeled with both T and wt% C**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **One-page Rapid Revision — Must-know facts for KTU 2024:**

- [x] **Phase Diagram Definition:** Graphical map of phase stability vs. T, P, and composition at **equilibrium**.
- [x] **Hume-Rothery Rules (4):** Size factor (<±15%), Same crystal structure, Similar valence, Similar electronegativity.
- [x] **Five Binary Diagram Types:** Isomorphous, Eutectic (no solubility), Eutectic (limited solubility), Peritectic, Compound formation.
- [x] **Coring:** Compositional gradient within a grain due to **non-equilibrium solidification**; remedied by **homogenization annealing**.
- [x] **Lever Rule:** $W_\alpha = (C_\beta - C_0)/(C_\beta - C_\alpha)$; $W_\beta = (C_0 - C_\alpha)/(C_\beta - C_\alpha)$. **Always draw the tie line first.**
- [x] **Gibbs Phase Rule (Condensed):** $F = C - P + 1$. Eutectic point is **invariant** (F = 0).
- [x] **Invariant Reactions:** Eutectic (L → α + β), Peritectic (L + α → β), Eutectoid (γ → α + β), Peritectoid (α + β → γ).
- [x] **Fe–Fe₃C — Critical Points:** A (1538°C), Peritectic (1493°C, 0.16% C), Eutectic C (1147°C, 4.30% C), Eutectoid S (727°C, 0.76% C).
- [x] **Pearlite:** 88% α + 12% Fe₃C (lamellar) at 727°C.
- [x] **Ledeburite:** Eutectic mix of γ + Fe₃C at 1147°C (cast irons).
- [x] **Steel vs. Cast Iron:** Steels < 2.14% C; Cast irons > 2.14% C.
- [x] **Heat Treatment:** Annealing (soft), Normalizing (refine), Quenching (hard martensite), Tempering (tough + hard), Spheroidizing (machinable).
- [x] **TTT Diagram:** Logarithmic time scale; **nose** at ~550°C for eutectoid steel; Mₛ ~200°C.
- [x] **Transformation Products (decreasing T):** Coarse Pearlite → Fine Pearlite → Bainite → Martensite.
- [x] **Martensite is BCT** (Body-Centered Tetragonal), formed by **diffusionless** (shear) transformation.
- [x] **Alloying elements shift TTT nose to the right** → increase **hardenability** (Ni, Mn, Cr, Mo).
- [x] **Austenite stabilizers:** Ni, Mn, C, N, Co. **Ferrite stabilizers:** Cr, Si, Mo, V, Ti, Nb.
- [x] **Mnemonic for 5 diagram types — "I-E-E-P-C"** (Isomorphous, Eutectic-no-sol, Eutectic-limited, Peritectic, Compound).

**End of Module 2 Comprehensive Notes — PEMRT526 Metallurgy and Material Engineering**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CCT Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT526 - METALLURGY AND MATERIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** CCT Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Continuous Cooling Transformation (CCT) Diagram:** A graphical plot of **temperature (°C) versus the logarithm of time (s)** that delineates the regions of phase stability and the start/end of phase transformations in a steel (or alloy) when it is **cooled continuously** from its austenitizing temperature to room temperature at various specified cooling rates.

Unlike the isothermal **TTT (Time–Temperature–Transformation)** diagram, a CCT diagram directly represents **non-isothermal, real-world industrial cooling** conditions such as furnace cooling, air cooling, oil quenching, and water quenching. The transformation curves are displaced **downward and to the right** (longer times, lower temperatures) compared to the TTT diagram, because continuous cooling effectively "shortens" the time available for diffusion-controlled transformations at any given temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of PEMRT526 specifically requires understanding the construction, interpretation, and engineering application of CCT diagrams for plain carbon steels and low-alloy steels. Students must be able to **predict microstructure** and **hardness** from a given cooling curve superimposed on the CCT diagram.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you are baking a cake and you have a **timer + thermometer chart** on the oven wall. The TTT diagram is like checking the cake at one fixed temperature (e.g., 180 °C) and seeing how it changes over time — *isothermal*. The **CCT diagram**, on the other hand, is like watching the cake **gradually cool from 200 °C down to 25 °C in your kitchen** — *continuous cooling*. The cooling curve in the kitchen is non-linear, and the final texture (soft, chewy, or hard) depends on **how fast** you cool it. The CCT diagram is essentially that "kitchen chart" for steel — it tells you, "if you cool at this rate, you get pearlite; if you cool faster, you get bainite; if you cool extremely fast, you get martensite."

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Standard KTU Glossary)

| Term | Definition | Engineering Significance |
|------|------------|------------------------|
| **Austenitizing Temperature** | Temperature (typically Ac₃ + 30–50 °C) at which steel is held to form 100% austenite (γ-FCC) | Starting point of all cooling curves |
| **Critical Cooling Rate (CCR)** | The minimum cooling rate required to **completely miss** the pearlite + bainite "noses" and form 100% martensite | Determines quenchant selection |
| **Nose of CCT Curve** | The leftmost (shortest time) point of the transformation curve, representing the **easiest transformation temperature** | Bottleneck for martensite formation |
| **M_s / M_f** | Martensite Start and Martensite Finish temperatures (independent of cooling rate, only of composition) | Determines residual austenite |
| **Proeutectoid Phase** | Phase (ferrite/cementite) that forms **before** the eutectoid reaction during cooling | Forms along prior austenite grain boundaries |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics (Bold Constants)

- **Ac₁ Temperature:** ~727 °C (for plain carbon eutectoid steel)
- **Ac₃ Temperature:** 727–910 °C depending on carbon content
- **M_s Temperature:** ~220 °C (for 0.8% C eutectoid steel)
- **M_f Temperature:** ~-50 °C (for 0.8% C eutectoid steel)
- **Critical Cooling Rate (water):** ~600 °C/s
- **Critical Cooling Rate (oil):** ~150 °C/s
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Construction Methodology of a CCT Diagram

The CCT diagram is constructed **experimentally** using techniques such as **dilatometry, metallography, and hardness testing** for a series of specimens cooled at different programmed rates. The logical steps are:

1. **Austenitize** several identical specimens above Ac₃ (typically 30–50 °C above) for sufficient time to achieve a homogeneous austenite.
2. **Cool each specimen continuously** at a different rate: very slow (furnace), slow (air), medium (oil), fast (water), very fast (brine/ice brine).
3. **Record the temperature–time history** for each cooling run.
4. **Detect transformation points** (kinks in the cooling curve) where the latent heat of transformation causes a deviation from the expected smooth cooling profile.
5. **Mark the start of transformation (Tₛ)** and **end of transformation (T_f)** for each cooling rate on a temperature–log(time) plot.
6. **Connect the start points** to form the *transformation-start* curve; connect the finish points to form the *transformation-finish* curve.
7. **Mark the M_s and M_f lines** (horizontal lines, cooling-rate independent).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are CCT curves shifted to the right and below TTT curves?** During continuous cooling, the specimen spends a finite time at *each* temperature. Transformation only occurs at temperatures where the incubation time is exceeded. Since the steel does not dwell at the nose temperature long enough, transformation is **delayed** to lower temperatures and longer overall times.

### 2.2 The Four Fundamental Regions of a CCT Diagram

1. **Austenite Stability Region (above the start curve):** γ-FCC austenite is metastable but untransformed.
2. **Pearlite Transformation Region (high-temperature nose):** Forms at temperatures between Ac₁ and ~550 °C, producing coarse-to-fine pearlite depending on the undercooling.
3. **Bainite Transformation Region (intermediate temperature):** Forms at temperatures between ~550 °C and M_s, producing upper or lower bainite.
4. **Martensite Transformation Region (below M_s):** Diffusionless shear transformation, producing BCT martensite with hardness proportional to carbon content.

### 2.3 The Three Critical Cooling Curves (Engineer's Triad)

| Cooling Medium | Approx. Rate (°C/s) | Resulting Microstructure | Hardness (HRC) |
|----------------|--------------------|--------------------------|-----------------|
| **Furnace Cooling (Annealing)** | 0.01 – 0.1 | Coarse Pearlite | 10–20 |
| **Air Cooling (Normalizing)** | 1 – 10 | Fine Pearlite / Proeutectoid Ferrite | 20–30 |
| **Oil Quenching** | 50 – 150 | Bainite + Martensite mixture | 40–55 |
| **Water Quenching** | 200 – 600 | Martensite (predominantly) | 55–65 |
| **Brine / Ice-Water Quenching** | > 600 | 100% Martensite | 60–65 (max) |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **HIGH-YIELD** for KTU ESE and are tested almost every cycle. Memorize both the formula and its physical meaning.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Critical Cooling Rate (CCR)} &= \frac{T_{\text{Austenitizing}} - M_{s,50\%}}{\tau_{\text{Nose}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\tau_{\text{Nose}}$ is the time at the nose of the CCT diagram and $M_{s,50\%}$ is the temperature at which 50% martensite has formed.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Martensite} &= \left[1 - \exp\left(-0.011 \cdot (M_{s} - T_{q})\right)\right] \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

(Koistinen-Marburger equation, where $T_q$ is the quench temperature)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hardness (HRC)} &\approx 60 \times (\%C)^{0.5} \quad \text{(for 100\% martensite)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Average Cooling Rate} &= \frac{T_{\text{Austenitizing}} - 500^{\circ}C}{\Delta t} \quad \left[\frac{^{\circ}C}{s}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Transformation \% (Avrami)}} &= f = 1 - \exp(-k t^{n})
\end{aligned}
$$

| Formula Symbol | Physical Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---------------|------------------|---------------|
| $T_{\text{Austenitizing}}$ | Soaking temperature of austenite | °C |
| $M_s$ | Martensite start temperature | °C |
| $\tau_{\text{Nose}}$ | Incubation time at nose temperature | seconds |
| $T_q$ | Quench arrest temperature | °C |
| $k, n$ | Avrami kinetic constants (n ≈ 1–4) | dimensionless |
| $f$ | Fraction transformed | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| $HRC$ | Rockwell C hardness | dimensionless scale |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

CCT diagrams are the **backbone of industrial heat treatment design**. Every quench-hardening operation in automotive gear manufacturing, rail-wheel production, and aerospace landing-gear forging relies on a CCT diagram to:

- **Select the correct quenchant** (water, oil, polymer, brine) for a given section thickness.
- **Predict distortion and cracking** by knowing if a cooling curve intersects the bainite nose.
- **Optimize weld heat-affected zone (HAZ) microstructures** using **weld CCT diagrams** (often called *SH-CCT* diagrams for steel).
- **Design dual-phase steels (DP steels)** for automotive body panels by controlled ferrite + martensite mixtures.
- **Forensic failure analysis**: when a shaft fails, metallurgists reconstruct the cooling history by comparing the observed microstructure against the CCT diagram to determine if the original heat treatment was adequate.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Critical Cooling Rate from a CCT Diagram

**Problem Statement:** A eutectoid steel (0.8% C) is austenitized at **780 °C**. From the CCT diagram, the nose of the transformation curve is located at **550 °C** with an incubation time of **1 second**. The M_s temperature is **220 °C**. Determine the **minimum (critical) cooling rate** required to obtain 100% martensite.

**Step 1 — Identify the bottleneck temperature.**
The cooling curve must pass **to the left of the nose** without intersecting the transformation-start curve. The nose temperature $T_{\text{nose}} = 550 \text{ °C}$ is the bottleneck.

**Step 2 — Identify the time constraint at the nose.**
The cooling rate must be fast enough that, when the steel passes through 550 °C, **less than 1 second** has elapsed since the start of cooling from the austenitizing temperature.

**Step 3 — Express the cooling rate as a linear average between two key points.**
For KTU board-level problems, the critical cooling rate is calculated as the slope of the steepest cooling line that just *tangentially* touches the nose:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CCR} &= \frac{T_{\text{Austenitizing}} - T_{\text{Nose}}}{\tau_{\text{Nose}}} \\
&= \frac{780 - 550}{1} \\
&= 230 \text{ °C/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**
Any cooling rate **≥ 230 °C/s** will miss the nose entirely and produce 100% martensite. This corresponds to a **water or brine quench** for typical section sizes. An oil quench (~100 °C/s) would be **insufficient** and would intersect the nose, producing a partial pearlite/bainite microstructure.

**Step 5 — Sanity check using hardenability concepts.**
The calculated CCR (230 °C/s) is consistent with published values for eutectoid plain-carbon steel. The addition of alloying elements (Ni, Cr, Mo) would shift the nose to longer times, **reducing the CCR** and improving hardenability.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Martensite Fraction (Koistinen-Marburger Application)

**Problem Statement:** A 1.0% C steel with $M_s = 200$ °C is quenched and reaches a uniform temperature of **80 °C** before being interrupted (e.g., interrupted quench for tempering). Calculate the **% martensite** formed.

**Step 1 — Recall the K-M equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{M} &= 1 - \exp\left[-0.011 \cdot (M_s - T_q)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{M} &= 1 - \exp\left[-0.011 \cdot (200 - 80)\right] \\
&= 1 - \exp\left[-0.011 \cdot 120\right] \\
&= 1 - \exp(-1.32) \\
&= 1 - 0.2670 \\
&= 0.7330
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to percentage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%M &= 0.7330 \times 100 = 73.3\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering implication.**
The remaining 26.7% is **retained austenite (γ)**. To reduce retained austenite (which causes dimensional instability), the part would require a **sub-zero treatment** (cryogenic quenching to below M_f ≈ -80 °C) or a **tempering operation** to decompose the retained austenite.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cooling Curve vs. Microstructure Prediction

| Cooling Curve | Intersection with CCT? | Resulting Microstructure | Hardness (HRC) | Engineering Use |
|---------------|------------------------|--------------------------|----------------|-----------------|
| Curve C₁ (Furnace) | Intersects pearlite nose at high T | 100% Coarse Pearlite | 10–15 | Annealing / Machinability |
| Curve C₂ (Air) | Intersects pearlite nose at moderate T | Fine Pearlite + Proeutectoid Ferrite | 20–30 | Normalizing / Structural Steel |
| Curve C₃ (Slow Oil) | Misses pearlite, intersects bainite | 100% Bainite | 40–45 | Austempering (e.g., gears, springs) |
| Curve C₄ (Fast Oil) | Misses pearlite + bainite, crosses M_s | Bainite + Martensite | 50–55 | High-strength components |
| Curve C₅ (Water) | Misses all noses, crosses M_s | 100% Martensite | 60–65 | Quench hardening (tools, dies) |
| Curve C₆ (Brine) | Extremely fast, misses all | 100% Martensite (finer) | 60–65 (max) | Maximum hardness applications |

### 3.4 Derivation 3: Lever Rule Application for Mixed Microstructure

**Problem Statement:** A cooling curve C₄ on the CCT diagram enters the bainite region, transforms **40% bainite**, and the remaining 60% austenite transforms to martensite below M_s. Using the lever rule, find the **relative phase fractions** if the bainite finish line is at 400 °C and the M_s is at 200 °C.

**Step 1 — State the lever rule principle.**
The fraction of a phase at a given temperature is determined by the **ratio of tie-line segments** on the phase diagram (or transformation diagram):

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\alpha} &= \frac{T - T_{\beta}}{T_{\alpha} - T_{\beta}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply to the bainite region at 300 °C (mid-point):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{Bainite}} &= \frac{400 - 300}{400 - 200} = \frac{100}{200} = 0.50 \quad (50\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Remaining austenite at M_s:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\gamma} &= 1 - 0.50 = 0.50 \quad (50\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final microstructure at room temperature (assuming complete martensite transformation):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Bainite} &= 50\% \\
\% \text{ Martensite} &= 50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Use the HRC blend rule for hardness estimation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HRC_{\text{Mix}} &= (HRC_{\text{Bainite}} \times f_B) + (HRC_{\text{Martensite}} \times f_M) \\
&= (45 \times 0.50) + (62 \times 0.50) \\
&= 22.5 + 31.0 = 53.5 \text{ HRC}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **typical hardness range for a quenched-and-tempered medium-carbon steel**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: CCT Diagram Architecture & Cooling-Curve Interpretation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start: Heated Steel at Ac3 + 50 C]) --> B0{Austenite Homogenized?}
    B0 -- Yes --> C0[Cooling Rate Selected]
    B0 -- No --> B1[Hold Longer in Furnace]
    B1 --> C0
    C0 --> D0{Cooling Rate Category}
    D0 -- Very Slow 0.01 C per s --> E1[Furnace Cooling C1]
    D0 -- Slow 1 to 10 C per s --> E2[Air Cooling C2 Normalize]
    D0 -- Medium 50 to 150 C per s --> E3[Oil Quench C3 C4]
    D0 -- Fast 200 to 600 C per s --> E4[Water Quench C5]
    D0 -- Very Fast above 600 C per s --> E5[Brine or Ice Brine C6]
    E1 --> F1[Curve Intersects Pearlite Nose at High T]
    E2 --> F2[Curve Intersects Pearlite Nose at Moderate T]
    E3 --> F3[Curve Misses Pearlite, May Hit Bainite]
    E4 --> F4[Curve Misses Both Noses, Crosses Ms Line]
    E5 --> F5[Curve Misses All Noses, Crosses Ms Line]
    F1 --> G1[100 percent Coarse Pearlite HRC 10 to 15]
    F2 --> G2[Fine Pearlite plus Proeutectoid Ferrite HRC 20 to 30]
    F3 --> G3[Upper or Lower Bainite HRC 40 to 50]
    F4 --> G4[Martensite plus Retained Austenite HRC 55 to 62]
    F5 --> G5[100 percent Martensite HRC 60 to 65]
    G1 --> H0[Final Microstructure and Hardness]
    G2 --> H0
    G3 --> H0
    G4 --> H0
    G5 --> H0
    H0 --> I0{Tempering Required?}
    I0 -- Yes --> J0[Temper at 150 to 650 C for Toughness]
    I0 -- No --> K0[Service Ready Component]
    J0 --> K0
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: CCT Diagram Data Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Parameters]
        A1[Chemical Composition percent C and Alloy]
        A2[Austenitizing Temperature]
        A3[Grain Size ASTM Number]
    end
    subgraph CCTDB[CCT Diagram Database]
        B1[Transformation Start Curve]
        B2[Transformation Finish Curve]
        B3[Bainite Region]
        B4[Martensite Start Ms Line]
        B5[Martensite Finish Mf Line]
    end
    subgraph ENGINE[Microstructure Engine]
        C1[Overlay Cooling Curve]
        C2[Detect Intersections]
        C3[Apply Avrami Kinetics]
        C4[Apply K M Equation]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Engineering Output]
        D1[Phase Fractions]
        D2[Hardness HRC and HV]
        D3[Distortion Risk Index]
        D4[Quenchant Recommendation]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C2 --> C4
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D2
    C2 --> D3
    D1 --> D4
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Comparative TTT vs. CCT Curve Positions

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph TTT_DIAGRAM[TTT Diagram Isothermal]
        T1[Pearlite Nose 1 s at 550 C]
        T2[Bainite Nose 30 s at 350 C]
        T3[Ms Line at 220 C horizontal]
    end
    subgraph CCT_DIAGRAM[CCT Diagram Continuous Cooling]
        C1[Pearlite Start Curve Shifted Right]
        C2[Pearlite Finish Curve]
        C3[Bainite Start Curve]
        C4[Ms Line at 220 C horizontal same as TTT]
    end
    T1 -.shifted to.-> C1
    T2 -.shifted to.-> C3
    T3 -- identical --> C4
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** Define a **CCT diagram**. How does it differ from a TTT diagram in terms of construction and industrial applicability?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **Continuous Cooling Transformation (CCT) diagram** is a plot of temperature versus the logarithm of time that shows the start and finish of phase transformations in a steel during **continuous cooling** from the austenitizing temperature. **(1 mark)**
> Differences from TTT diagram:
> 1. TTT diagrams are constructed under **isothermal** conditions (rapid quench to a fixed T, then hold); CCT diagrams use **continuous cooling** at various rates. **(1 mark)**
> 2. CCT transformation curves are **displaced to longer times and lower temperatures** than TTT curves, because continuous cooling limits the incubation time at any temperature. **(0.5 mark)**
> 3. CCT diagrams are **directly applicable** to industrial heat treatment (quenching, normalizing) whereas TTT diagrams are largely **academic/research tools**. **(0.5 mark)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** What is the **critical cooling rate (CCR)**? Explain its significance in quenching operations.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **Critical Cooling Rate (CCR)** is defined as the **minimum cooling rate** required for a steel to **completely bypass the pearlite and bainite transformation noses** in the CCT diagram and form **100% martensite**. **(1.5 marks)**
> Mathematically, it equals the slope of the line drawn from the austenitizing temperature to the nose of the transformation-start curve: $CCR = (T_{\text{Aust}} - T_{\text{Nose}}) / \tau_{\text{Nose}}$. **(1 mark)**
> **Significance:** It determines the **quenchant selection** (water, oil, polymer, brine). If the actual cooling rate at the core of a component is **less** than the CCR, mixed microstructures (ferrite + pearlite + bainite) form, reducing hardness. **(0.5 mark)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU ESE Module 2 – Past Pattern Question, Dec 2023]**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat **schematic CCT diagram for a eutectoid steel**, describe the various phase fields, the M_s and M_f lines, and superimpose **three cooling curves** representing furnace cooling, oil quenching, and water quenching. Explain the resulting microstructures. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A plain-carbon eutectoid steel (0.8% C) is austenitized at **850 °C**. From the CCT diagram, the pearlite nose is at **600 °C** with an incubation time of **5 s**, and the M_s temperature is **220 °C**. Calculate the **critical cooling rate** and recommend a suitable quenchant for a 25 mm thick plate. **(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Diagram (sketch description, 3 marks):**
Draw a temperature–log(time) plot with:
- Y-axis: Temperature from 0 to 900 °C
- X-axis: log time from 0.1 s to 10⁵ s
- Mark the **A₁ line at 727 °C** (horizontal dashed line)
- Draw the **transformation-start C-curve** (pearlite nose at ~600 °C, 5 s) and the **transformation-finish curve** below it
- Mark the **bainite region** (~550–250 °C)
- Mark the **M_s line at 220 °C** and **M_f line at -50 °C** (horizontal lines)

**Cooling curves (2 marks):**
- **Curve 1 (Furnace cooling):** Gentle slope, intersects pearlite nose at high temperature → **coarse pearlite**.
- **Curve 2 (Oil quench):** Steeper slope, misses pearlite nose but cuts the **bainite region** → **bainite + martensite**.
- **Curve 3 (Water quench):** Very steep slope, misses all noses, crosses M_s line → **100% martensite**.

**Microstructure explanation (2 marks):**
The position of intersection determines the **undercooling (ΔT)** below A₁. Larger undercooling → finer microstructure → higher hardness. Water quenching provides the largest ΔT before transformation begins, yielding the finest, hardest martensite.

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- $T_{\text{Austenitizing}} = 850 \text{ °C}$
- $T_{\text{Nose}} = 600 \text{ °C}$
- $\tau_{\text{Nose}} = 5 \text{ s}$
- $M_s = 220 \text{ °C}$
- Plate thickness = 25 mm

**Step 1 — Apply the critical cooling rate formula:** **(3 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CCR} &= \frac{T_{\text{Austenitizing}} - T_{\text{Nose}}}{\tau_{\text{Nose}}} \\
&= \frac{850 - 600}{5} \\
&= \frac{250}{5} \\
&= 50 \text{ °C/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Interpret the result:** **(1 mark)**
The minimum cooling rate required to form 100% martensite is **50 °C/s**.

**Step 3 — Compare with quenchant capabilities:** **(2 marks)**

| Quenchant | Typical Rate (°C/s) | Suitable? |
|-----------|---------------------|-----------|
| Furnace | 0.05 | No |
| Still Air | 1–5 | No |
| Oil | 50–100 | **Borderline / Acceptable** |
| Water | 200–600 | Yes (overkill) |
| Brine | 600–1000 | Yes (with caution — risk of quench cracking) |

**Step 4 — Recommendation:** **(1 mark)**
For a **25 mm thick eutectoid steel plate**, an **oil quench (~80–100 °C/s)** is recommended. The oil provides sufficient cooling rate (>50 °C/s) to bypass the pearlite nose while reducing the thermal shock and risk of quench cracking compared to water. For thicker sections, an alloyed steel (e.g., 4140, 4340) would be necessary to lower the CCR via the nose-shifting effect of Cr, Mo, and Ni.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **TTT and CCT diagrams** with respect to construction method, curve position, applicability, and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A medium-carbon steel (0.4% C) is quenched from 850 °C and **interrupted** at 150 °C before reaching room temperature. Using the **Koistinen–Marburger equation**, calculate the **percentage of martensite** formed at 150 °C, given $M_s = 300$ °C. What **metallurgical consequence** does the remaining untransformed austenite have on the component? **(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks**

| Parameter | TTT Diagram | CCT Diagram |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| **Construction** | Specimen rapidly quenched to a fixed temperature and held isothermally; transformation monitored by metallography or dilatometry | Specimen cooled continuously at a programmed rate; transformation points detected from cooling-curve inflections |
| **Curve Position** | Pearlite/bainite noses at the shortest times | Curves shifted **right and down** (longer times, lower T) |
| **Applicability** | Mostly academic and theoretical; useful for isothermal processes like austempering | Directly applicable to **industrial heat treatment** (annealing, normalizing, quenching) |
| **Limitations** | Does not account for continuous cooling history; cannot predict the final microstructure of quenched parts | More complex to construct; section thickness and quenchant effects must be considered separately via **heat-transfer analysis** |

**Key points (1 mark each):**
1. TTT ignores thermal gradients; CCT incorporates them.
2. TTT assumes infinite quench rate to test temperature; CCT uses finite rates.
3. TTT predicts isothermal bainite (austempering); CCT predicts mixed microstructures.
4. CCT is **complemented** by hardenability data (Jominy end-quench test) for thick sections.

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $M_s = 300$ °C, $T_q = 150$ °C

**Step 1 — Apply K-M equation:** **(3 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_M &= 1 - \exp\left[-0.011 \cdot (M_s - T_q)\right] \\
&= 1 - \exp\left[-0.011 \cdot (300 - 150)\right] \\
&= 1 - \exp(-1.65) \\
&= 1 - 0.1920 \\
&= 0.808
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to percentage:** **(1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%M = 80.8\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Retained austenite calculation:** **(1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\gamma_{\text{retained}} = 100 - 80.8 = 19.2\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Metallurgical consequence (2 marks):**
The presence of **~19% retained austenite** is **metallurgically undesirable** because:
- Retained austenite is **metastable** and can transform to brittle martensite over time (in service) or upon subsequent heating, causing **dimensional instability** and **cracking** (a phenomenon called *delayed cracking*).
- It reduces the **effective hardness** and **wear resistance** of the component.
- It is particularly problematic in **precision components** (e.g., bearings, gauges, gears).
- **Remedies:** (i) **Sub-zero treatment** (cryogenic quench in liquid nitrogen at -196 °C) to convert retained austenite to martensite, (ii) **Tempering** at 150–200 °C to allow gradual transformation, or (iii) **Double tempering** to ensure complete decomposition.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (Dec 2023 / May 2024 examiner reports):**
>
> 1. **Confusing M_s with the nose temperature** — M_s is a horizontal line for martensite start; the nose is the leftmost point of the C-curve. *Lost marks: 1–2 per question.*
> 2. **Forgetting to convert °C/s units** when comparing with published quenchant tables. Always state units explicitly.
> 3. **Using the lever rule incorrectly on a CCT diagram** — The lever rule applies to phase diagrams (equilibrium), not directly to non-equilibrium CCT diagrams. Use **Avrami kinetics** or the **K-M equation** instead.
> 4. **Drawing the C-curve in the wrong direction** — Time increases to the **right** (log scale); temperature increases **upward**. Inverted diagrams are a frequent error.
> 5. **Not mentioning the role of alloying elements** — Cr, Mo, Ni, Mn shift the nose to the right (improve hardenability). This is a **3-mark keyword** in most ESE questions.
> 6. **Failing to distinguish upper vs. lower bainite** in the bainite region — Upper bainite forms at higher T (~500–350 °C) and is feathery; lower bainite forms near M_s and is acicular.
> 7. **Ignoring the Ac₁ and Ac₃ lines** on the diagram. They define the **starting boundary** of austenite formation and must always be shown.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- A **CCT diagram** is a T–log(t) plot for **continuous cooling**; it is the **industrial counterpart** of the TTT diagram.
- CCT curves are **displaced to longer times and lower temperatures** compared to TTT curves.
- The **nose** of the CCT curve is the **bottleneck** for martensite formation.
- The **critical cooling rate (CCR)** = $(T_{\text{Austenitizing}} - T_{\text{Nose}}) / \tau_{\text{Nose}}$.
- Cooling rate hierarchy: **Furnace < Air < Oil < Water < Brine** in increasing severity.
- Microstructure hierarchy (with increasing cooling rate): **Coarse Pearlite → Fine Pearlite → Bainite → Martensite**.
- **M_s and M_f** are **horizontal lines** on the CCT diagram and are **independent of cooling rate** (depend only on composition).
- The **Koistinen–Marburger equation** governs martensite fraction: $f_M = 1 - \exp[-0.011 \cdot (M_s - T_q)]$.
- **Retained austenite** is a direct consequence of incomplete cooling below M_f; remedy by **sub-zero treatment** or **tempering**.
- **Alloying elements (Ni, Cr, Mo, Mn)** shift the CCT nose to the **right** (longer incubation time), **lowering the CCR** and improving **hardenability**.
- The **Jominy end-quench test** is the experimental method used to generate CCT data for thick sections.
- A **weld CCT (SH-CCT)** diagram is a specialized variant used in **welding metallurgy** to predict HAZ microstructures.
- **Hardness of 100% martensite** follows: $HRC \approx 60 \times (\%C)^{0.5}$ (for plain carbon steels).
- For **dual-phase (DP) steels**, controlled cooling produces a ferrite + martensite mixture by **intentionally** intersecting the ferrite nose while bypassing the bainite nose.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications - Tempering - Hardenability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT526 - METALLURGY AND MATERIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications - Tempering - Hardenability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Applications of Heat Treatment

Heat treatment is the controlled sequence of heating and cooling operations applied to metals/alloys in the solid state to obtain desired **microstructure** and **mechanical properties** without changing the product shape. The four canonical operations are: **annealing, normalizing, hardening, and tempering**, supplemented by surface treatments (carburizing, nitriding, induction hardening).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Applications of heat treatment is a high-frequency topic in the End Semester Examination (ESE) Part A (3-mark) and frequently appears as an introductory sub-part in 14-mark questions.

### 1.1.1 Engineering Applications by Component Type

| Component / Industry | Heat Treatment Used | Purpose / Property Achieved |
|---|---|---|
| Gears, shafts, axles (Automotive) | Induction / Flame Hardening, Carburizing | Surface wear resistance + tough core |
| Cutting tools, dies, drills | Quenching + Double Tempering | High hardness (HRC 60–65) + dimensional stability |
| Leaf springs, coil springs | Quench + Temper (400–500 °C) | High yield strength + toughness (tempered martensite) |
| Railway rails | Controlled cooling / Normalizing | Fine pearlite for wear + fatigue resistance |
| Crankshafts, connecting rods | Through-hardening + Tempering | Uniform toughness and fatigue life |
| Bearing races (SKF-grade) | Austempering (Bainitic hardening) | Dimensional accuracy + high hardness |
| Pressure vessels, boiler tubes | Normalizing + Stress Relief | Uniform grain size, weldability |
| Turbine blades (Inconel) | Solution Treatment + Aging | Creep resistance, $\gamma'$ precipitation |
| Punches, chisels | Quench + Low-Temperature Temper (180 °C) | High surface hardness, fracture resistance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition:** Think of heat treatment as *cooking* a metal. Just as a chef controls oven temperature and time to make bread soft or crusty, the metallurgist controls austenitizing temperature, soaking time, and cooling rate to make a steel part *hard on the outside and tough inside* (case hardening) or *uniformly tough* (through-hardening).

### 1.1.2 Selection Logic — Which Treatment for Which Property?

The choice of heat treatment is governed by three **KTU board-favorite** selection rules:

**Rule 1 — Hardness requirement (surface vs through-section):**
- *Surface hardening only* $\Rightarrow$ Induction / Flame / Laser hardening, Carburizing, Nitriding.
- *Through-section hardness* $\Rightarrow$ Quench hardening (requires sufficient hardenability).

**Rule 2 — Carbon content:**
- $C < 0.25\%$ $\Rightarrow$ Cannot be hardened by quenching alone $\Rightarrow$ use *case hardening* (carburizing + quench).
- $0.25\% \le C \le 0.6\%$ $\Rightarrow$ Suitable for through-hardening + tempering.
- $C > 0.6\%$ $\Rightarrow$ Cutting tools, dies; quench + temper (risk of cracking).

**Rule 3 — Distortion / dimensional tolerance:**
- Strict tolerances $\Rightarrow$ Austempering (no martensite $\Rightarrow$ less distortion) or tempering at sub-zero temperatures.

---

## 1.2 Tempering — Formal Definition

**Tempering** is a sub-critical heat treatment process in which a *quench-hardened (martensitic) steel* is reheated to a temperature **below the eutectoid temperature ($A_1 \approx 723\ °C$)**, held (soaked) for a controlled time, and then cooled (usually in air) to obtain a desired combination of hardness, strength, and toughness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Use verbatim in answers):**
> *"Tempering is the process of reheating quench-hardened steel to a temperature below the lower critical temperature $A_1$, holding at that temperature, and cooling at a suitable rate, primarily to relieve internal stresses and to improve toughness at the expense of a small loss in hardness."*

**Key parameters of tempering:**

- **Tempering temperature ($T_t$):** $100\ °C$ to $650\ °C$ (typical range).
- **Soaking time:** $1$ to $4$ hours depending on section size.
- **Cooling medium:** Usually still air; water/oil avoided to prevent thermal shock.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition (Water vs Steel analogy):** Imagine a freshly quenched steel as a *glass* that is very hard (hardness = 65 HRC) but extremely *brittle* — drop it and it shatters. Tempering is like carefully warming the glass in an oven at a controlled, low temperature. You sacrifice a little hardness but the steel becomes ductile, tough, and dimensionally stable. *Lower tempering temperature = harder but more brittle; higher temperature = softer but tougher.*

### 1.2.1 Why is Tempering Necessary?

After quenching, the steel suffers from four problems that tempering solves:

1. **Brittleness of martensite** (BCT structure with high dislocation density).
2. **High internal (residual) stresses** due to non-uniform contraction during quenching.
3. **Presence of retained austenite** (especially in high-C, high-alloy steels) which is unstable at room temperature.
4. **Lack of ductility and impact toughness** — quench-hardened steel has almost zero % elongation.

---

## 1.3 Hardenability — Formal Definition

**Hardenability** is *not* the same as hardness. It is the **ability of a steel to form martensite when quenched**, and is measured as the **depth of hardening** (or the *distribution of hardness*) from the surface inward.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Use verbatim):**
> *"Hardenability is the property of a steel that determines the depth and distribution of hardness obtained by quenching. It is a measure of the ease with which martensite can be formed throughout the section."*

- **Hardness** $\Rightarrow$ Property of the *material/phase* (resistance to indentation, in HRC/HV/HB).
- **Hardenability** $\Rightarrow$ Property of the *steel grade + section size + quenching medium* (depth of martensitic transformation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition (Sponge vs Brick analogy):** Imagine dipping two bricks (one made of *sponge-like high-hardenability steel* and another of *low-hardenability steel*) into a bucket of water. The sponge brick gets *wet all the way through* (martensite forms throughout the section), while the other brick only gets *wet on the outside* (martensite forms only at the surface, while the core transforms to softer pearlite/bainite). Both bricks may have the *same surface hardness*, but their *depth of hardening* is vastly different. This depth is **hardenability**.

### 1.3.1 Quantitative Expression of Hardenability

Two engineering metrics quantify hardenability:

- **Jominy Distance ($J$):** Distance from the quenched end of a Jominy specimen, in **mm or 1/16 inch (J)**.
- **Grossmann's Ideal Critical Diameter ($D_I$):** The largest diameter of a *bar that will form 50\% martensite at the center* when quenched in an *ideal quench* (infinite cooling rate at surface). Measured in **inches or mm**.

### 1.3.2 The Big Picture — Where Tempering and Hardenability Fit

```
[Steel Casting/Forging]
        |
        v
[Austenitizing: Heat to 30–50 °C above A3/Acm]
        |
        v
[Quenching] — Cooling rate > Critical Cooling Rate (CCR)
        |
        v
[As-Quenched Martensite] — Hard (HRC 60+) but Brittle, Stressed
        |
        |------> [Hardenability Test] (measures depth of martensite formation)
        |
        v
[TEMPERING] — Reheat below A1, soak, air cool
        |
        v
[Tempered Martensite] — Hardness slightly lower, Toughness greatly improved
        |
        v
[FINAL COMPONENT: Gears / Tools / Springs / Shafts]
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Mechanics of Tempering

### 2.1.1 Structural Changes During Tempering

Quenched steel contains metastable martensite (BCT, supersaturated with C) plus retained austenite. Tempering is a **diffusion-controlled** process where carbon escapes from the strained BCT lattice to form thermodynamically stable carbides.

**The Four Sequential Stages of Tempering (HIGH-YIELD FOR KTU):**

| Stage | Temp Range (°C) | Phenomenon / Transformation | Carbide Formed | Effect on Properties |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Stage 0** | $25$ – $100$ | Carbon segregation to dislocations, clustering | $\varepsilon$-carbide precursors | Slight hardness increase (*secondary hardening onset*) |
| **Stage 1** | $100$ – $250$ | Precipitation of *transition* carbide from supersaturated martensite | $\varepsilon$-carbide ($\text{Fe}_{2.4}\text{C}$), $\eta$-carbide | Hardness falls slightly; *tempered martensite* forms; *black tempered color* on polished surface |
| **Stage 2** | $200$ – $300$ | Decomposition of **retained austenite** to bainitic ferrite + carbide | $\text{Fe}_3\text{C}$ (cementite) | Slight *secondary hardening*; dimensional change |
| **Stage 3** | $250$ – $400$ | Cementite precipitation and coarsening; recovery of ferrite | $\text{Fe}_3\text{C}$ (cementite) | Significant drop in hardness, sharp rise in toughness |
| **Stage 4** | $400$ – $700$ | Sub-grain growth, recrystallization, carbide spheroidization | $\text{Fe}_3\text{C}$ (coarsened) | Further softening, maximum ductility; **equiaxed ferrite + spheroidized cementite** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Memory Trick — "B-C-S-R":** The four tempering stages (1, 2, 3, 4) remember as **B**ainite (from RA), **C**ementite, **S**pheroidization, **R**ecrystallization — though the actual KTU-favored mnemonic is *"1-ε, 2-RA, 3-Cementite, 4-Spheroidize"*.

### 2.1.2 Temper Colors (Visible Surface Oxide Tints)

For plain-carbon steel tools, the tempering color visible on a polished surface is a quick field indicator of approximate temperature:

| Temper Color | Approx. Temperature (°C) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| Pale yellow | $200$ | Lathe tools, scrapers, razors |
| Straw yellow | $220$ | Drill bits, milling cutters |
| Brown / Bronze | $245$ | Wood chisels, axes |
| Purple | $275$ | Cold chisels, screwdrivers |
| Dark blue | $295$ | Springs, sword blades |
| Pale blue | $320$ | Saws, springs (high-toughness req.) |

### 2.1.3 Effect of Alloying Elements on Tempering

- **Carbide formers (Cr, Mo, V, W, Ti):** Form *alloy carbides* ($M_{23}C_6$, $M_7C_3$, $MC$) that are *fine, stable, and slow to coarsen*. This produces **secondary hardening** peak at $500$ – $600\ °C$.
- **Non-carbide formers (Ni, Si, Mn):** *Retard* cementite coarsening $\Rightarrow$ shift softening to higher temperatures.
- **Si specifically** $\Rightarrow$ Strongly *suppresses Stage 3* carbide coarsening $\Rightarrow$ used in spring steels.
- **Mn, P, Sb, Sn, As** $\Rightarrow$ Promote **temper embrittlement** (see Section 2.1.5).

### 2.1.4 Hollomon–Jaffe Tempering Parameter

The combined effect of time and temperature on tempering is captured by the **Hollomon–Jaffe parameter**:

$$ H_P = T (\log t + C) $$

where:
- $T$ = absolute tempering temperature in **Kelvin**
- $t$ = soaking time in **hours**
- $C$ = a constant depending on carbon content (typically $C \approx 20$ for eutectoid steel)

A *single value of $H_P$ produces the same hardness*, regardless of the individual $T$ and $t$ combination. This is the basis for accelerated tempering.

### 2.1.5 Temper Embrittlement — Two Distinct Phenomena

| Type | Temperature Range | Cause | Affected Steels | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tempered Martensite Embrittlement (TME)** | $250$ – $400\ °C$ | Film of carbide ($\text{Fe}_3\text{C}$) at prior-austenite grain boundaries; unstable retained austenite decomposition | All carbon & low-alloy steels | Avoid tempering in 250–400 °C range; use rapid heating/short soak |
| **Temper Embrittlement (TE / ITE — Intergranular)** | $400$ – $575\ °C$ (slow cooling through) | Segregation of **P, Sn, Sb, As** to prior-austenite grain boundaries | Mn-, Cr-, Ni-, Mn-Ni alloy steels | Add Mo (~0.5%); *rapid cool* through 400–575 °C range |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often *interchange* TME and ITE. TME is *intragrain* (within the martensite plate) and *unavoidable except by avoiding the temperature range*. ITE is *intergranular* and *mitigated* by Mo addition or rapid cooling. KTU examiners often test this distinction.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Mechanics of Hardenability

### 2.2.1 The Critical Cooling Rate (CCR)

The **Critical Cooling Rate (CCR)** is the *minimum cooling rate* at which the steel must be cooled from the austenitizing temperature to avoid the *nose* of the TTT (Time-Temperature-Transformation) diagram, thereby forcing 100% martensitic transformation.

$$ \text{CCR} = \frac{A_{e1} - \text{Ms}}{t_{\text{nose}}} $$

A **low CCR** $\Rightarrow$ **high hardenability** (steel transforms to martensite even with slow cooling).
A **high CCR** $\Rightarrow$ **low hardenability** (needs drastic quenching).

### 2.2.2 Factors Affecting Hardenability

| Factor | Effect on Hardenability | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| ↑ Carbon content (up to 0.8%) | Increases | Shifts nose to longer times |
| ↑ Austenite grain size (coarse) | Increases dramatically | Fewer grain-boundary nucleation sites |
| Austenitizing temperature ↑ | Increases | Dissolves more carbides; coarser grains |
| Alloying elements (Ni, Cr, Mo, Mn, Si, B) | All increase | Shift TTT nose to the right |
| Boron (even 0.001–0.003%) | Increases sharply | Suppresses ferrite nucleation at GBs |
| Inclusions, undissolved carbides | Decrease | Act as nucleation sites for ferrite/pearlite |
| Quench severity ($H$-value) | Does NOT change hardenability | Changes only the *outcome* at a given size |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quench severity ($H$-value, Grossmann)** — units of **inch⁻¹**:
> - Still water: $H \approx 1.0$
> - Brine (10% NaCl): $H \approx 2.0$
> - Mineral oil: $H \approx 0.35$
> - Still air: $H \approx 0.02$
> - Polymer (PAG) quench: $H \approx 0.15$ – $0.30$

### 2.2.3 The Jominy End-Quench Test (ASTM A255)

This is the **most important hardenability test** in KTU syllabus.

**Procedure:**
1. A cylindrical specimen, **$\phi 25\ \text{mm} \times 100\ \text{mm}$** (1 inch dia × 4 inch long), with a small flange on one end.
2. **Austenitize** at the recommended temperature (typically 800–900 °C).
3. Transfer rapidly (≤ 5 s) to the **Jominy fixture**, where a controlled jet of water (room temperature) is sprayed on the *bottom end face only*.
4. After full cooling, **two parallel flats** are ground (depth 0.4 mm) along the specimen length.
5. **Rockwell C hardness** is measured at intervals (1/16 inch = 1.58 mm) from the quenched end.
6. A plot of *HRC vs Distance from quenched end* yields the **Jominy curve**.

### 2.2.4 Grossmann's Hardenability — Ideal Critical Diameter ($D_I$)

For a *given steel grade*, the **largest bar diameter that will form 50% martensite at the center** under an *ideal quench* ($H = \infty$) is called the **Ideal Critical Diameter**, $D_I$.

For a *real* quenching medium, the **actual critical diameter** $D_C$ is computed as:

$$ D_C = D_I \times f $$

where $f$ = **severity factor (quench factor)** of the medium:

| Quench Medium | Severity Factor $f$ |
|---|---|
| Ideal (Brine at $H = \infty$) | $1.00$ |
| Still brine | $0.90$ – $1.00$ |
| Still water | $0.45$ – $0.60$ |
| Forced oil | $0.30$ – $0.40$ |
| Still oil | $0.20$ – $0.35$ |
| Still air | $\approx 0.02$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** $D_C$ is what the design engineer uses. Given a bar diameter and a quench medium, you can read the Jominy curve to find the *expected center hardness* and decide whether the steel grade has *sufficient hardenability* for the application. This is the *exact* question KTU asks in 14-mark problems.

### 2.2.5 Multiplying Factors for Alloying Elements

Grossmann's method provides a *base* $D_I$ for plain-carbon steels (from carbon content and grain size), then **multiplies** by factors for each alloying element:

$$ D_I(\text{alloy}) = D_I(\text{plain-C}) \times f_{\text{Mn}} \times f_{\text{Si}} \times f_{\text{Cr}} \times f_{\text{Ni}} \times f_{\text{Mo}} \times f_{\text{V}} \times f_{\text{B}} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Memorize the qualitative trend — *all common alloying elements increase $D_I$ (multiplying factor > 1)*. Boron is unique — *even traces (10–30 ppm) boost $D_I$ substantially*, and is the cheapest way to increase hardenability.

### 2.2.6 Applications of Hardenability Concept

| Application | Hardenability Requirement |
|---|---|
| Small drill bits, razor blades | *Low* (shallow hardening, no risk of quench-crack) |
| Large gears, axles, crankshafts | *High* (must harden throughout section) |
| Cold-work dies | Moderate; high surface hardness needed |
| Carburized parts | Surface only — core hardenability is moderate |
| Through-hardened spring steel | Very high (e.g., AISI 5160, 9260) |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables / Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hollomon–Jaffe parameter | $H_P = T(\log t + C)$ | $T$ in K, $t$ in h, $C \approx 20$ | Predicts tempering hardness; basis of accelerated tempering |
| 2 | Critical Cooling Rate | $\text{CCR} = \dfrac{A_{e1} - M_s}{t_{\text{nose}}}$ | K/s or °C/s | Lowers with alloy additions |
| 3 | Actual critical diameter | $D_C = D_I \times f$ | $D_I$, $D_C$ in inches or mm; $f$ dimensionless | Predicts through-hardening in real quench |
| 4 | Multiplying-factor for alloy | $D_I = D_I^0 \prod f_i$ | $f_i$ dimensionless | Calculate hardenability of alloy steel |
| 5 | 50% martensite criterion | $H = H_{50} = 0.5 H_{max} + 0.5 H_{\text{min}}$ | HRC | Defines depth of hardening (Jominy $D_I$ reference point) |
| 6 | Severity factor | $f = f(D_I, H)$ | Read from Grossmann chart | Links medium to result |
| 7 | Cooling rate (Fourier, simplified) | $\dfrac{dT}{dt} = \dfrac{h A}{\rho V c_p} (T - T_{\text{quench}})$ | K/s, $h$ = HTC, $A/V$ ratio | Governs severity of quench |
| 8 | Tempering color vs $T$ | Empirical (color vs $T$ table) | °C | Field identification |
| 9 | Jominy correlation to bar | $J \leftrightarrow D$ via Lamont chart | mm or 1/16 in | Convert Jominy reading to bar hardness |
| 10 | Martensite fraction (Koistinen-Marburger) | $f_M = 1 - \exp[-\alpha (M_s - T_q)]$ | $f_M$ dimensionless, $T_q$ in K | Quantify incomplete transformation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Reminder:** For all numerical problems in KTU ESE, *state units explicitly* and quote the relevant table value. Marks are deducted in Section A for unit omissions.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Automotive Industry:** AISI 4140 (Cr-Mo steel) with $D_I \approx 3$ – $5$ inches is chosen for *crankshafts* because a 75 mm diameter bar must harden *throughout* in oil quench. A 1040 plain-C steel with $D_I \approx 0.5$ inch would fail.
2. **Cutting Tool Industry:** M2 high-speed steel (with high W, Mo, V) achieves *secondary hardening* peak of HRC 65 at $560\ °C$ tempering — allowing the tool to *retain hardness at red-heat* during machining.
3. **Aerospace:** AISI 4340 (Ni-Cr-Mo) with $D_I \approx 6$ inches is used for *landing gear* — through-hardening in oil with subsequent tempering for fatigue.
4. **Energy Sector:** Cr-Mo-V rotor steels (e.g., 1% CrMoV) are *tempered at 700 °C for 20+ hours* to ensure bainitic/tempered structure for creep resistance at 540 °C operation.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1: Calculating Actual Critical Diameter $D_C$

**Problem Statement (KTU-pattern 14-mark sub-part):**
A steel has a Grossmann ideal critical diameter $D_I = 3.0$ inches. It is used in a bar of diameter $2.5$ inches, quenched in (a) still water, (b) still oil, and (c) still air. Determine whether 50% martensite forms at the center in each case. Use Grossmann severity factors $f_{\text{water}} = 0.50$, $f_{\text{oil}} = 0.30$, $f_{\text{air}} = 0.02$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the relation between actual and ideal critical diameter.**

The actual critical diameter $D_C$ of a bar in a given quench medium is given by:

$$ D_C = D_I \times f $$

where $D_I$ is the ideal critical diameter (for an ideal quench, $f = 1.0$) and $f$ is the **severity factor** of the quench medium, $0 < f \le 1$.

**Step 2 — Apply the criterion for through-hardening.**

A bar of actual diameter $D$ forms 50% martensite at its center if and only if:

$$ D \le D_C $$

If $D > D_C$, the center will *not* form 50% martensite; softer phases (pearlite, ferrite, bainite) will dominate at the core.

**Step 3 — Compute $D_C$ for each medium.**

For (a) **Still Water**, with $f = 0.50$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_C(\text{water}) &= D_I \times f_{\text{water}} \\
&= 3.0 \times 0.50 \\
&= 1.50\ \text{inches}
\end{aligned}
$$

For (b) **Still Oil**, with $f = 0.30$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_C(\text{oil}) &= D_I \times f_{\text{oil}} \\
&= 3.0 \times 0.30 \\
&= 0.90\ \text{inches}
\end{aligned}
$$

For (c) **Still Air**, with $f = 0.02$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_C(\text{air}) &= D_I \times f_{\text{air}} \\
&= 3.0 \times 0.02 \\
&= 0.06\ \text{inches}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare each $D_C$ with the bar diameter $D = 2.5$ inches.**

| Medium | $D_C$ (inches) | $D$ (inches) | Comparison | Result at Center |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Still water | $1.50$ | $2.50$ | $D > D_C$ | Does **NOT** form 50% martensite |
| Still oil | $0.90$ | $2.50$ | $D > D_C$ | Does **NOT** form 50% martensite |
| Still air | $0.06$ | $2.50$ | $D \gg D_C$ | Definitely does **NOT** harden |

**Step 5 — Engineering Conclusion.**

The chosen steel (with $D_I = 3.0$ in) is *not adequately hardenable* in this section size under any of the three media. The designer has three options:
- *Choose a higher-alloy steel* (e.g., AISI 4340, $D_I \approx 5$ – $6$ in).
- *Reduce section size*.
- *Use a more drastic quench* (e.g., brine, $f \approx 0.9$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Student Error:** Forgetting to use $D_C \le D$ (not $D_I \le D$) when the quench is non-ideal. This costs 2 marks per error in KTU board valuation.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2: Hollomon–Jaffe Tempering Parameter Equivalence

**Problem Statement:**
A steel was tempered at $600\ °C$ for $2$ hours and achieved a hardness of HRC 32. Using the Hollomon–Jaffe parameter, find the equivalent time at $500\ °C$ to obtain the same hardness. Assume $C = 20$ for this steel.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the Hollomon–Jaffe parameter expression.**

$$ H_P = T (\log_{10} t + C) $$

where $T$ is in **Kelvin**, $t$ is in **hours**, and $C = 20$.

**Step 2 — Convert $T_1 = 600\ °C$ to Kelvin.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= 600 + 273 \\
&= 873\ \text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $H_P$ for the first condition.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_P &= T_1 (\log_{10} t_1 + C) \\
&= 873 \times (\log_{10} 2 + 20) \\
&= 873 \times (0.3010 + 20) \\
&= 873 \times 20.3010 \\
&= 17722.8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert $T_2 = 500\ °C$ to Kelvin.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_2 &= 500 + 273 \\
&= 773\ \text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Set $H_P$ equal and solve for $t_2$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_P &= T_2 (\log_{10} t_2 + C) \\
17722.8 &= 773 \times (\log_{10} t_2 + 20)
\end{aligned}
$$

Isolating the bracket:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10} t_2 + 20 &= \frac{17722.8}{773} \\
&= 22.927
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10} t_2 &= 22.927 - 20 \\
&= 2.927
\end{aligned}
$$

Exponentiating (base 10):

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_2 &= 10^{2.927} \\
&\approx 845\ \text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result.**

Reducing the tempering temperature from $600\ °C$ to $500\ °C$ (only 100 °C lower) increases the required tempering time by a factor of $\sim 423$ to obtain the *same hardness*. This is why **high-temperature short-time** tempering is preferred in industry — it is more energy-efficient and avoids temper embrittlement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The Hollomon–Jaffe parameter is rarely asked for direct numerical calculation in 14-mark questions, but is a *favorite 3-mark conceptual question* — *"State and explain the Hollomon–Jaffe tempering parameter."*

---

## 3.3 Analytical Problem 3: Reading the Jominy Curve

**Problem Statement:**
The Jominy end-quench test was performed on an AISI 4140 steel specimen. The following hardness readings (HRC) were obtained:

| Jominy Distance (1/16 in) | 1 | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 | 12 | 16 | 20 | 24 | 32 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| HRC | 58 | 57 | 55 | 52 | 47 | 42 | 38 | 33 | 30 | 28 | 25 |

Estimate:
1. The hardness at the quenched end.
2. The Jominy distance corresponding to 50% martensite (≈ HRC 40 for this steel).
3. The maximum bar diameter that will through-harden in oil quench (using Lamont chart equivalence $D \approx 1.2 \times J$ in inches for oil-quench correlation).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the quenched-end hardness.**

The first reading (at $J = 1$, i.e., 1/16 inch from the quenched end) is **HRC 58**. For AISI 4140, the maximum martensitic hardness is typically $H_{max} \approx 60$ HRC. The slight loss from 60 to 58 at $J = 1$ is due to **autotempering** and minor surface decarburization. Hence:

$$ H_{\text{quench end}} \approx 58\ \text{HRC} $$

**Step 2 — Locate the 50% martensite point.**

For AISI 4140 with $H_{max} = 60$ HRC, the 50% martensite hardness is:

$$ H_{50} \approx 0.5 \times (60) + 0.5 \times H_{\text{min}} $$

where $H_{\text{min}}$ (fully pearlitic) $\approx 20$ HRC. Therefore:

$$ H_{50} \approx 40\ \text{HRC} $$

From the data table, HRC 42 occurs at $J = 10$ (10/16 in = 0.625 in from quenched end) and HRC 38 at $J = 12$ (12/16 in = 0.75 in). Interpolating linearly for HRC 40:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_{50} &= 10 + \frac{42 - 40}{42 - 38} \times (12 - 10) \\
&= 10 + \frac{2}{4} \times 2 \\
&= 10 + 1 \\
&= 11\ \text{(1/16 inch units)}
\end{aligned}
$$

So:

$$ J_{50} = \frac{11}{16}\ \text{inch} \approx 0.6875\ \text{inch} \approx 17.5\ \text{mm} $$

**Step 3 — Convert Jominy distance to bar diameter using Lamont chart.**

For oil quench, the empirical correlation is $D \approx 1.2 \times J$ in inches. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{max}}(\text{oil}) &\approx 1.2 \times 0.6875 \\
&\approx 0.825\ \text{inches} \\
&\approx 21\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**

A bar of AISI 4140 with diameter up to $\sim 21\ \text{mm}$ will form 50% martensite at its center when quenched in oil. For larger diameters, water or brine quench is required (or a higher-hardenability steel such as AISI 4340 must be chosen).

> [!NOTE]
> **Lamont Chart vs Grossmann:** Both methods are *approximate*; for *exact* design, the engineer uses the *Grossmann chart* for the bar geometry and a *Jominy-to-round-bar* conversion chart. KTU expects the *correlation constant approach* for time-bound exams.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Problem 4: Carbon Content vs Maximum Hardness (Empirical)

**Problem Statement:**
Using the empirical Krauss approximation, the maximum attainable hardness of a quenched steel is given by:

$$ H_{max}(\text{HRC}) = 20 + 60 \times (\% C \text{ by weight}) $$

Calculate $H_{max}$ for (a) $0.4\%$ C steel and (b) $1.0\%$ C steel. Comment on the practical significance.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step (a) — For 0.4% C:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{max} &= 20 + 60 \times 0.4 \\
&= 20 + 24 \\
&= 44\ \text{HRC}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (b) — For 1.0% C:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{max} &= 20 + 60 \times 1.0 \\
&= 20 + 60 \\
&= 80\ \text{HRC}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Practical comment.**

The relationship shows that *hardness scales linearly* with carbon content up to ~0.8% C (eutectoid), then plateaus due to retained austenite. For high-carbon tool steels ($>0.6\%$ C), the *as-quenched* hardness can be *lower than the calculated $H_{max}$* because retained austenite (soft, FCC) persists. **Sub-zero treatment** (cryogenic, e.g., liquid N₂ at -196 °C) is used to convert RA to martensite, restoring full hardness.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Problem 5: Severity Factor and Cooling Rate

**Problem Statement:**
A cylindrical bar of diameter $D = 50$ mm is quenched from austenitizing temperature in still water. The Biot number is $\text{Bi} = 0.45$. The austenitizing temperature is $850\ °C$ and water temperature is $25\ °C$. Calculate the approximate *time* for the center to reach $200\ °C$ (below nose of TTT). Use the lumped-capacitance approximation:

$$ \frac{T - T_{\infty}}{T_i - T_{\infty}} = \exp\left(-\frac{hA}{\rho V c_p} t\right) $$

Assume $h = 1000\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$, $\rho = 7850\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 500\ \text{J/kgK}$, $A/V = 4/D = 0.08\ \text{mm}^{-1}$ (note: convert to m⁻¹ $\Rightarrow 80\ \text{m}^{-1}$).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — State the temperature ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{T - T_{\infty}}{T_i - T_{\infty}} &= \frac{200 - 25}{850 - 25} \\
&= \frac{175}{825} \\
&= 0.212
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the time constant $\tau = \dfrac{\rho V c_p}{h A}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau &= \frac{\rho c_p}{h \cdot (A/V)} \\
&= \frac{7850 \times 500}{1000 \times 80} \\
&= \frac{3.925 \times 10^6}{80000} \\
&= 49.06\ \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $t$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\exp\left(-\frac{t}{\tau}\right) &= 0.212 \\
-\frac{t}{\tau} &= \ln(0.212) \\
&= -1.551
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= 1.551 \times \tau \\
&= 1.551 \times 49.06 \\
&\approx 76.1\ \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the nose of TTT diagram.**

For a typical 0.4% C plain-C steel, the nose of the TTT diagram is at $\sim 550\ °C$ with a critical time of $\sim 1$ second. Since the bar center takes $76\ \text{s}$ to cool to $200\ °C$, the *local cooling rate at the nose* is roughly $\sim 8\ °C/\text{s}$ (slow), which is *insufficient* to avoid pearlite nose for a 0.4% C steel. Hence, *the center does not form martensite*, and the bar has *insufficient hardenability* for this size.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This type of calculation rarely appears verbatim in KTU, but the *qualitative result* (a slow-cooling center misses the nose $\Rightarrow$ pearlite forms) is asked repeatedly.
