# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define tempering. List the four stages of tempering with their corresponding temperature ranges.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Tempering is a sub-critical heat treatment process in which quench-hardened steel is reheated to a temperature below the lower critical temperature $A_1$ ($723\ °C$), held for a controlled time, and then cooled (usually in air) to improve toughness and relieve internal stresses at the expense of a small loss in hardness.

The four stages of tempering are:

| Stage | Temperature Range (°C) | Phenomenon |
|---|---|---|
| Stage 1 | $100$ – $250$ | Precipitation of $\varepsilon$-carbide ($\text{Fe}_{2.4}\text{C}$) from supersaturated martensite |
| Stage 2 | $200$ – $300$ | Decomposition of retained austenite to bainitic ferrite + cementite |
| Stage 3 | $250$ – $400$ | Cementite precipitation and coarsening |
| Stage 4 | $400$ – $700$ | Spheroidization of cementite and recrystallization of ferrite |

*(1 mark for definition + 1.5 marks for stages with temperature ranges + 0.5 mark for phenomena.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Distinguish between hardness and hardenability.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Hardness | Hardenability |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Resistance of a material to localized plastic deformation (indentation) | Ability of a steel to form martensite throughout the section on quenching |
| Type of property | *Material property* (intrinsic) | *Steel grade + section + quench* property |
| Measure | HRC, HB, HV (at a point) | Jominy distance, $D_I$ (depth) |
| Affected by | Heat treatment, carbon content, work hardening | Alloying elements, grain size, carbon content, austenitizing temperature |
| Example | A glass plate is hard; a rubber ball is soft | A 0.4% C steel bar in oil may not through-harden, even if surface is "hard" |

*(2 marks for table + 1 mark for concluding example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]`

**(a) Explain the Jominy end-quench test for measuring hardenability with a neat sketch. State the procedure, plot the typical Jominy curve, and explain the significance of the curve. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
The Jominy end-quench test (standardized by **ASTM A255**) is the most widely used experimental method to measure the hardenability of steels. The result is presented as a *Jominy curve* — a plot of hardness vs distance from the quenched end.

**2. Specimen (0.5 mark):**
A standard cylindrical specimen of dimensions $\phi 25\ \text{mm} \times 100\ \text{mm}$ (1 inch × 4 inch) with a small flange/collar on the upper end for hanging in the fixture.

**3. Procedure (3 marks):**
1. **Austenitize:** Heat the specimen in a furnace at the *recommended austenitizing temperature* (typically $800$ – $900\ °C$ depending on grade) for 30 minutes to ensure uniform austenite.
2. **Transfer:** Quickly (within 5 seconds) transfer the specimen to the **Jominy fixture** so that the *bottom end face* is positioned 12.5 mm below a water jet nozzle.
3. **Quench:** Spray a column of water (room temperature, ~25 °C) from a height of 65 mm on the bottom face *only*. The rest of the specimen cools by conduction, radiation, and convection in still air.
4. **Cool fully:** Allow the specimen to cool completely to room temperature.
5. **Surface preparation:** Grind two parallel flats (depth 0.40 mm) along the length, $180°$ apart, to remove decarburized surface layer.
6. **Hardness measurement:** Measure **Rockwell C** hardness at intervals of 1/16 inch (1.5875 mm) along the flats, starting from the quenched end ($J = 1$) up to $J = 32$ (or as far as hardness levels off).
7. **Plot the curve:** Plot HRC (y-axis) vs Jominy distance $J$ in 1/16 inch (x-axis).

**4. Typical Jominy Curve Sketch (1.5 marks):**
The curve starts at maximum hardness (e.g., 60 HRC) near the quenched end, remains nearly flat for a while, and then *drops* to a lower plateau (e.g., 25 HRC) at large distances.

**Significance of the curve (1 mark):**
- The *initial flat portion* (low $J$) shows the *fully martensitic* region.
- The *steep drop* corresponds to the *mixed structure* (martensite + bainite + pearlite).
- The *final plateau* corresponds to *pearlitic/bainitic* structure (low hardness).
- *Steep drop* = *low hardenability*; *gradual drop* = *high hardenability*.
- The Jominy curve can be converted to a *bar-end-quench* or *round-bar* hardness distribution using a **Lamont chart** for actual engineering design.

---

**(b) Explain the different stages of tempering with a labeled microstructural diagram. Discuss the effect of alloying elements on tempering. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. As-Quenched Starting Structure (0.5 mark):**
Quenched steel consists of *BCT martensite* (supersaturated with carbon, high dislocation density) + *retained austenite* (FCC, soft). This is hard (HRC 60+) but extremely brittle.

**2. The Four Stages of Tempering (4 marks):**

| Stage | Temp (°C) | Microstructural Change | Effect on Properties |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | $100$ – $250$ | Precipitation of transition $\varepsilon$-carbide ($\text{Fe}_{2.4}\text{C}$); tempered martensite (tempered color: pale yellow → brown) | Slight hardness loss; stress relief begins |
| **2** | $200$ – $300$ | Decomposition of retained austenite to bainitic ferrite + cementite | Possible secondary hardening; dimensional change |
| **3** | $250$ – $400$ | Cementite ($\text{Fe}_3\text{C}$) precipitates and coarsens; recovery of ferrite | Significant hardness drop, toughness up |
| **4** | $400$ – $700$ | Spheroidization of cementite, recrystallization of ferrite | Further softening, maximum ductility |

> **Microstructural diagram (described):** Sketch showing (a) needle-like as-quenched martensite → (b) tempered martensite with fine $\varepsilon$-carbide particles → (c) tempered martensite with cementite dots → (d) equiaxed ferrite grains with spheroidized cementite.

**3. Effect of Alloying Elements on Tempering (2 marks):**

- **Carbide formers (Cr, Mo, V, W, Ti):** Form *fine, stable alloy carbides* ($M_{23}C_6$, $MC$) at $500$ – $600\ °C$ → **secondary hardening peak** (HSS tools, M2 steel). *Retard softening* significantly.
- **Si** (non-carbide former but carbide-coarsening inhibitor): Shifts Stage 3 to higher temperatures, used in *spring steels*.
- **Ni, Mn (mild):** Shift softening to higher temperatures, but Mn promotes *tempered martensite embrittlement* (TME).
- **P, Sb, Sn, As (impurities):** Promote **temper embrittlement** (ITE) in the $400$ – $575\ °C$ range.
- **Mo (0.5%):** Mitigates temper embrittlement (ITE).

**4. Concluding Remark (0.5 mark):**
Tempering is essential to convert brittle, stressed as-quenched martensite into a tough, dimensionally stable microstructure suitable for engineering service. Alloying elements can be tailored to either *enhance* tempering resistance (high-speed steels) or *avoid* embrittlement (Cr-Mo-V rotor steels).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Internal Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Define hardenability. Explain Grossmann's method of determining hardenability. Discuss multiplying factors for alloying elements. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
Hardenability is the property of a steel that determines the *depth and distribution of hardness* obtained on quenching. It is a measure of the *ease with which martensite can be formed throughout the section*.

**2. Grossmann's Method (4 marks):**

Grossmann's method is a *quantitative, calculation-based* approach to hardenability. The central concept is the **Ideal Critical Diameter, $D_I$**.

**Definition of $D_I$:** $D_I$ is the largest diameter of a round bar in which 50% martensite forms at the *center*, when quenched in an *ideal quench* (i.e., the surface is instantly brought to bath temperature — equivalent to $H = \infty$, $f = 1$).

**Procedure:**
1. Look up the *base $D_I$* for the *plain-carbon equivalent* of the steel from the Grossmann chart, based on its *carbon content* and *austenite grain size* (ASTM No.).
2. Determine the *multiplying factors* $f_i$ for each alloying element present (e.g., $f_{Mn}$, $f_{Si}$, $f_{Cr}$, $f_{Ni}$, $f_{Mo}$, $f_{V}$).
3. Compute the *alloyed $D_I$*:

$$ D_I(\text{alloy}) = D_I^0 \times f_{Mn} \times f_{Si} \times f_{Cr} \times f_{Ni} \times f_{Mo} \times f_{V} \times f_{B} $$

4. Look up the *severity factor* $f$ of the chosen quench medium (water, oil, brine, etc.).
5. Compute the *actual critical diameter* $D_C$:

$$ D_C = D_I \times f $$

6. Compare with the *actual bar diameter* $D$. If $D \le D_C$, the bar through-hardens with 50% martensite at the center.

**3. Multiplying Factors for Alloying Elements (1.5 marks):**

| Element | Multiplying Factor (typical, for moderate %) |
|---|---|
| Mn | $1.2$ – $4.0$ (very strong) |
| Si | $1.1$ – $1.5$ |
| Cr | $1.2$ – $3.0$ |
| Ni | $1.1$ – $1.6$ |
| Mo | $1.3$ – $2.0$ |
| V | $1.2$ – $1.5$ |
| B (0.001 – 0.003%) | $1.5$ – $3.0$ (extremely effective) |

All multiplying factors are > 1 (all alloying elements *increase* hardenability).

**4. Comparison with Jominy (0.5 mark):**
The Grossmann method is *approximate* but fast (uses charts/tables), while the Jominy method is *experimental* and more accurate. For *new grades* and *research*, Jominy is preferred; for *shop-floor quick design*, Grossmann is used.

---

**(b) Explain temper embrittlement. Discuss the two types — Tempered Martensite Embrittlement (TME) and Temper Embrittlement (TE) — with their causes, temperature ranges, and remedies. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Introduction (0.5 mark):**
Temper embrittlement refers to the *reduction in toughness* of a hardened-and-tempered steel when tempered (or cooled) in a *specific temperature range*. It is one of the most studied phenomena in physical metallurgy because it can lead to *catastrophic brittle fracture* in service (e.g., rotor steels in power plants, turbine discs in aerospace).

**2. Type 1 — Tempered Martensite Embrittlement (TME / 350 °C embrittlement) (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| Temperature range | $250$ – $400\ °C$ (peak near $350\ °C$) |
| Cause | Precipitation of a *thin film* of cementite ($\text{Fe}_3\text{C}$) along *prior-austenite grain boundaries* AND decomposition of interlath retained austenite to brittle carbides. |
| Affected steels | *All* quenched carbon and low-alloy steels, regardless of composition. |
| Toughness loss | 30 – 50% reduction in Charpy impact energy. |
| Fracture mode | *Intergranular* (along prior-austenite grain boundaries). |
| Remedy | **Avoid** tempering in the $250$ – $400\ °C$ range. If a temper in this range is mandatory, use *short soaking time + rapid quench*. Alternatively, use *austempering* to bypass martensite formation. |

**3. Type 2 — Temper Embrittlement (TE / 500 °C embrittlement) (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| Temperature range | $400$ – $575\ °C$ (peak near $475$ – $500\ °C$) |
| Cause | *Grain-boundary segregation* of impurity elements — **P, Sn, Sb, As** — to prior-austenite grain boundaries. These reduce the *cohesive strength* of the GBs. |
| Affected steels | Mn-, Cr-, Ni-, and Mn-Ni-containing alloy steels (especially high-purity, low-S steels after long-time service at $400$ – $500\ °C$). |
| Toughness loss | Sharp *ductile-to-brittle transition temperature (DBTT)* shift to higher temperatures (loss of low-T toughness). |
| Fracture mode | *Intergranular* (along prior-austenite grain boundaries) — sometimes called *Intergranular Temper Embrittlement* (ITE). |
| Remedy | (a) **Add Mo** (~0.5%): Mo segregates to GBs and *blocks* the impurity atoms. (b) **Rapid cool** through the $400$ – $575\ °C$ range after tempering. (c) Use *high-purity* steel (low P, Sn, Sb, As content). |

**4. Comparative Summary Table (0.5 mark):**

| Parameter | TME (350 °C) | TE / ITE (500 °C) |
|---|---|---|
| Temp range | $250$ – $400\ °C$ | $400$ – $575\ °C$ |
| Cause | Carbide film on GBs | P, Sn, Sb, As segregation |
| Reversible? | Yes (re-temper at higher T) | No (irreversible) |
| Mitigation | Avoid the range | Add Mo; rapid cool |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse TME and TE.** They occur in *different temperature ranges*, have *different causes*, and require *different remedies*. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 2. **Do not say "tempering causes brittleness."** State precisely *which type* in *which range*. This is a 2-mark differentiator.
> 3. **Always quote the temperature range** ($250$ – $400\ °C$ for TME, $400$ – $575\ °C$ for TE) in your answer; missing this loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Avoid vague statements** like "alloying reduces embrittlement." Be specific: *Mo (0.5%) mitigates TE*; *Si (in spring steels) suppresses Stage 3 coarsening*; *Ni increases impact toughness but does not eliminate embrittlement*.
> 5. **In Jominy curve questions:** Always state the *specimen size* ($\phi 25 \times 100$ mm), *water jet height* (65 mm), and *distance interval* (1/16 inch). Missing any one of these loses 0.5–1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Print & Carry)**

- [ ] **Tempering** is reheating quench-hardened steel to a temperature *below* $A_1$ ($723\ °C$) to relieve stresses, decompose retained austenite, and improve toughness.
- [ ] **Four stages of tempering:** 1 = $\varepsilon$-carbide ($100$ – $250\ °C$); 2 = RA decomposition ($200$ – $300\ °C$); 3 = cementite precipitation/coarsening ($250$ – $400\ °C$); 4 = spheroidization + recrystallization ($400$ – $700\ °C$).
- [ ] **Tempering colors** (in order of increasing temperature): pale yellow $\rightarrow$ straw $\rightarrow$ brown $\rightarrow$ purple $\rightarrow$ dark blue $\rightarrow$ pale blue ($200$ – $320\ °C$).
- [ ] **Hollomon–Jaffe parameter:** $H_P = T (\log t + C)$ with $C \approx 20$; *same $H_P \Rightarrow$ same hardness*.
- [ ] **Secondary hardening** is a *peak* in hardness at $500$ – $600\ °C$ produced by *fine alloy carbides* (Cr, Mo, V, W) in HSS like M2.
- [ ] **TME** occurs at $250$ – $400\ °C$, is *unavoidable* except by avoiding the range; caused by *carbide film* at prior-A GBs.
- [ ] **TE / ITE** occurs at $400$ – $575\ °C$, caused by P/Sn/Sb/As segregation; mitigated by **0.5% Mo** and *rapid cooling*.
- [ ] **Hardenability ≠ Hardness.** Hardness is a *material/phase* property (HRC). Hardenability is a *steel-grade + section + quench* property (depth of martensite).
- [ ] **Critical Cooling Rate (CCR)** is the *minimum cooling rate* to miss the TTT nose and form 100% martensite.
- [ ] **Alloying elements** (Mn, Cr, Ni, Mo, Si, B) all *increase* hardenability. Boron is the most potent (cheap and effective at $10$ – $30$ ppm).
- [ ] **Coarse austenite grains** $\Rightarrow$ *higher* hardenability (fewer nucleation sites for ferrite/pearlite).
- [ ] **Jominy test** (ASTM A255): specimen $\phi 25 \times 100$ mm, austenitized, end-quenched with water, hardness measured at 1/16 inch intervals.
- [ ] **Jominy curve:** steep drop = low hardenability; gradual drop = high hardenability.
- [ ] **Grossmann's $D_I$** = largest bar diameter for 50% martensite at center in *ideal quench*; obtained from base $D_I$ × $\prod f_i$ (alloy factors).
- [ ] **$D_C = D_I \times f$**, where $f$ = severity factor of the quench medium (water = 0.5, oil = 0.3, air = 0.02).
- [ ] **Quench severity (H-value):** brine > water > oil > polymer > air. Severity factor $f$ follows the same order.
- [ ] **Applications of heat treatment** (high-frequency 3-marker): gears/shafts $\to$ induction/case hardening; tools $\to$ quench + low-T temper; springs $\to$ quench + 400 – 500 °C temper; boiler tubes $\to$ normalizing; turbine blades $\to$ solution + aging.
- [ ] **Selection rules for heat treatment:**
  - $C < 0.25\% \to$ cannot be through-hardened $\to$ use *carburizing*.
  - $0.25 \le C \le 0.6\% \to$ suitable for quench + temper.
  - $C > 0.6\% \to$ tools/dies, risk of cracking, may need cryo-treatment.
- [ ] **Lamont chart** converts *Jominy distance $J$* to *equivalent round-bar center hardness* for a given quench medium.
- [ ] **Common 3-mark traps:** (i) confusing hardness with hardenability; (ii) stating "tempering causes embrittlement" without specifying *TME* or *ITE*; (iii) forgetting that the Jominy specimen is *not* cooled in the fixture — only the bottom face is sprayed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Jominy End Quench Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT526 - METALLURGY AND MATERIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Jominy End Quench Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Jominy End Quench Test** (standardized by ASTM A255) is a standardized experimental procedure used to determine the **hardenability** of a ferrous alloy (primarily steels). Hardenability is defined as the ability of a steel to form martensite when quenched from a temperature above its critical (austenitizing) temperature, characterized by the depth and distribution of hardness obtained through quenching.

The test evaluates how deeply a steel can be hardened below the surface and provides a comparative measure of the steel's response to rapid cooling, plotted as hardness versus distance from the quenched end (called the **Jominy distance**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardenability vs. Hardness — A Critical Distinction for KTU Exams**
> - **Hardness** is a material property measuring resistance to localized plastic deformation (e.g., HRC value at a point).
> - **Hardenability** is a metallurgical property indicating the *depth* and *distribution* of hardness achievable under specified quenching conditions. It depends primarily on the **alloying content** and **grain size**, NOT on the cooling medium's severity.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine baking a thick chocolate cake. When you take it out of a hot oven and place it on a cold marble counter, the surface touching the marble cools almost instantly (becoming firm and "set"), but the top center remains hot and soft for a long time. If you sliced the cake and measured firmness at each level, you would find a gradient — firm at the bottom, soft at the top.

**The Jominy test works exactly like this, but for steel.**

- The bottom (quenched end) of a cylindrical steel specimen is sprayed with a jet of cold water.
- That end cools **very fast** — the austenite transforms into hard, brittle **martensite**.
- As you move *up* the specimen, away from the water jet, the cooling rate slows down because heat has to travel through more metal before escaping.
- At slow cooling rates, softer microstructures like **pearlite**, **ferrite**, or **bainite** form instead of martensite.
- The result: a hardness *gradient* from the quenched end (hard) to the far end (soft).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Test Parameters (Recall these for KTU exams):**
> - Specimen: Cylindrical, **25 mm (1 inch)** diameter, **100 mm (4 inches)** long.
> - Austenitizing Temperature: Typically **800 °C to 900 °C** (depends on steel composition).
> - Austenitizing Time: ~**30–45 minutes** in a controlled atmosphere furnace.
> - Cooling: Water jet at the bottom end, with a **free water height of ~65 mm**, nozzle diameter **12.5 mm**.
> - Hardness measured on **two parallel flats** ground along the length, at intervals from the quenched end.
> - Standard measurement points: Jominy distances of **1.5, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50 mm**, etc.

### 1.3 Why the Jominy Test Matters in Engineering

A KTU examiner expects you to know *why* this test exists. The hardenability of a steel dictates:

- The **type of quenching medium** (water, oil, polymer, air) needed.
- The **section thickness** that can be fully hardened through.
- Whether expensive **alloy steels** are needed (e.g., for gears, axles, crankshafts).
- The **distortion and cracking risk** during quenching.

In a KTU answer, always tie hardenability back to its practical application — selecting a steel grade for a specific component (gear, shaft, tool) that will be quenched.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Test Specimen and Preparation (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Specimen Geometry**: A standard round bar, **25.4 mm diameter × 101.6 mm length**, with a small flange/collar at one end for suspension in the furnace.
2. **Heating (Austenitization)**: The specimen is heated above the upper critical temperature (Ac₃) to obtain a uniform **austenitic (γ-FCC) phase**. Holding time: ~**20–30 minutes** after reaching temperature, to ensure carbon dissolution.
3. **Quenching Setup**: The specimen is transferred rapidly to a fixture where a controlled water jet strikes one end (the **quenched end**).
4. **Cooling Profile Generation**: Heat is extracted axially along the bar — the quenched end experiences the highest cooling rate (~**600 °C/s** at 700 °C), while points further away cool progressively slower.
5. **Hardness Survey**: After cooling to room temperature, **two parallel flats** are ground (depth ~**0.4 mm** below the original surface) along the length, on opposite sides. Hardness is measured (typically **Rockwell C scale, HRC**) at standardized Jominy distances.
6. **Plotting**: Hardness (HRC) is plotted on the y-axis vs. Jominy distance (in mm or 1/16 inch units) on the x-axis → the famous **Jominy hardenability curve** (or **S-curve**).

### 2.2 Microstructural Evolution Along the Bar

| Region (Jominy Distance) | Cooling Rate at ~700 °C | Resulting Microstructure | Hardness |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0–5 mm | Very high (>300 °C/s) | **100% Martensite** | Maximum (~HRC 60–65) |
| 5–15 mm | High (50–300 °C/s) | **Martensite + minor bainite** | High (HRC 50–60) |
| 15–25 mm | Medium (10–50 °C/s) | **Bainite + martensite** | Intermediate (HRC 35–50) |
| >25 mm | Low (<10 °C/s) | **Ferrite + Pearlite** | Low (HRC 20–35) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cooling Rate Reference (Memorize this range for KTU):**
> - At 700 °C (nose of the TTT diagram), the cooling rate at the quenched end ≈ **~600 °C/s**.
> - At 25 mm from quenched end: ~ **3 °C/s**.
> - The critical cooling rate for martensite formation is reached only at the quenched end (and nearby region) for plain carbon steels.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below summarizes all critical equations and conversions for the Jominy End Quench Test. The empirical relationship between Jominy distance and cooling rate is **exam-favorite** content.

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Jominy distance (J) — unit conversion | $J \text{ (in 1/16 inch)} = 16 \times d \text{ (in inches)}$ | $d$ = distance from quenched end |
| Cooling rate at 700 °C (empirical, Grossman) | $\log_{10}(CR) = -0.72 \cdot \log_{10}(J) + 2.61$ | $CR$ in °C/s; $J$ in 1/16 inch (Jominy distance) |
| Cooling rate at 700 °C — alternative | $CR \approx \dfrac{600}{J + 10}$ for $J$ in mm (approx) | Used for quick KTU estimates |
| Ideal Critical Diameter ($D_I$) | $D_I = D_o \cdot f_{Mn} \cdot f_{Si} \cdot f_{Ni} \cdot f_{Cr} \cdot f_{Mo} \cdot f_{V}$ | $D_o$ = base diameter from carbon content |
| Multiplicative Hardenability Factor (Grossman) | $f_i = $ factor for each alloying element (from tables) | Product of all $f_i$ for alloyed steel |
| Carbon content (C) → $D_o$ lookup | Plain carbon steel: $D_o$ rises steeply with C up to ~0.8% | $D_o$ maxes at eutectoid composition |
| Hardness from Jominy at $J = 0$ | $HRC \approx 60 \cdot C\% + 20$ (rough rule) | Approximation only |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Alloy Steel Selection**: The Jominy curve lets metallurgists choose between a cheap plain-carbon steel (sharp hardness drop-off) vs. an alloy steel (gradual, deep hardness). For a **50 mm shaft** that must be hard throughout, an alloy steel like **4340** is mandatory.
2. **Heat Treatment Process Design**: From the curve, engineers determine the **quenching medium** (water, oil, polymer). Sharp curve → use oil to avoid cracking. Gradual curve → water quench acceptable.
3. **Quality Control**: Steel mills use Jominy tests to certify batches — if the measured curve deviates beyond a tolerance band, the batch is rejected.
4. **Failure Analysis**: After a heat-treated component fails (e.g., a gear tooth), the Jominy curve of the original steel is consulted to check if the heat treater received suitable material.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Jominy Distance vs. Cooling Rate Relationship

The empirical formula derived from extensive experimental data (originally by Grossman, later refined) relates the cooling rate at **700 °C** to the Jominy distance. The derivation proceeds from a heat-flow analysis of a semi-infinite cylinder losing heat to a constant-temperature boundary.

> **Physical Setup:** A cylindrical bar of radius $r$ is quenched at one end. The temperature at distance $x$ from the quenched end, at time $t$, satisfies the 1-D heat conduction equation.

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the thermal diffusivity (m²/s) of the steel.

**Boundary Conditions (Step-by-Step):**

- At $x = 0$ (quenched end): $T(0, t) = T_{\text{water}} \approx 25\,^\circ\text{C}$ for $t > 0$ (sudden quench).
- At $x \to \infty$: $T(x, t) \to T_{\text{aust}} \approx 850\,^\circ\text{C}$ (far end still at furnace temperature initially).
- Initial condition: $T(x, 0) = T_{\text{aust}}$ for all $x > 0$.

**Solution via Error Function (Carslaw & Jaeger):**

$$
T(x, t) = T_{\text{water}} + (T_{\text{aust}} - T_{\text{water}}) \cdot \text{erf}\!\left( \frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}} \right)
$$

**Step 1:** Compute the cooling rate at 700 °C by differentiating the temperature with respect to time:

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = (T_{\text{aust}} - T_{\text{water}}) \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\text{erf}\!\left( \frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}} \right)
$$

**Step 2:** Using $\dfrac{d}{du}\text{erf}(u) = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} e^{-u^2}$ and the chain rule, with $u = \dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}}$, we obtain:

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = (T_{\text{aust}} - T_{\text{water}}) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi}} \cdot \frac{x}{2 \alpha^{1/2} t^{3/2}} \cdot \exp\!\left( -\frac{x^{2}}{4 \alpha t} \right)
$$

**Step 3:** To find the maximum cooling rate at 700 °C, set $T = 700\,^\circ\text{C}$ and recognize that the cooling rate peaks at a specific combination of $x$ and $t$. Empirical fitting to measured data yields the **Grossman relationship**:

$$
\log_{10}(CR) = -0.72 \cdot \log_{10}(J) + 2.61
$$

where $J$ is the Jominy distance in **1/16 inch units** and $CR$ is the cooling rate at 700 °C in **°C/s**.

**Step 4 (Conversion to SI):** Converting $J$ (in 1/16 inch) to $d$ in mm: $J = 16 \times \dfrac{d}{25.4}$. Substituting:

$$
CR(d) = 10^{2.61} \cdot \left( \frac{25.4}{16\,d} \right)^{0.72} = 407.4 \cdot (1.5875 / d)^{0.72} \approx \frac{600}{d + 10}
$$

where the last approximation is the KTU-friendly form (valid for $d$ in mm, gives a quick mental estimate).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A Jominy specimen is prepared from a medium-carbon steel (0.4% C, 1% Mn, 0.5% Cr). Hardness measurements are: HRC 58 at 1.5 mm, HRC 52 at 5 mm, HRC 42 at 10 mm, HRC 32 at 20 mm. Calculate the cooling rate at 10 mm from the quenched end and identify the dominant microstructure.

**Step 1 — Convert Jominy distance to 1/16 inch units:**

$$
J = \frac{10 \text{ mm}}{25.4 \text{ mm/inch}} \times 16 = 6.30 \text{ (1/16 inch units)}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Grossman formula:**

$$
\log_{10}(CR) = -0.72 \cdot \log_{10}(6.30) + 2.61
$$

$$
= -0.72 \cdot 0.7993 + 2.61 = -0.5755 + 2.61 = 2.0345
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for cooling rate:**

$$
CR = 10^{2.0345} \approx 108.4\,^\circ\text{C/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification using the approximate formula:**

$$
CR \approx \frac{600}{10 + 10} = 30\,^\circ\text{C/s}
$$

(The empirical formula gives a more accurate value; the simple approximation is for quick KTU mental math.)

**Step 5 — Microstructure Inference (from TTT diagram knowledge):**

At 700 °C cooling rate ~108 °C/s, for 0.4% C steel, this rate is **above the critical cooling rate** but at the lower boundary. The microstructure will be predominantly **martensite with some bainite**, consistent with the measured HRC 42.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (High-Carbon vs. Alloy vs. Plain-Carbon Steels)

| Parameter | Plain Carbon (0.4% C) | Alloy Steel (0.4% C + Ni-Cr-Mo) | High Carbon (0.8% C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hardness at quenched end (HRC) | ~55 | ~58 | ~65 |
| Hardness at 25 mm (HRC) | ~25 | ~50 | ~35 |
| Curve shape | **Steep drop** | **Gradual plateau** | **Steep, but high start** |
| Hardenability | Low | **High** | Moderate |
| Quench medium needed | Water (severe) | Oil (mild) | Water/Oil |
| Application | Cheap fasteners, low-stress parts | Gears, axles, crankshafts | Springs, cutting tools |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Jominy Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Prepare Specimen\n25 mm dia x 100 mm long\nwith flange] --> B[Step 2: Austenitize\nHeat to 800-900 C\nHold 30-45 min]
    B --> C[Step 3: Transfer to Fixture\nLess than 5 seconds\nto avoid air cooling]
    C --> D[Step 4: Quench End\nWater jet at 25 C\nHeight 65 mm]
    D --> E[Step 5: Air Cool to Room Temp\nSpecimen cools naturally\nexcept quenched end]
    E --> F[Step 6: Grind Two Flats\n0.4 mm depth\nOpposite sides]
    F --> G[Step 7: Measure Hardness\nRockwell C scale\nAt standard Jominy distances]
    G --> H[Step 8: Plot Jominy Curve\nHRC vs Jominy distance]
    H --> I[Step 9: Interpret Results\nDetermine hardenability\nCompare with reference curves]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of Hardenability Testing

```mermaid
graph LR
    A0[Specimen] --> B0[Austenitization Furnace]
    B0 --> C0[Quenching Fixture with Water Jet]
    C0 --> D0[Cooled Specimen with Hardness Gradient]
    D0 --> E0[Surface Grinding Machine]
    E0 --> F0[Hardness Testing Machine - Rockwell C]
    F0 --> G0[Data Acquisition - HRC at Each Jominy Distance]
    G0 --> H0[Jominy Curve Plot - HRC vs Distance]
    H0 --> I0[Comparison with Reference Band]
    I0 --> J0{Acceptance Decision}
    J0 -->|Within Band| K0[Steel Batch Approved]
    J0 -->|Outside Band| L0[Steel Batch Rejected]
    H0 --> M0[Hardenability Database]
    M0 --> N0[Engineering Design Selection]
    N0 --> O0[Component Application - Gear, Shaft, Axle]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Hardness vs. Jominy Distance — Three Steel Types

```mermaid
graph TD
    X[Jominy Distance - mm] --> Y[Hardness HRC]
    subgraph Curve_A
        P1[Quenched End: HRC 55] --> P2[At 10 mm: HRC 30] --> P3[At 25 mm: HRC 20]
    end
    subgraph Curve_B
        Q1[Quenched End: HRC 58] --> Q2[At 10 mm: HRC 50] --> Q3[At 25 mm: HRC 45]
    end
    subgraph Curve_C
        R1[Quenched End: HRC 65] --> R2[At 10 mm: HRC 50] --> R3[At 25 mm: HRC 32]
    end
    P3 -. Plain Carbon.-> LABEL[Curve A: Plain Carbon 1040]
    Q3 -. Alloy Steel.-> LABEL2[Curve B: Alloy Steel 4340]
    R3 -. High Carbon.-> LABEL3[Curve C: High Carbon 1080]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram (for KTU descriptive answers):**
> - **Curve A** drops sharply → **low hardenability** → only thin sections can be through-hardened.
> - **Curve B** has a gentle slope → **high hardenability** → large sections can be uniformly hardened.
> - **Curve C** starts high (due to high carbon) but drops eventually → **moderate hardenability** with high peak hardness.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define hardenability. How is it different from hardness?*

**Model Answer:**
Hardenability is the ability of a steel to form martensite when quenched from the austenitic state, characterized by the depth and distribution of hardness below the surface. It is determined experimentally by the Jominy End Quench Test. In contrast, hardness is the resistance of a material to localized plastic deformation, measured at a single point (e.g., HRC value). Hardenability depends on **alloying content and grain size**, while hardness at a point depends on the local microstructure. **Hardenability is a property of the steel grade; hardness is a property of the microstructure at a specific location.** (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the standard specimen dimensions and quenching conditions used in the Jominy End Quench Test.*

**Model Answer:**
The standard Jominy specimen is a cylindrical bar of **25.4 mm (1 inch) diameter and 100 mm (4 inches) length**, with a small flange at one end for suspension. The specimen is austenitized at **800–900 °C** for **30–45 minutes** in a furnace, then transferred quickly to a fixture and quenched at one end by a **water jet at room temperature**, with the water column held at a **free height of 65 mm** above a **12.5 mm diameter nozzle**. Hardness is measured on ground flats along the bar at specified distances from the quenched end. (3 marks)

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the procedure of the Jominy End Quench Test for determining the hardenability of steel. Mention the standard specimen dimensions, austenitizing conditions, and quenching medium. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Specimen Preparation (1 mark):**
A standard cylindrical specimen of **25.4 mm diameter × 101.6 mm length** is prepared, with a small flange/collar at one end for hanging in the furnace. The specimen is machined from a representative sample of the steel batch.

**Step 2 — Austenitization (1 mark):**
The specimen is heated in a furnace to a temperature **30–50 °C above the upper critical temperature (Ac₃)** of the steel — typically **800–900 °C** for most carbon and alloy steels. The specimen is held for **30–45 minutes** to ensure complete transformation to **austenite (γ-FCC)** and uniform carbon dissolution.

**Step 3 — Quenching Setup (2 marks):**
The heated specimen is rapidly transferred (within **5 seconds**) to a Jominy test fixture. A water jet at **room temperature (~25 °C)** is directed at the bottom (flange) end of the specimen from a nozzle of **12.5 mm diameter**, with a **free water column of 65 mm**. The water jet impinges on the bottom face, providing a constant, high heat-extraction rate at that location.

**Step 4 — Cooling (1 mark):**
The specimen cools in air except at the quenched end. The water jet creates a one-dimensional heat flow along the bar's length. The quenched end cools at ~**600 °C/s** at 700 °C, while the cooling rate decreases exponentially with distance from the quenched end.

**Step 5 — Hardness Measurement (2 marks):**
After the specimen reaches room temperature, **two parallel flats** are ground along the length to a depth of **~0.4 mm** on opposite sides. **Rockwell C (HRC) hardness** is measured at standardized Jominy distances: **1.5, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50 mm** from the quenched end. (The flats must be ground, not turned, to avoid work-hardening effects.)

**Step 6 — Plotting the Curve:**
Hardness (HRC) is plotted against Jominy distance. The resulting **Jominy hardenability curve** (or S-curve) is compared with reference curves for the steel grade to assess hardenability.

---

**(b)** Explain how the cooling rate varies along the Jominy specimen, and derive the empirical relationship between Jominy distance and cooling rate at 700 °C. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cooling Rate Profile (2 marks):**
The water jet extracts heat rapidly from the quenched end. Heat flows **one-dimensionally** along the bar from the hot interior to the cold end. As a result, the **quenched end** experiences the **highest cooling rate** (≈ 600 °C/s at 700 °C), while points farther from the quenched end cool **progressively slower** because they are insulated by more metal between them and the water jet. The cooling rate at the far end (100 mm) approaches that of an air-cooled specimen (~0.1–1 °C/s).

**Step 2 — Heat Conduction Equation (2 marks):**
The temperature distribution $T(x, t)$ at distance $x$ from the quenched end, at time $t$, satisfies the 1-D heat equation:

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}
$$

with boundary conditions $T(0, t) = T_w$ (water temperature) and $T(\infty, t) = T_0$ (austenitizing temperature), and initial condition $T(x, 0) = T_0$.

**Step 3 — Solution and Differentiation (2 marks):**
The solution using the error function gives:

$$
T(x, t) = T_w + (T_0 - T_w)\text{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}}\right)
$$

Differentiating w.r.t. time and evaluating at $T = 700\,^\circ\text{C}$, then fitting to experimental data, yields the **empirical Grossman formula**:

$$
\log_{10}(CR) = -0.72 \cdot \log_{10}(J) + 2.61
$$

where $CR$ is in °C/s and $J$ is the Jominy distance in **1/16 inch units**.

**Step 4 — Practical Example (1 mark):**
For $J = 10$ (1/16 inch) ≈ 16 mm:

$$
CR = 10^{(-0.72 \times 1 + 2.61)} = 10^{1.89} \approx 77.6\,^\circ\text{C/s}
$$

This shows that at just 16 mm from the quenched end, the cooling rate has dropped by nearly an order of magnitude from the peak at the quenched end.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss the factors affecting the hardenability of steel. Explain how the Jominy test helps in selecting the appropriate quenching medium for a given steel and component. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition Recap (1 mark):**
Hardenability is the ability of a steel to harden deeply upon quenching. It is measured by the Jominy End Quench Test and is influenced by several metallurgical factors.

**Step 2 — Factors Affecting Hardenability (3 marks):**

| Factor | Effect on Hardenability |
|---|---|
| **Carbon content** | Increases hardenability up to ~0.8% C (eutectoid), then plateaus |
| **Alloying elements** (Mn, Ni, Cr, Mo, V) | **Increase** hardenability; Mn and Mo are most effective per unit weight |
| **Grain size** | **Coarser austenite grain** = higher hardenability (fewer grain boundary nucleation sites for pearlite) |
| **Austenitizing temperature** | Higher temperature → coarser grain → slightly higher hardenability |
| **Inclusions/non-metallics** | Reduce hardenability by acting as pearlite nucleation sites |
| **Homogeneity of austenite** | Incomplete dissolution of carbides lowers hardenability |

**Step 3 — Quenching Medium Selection (3 marks):**
The Jominy curve directly informs quenching medium selection:

- **Steep Jominy curve (plain carbon steel)**: A severe quench (water or brine) is required to achieve martensite at the core. Risk of quench cracking is high.
- **Gradual Jominy curve (alloy steel)**: A milder quench (oil or polymer) suffices. This reduces residual stress, distortion, and cracking.
- **Component size consideration**: The Jominy curve allows engineers to predict the hardness distribution in components of various sizes by applying the **Grossman method** (correlation of cooling rate at the bar center to a Jominy distance).

> **Example:** A gear of 50 mm diameter made of plain carbon steel 1040 has a Jominy curve that drops below HRC 40 within 10 mm. To get a usable surface hardness, **induction hardening** (surface-only heating) must be used — through-hardening is impossible. The same gear made of 4340 alloy steel can be **through-hardened** by oil quenching.

---

**(b)** A Jominy specimen of a steel with 0.4% C, 1% Mn, 0.8% Cr gives a hardness of HRC 45 at 10 mm from the quenched end. Using the Grossman empirical formula, calculate the cooling rate at this location and identify the likely microstructure. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert Jominy distance (1 mark):**

$$
J = \frac{10 \text{ mm}}{25.4 \text{ mm/inch}} \times 16 = 6.30 \text{ (1/16 inch units)}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Grossman formula (2 marks):**

$$
\log_{10}(CR) = -0.72 \cdot \log_{10}(6.30) + 2.61
$$

Computing $\log_{10}(6.30) = 0.7993$:

$$
\log_{10}(CR) = -0.72 \times 0.7993 + 2.61 = -0.5755 + 2.61 = 2.0345
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate cooling rate (1 mark):**

$$
CR = 10^{2.0345} = 108.4\,^\circ\text{C/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Microstructure Identification (2 marks):**
For 0.4% C steel, the **critical cooling rate** for full martensite formation is approximately **200–300 °C/s**. At 108 °C/s, the cooling rate is **below** the critical value, so full martensite will not form. The microstructure will be a **mixture of martensite and bainite** (upper bainite likely, due to the moderate cooling rate). The HRC 45 reading is consistent with a **bainitic-martensitic mix**, where bainite reduces the hardness from the maximum ~HRC 55 (full martensite) to ~HRC 45.

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation (1 mark):**
This Jominy result indicates **moderate hardenability**. For a component requiring surface hardness, oil quenching of a thin section is appropriate; water quenching would be excessive and could cause distortion.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Jominy Test Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing hardenability with hardness** — these are distinct concepts. Examiners explicitly test this distinction. Always state the difference in a 3-mark question.
> 2. **Wrong units in the Grossman formula** — Jominy distance $J$ must be in **1/16 inch units**, not mm. Convert correctly. A unit error here costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the 700 °C reference temperature** — the cooling rate in the Jominy formula is **at 700 °C**, not an average or surface value. Examiners often specify this.
> 4. **Wrong specimen dimensions** — common wrong answers are 10 mm × 50 mm or 50 mm × 200 mm. The standard is **25.4 mm × 101.6 mm** (or 25 mm × 100 mm in rounded form). Writing the wrong dimensions is an instant 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Not mentioning "ground flats"** — hardness is measured on ground flats, not on the cylindrical surface. Many students miss this in the procedure.
> 6. **Plotting the Jominy curve on the wrong axis** — HRC is on the **y-axis**, Jominy distance is on the **x-axis**. Examiners deduct marks for reversed axes.
> 7. **Missing the role of alloying elements** — the Jominy curve interpretation must mention that alloying elements shift the curve to the right (i.e., higher hardness at greater distances), and this is what makes alloy steels "deep-hardenable."

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Hardenability ≠ Hardness.** Hardenability = depth/distribution; Hardness = local resistance to deformation. This is the #1 KTU exam pitfall.
- **Standard Jominy specimen:** **25.4 mm dia × 101.6 mm long**, flange at one end.
- **Austenitizing:** 800–900 °C, 30–45 min hold.
- **Quenching:** Water jet at 25 °C, 12.5 mm nozzle, 65 mm free water height, quenched end only.
- **Hardness measurement:** **Rockwell C (HRC)** on **ground flats** (0.4 mm depth), at standard Jominy distances: 1.5, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50 mm.
- **Plot:** HRC (y-axis) vs. Jominy distance in mm (x-axis). The result is the **Jominy S-curve**.
- **Key empirical formula (KTU high-yield):**

$$
\log_{10}(CR) = -0.72 \cdot \log_{10}(J) + 2.61
$$

with $J$ in 1/16 inch units and $CR$ in °C/s at 700 °C.

- **Approximate SI form:** $CR \approx \dfrac{600}{d + 10}$ °C/s, with $d$ in mm.
- **Microstructure gradient:** Martensite at quenched end → Bainite → Ferrite + Pearlite at far end.
- **Factors that increase hardenability:** Higher carbon (up to 0.8%), alloying elements (Mn, Ni, Cr, Mo, V), coarser austenite grain.
- **Quenching medium selection rule (memorize):** Steep Jominy curve → water/brine; Gradual Jominy curve → oil/polymer.
- **Alloy steels (e.g., 4340)** shift the Jominy curve to the **right** (higher hardness at greater distances) → high hardenability.
- **Plain carbon steels (e.g., 1040)** have a **steep** Jominy curve → low hardenability → require severe quench.
- **High-carbon steels (e.g., 1080)** have a **high starting hardness** but moderate hardenability (pearlite nose shifts).
- **Grossman's ideal critical diameter $D_I$** is the largest bar diameter that will form 100% martensite at the center with an **ideal (infinite) quench** — useful for comparing steels.
- **Engineering applications:** Gears, axles, crankshafts, springs, cutting tools — all selected based on Jominy-derived hardenability.
- **One-line exam summary:** *"The Jominy End Quench Test quantifies a steel's ability to harden deeply by measuring HRC along a water-quenched standard bar, producing a unique hardenability curve."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications - Surface Hardening Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT526 - METALLURGY AND MATERIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications - Surface Hardening Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Surface Hardening

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surface Hardening** is a class of heat-treatment processes in which the **surface layer** of a ferrous (or in some cases, non-ferrous) component is selectively hardened to a predetermined depth, while the **bulk core** retains its original toughness, ductility, and impact resistance. The process relies on three physical principles: **(i) thermal diffusion of interstitial atoms (C/N)**, **(ii) rapid phase transformation via quenching**, and **(iii) local surface energy deposition**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, surface hardening is formally classified under **"Selective Surface Modification"** and is a sub-domain of **Thermochemical Heat Treatment (TCHT)** and **Surface Engineering**.

### 1.2 Key Engineering Metrics (must be memorised)

| Metric | Typical Range | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Case Depth** | 0.2 mm – 6.0 mm | Distance from surface where hardness $\geq$ 50 HRC (effective case) |
| **Surface Hardness** | 50 – 1000 HV | Vickers hardness measured at the topmost 50 $\mu$m |
| **Core Hardness** | 20 – 40 HRC | Hardness in the unaltered bulk material |
| **Process Temperature** | 500°C – 1100°C | Depending on the method chosen |
| **Hardness Drop-off** | 3 – 5 HRC/mm | Rate at which hardness decays with depth |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bread with Crust" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a soft bread loaf (core) baked with a very crispy, hard crust (case).** The crust is hard and can resist scratches, dust, and wear — but if you press your finger into it, the soft inside yields. Now scale this to a steel gear: the **case** is the hard, wear-resistant skin that resists pitting, scuffing, and contact fatigue, while the **core** is the tough, ductile heart that absorbs shock loads, vibration, and bending stresses **without fracturing**. This is *exactly* what a carburised gear tooth or nitrided crankshaft does.

The crust is formed by one of three physical "baking" routes:
- **Diffusion baking** (Carburising, Nitriding) — atoms migrate inward.
- **Frying with flame/coil** (Flame hardening, Induction hardening) — heat is deposited only on the surface.
- **High-energy beam grilling** (Laser, Electron Beam) — precision surface transformation.

## 1.4 Why Surface Hardening? — The Engineering Justification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Failure Mode Reality Check:** More than **70% of in-service mechanical failures** (gear pitting, bearing spalling, cam wear, shaft fatigue) initiate at the **surface**, not the core. If the entire cross-section is hardened (through-hardening), the component becomes **brittle** and fails catastrophically under impact. Surface hardening is therefore the **optimum engineering compromise** between surface wear resistance and bulk fracture toughness.

## 1.5 Classification Overview

Surface hardening methods are divided into **two fundamental families**:

**Family A — Diffusion-Based (Thermochemical) Methods:**
1. Carburising (Pack / Liquid / Gas)
2. Nitriding (Gas / Salt Bath / Plasma)
3. Cyaniding
4. Carbonitriding
5. Boriding (Borochromising)

**Family B — Selective-Heating Methods:**
1. Flame Hardening
2. Induction Hardening
3. Laser Beam Hardening
4. Electron Beam Hardening

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** Students are **required** to know at least **two methods in detail** from each family for the 14-mark ESE questions. Memorise process parameters, media used, case depth ranges, and at least **two industrial applications** per method.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermochemical (Diffusion) Methods — The Core Theory

### 2.1.1 Carburising

**Definition:** Carburising is the process of **enriching the surface layer of low-carbon steel (0.1 – 0.25 %C)** with carbon by holding the component at an elevated temperature (above the $A_{c3}$ line) in a carbonaceous medium until the desired case depth is obtained, followed by **quenching and tempering**.

**Why does it work?** At temperatures above **910°C (the eutectoid temperature)**, iron has an FCC (Face-Centered Cubic) lattice known as **$\gamma$-austenite**, which has a **larger interstitial void** than BCC ferrite. This expanded lattice can **dissolve up to 2.0 wt% carbon**, allowing rapid carbon diffusion into the surface. Upon quenching, the carbon-supersaturated austenite transforms to **martensite**, which is exceptionally hard.

**Variants:**
- **Pack Carburising:** Steel packed in sealed boxes with **charcoal + $BaCO_3$ energiser** at 900–950°C.
- **Liquid Carburising:** Steel immersed in molten **NaCN / NaCl / $Na_2CO_3$** bath at 840–900°C.
- **Gas Carburising:** Steel heated in **endothermic gas + propane/methane** at 850–950°C (most industrial).

### 2.1.2 Nitriding

**Definition:** Nitriding is the diffusion of **atomic nitrogen** into the surface of specially alloyed steels (containing Cr, Al, Mo, V, Ti — the *nitride formers*) by holding at **500–550°C** in an ammonia atmosphere.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key difference from Carburising:** Nitriding is performed **below the $A_{c1}$ temperature**, so **no phase transformation occurs**, and **no quenching is required**. The result: **minimal distortion** and **retained dimensional accuracy**.

The hard surface is formed by the precipitation of **alloy nitrides** (CrN, AlN, $Mo_2$N, VN, TiN) — which give hardness values up to **1000 HV** (≈ 70 HRC). Typical case depth: **0.3 – 0.75 mm**.

### 2.1.3 Cyaniding

A short-duration, shallow-case process where steel is immersed in a **molten cyanide salt bath** (e.g., 30% NaCN + 40% NaCl + 30% $Na_2CO_3$) at **800–870°C**. Both **carbon and nitrogen** are diffused simultaneously, giving a very hard but thin case (**0.1 – 0.5 mm**) in **30 minutes to 2 hours**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Safety/Environmental Note:** Cyanide salts are **highly toxic** and increasingly restricted under environmental regulations. Most industries have shifted to **plasma nitriding** or **gas nitrocarburising** as safer replacements.

### 2.1.4 Carbonitriding

A **gaseous variant of cyaniding** performed at **700–900°C** in a mixture of **ammonia ($NH_3$) + hydrocarbon gas (propane/methane) + carrier gas**. Performed at temperatures **below the $A_{c1}$** for some variants. Produces a nitrogen-rich case with improved wear and fatigue resistance.

### 2.1.5 Boriding (Boronising)

Diffusion of boron atoms into the surface to form **iron boride ($Fe_2B$ / FeB)**. Achieves surface hardness of **1400 – 2000 HV** — the **hardest of all diffusion treatments**. Performed at **800–1000°C** using $B_4C$ powder, $KBF_4$ activator, or gaseous $BCl_3$/$H_2$ mixtures.

## 2.2 Selective-Heating Methods

### 2.2.1 Flame Hardening

An **oxy-acetylene (or oxy-propane) flame** is moved rapidly across the steel surface, raising the temperature locally above $A_{c3}$ (850–900°C). Immediately after the flame passes, the region is **quenched** with a water spray (self-quenching by conduction is also used for very thick sections). Used for **large components** (e.g., crane wheels, gear teeth, large shafts) where induction coils would be impractical.

### 2.2.2 Induction Hardening

The component is placed inside (or passed through) a **water-cooled copper coil** carrying **high-frequency alternating current (kHz to MHz range)**. By Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, **eddy currents** are generated in the steel surface, which heat it via **Joule heating ($I^2R$)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Skin Effect:** The depth of current penetration is given by the **standard reference depth formula** — this is a *highly* KTU-relevant formula (see formula sheet below). The higher the frequency, the **shallower** the case depth.

### 2.2.3 Laser Beam Hardening

A **high-power $CO_2$ or Nd:YAG laser** is scanned across the surface, rapidly heating a thin layer (0.2 – 1.0 mm deep) to austenitising temperature. The bulk material acts as a **quench** through conduction. **No external coolant** is required. Used for **selective areas** like piston-ring grooves, cam lobes, and valve seats.

### 2.2.4 Electron Beam Hardening

Performed in a **vacuum chamber** using a focused beam of **accelerated electrons**. Energy deposition is similar to laser hardening but the energy-coupling efficiency to the metal is much higher (~70% vs 30% for laser). Used for **aerospace, automotive precision components**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table lists **all equations** you must be able to write from memory in the KTU 2024 ESE exam.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Boundary Conditions / Limits |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fick's First Law (steady-state diffusion)** | $J = -D \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | $J$ = flux (kg/m²·s), $D$ = diffusivity (m²/s), $C$ = concentration (kg/m³) | Valid when $\dfrac{dC}{dx}$ = constant |
| 2 | **Fick's Second Law (transient diffusion)** | $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ | $t$ = time (s), $x$ = depth (m) | Semi-infinite medium assumption |
| 3 | **Case Depth vs. Time (parabolic growth)** | $x = K \sqrt{t}$ | $x$ = case depth (mm), $K$ = parabolic rate constant (mm/s$^{1/2}$), $t$ = time (s) | Valid for **isothermal** diffusion with constant surface concentration |
| 4 | **Arrhenius Diffusion Coefficient** | $D = D_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$ | $D_0$ = pre-exponential (m²/s), $Q$ = activation energy (J/mol), $R$ = 8.314 J/mol·K, $T$ = temperature (K) | $D$ **doubles** for every $\approx$ 20°C rise near 1000°C |
| 5 | **Induction Skin Depth (Reference Depth)** | $\delta = 5030 \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\mu_r f}}$ | $\delta$ = depth in mm, $\rho$ = resistivity ($\mu\Omega$-cm), $\mu_r$ = relative permeability, $f$ = frequency (Hz) | $f$ must be in **Hz**; $\delta$ in **mm**; $\rho$ in **$\mu\Omega\cdot$cm** |
| 6 | **Hardness Conversion (Empirical)** | $HV \approx 0.95 \cdot HB$ | $HV$, $HB$ in standard units | Valid for steels 100 – 500 HV |
| 7 | **Hollomon–Jaffe Tempering Parameter** | $P = T\,(\log t + C)$ | $T$ = tempering temp (K), $t$ = tempering time (h), $C \approx 20$ (for steels) | Used to predict **hardness retention** after tempering |
| 8 | **Larson–Miller Parameter (creep/tempering)** | $LMP = T\,(\log t + C)$ | $T$ in Rankine, $C \approx 20$ | Used for elevated-temp service |
| 9 | **Effective Case Depth Definition** | Hardness $\geq$ 50 HRC | At depth $x_{eff}$ | Standard KTU definition |
| 10 | **Total Case Depth Definition** | Visible microstructure change under Nital etch | $x_{tot}$ | Includes transition zone |
| 11 | **Jominy Hardenability (related, not same topic)** | $J = f(D_i, v_{cool})$ | $D_i$ = ideal critical diameter | Not directly surface hardening but related |

> [!NOTE]
> **Equation 3** ($x = K\sqrt{t}$) is the **single most important formula** for KTU surface-hardening numericals. You will be expected to derive it from Fick's second law and use it to calculate case depth, treatment time, or process temperature.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Component | Method Used | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Gear teeth, camshafts, crankshafts | Carburising, Induction | High contact fatigue resistance |
| **Aerospace** | Landing gear, turbine shafts | Nitriding, Induction | Dimensional accuracy + wear resistance |
| **Hydraulic Systems** | Cylinders, piston rods | Induction, Hard Chrome | Surface wear under sliding |
| **Tool & Die** | Punches, moulds, dies | Nitriding, Boriding | Very high surface hardness |
| **Mining & Earth-moving** | Bucket teeth, crusher jaws | Carburising, Boriding | Extreme abrasion resistance |
| **Power Generation** | Large shafts, bearings | Flame Hardening | Large component size |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Case Depth Equation $x = K\sqrt{t}$ from Fick's Second Law

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU ESE Module 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumptions (state these explicitly to get full marks):**
> 1. Semi-infinite medium (component thickness $\gg$ case depth).
> 2. Constant surface concentration $C_s$ throughout the process.
> 3. Constant diffusivity $D$ (isothermal treatment).
> 4. Initially uniform bulk concentration $C_0$.

**Starting point — Fick's Second Law:**

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

**Step 1 — Apply boundary & initial conditions:**

$$C(x, 0) = C_0 \quad \text{for all } x \geq 0$$
$$C(0, t) = C_s \quad \text{for all } t \geq 0$$
$$C(\infty, t) = C_0 \quad \text{for all } t \geq 0$$

**Step 2 — Define a similarity variable to convert the PDE to an ODE:**

Let us introduce a dimensionless variable $\eta$:

$$\eta = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}$$

This substitution is called the **Boltzmann transformation** and is valid for semi-infinite media with the boundary conditions above.

**Step 3 — Apply the chain rule to transform derivatives:**

First, the time derivative:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial C}{\partial \eta} \cdot \frac{\partial \eta}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial C}{\partial \eta} \cdot \left( -\frac{x}{4 t \sqrt{Dt}} \right) = -\frac{\eta}{2t} \frac{dC}{d\eta}$$

Next, the spatial second derivative:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial C}{\partial \eta} \cdot \frac{\partial \eta}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{Dt}} \frac{dC}{d\eta}$$

$$\frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2} = \frac{1}{4Dt} \frac{d^2 C}{d\eta^2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into Fick's Second Law:**

$$-\frac{\eta}{2t} \frac{dC}{d\eta} = D \cdot \frac{1}{4Dt} \frac{d^2 C}{d\eta^2}$$

The time variable $t$ cancels out:

$$-\frac{\eta}{2} \frac{dC}{d\eta} = \frac{1}{4} \frac{d^2 C}{d\eta^2}$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange into a standard ODE:**

$$\frac{d^2 C}{d\eta^2} + 2\eta \frac{dC}{d\eta} = 0$$

**Step 6 — Integrate once (let $p = \dfrac{dC}{d\eta}$):**

$$\frac{dp}{d\eta} + 2\eta \, p = 0$$

$$\frac{dp}{p} = -2\eta \, d\eta$$

$$\ln p = -\eta^2 + \text{const}_1$$

$$p = A_1 \exp(-\eta^2)$$

**Step 7 — Integrate a second time:**

$$\frac{dC}{d\eta} = A_1 \exp(-\eta^2)$$

$$C(\eta) = A_1 \int_0^\eta \exp(-u^2) \, du + A_2$$

**Step 8 — Apply boundary conditions to find $A_1$ and $A_2$:**

As $\eta \to \infty$, $C \to C_0$, so:

$$C_0 = A_1 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} + A_2$$

At $\eta = 0$ (surface), $C \to C_s$:

$$C_s = 0 + A_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_2 = C_s$$

Therefore:

$$A_1 = \frac{2(C_0 - C_s)}{\sqrt{\pi}}$$

**Step 9 — Write the final concentration profile:**

$$C(x,t) - C_s = (C_0 - C_s) \cdot \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \int_0^{x/(2\sqrt{Dt})} \exp(-u^2) \, du$$

$$C(x,t) = C_s - (C_s - C_0) \cdot \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$

**Step 10 — Define "case depth" at a target concentration $C^*$:**

At depth $x = x_{eff}$, the concentration equals some target value $C^*$:

$$\text{erf}\!\left(\frac{x_{eff}}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right) = \frac{C_s - C^*}{C_s - C_0} = \text{constant} = \beta$$

The inverse error function of $\beta$ is a constant ($K$):

$$x_{eff} = 2\sqrt{Dt} \cdot \text{erf}^{-1}(\beta)$$

**Step 11 — Recognise the parabolic relationship:**

$$x_{eff} = K \sqrt{t}$$

where:

$$K = 2\sqrt{D} \cdot \text{erf}^{-1}(\beta) \quad \text{(a constant at a given temperature)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the final, exam-ready result.** Notice the key engineering insight: **doubling the treatment time increases case depth by only $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$**, not 2. Therefore, achieving very deep cases (>3 mm) is uneconomical — this is why induction or laser methods are preferred for deep local hardening.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Induction Heating Skin Depth (Reference Depth)

The **skin depth $\delta$** is defined as the depth below the surface at which the **eddy current density has decayed to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$** of its surface value.

**Step 1 — Maxwell's Equation for a planar conductor:**

Starting from Maxwell's equation in a conductor with permeability $\mu$ and conductivity $\sigma = 1/\rho$:

$$\nabla^2 H = \mu \sigma \frac{\partial H}{\partial t}$$

For sinusoidal excitation $H = H_0 e^{j\omega t}$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 H}{\partial x^2} = j\omega\mu\sigma H$$

**Step 2 — Solve the ODE with the boundary condition $H \to 0$ as $x \to \infty$:**

$$H(x) = H_0 \exp\!\left(-x\sqrt{\frac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}\right) \cdot \exp\!\left(-j x\sqrt{\frac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Define the skin depth as the $1/e$ decay distance:**

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\omega = 2\pi f$ and $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$, with $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m:**

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{2\pi f \cdot \mu_0 \mu_r \cdot (1/\rho)}}$$

**Step 5 — Convert units so that $\delta$ is in mm, $\rho$ in $\mu\Omega$-cm, $f$ in Hz:**

After unit conversion (the famous 5030 constant arises from the unit juggling), we obtain:

$$\boxed{\delta = 5030 \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\mu_r f}} \;\; \text{[mm]}}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering implication:**

| Frequency $f$ | Skin Depth $\delta$ (steel) | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| 50 Hz (mains) | $\approx$ 70 mm | Deep bulk heating |
| 1 kHz | $\approx$ 5 mm | General induction hardening |
| 10 kHz | $\approx$ 1.5 mm | Medium case |
| 100 kHz | $\approx$ 0.5 mm | Shallow case |
| 1 MHz | $\approx$ 0.15 mm | Very shallow case |

> [!NOTE]
> **This explains the design rule of thumb in industry:** For a case depth of 1 mm, use $\approx$ 10 kHz. For 0.3 mm case, use $\approx$ 100 kHz.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Arrhenius Temperature Dependence of $K$

The rate constant $K$ in $x = K\sqrt{t}$ is proportional to $\sqrt{D}$, and $D$ follows Arrhenius:

$$D = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

Therefore:

$$K = 2\sqrt{D} \cdot \text{erf}^{-1}(\beta) = 2 \, \text{erf}^{-1}(\beta) \sqrt{D_0} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{2RT}\right)$$

$$\ln K = \ln\!\left(2 \sqrt{D_0} \cdot \text{erf}^{-1}(\beta)\right) - \frac{Q}{2RT}$$

A plot of $\ln K$ vs $1/T$ is a **straight line** with slope $-Q/(2R)$, allowing experimental determination of activation energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **For Carbon in $\gamma$-Fe:** $D_0 \approx 2.3 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $Q \approx 148$ kJ/mol. **For Nitrogen in $\alpha$-Fe:** $D_0 \approx 6.6 \times 10^{-7}$ m²/s, $Q \approx 76$ kJ/mol.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A low-carbon steel gear is gas-carburised at 927°C (1200 K) for 5 hours. The diffusion coefficient of carbon in $\gamma$-iron at this temperature is $D = 6 \times 10^{-12}$ m²/s. Assuming a target surface-to-core concentration ratio giving $\text{erf}^{-1}(\beta) = 0.95$, calculate the effective case depth.

**Solution:**

$$x_{eff} = 2\sqrt{Dt} \cdot \text{erf}^{-1}(\beta)$$

Step 1 — Compute $Dt$:

$$Dt = 6 \times 10^{-12} \times (5 \times 3600) = 6 \times 10^{-12} \times 18000 = 1.08 \times 10^{-7} \;\; \text{m}^2$$

Step 2 — Compute $\sqrt{Dt}$:

$$\sqrt{Dt} = \sqrt{1.08 \times 10^{-7}} = 3.286 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{m} \approx 0.329 \;\; \text{mm}$$

Step 3 — Compute $x_{eff}$:

$$x_{eff} = 2 \times 0.329 \times 0.95 = 0.625 \;\; \text{mm}$$

**Answer:** Case depth $\approx$ **0.625 mm** (i.e., $\approx$ 0.6 mm, a typical industrial value).

> **Bonus — What time gives a 1 mm case at the same temperature?**
> $$1.0 = 2 \times 0.329 \times 0.95 \times \sqrt{(t/t_0)}$$
> $$\sqrt{(t/t_0)} = \frac{1.0}{0.625} = 1.6$$
> $$t = 1.6^2 \times 5 = 12.8 \;\; \text{hours}$$

Notice again: doubling the time would give $\sqrt{2} \times 0.625 = 0.88$ mm — **not 1.25 mm**. This is the **parabolic growth** in action.

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Method

| Selection Criterion | Carburising | Nitriding | Induction | Flame | Laser / EB |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Process Temp (°C)** | 850 – 950 | 500 – 550 | 850 – 950 (local) | 850 – 900 (local) | 1000 – 1100 (local) |
| **Quench Required?** | **Yes** | **No** | **Yes (self)** | **Yes** | **No (self)** |
| **Case Depth (mm)** | 0.5 – 3.0 | 0.3 – 0.75 | 0.5 – 6.0 | 1.0 – 6.0 | 0.2 – 1.0 |
| **Surface Hardness (HV)** | 700 – 850 | 800 – 1000 | 700 – 850 | 700 – 850 | 700 – 900 |
| **Distortion** | High | **Very Low** | Moderate | Moderate | **Very Low** |
| **Cost per piece** | Low | Medium | High (coil cost) | Low | **Very High** |
| **Suitable for** | Mass production | Precision parts | Shafts, gears | Large parts | Selective / small zones |
| **Distortion-prone?** | **Yes** | No | Yes (mild) | Yes | No |
| **Suitable Steel** | Low C (0.15–0.25%) | Alloyed (Cr/Al/Mo) | Medium C (0.35–0.5%) | Medium C (0.35–0.5%) | Medium C (0.35–0.5%) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (loses 2 marks):** Students often confuse **carburising** (C-diffusion above $A_{c3}$) with **nitriding** (N-diffusion below $A_{c1}$). Always state the **phase transformation** and **quench requirement** in the first line of your answer.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions (Module 2 — Surface Hardening)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024 Pattern]**

### Q1. Define "case depth" in surface hardening. Differentiate between effective case depth and total case depth. **\[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

Case depth is the **distance from the surface of a hardened component to the point below which the hardness falls below a defined value** (typically 50 HRC for steel). It is a critical specification in surface hardening because it governs the wear life and load-bearing capacity of the component.

- **Effective Case Depth ($x_{eff}$):** The depth at which the **hardness is exactly 50 HRC** (≈ 550 HV for steels). This is the value used for engineering design calculations.
- **Total Case Depth ($x_{tot}$):** The depth at which the **microstructure is no longer distinguishable from the core** when viewed under a microscope after metallographic etching with 2% Nital. It is **always greater** than $x_{eff}$ because it includes the gradual transition zone.

> [!NOTE]
> In a typical carburised gear: $x_{tot} \approx 1.4 \, x_{eff}$.

### Q2. Why is **nitriding** performed below the $A_{c1}$ temperature, while **carburising** is performed above the $A_{c3}$? **\[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The temperature selection is dictated by the **diffusion mechanism** and the **desired phase transformation**:

- **Carburising** is performed above $A_{c3}$ (typically 850–950°C) because **carbon diffuses rapidly in the FCC $\gamma$-austenite phase** (interstitial solubility $\approx$ 2 wt%). The high temperature also enables the required austenite-to-martensite phase transformation upon quenching, which produces the hard case.

- **Nitriding** is performed below $A_{c1}$ (typically 500–550°C) because:
  1. The aim is to form **fine alloy nitrides** (CrN, AlN, $Mo_2$N, TiN) by **solid-state reaction**, not by phase transformation.
  2. No quench is needed → **minimum distortion** and dimensional stability.
  3. The low temperature retains the **core's prior quenched and tempered microstructure** (e.g., sorbite/tempered martensite), preserving toughness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The temperature difference exists because the **hardening mechanism is different** — carburising is a **diffusion + quench hardening** process, whereas nitriding is a **precipitation hardening** process.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module 2 Pattern with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **gas carburising process** in detail. List the **process parameters**, the **microstructural changes** during the treatment, and the **post-quench hardness distribution** from the surface to the core. **\[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A low-carbon steel component containing 0.18% C is gas-carburised at 1000°C for 6 hours. The diffusion coefficient of carbon in $\gamma$-iron at 1000°C is $D = 1.6 \times 10^{-11}$ m²/s. Calculate the **effective case depth** if the surface-to-core concentration ratio yields $\text{erf}^{-1}(\beta) = 0.85$. If the same component were processed for **15 hours** at the same temperature, what would be the new case depth? Comment on the relationship. **\[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Gas Carburising Process:**

1. **Setup:** The component (e.g., a low-carbon steel gear with 0.15–0.25% C) is loaded into a **sealed gas-tight furnace** (typically a pit furnace or sealed quench furnace with controlled atmosphere capability).

2. **Atmosphere Generation:** An **endothermic gas carrier** (a mixture of $N_2$, $H_2$, and small amounts of $CO$ and $CH_4$) is produced by reacting natural gas with air in a heated catalyst chamber at 950°C. The endothermic reaction is:

$$\text{2CH}_4 + \text{O}_2 + \text{7N}_2 \rightarrow \text{2CO} + \text{4H}_2 + \text{7N}_2$$

3. **Enrichment Gas Addition:** A controlled amount of **propane ($C_3H_8$) or methane ($CH_4$)** is added as the **enrichment gas** to provide the active carbon. The decomposition reactions are:

$$\text{CH}_4 \rightarrow \text{C (atomic)} + \text{2H}_2$$
$$\text{2CO} \rightarrow \text{C (atomic)} + \text{CO}_2$$

The **atomic carbon** diffuses into the steel surface because of the concentration gradient (surface has 0% free C initially, atmosphere has high carbon potential).

4. **Process Parameters:**
   - **Temperature:** 900–950°C (above $A_{c3}$ to ensure full austenitisation)
   - **Time:** 4–12 hours (depending on case depth required)
   - **Carbon Potential:** 0.8 – 1.2 wt% C (controlled by gas composition)
   - **Quench medium:** Oil or polymer (water for shallow cases)

5. **Microstructural Changes (surface to core):**
   - **Top 0.05 mm:** Hypereutectoid zone — pearlite + proeutectoid cementite network (network carbides).
   - **Next zone (0.1–0.8 mm):** Eutectoid zone — fully pearlitic, very fine structure.
   - **Transition zone:** Hypoeutectoid — pearlite + ferrite, with ferrite content increasing with depth.
   - **Core:** Unaffected ferrite + pearlite (proportional to original 0.18% C).

6. **Post-Quench Hardness Profile:** After quenching, the case transforms to **martensite + retained austenite** (retained austenite $\approx$ 10–15% near surface). Surface hardness reaches **800–850 HV (60–62 HRC)**, decaying parabolically to the core hardness of $\approx$ 150 HV (25 HRC).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Identify the formula:**

$$x_{eff} = 2\sqrt{Dt} \cdot \text{erf}^{-1}(\beta)$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $Dt$ for the first case ($t = 6$ h):**

$$t_1 = 6 \times 3600 = 21{,}600 \;\; \text{s}$$

$$D \cdot t_1 = 1.6 \times 10^{-11} \times 21{,}600 = 3.456 \times 10^{-7} \;\; \text{m}^2$$

$$\sqrt{D t_1} = \sqrt{3.456 \times 10^{-7}} = 5.879 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{m} = 0.5879 \;\; \text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $x_{eff}$ for $t_1$:**

$$x_{eff,1} = 2 \times 0.5879 \times 0.85 = 0.9994 \;\; \text{mm} \approx \mathbf{1.0 \;\; mm}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate $x_{eff}$ for the second case ($t_2 = 15$ h):**

$$t_2 = 15 \times 3600 = 54{,}000 \;\; \text{s}$$

$$D \cdot t_2 = 1.6 \times 10^{-11} \times 54{,}000 = 8.64 \times 10^{-7} \;\; \text{m}^2$$

$$\sqrt{D t_2} = \sqrt{8.64 \times 10^{-7}} = 9.295 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{m} = 0.9295 \;\; \text{mm}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate $x_{eff}$ for $t_2$:**

$$x_{eff,2} = 2 \times 0.9295 \times 0.85 = 1.5801 \;\; \text{mm} \approx \mathbf{1.58 \;\; mm}$$

**Step 6 — Comment on the relationship:**

The time increased from 6 h to 15 h, i.e., a factor of **2.5×**. The case depth increased from 1.0 mm to 1.58 mm, a factor of **1.58×**. We can verify:

$$\sqrt{2.5} \approx 1.58 \;\; \checkmark$$

This confirms the **parabolic growth law** $x = K\sqrt{t}$. The case depth increases with the **square root of time**, not linearly. Therefore, **doubling the treatment time increases the case depth by only $\sqrt{2} \approx 41\%$**, not 100%. This has major economic implications: achieving very deep cases (>3 mm) by carburising is **time-inefficient** and not cost-effective, which is why **induction hardening** is preferred for deep local cases.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative to Question A)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Pattern]

**(a)** Explain **induction surface hardening** with a neat schematic. Discuss the effect of **frequency** on case depth, derive the relationship, and explain why this process is preferred for **mass production of shaft-like components**. **\[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A medium-carbon steel shaft (0.4% C) is induction-hardened using a frequency of 50 kHz. The resistivity of steel at the hardening temperature is $\rho = 100$ $\mu\Omega\cdot$cm and $\mu_r = 100$. Calculate the **reference skin depth** $\delta$. If a case depth of 0.5 mm is desired, what frequency should be used? **\[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Principle of Induction Hardening:**

A high-frequency **alternating current (AC)** is passed through a **water-cooled copper inductor coil** placed around or near the steel component. By **Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction**, an **alternating electromotive force (emf)** is induced in the steel, which in turn drives **eddy currents** in the surface region. These eddy currents dissipate energy as **Joule heat ($I^2R$)**, rapidly raising the surface temperature above $A_{c3}$. The bulk of the component remains cool and acts as a heat sink — when the heating current is switched off, the surface **self-quenches** by conduction into the cold core, forming martensite.

**Derivation of the skin depth formula (Reference Depth):**

The reference depth $\delta$ is defined as the depth at which the eddy current density has decayed to $1/e$ (37%) of its surface value. Starting from Maxwell's equation for a planar conductor:

$$\frac{\partial^2 H}{\partial x^2} = \mu \sigma \frac{\partial H}{\partial t}$$

For sinusoidal excitation $H \propto e^{j\omega t}$, the spatial solution gives:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}}$$

Substituting $\omega = 2\pi f$, $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$, $\sigma = 1/\rho$, and performing the unit conversion ($\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m, $\rho$ in $\mu\Omega$-cm, $f$ in Hz, $\delta$ in mm):

$$\delta = 5030 \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\mu_r f}} \;\; \text{mm}$$

**Effect of frequency on case depth:**

| Frequency | Skin Depth | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 kHz | $\approx$ 5 mm | Deep cases on large parts |
| 50 kHz | $\approx$ 0.7 mm | Medium case, common in industry |
| 200 kHz | $\approx$ 0.35 mm | Shallow case, fine teeth |
| 1 MHz | $\approx$ 0.15 mm | Very shallow case, surface only |

**Why preferred for mass production of shafts:**

1. **Speed:** Heating takes only 1–5 seconds (vs. hours for carburising).
2. **Localised heating:** Only the area inside the coil is heated, so no masking is needed.
3. **In-line integration:** Can be placed directly in a CNC production line.
4. **Repeatable case depth:** Frequency is the precise control knob.
5. **Self-quenching:** No separate quench tank needed for some geometries.
6. **No atmosphere control:** Unlike gas carburising, no endothermic gas generator is required.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Apply the reference depth formula:**

$$\delta = 5030 \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\mu_r f}}$$

Given: $\rho = 100$ $\mu\Omega\cdot$cm, $\mu_r = 100$, $f = 50{,}000$ Hz.

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\delta = 5030 \times \sqrt{\frac{100}{100 \times 50{,}000}} = 5030 \times \sqrt{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 5030 \times 4.472 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\delta = 22.49 \;\; \text{mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> At 50 kHz, the reference depth is $\approx$ **22.5 mm**, which is much deeper than the case depth typically needed (1–2 mm). This is because $\delta$ is the **$1/e$ depth**, and practical case depth is **$\approx 0.4 \delta$ to $0.5 \delta$** (the **effective heating depth**, where the heating power is sufficient to reach $A_{c3}$).

**Step 3 — Calculate frequency for $x_{eff} = 0.5$ mm:**

Rule of thumb: $x_{eff} \approx 0.4 \delta$ → $\delta_{required} = 0.5 / 0.4 = 1.25$ mm.

Using the formula (assuming $\rho$ and $\mu_r$ unchanged at heating temperature):

$$1.25 = 5030 \sqrt{\frac{100}{100 \times f}}$$

$$f = 5030^2 \times \frac{100}{100 \times 1.25^2} = \frac{2.53 \times 10^{7}}{1.5625}$$

$$f = 1.619 \times 10^{7} \;\; \text{Hz} \approx \mathbf{16.2 \;\; MHz}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This is a very high frequency (16 MHz) — typical RF range, not standard industrial RF.** The discrepancy reveals that the *simple* $\delta$ formula gives the electromagnetic depth, not the metallurgical case depth. The actual case depth is also governed by **thermal diffusivity** of the steel and the **duration of heating**. In practice, the relationship between frequency and case depth is empirical, and the rule of thumb is: **for 0.5 mm case → use 200–500 kHz**, not 16 MHz.

**Final answer for case (b):**
- Reference skin depth at 50 kHz: $\delta \approx 22.5$ mm.
- For 0.5 mm practical case depth, frequency should be in the range **200 kHz – 500 kHz** (using empirical rules; the strict 1/e formula overestimates the required frequency because the actual case corresponds to about $0.05\delta$, not $0.4\delta$).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Points:**
> 1. **Confusing skin depth ($\delta$) with case depth** — the relationship is **NOT** 1:1. $\delta$ is the electromagnetic depth, case depth is metallurgical. Maximum marks: explicitly state $x_{eff} \approx 0.4 \delta$ (or similar empirical ratio) and justify it.
> 2. **Forgetting units in $\delta$ formula** — the constant **5030** is for $\delta$ in **mm**, $\rho$ in **$\mu\Omega\cdot$cm**, $f$ in **Hz**. Using the wrong unit system is a **silent** error worth **2 marks** lost.
> 3. **Stating "carburising needs no quench"** — **WRONG**. Carburising absolutely requires a quench to transform austenite to martensite. This is one of the most common KTU errors.
