> 4. **Forgetting to mention atmosphere control in gas carburising** — examiners expect the term **"endothermic gas + enrichment gas"** explicitly.
> 5. **Confusing total and effective case depth** — these are **two different quantities** and must be defined separately for full marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Read this 5 times before the exam — it is the highest-yield content.**

### A. Definition & Purpose
- [ ] **Surface hardening** = hard skin + tough core. Failure-mode-driven design.
- [ ] Case depth follows **$x = K\sqrt{t}$** — parabolic growth from Fick's second law.
- [ ] **Effective case depth** = depth to 50 HRC; **Total case depth** = depth to bulk microstructure.

### B. Thermochemical Methods
- [ ] **Carburising** = C diffusion above $A_{c3}$ (900–950°C) + **quench required** + low-carbon steel (0.15–0.25% C).
- [ ] **Nitriding** = N diffusion below $A_{c1}$ (500–550°C) + **no quench** + alloyed steel (Cr, Al, Mo).
- [ ] **Cyaniding** = shallow case (0.1–0.5 mm) in molten cyanide salt — both C and N.
- [ ] **Carbonitriding** = gaseous C+N below or near $A_{c1}$.
- [ ] **Boriding** = B diffusion → $Fe_2B$ (1400–2000 HV — hardest).

### C. Selective Heating Methods
- [ ] **Flame hardening** = oxy-acetylene flame, large parts, deep case possible.
- [ ] **Induction hardening** = eddy-current Joule heating, $\delta = 5030\sqrt{\rho/(\mu_r f)}$ mm.
- [ ] **Laser / EB** = high-energy beam, no external quench, very localised.

### D. Key Formula Set
- [ ] $x = K\sqrt{t}$ — **must be able to derive this from Fick's Second Law** in the exam.
- [ ] $\delta = 5030\sqrt{\rho/(\mu_r f)}$ — units are **mm, $\mu\Omega\cdot$cm, Hz**.
- [ ] $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$ — Arrhenius, used to relate $K$ to temperature.
- [ ] Hollomon–Jaffe $P = T(\log t + C)$ — for tempering hardness prediction.

### E. Materials & Applications
- [ ] **Low-carbon steels** for carburising.
- [ ] **Alloy steels (38CrSi, 40Cr, 34CrAlMo5)** for nitriding.
- [ ] **Medium-carbon steels (AISI 1045, 4140)** for induction hardening.
- [ ] **Gears, shafts, bearings, axles, cams, piston pins, tool spindles** are the primary components.

### F. Industrial Process Selection Rule of Thumb
- [ ] **Need high production rate + localised?** → **Induction**.
- [ ] **Need deep, uniform case + low cost?** → **Gas Carburising**.
- [ ] **Need minimum distortion + high precision?** → **Nitriding** or **Laser**.
- [ ] **Need very high surface hardness (>1000 HV)?** → **Boriding** or **Plasma Nitriding**.
- [ ] **Need to harden huge parts (e.g., mill rolls)?** → **Flame Hardening**.

### G. Two Sub-Families Summary
- [ ] **Diffusion (Thermochemical) = ATOMISTIC approach** — atoms are added to the surface.
- [ ] **Selective Heating = THERMAL approach** — only the surface is heated; no chemical change in composition (for plain steels).
- [ ] Exception: **Laser alloying / laser cladding** can add alloying elements via selective heating.

### H. Mistake Avoidance
- [ ] Do **NOT** say "carburising does not require quench" — it does.
- [ ] Do **NOT** confuse "effective" and "total" case depths.
- [ ] Do **NOT** apply $\delta = 5030\sqrt{\rho/(\mu_r f)}$ without **units** check.
- [ ] Do **NOT** use cyaniding in new design — it is **environmentally restricted**.

> **END OF MODULE 2 — SURFACE HARDENING METHODS NOTES**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strengthening Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT526 - METALLURGY AND MATERIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Strengthening Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Strengthening Mechanisms** in materials science and metallurgy refer to the deliberate microstructural and compositional modifications applied to a metallic material to increase its resistance to plastic deformation, thereby improving its **yield strength (σ_y)**, **ultimate tensile strength (UTS)**, and **hardness**, often without compromising essential engineering properties such as ductility or toughness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEMRT526 – Module 3):**
> Strengthening mechanisms are the various metallurgical strategies used to impede the motion of **dislocations** (line defects in the crystal lattice), because plastic deformation in crystalline solids occurs primarily through dislocation glide. By obstructing dislocation motion, the material becomes mechanically stronger.

The five classical strengthening mechanisms emphasized in the KTU 2024 scheme are:

| # | Mechanism | Barrier to Dislocation Motion |
|---|-----------|------------------------------|
| 1 | **Grain Boundary Strengthening** | Grain boundaries act as obstacles |
| 2 | **Solid Solution Strengthening** | Lattice strain fields |
| 3 | **Strain Hardening (Work Hardening)** | Increased dislocation density |
| 4 | **Precipitation / Age Hardening** | Second-phase particles |
| 5 | **Dispersion Strengthening** | Hard, non-coherent particles |
| 6 | **Fiber / Composite Strengthening** | High-strength fibers |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Crowd in a Stadium"**
> 
> Imagine a stadium full of people trying to push toward the exit (the exit = the direction of applied force). The crowd represents **dislocations**, and the exit represents the slip plane along which deformation occurs.
> 
> - **Empty stadium** → Few obstacles → Crowd flows easily → **Soft metal (e.g., pure aluminum)**
> - **Bigger crowd (more people = dislocations)** → Crowd collides with itself → **Strain hardening**
> - **Tall pillars placed in the exit path** → Crowd must detour → **Precipitation / Dispersion strengthening**
> - **Different-sized people in the crowd (impurity atoms)** → Slower movement → **Solid solution strengthening**
> - **Many small rooms (grains) instead of one big hall** → Must squeeze through narrow doorways → **Grain boundary strengthening**

The unifying principle is simple: **any microstructural feature that "gets in the way" of dislocations increases strength**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold)

- **Shear Modulus (G)** = ~ 26 GPa for Al, ~ 80 GPa for Fe, ~ 44 GPa for Cu (at room temperature)
- **Burgers vector magnitude (b)** = 0.25 – 0.30 nm for most metals
- **Strengthening increment (Δσ)** is typically reported in **MPa**
- **Grain size (d)** is reported in **micrometers (μm)** or **millimeters (mm)** depending on scale
- **Dislocation density (ρ)** is reported in **m/m³** or **lines/m²**
- **Room Temperature (T = 298 K)** is the standard reference for comparative strength data

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU Board Exams:**
> Almost every strengthening mechanism can be written in the generic form 
> **σ_y = σ_0 + Σ (Δσ_i)** where σ_0 is the intrinsic lattice friction stress and Δσ_i are the individual contributions added together. This is a recurring answer pattern in KTU valuations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism-by-Mechanism Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Grain Boundary Strengthening (Hall–Petch Mechanism)

**Why it works:**
- Grain boundaries are regions of atomic misfit where the crystal orientation changes abruptly.
- A dislocation traveling on a slip plane must either (a) **pile-up** at the boundary, or (b) **change slip plane direction** to continue gliding in the next grain.
- Smaller grains → more grain boundary area per unit volume → more barriers.

**How it operates:**
- The applied shear stress concentrates at the tip of a dislocation pile-up.
- When the stress at the pile-up tip reaches a critical value, slip is nucleated in the adjacent grain.
- The finer the grain, the shorter the pile-up length, so a higher applied stress is needed to trigger yielding.

---

### 2.1.2 Solid Solution Strengthening

**Why it works:**
- Substitutional or interstitial solute atoms differ in size from solvent atoms.
- This size mismatch creates a **lattice strain field (ε)** around each solute.
- The strain field interacts elastically with the strain field of a moving dislocation, increasing the stress required for motion.

**How it operates:**
- For **substitutional solutes** (e.g., Zn in Cu, Al in Mg) — atomic size mismatch causes elastic distortion.
- For **interstitial solutes** (e.g., C in Fe, N in Ti) — much greater size mismatch → stronger strengthening per at%.
- Strengthening scales with **(solute concentration)^(1/2)** to **(solute concentration)^(2/3)** depending on the model.

---

### 2.1.3 Strain Hardening (Work Hardening / Cold Working)

**Why it works:**
- Plastic deformation multiplies dislocations through mechanisms such as **Frank–Read sources**.
- Dislocations interact, entangle, and form jogs that act as obstacles to further motion.

**How it operates:**
- As plastic strain (ε_p) increases, dislocation density (ρ) increases.
- Higher ρ → shorter mean free path for gliding dislocations → higher flow stress.
- The phenomenon is captured empirically by the **Hollomon equation**.

---

### 2.1.4 Precipitation (Age) Hardening

**Why it works:**
- A supersaturated solid solution is heat-treated to precipitate fine, hard, second-phase particles.
- These particles block dislocation motion by either the **dislocation cutting mechanism** (small, coherent particles) or the **Orowan looping mechanism** (large, non-coherent particles).

**How it operates:**
- **Stage I — Solutionizing:** Heat above solvus, quench rapidly to retain solutes in solution.
- **Stage II — Aging:** Heat at moderate temperature; fine precipitates form.
- **Peak hardness** occurs at an optimum particle size and spacing.

---

### 2.1.5 Dispersion Strengthening

**Why it works:**
- Hard, thermally stable, **non-coherent oxide or carbide particles** (e.g., Al₂O₃, ThO₂, SiC) are dispersed in a softer metallic matrix.
- These particles do NOT dissolve at high temperature (unlike precipitates), so the strengthening is retained at elevated service temperatures.

**How it operates:**
- Dislocations cannot cut the hard particles → they **bow around** them (Orowan loops).
- Examples: **SAP (Sintered Aluminum Powder)**, **TD-nickel (ThO₂ dispersed Ni)**.

---

### 2.1.6 Fiber / Composite Strengthening

**Why it works:**
- High-strength, high-modulus fibers (e.g., boron, carbon, glass, SiC) are embedded in a metallic matrix.
- Load is transferred from the soft matrix to the strong fibers through the fiber–matrix interface.

**How it operates:**
- The rule of mixtures governs the composite strength.
- Fibers carry the major share of the load; matrix transfers stress and protects fibers.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Mechanism | Governing Equation | Variables & Units | Notes |
|-----------|--------------------|-------------------|-------|
| **Hall–Petch** | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$ | d = grain diameter (m or μm), k_y = Hall–Petch coefficient (MPa·m^(1/2)) | Lower d → higher σ_y |
| **Solid Solution** | $\Delta \tau = G \cdot \varepsilon^{3/2} \cdot \sqrt{c / A}$ | G = shear modulus (Pa), ε = misfit strain, c = solute concentration (at. fraction), A = constant | τ is critical resolved shear stress |
| **Strain Hardening (Hollomon)** | $\sigma = K \cdot \varepsilon_p^{n}$ | K = strength coefficient (MPa), n = strain-hardening exponent (dimensionless) | Valid in plastic region |
| **Taylor Relation (dislocation-based)** | $\tau = \tau_0 + \alpha \cdot G \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{\rho}$ | ρ = dislocation density (m/m³), α = 0.3–0.5, b = Burgers vector (m) | Connects ρ to flow stress |
| **Orowan Looping (precipitation / dispersion)** | $\Delta \tau = \dfrac{G \cdot b}{L - 2r}$ | L = inter-particle spacing (m), r = particle radius (m) | Applies when particle is non-shearable |
| **Rule of Mixtures (composites)** | $\sigma_c = V_f \cdot \sigma_f + V_m \cdot \sigma_m$ | V_f, V_m = volume fractions, σ_f, σ_m = strengths of fiber, matrix | V_f + V_m = 1 |
| **Combined Strengthening** | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \Delta\sigma_{gb} + \Delta\sigma_{ss} + \Delta\sigma_{sh} + \Delta\sigma_{ppt}$ | Each Δσ is additive | Most-used KTU answer form |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Whenever a question asks "Discuss the strengthening mechanisms operating in a cold-worked, age-hardened Al–Cu alloy", the answer must list all applicable mechanisms (solid solution + precipitation + strain hardening + grain boundary) and add their contributions.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application | Dominant Strengthening Mechanism |
|----------|-------------|----------------------------------|
| Aerospace | Al–Cu alloy aircraft skins (2014, 2024) | Precipitation + Solid Solution |
| Automotive | High-strength steel car bodies (DP, TRIP) | Grain refinement + Strain Hardening |
| Power plants | Inconel superalloy turbine blades | Solid Solution + Precipitation + Grain Boundary |
| Cutting tools | WC–Co cemented carbides | Dispersion / Composite |
| Structural | Hot-rolled low-alloy steel sections | Grain Refinement + Solid Solution |
| Electrical | Cu wire (drawn to fine gauges) | Strain Hardening |
| Nuclear | ODS (Oxide Dispersion Strengthened) steels | Dispersion Strengthening |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Hall–Petch Equation — Exhaustive Derivation

The Hall–Petch relationship is a **semi-empirical relation** that ties the lower yield stress (σ_y) of a polycrystalline metal to the average grain diameter (d).

### 3.1.1 Starting Physical Picture

Consider a grain of diameter **d**. A dislocation pile-up forms against a grain boundary under an applied shear stress **τ**. The number of dislocations **n** in the pile-up is given by classical elasticity theory.

### 3.1.2 Pile-up Length Analysis

The shear stress at the tip of a pile-up of **n** dislocations is amplified by a factor of **n** over the applied stress:

$$
\tau_{\text{tip}} = n \cdot \tau
$$

The equilibrium length **L** of the pile-up is approximately equal to the grain diameter:

$$
L \approx d
$$

The relationship between **n**, **τ**, and **L** (from dislocation pile-up theory by Eshelby, Frank & Stroh) is:

$$
n = \dfrac{k_1 \cdot \tau \cdot L}{G \cdot b}
$$

where:
- **k_1** = geometric constant (≈ 1)
- **G** = shear modulus
- **b** = Burgers vector magnitude

### 3.1.3 Condition for Slip Transfer

Slip is activated in the next grain when the stress at the pile-up tip reaches a **critical value τ_c** required to nucleate dislocations across the boundary:

$$
\tau_{\text{tip}} = \tau_c
$$

Substituting:

$$
n \cdot \tau = \tau_c
$$

$$
\left( \dfrac{k_1 \cdot \tau \cdot L}{G \cdot b} \right) \cdot \tau = \tau_c
$$

$$
\dfrac{k_1 \cdot \tau^2 \cdot L}{G \cdot b} = \tau_c
$$

Solving for the applied shear stress **τ**:

$$
\tau^2 = \dfrac{\tau_c \cdot G \cdot b}{k_1 \cdot L}
$$

$$
\tau = \sqrt{ \dfrac{\tau_c \cdot G \cdot b}{k_1 \cdot L} }
$$

### 3.1.4 Conversion to Tensile Yield Stress

Since **L ≈ d** and converting shear stress to tensile stress using the Taylor factor **M ≈ 2**:

$$
\sigma_y = M \cdot \tau = M \cdot \sqrt{ \dfrac{\tau_c \cdot G \cdot b}{k_1 \cdot d} }
$$

This can be rewritten in the canonical Hall–Petch form:

$$
\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}
$$

where:
- $\sigma_0 = M \cdot \sqrt{ \dfrac{\tau_c \cdot G \cdot b \cdot C_0}{k_1} }$ — lattice friction stress
- $k_y = M \cdot \sqrt{ \dfrac{\tau_c \cdot G \cdot b}{k_1} }$ — Hall–Petch slope

> **Conversion Logic Explained:**
> The constant term σ_0 physically represents the stress needed to move dislocations within a single grain (intra-granular friction), while the d^(–1/2) term captures the **inter-granular** resistance offered by grain boundaries.

### 3.1.5 Graphical Representation of Hall–Petch

A plot of **σ_y vs d^(–1/2)** is a straight line:
- **Intercept** on y-axis → σ_0
- **Slope** → k_y
- Steeper slope ⇒ stronger grain-boundary effect (typical of bcc steels, k_y ≈ 0.7 MPa·m^(1/2))
- Gentle slope ⇒ weaker grain-boundary effect (typical of fcc Al, k_y ≈ 0.04 MPa·m^(1/2))

---

## 3.2 Solid Solution Strengthening — Mott–Nabarro Derivation

### 3.2.1 Concept of Misfit Strain

A solute atom of radius **r_solute** in a solvent of radius **r_solvent** produces a misfit strain:

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{r_{\text{solute}} - r_{\text{solvent}}}{r_{\text{solvent}}}
$$

### 3.2.2 Average Internal Stress

Solute atoms are randomly distributed. The local stress from a single solute at distance **r** is:

$$
\tau_{\text{local}} \approx G \cdot \varepsilon \cdot \left( \dfrac{b}{r} \right)^3
$$

Averaging this over the solute population of concentration **c** and using statistical mechanics:

$$
\Delta \tau \propto G \cdot \varepsilon \cdot c^{1/2}
$$

The full Mott–Nabarro form (with **A** as a numerical constant depending on solute type and modulus effects):

$$
\Delta \tau = G \cdot \varepsilon^{3/2} \cdot \sqrt{ \dfrac{c}{A} }
$$

> **Conversion Logic Explained:**
> The exponent 3/2 on ε arises because both the size-misfit strain AND the elastic energy (∝ ε²) contribute to the obstacle strength.

---

## 3.3 Strain Hardening — Taylor Derivation

### 3.3.1 Dislocation Multiplication

Each increment of plastic strain (dε_p) increases the dislocation density (ρ) by a process known as **multiplication** via Frank–Read sources:

$$
d\rho = \dfrac{1}{b \cdot L_s} \cdot d\varepsilon_p
$$

where L_s = mean slip distance of a dislocation.

### 3.3.2 Shear Stress Required to Move Dislocation

The average spacing between dislocations is **ρ^(–1/2)**. The shear stress required to push one dislocation past its neighbors is:

$$
\tau = \alpha \cdot G \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{\rho}
$$

Combining the two relationships and integrating (with initial dislocation density ρ_0):

$$
\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \alpha \cdot G \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{\rho}
$$

This is the **Taylor relation** linking macroscopic yield stress to microscopic dislocation density.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:**
> A low-carbon steel has a yield strength of **210 MPa** at a grain size of **50 μm**. After thermomechanical processing, the grain size is reduced to **10 μm**. The Hall–Petch slope for this steel is **k_y = 0.65 MPa·m^(1/2)**. Calculate the new yield strength.

### Step 1: Convert grain sizes to meters

$$
d_1 = 50 \ \mu m = 50 \times 10^{-6} \ m
$$

$$
d_2 = 10 \ \mu m = 10 \times 10^{-6} \ m
$$

### Step 2: Compute d^(–1/2) for both cases

$$
d_1^{-1/2} = (50 \times 10^{-6})^{-1/2} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{50 \times 10^{-6}}}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{1}{7.071 \times 10^{-3}} = 141.42 \ m^{-1/2}
$$

$$
d_2^{-1/2} = (10 \times 10^{-6})^{-1/2} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{10 \times 10^{-6}}}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{1}{3.162 \times 10^{-3}} = 316.23 \ m^{-1/2}
$$

### Step 3: Use Hall–Petch to find σ_0

$$
\sigma_{y1} = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d_1^{-1/2}
$$

$$
210 = \sigma_0 + 0.65 \times 141.42
$$

$$
210 = \sigma_0 + 91.92
$$

$$
\sigma_0 = 210 - 91.92 = 118.08 \ MPa
$$

### Step 4: Calculate new yield strength at d_2

$$
\sigma_{y2} = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d_2^{-1/2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{y2} = 118.08 + 0.65 \times 316.23
$$

$$
\sigma_{y2} = 118.08 + 205.55 = 323.63 \ MPa
$$

### Step 5: State the result with units and interpretation

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{y2} \approx 324 \ MPa}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Reducing the grain size from 50 μm to 10 μm increases the yield strength by approximately **114 MPa** (a **54% increase**), demonstrating the powerful strengthening effect of grain refinement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Pitfall Avoidance:** Many KTU students forget to convert μm to m before applying the Hall–Petch equation. This leads to wrong answers by a factor of ~ 200. Always check units first.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Hall–Petch | Solid Solution | Strain Hardening | Precipitation |
|----------|------------|----------------|------------------|---------------|
| **Obstacle type** | Grain boundary | Solute atom | Dislocation | Second-phase particle |
| **Effect on ductility** | Improves (toughness ↑) | Reduces slightly | Reduces significantly | Reduces significantly |
| **Temperature stability** | Stable up to 0.5 T_m | Reduces at high T | Eliminates at recrystallization | Coarsens at high T |
| **Magnitude of Δσ** | 20–200 MPa | 10–100 MPa | Up to 500 MPa | 50–500 MPa |
| **Reverse process** | Grain growth | Over-aging | Annealing / Recrystallization | Over-aging |
| **Best suited for** | Structural steels | Solid-solution alloys | Drawn wires, sheets | Heat-treatable Al, Ni alloys |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Block Diagram — Strengthening Mechanisms Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polycrystalline Metal] --> B[Strengthening Strategy Selection]
    B --> C1[Grain Boundary Refinement]
    B --> C2[Alloying - Solid Solution]
    B --> C3[Plastic Deformation]
    B --> C4[Heat Treatment]
    B --> C5[Composite Processing]
    C1 --> D1[Hall-Petch Strengthening]
    C2 --> D2[Mott-Nabarro Strengthening]
    C3 --> D3[Strain Hardening - Taylor]
    C4 --> D4[Precipitation Hardening]
    C5 --> D5[Dispersion and Fiber Strengthening]
    D1 --> E[Increased Yield Strength]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F1[Aerospace Alloys]
    E --> F2[Automotive Steels]
    E --> F3[Turbine Blades]
    E --> F4[Cutting Tools]
    E --> F5[Structural Members]
```

## 4.2 Dislocation–Obstacle Interaction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Applied Shear Stress] --> B[Dislocation Line on Slip Plane]
    B --> C{Obstacle Encountered}
    C -->|Grain Boundary| D1[Pile-up Forms]
    C -->|Solute Atom| D2[Elastic Distortion Field]
    C -->|Forest Dislocation| D3[Jog Formation]
    C -->|Hard Particle| D4[Orowan Loop Bowing]
    D1 --> E[Stress Concentration at Tip]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F{Stress Exceeds Critical Value}
    F -->|Yes| G[Slip Propagation - Plastic Deformation]
    F -->|No| H[Material Continues to Resist - Higher Strength]
```

## 4.3 Hall–Petch Linearized Plot Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Experimental Data - sigma_y and d] --> B[Compute d to the power of minus half]
    B --> C[Plot sigma_y on Y axis]
    B --> D[Plot d inverse half on X axis]
    C --> E[Linear Regression Fit]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Intercept equals sigma_0]
    E --> G[Slope equals k_y]
    F --> H[Hall-Petch Equation Obtained]
    G --> H
```

## 4.4 Precipitation Hardening Heat Treatment Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Alloy in As-Cast Condition] --> B[Solution Treatment - Above Solvus]
    B --> C[Quenching - Retain Supersaturated Solid Solution]
    C --> D{Type of Aging}
    D -->|Room Temperature| E1[Natural Aging - GP Zones]
    D -->|Elevated Temperature| E2[Artificial Aging - Fine Precipitates]
    E1 --> F[Peak Hardness Condition]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Over-Aging - Coarse Particles - Softening]
```

## 4.5 Mechanism Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Required Service Temperature] --> B{Low T below 0.4 Tm}
    A --> C{High T above 0.4 Tm}
    B --> D[Required Ductility]
    D -->|High| E1[Grain Boundary Strengthening]
    D -->|Moderate| E2[Solid Solution Strengthening]
    D -->|Low| E3[Precipitation Hardening]
    C --> F[High Temperature Strength Needed]
    F -->|Yes| G1[Dispersion Strengthening - ODS]
    F -->|No| G2[Coarse Grain - Creep Resistance]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the Hall–Petch relationship and explain the significance of the term $d^{-1/2}$.**

**Model Answer:**

The Hall–Petch relationship is an empirical equation that relates the lower yield stress (σ_y) of a polycrystalline metal to the square root of the average grain diameter (d).

$$
\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}
$$

**Significance of d^(–1/2):**
- The grain diameter is expressed as d^(–1/2) because dislocation pile-up theory predicts an inverse-square-root dependence.
- Smaller grain size ⇒ larger d^(–1/2) value ⇒ higher yield strength.
- A plot of σ_y versus d^(–1/2) gives a straight line with slope k_y and intercept σ_0.

*(Word limit: ~80 words. Full marks require the equation + significance.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between precipitation hardening and dispersion strengthening.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Precipitation Hardening | Dispersion Strengthening |
|---------|-------------------------|---------------------------|
| Source of particles | Formed by heat treatment of a supersaturated alloy | Mechanically added, externally introduced |
| Coherency | Particles may be coherent or semi-coherent | Particles are always non-coherent |
| Temperature stability | Dissolves at high T (over-aging) | Stable up to near melting point |
| Size | Fine (nm) | Coarse (μm) |
| Example | Al–Cu (θ″ precipitates) | SAP (Al₂O₃ in Al) |
| Strengthening mechanism | Cutting or Orowan looping | Always Orowan looping |

*(Word limit: ~80 words. A 3-mark answer should ideally be a 4–5 line table comparison.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Format:** KTU Module End questions offer two full-14-mark alternatives. Both are solved below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) Explain the various strengthening mechanisms in metals with suitable equations. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Plastic deformation in metals occurs by dislocation motion. Strengthening is achieved by **impeding dislocation motion**. The principal mechanisms are:

**1. Grain Boundary Strengthening (Hall–Petch)**

$$
\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}
$$

Grain boundaries block slip. Finer grains → more boundaries → higher strength. The pile-up of dislocations against the boundary must generate sufficient stress to nucleate slip in the next grain.

**2. Solid Solution Strengthening**

$$
\Delta \tau = G \cdot \varepsilon^{3/2} \cdot \sqrt{ \dfrac{c}{A} }
$$

Solute atoms create lattice strain fields that interact with dislocations. Both **size mismatch (ε)** and **modulus mismatch (η)** contribute. Interstitial solutes (e.g., C in Fe) are far more effective per atomic percent than substitutional solutes.

**3. Strain Hardening (Work Hardening)**

$$
\sigma = K \cdot \varepsilon_p^{n}
$$

Dislocation density rises with plastic strain, increasing the resistance to further motion. Based on the Taylor relation:

$$
\tau = \tau_0 + \alpha \cdot G \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{\rho}
$$

**4. Precipitation Hardening**

Fine second-phase particles obstruct dislocations. For small, shearable particles, dislocations cut through (cutting mechanism). For larger, non-shearable particles, the Orowan looping mechanism operates:

$$
\Delta \tau = \dfrac{G \cdot b}{L - 2r}
$$

**5. Dispersion Strengthening**

Hard, non-coherent, thermally stable particles (e.g., oxides) impede dislocations via Orowan looping; strength is retained at high temperatures.

**6. Fiber / Composite Strengthening**

The rule of mixtures applies:

$$
\sigma_c = V_f \cdot \sigma_f + V_m \cdot \sigma_m
$$

**Combined Effect:** The contributions are additive:

$$
\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \Delta\sigma_{gb} + \Delta\sigma_{ss} + \Delta\sigma_{sh} + \Delta\sigma_{ppt}
$$

---

**(b) A brass alloy has a yield strength of 130 MPa at a grain size of 80 μm. After processing, the yield strength becomes 210 MPa. The Hall–Petch coefficient is k_y = 0.35 MPa·m^(1/2). Calculate the new grain size. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert grain size to meters.

$$
d_1 = 80 \ \mu m = 80 \times 10^{-6} \ m
$$

**Step 2:** Find σ_0 from the first condition.

$$
\sigma_{y1} = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d_1^{-1/2}
$$

$$
130 = \sigma_0 + 0.35 \times (80 \times 10^{-6})^{-1/2}
$$

$$
d_1^{-1/2} = (80 \times 10^{-6})^{-1/2} = \dfrac{1}{8.944 \times 10^{-3}} = 111.80 \ m^{-1/2}
$$

$$
130 = \sigma_0 + 0.35 \times 111.80 = \sigma_0 + 39.13
$$

$$
\sigma_0 = 130 - 39.13 = 90.87 \ MPa
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the Hall–Petch equation for the second condition.

$$
\sigma_{y2} = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d_2^{-1/2}
$$

$$
210 = 90.87 + 0.35 \cdot d_2^{-1/2}
$$

$$
0.35 \cdot d_2^{-1/2} = 119.13
$$

$$
d_2^{-1/2} = 340.37 \ m^{-1/2}
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for d_2.

$$
d_2 = (340.37)^{-2} = 8.63 \times 10^{-6} \ m
$$

**Step 5:** Convert back to μm.

$$
\boxed{d_2 \approx 8.6 \ \mu m}
$$

**Interpretation:** The grain size was reduced by an order of magnitude (80 μm → 8.6 μm), giving a 62% increase in yield strength.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) With a neat stress–strain diagram, explain strain hardening. Derive the Hollomon equation and discuss its limitations. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:**
Strain hardening (work hardening) is the phenomenon by which a metal becomes stronger and harder as it is plastically deformed below its recrystallization temperature. The flow stress increases with increasing plastic strain.

**Stress–Strain Diagram (description):**
- **Elastic region (0 → A):** Linear, slope = Young's modulus E.
- **Yield point (A):** Onset of plastic deformation.
- **Plastic region (A → B):** Curve rises monotonically; this rising portion IS the strain-hardening region.
- **Ultimate tensile strength (point B):** Maximum stress; beyond this, necking begins.
- **Fracture (point C):** Final failure.

The relationship between true stress (σ) and true plastic strain (ε_p) in the strain-hardening region is:

$$
\sigma = K \cdot \varepsilon_p^{n}
$$

This is the **Hollomon equation**.

**Derivation of Hollomon Equation:**

In a tensile test, the true stress is related to the applied load and instantaneous area. For plastic deformation at constant volume:

$$
A_0 \cdot L_0 = A \cdot L
$$

The true strain is:

$$
\varepsilon_T = \ln(L / L_0)
$$

The plastic component is:

$$
\varepsilon_p = \varepsilon_T - \sigma / E
$$

For the work-hardened region, experimental curves are well fitted by a power law. The Hollomon equation is **empirically derived** by fitting the plastic portion of the true stress–true strain curve to:

$$
\sigma = K \cdot \varepsilon_p^{n}
$$

where:
- **K** = strength coefficient (MPa)
- **n** = strain-hardening exponent (dimensionless, 0 < n < 1)

Taking logarithms:

$$
\ln \sigma = \ln K + n \cdot \ln \varepsilon_p
$$

So a log–log plot of σ vs ε_p is a straight line of slope **n** and intercept **ln K**.

**Limitations of the Hollomon Equation:**
1. Valid only in the **plastic deformation region** (not in elastic or necking regions).
2. Does **not** hold at very low strains (below the yield point) or very high strains (near fracture).
3. Sensitive to **temperature**: n decreases with rising temperature.
4. Sensitive to **strain rate**: high strain rates increase K and may alter n.
5. Assumes a **uniform** stress–strain response; necking causes deviations.
6. Cannot predict the **onset of plastic instability** (Considère criterion needed separately).
7. The constants K and n are **not material invariants** — they depend on microstructure, prior thermal history, and test conditions.

---

**(b) Describe precipitation hardening with the help of a suitable time–temperature–transformation (TTT) / strength-vs-time curve. Discuss the role of GP zones in Al–Cu alloys. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
Precipitation (age) hardening is a heat-treatment process in which fine particles of a second phase are formed within a metal matrix to impede dislocation motion, thereby increasing strength.

**Three Stages of Heat Treatment:**

1. **Solutionizing:** The alloy is heated above the solvus temperature to dissolve the second phase into a single-phase solid solution.
2. **Quenching:** Rapid cooling (water/oil) to room temperature retains the solute atoms in a **supersaturated solid solution (SSSS)**.
3. **Aging:** The quenched alloy is held at an intermediate temperature (or at room temperature) to allow controlled precipitation.

**Strength-vs-Time (or Aging) Curve:**

The curve has three regions:
- **Under-aged region:** Hardness rises as fine precipitates nucleate.
- **Peak-aged region:** Maximum hardness; optimum particle size and spacing.
- **Over-aged region:** Hardness falls as precipitates coarsen (Orowan spacing L increases, Δτ decreases).

**Precipitation Sequence in Al–Cu (Al–4% Cu):**

The classical precipitation sequence is:

$$
\text{SSSS} \rightarrow \text{GP-I zones} \rightarrow \text{GP-II zones (θ″)} \rightarrow \theta' \rightarrow \theta \ (\text{CuAl}_2)
$$

**Role of GP Zones (Guinier–Preston Zones):**

- GP zones are **fully coherent**, nanometer-scale clusters of Cu atoms on the {100} planes of the Al matrix.
- They form during the **early stages of aging** (natural aging at room temperature for Al–Cu).
- They create **strong elastic strain fields** in the surrounding Al matrix.
- Dislocations must **cut through** these zones → this raises the critical resolved shear stress.
- **GP-I zones** are single Cu-rich atomic layers.
- **GP-II zones (θ″)** are multiple Cu-rich layers with greater strain.
- As aging progresses, GP zones transform into **θ′** (semi-coherent) and then **θ** (non-coherent, equilibrium CuAl₂).
- The **peak hardness** in Al–Cu corresponds to a mixture of GP-II zones and θ′ precipitates.
- Over-aging leads to coarse θ, which is largely incoherent; dislocations bypass them via Orowan looping, and strength drops.

> **Engineering Note:** In commercial practice, Al–Cu alloys (e.g., 2014, 2024) are artificially aged to the T6 temper (peak strength) or T7 temper (slightly over-aged for improved stress-corrosion resistance).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones in KTU Valuation:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in Hall–Petch Problems (–1 to –2 marks):**
>    Students plug d in μm directly into $d^{-1/2}$ without converting to meters. Always convert to SI units (m) first. A grain size of "50 μm" means $50 \times 10^{-6}$ m, not 50 m.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the σ_0 Term (–1 mark):**
>    In Hall–Petch, the equation is **σ_y = σ_0 + k_y·d^(–1/2)**, NOT σ_y = k_y·d^(–1/2). Some students write the simplified form and lose the lattice friction term.
> 
> 3. **Confusing GP Zones with θ Precipitates (–1 mark):**
>    GP zones are coherent Cu-rich clusters; θ is the equilibrium non-coherent CuAl₂ phase. They are different stages, NOT synonyms.
> 
> 4. **Omitting "Why" and Writing Only Equations (–2 to –3 marks):**
>    A 7-mark question requires the *physics* behind the equation. Always state the dislocation mechanism that gives rise to the formula.
> 
> 5. **Missing the Combined-Strength Additivity Principle (–1 mark):**
>    When multiple mechanisms operate simultaneously (e.g., in an age-hardened, cold-worked alloy), the contributions are additive. Always use the sum form.
> 
> 6. **Confusing "Orowan Looping" with "Cutting Mechanism" (–1 mark):**
>    - Small, coherent, shearable particles → **cutting** mechanism.
>    - Large, non-coherent, non-shearable particles → **Orowan looping** mechanism.
> 
> 7. **Skipping Diagrams in Long Essays (–1 mark):**
>    A 14-mark question almost always expects at least one labeled diagram or graph (stress–strain curve, aging curve, or Hall–Petch plot).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Strengthening Mechanisms**

- **Dislocation motion = plastic deformation.** All strengthening mechanisms impede dislocations.
- **Hall–Petch equation:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$ — finer grains → higher strength.
- **Solid solution strengthening** depends on ε (size misfit) and c^(1/2) (concentration); interstitials are stronger strengthener per at% than substitutionals.
- **Strain hardening** follows the Taylor relation: $\tau = \tau_0 + \alpha G b \sqrt{\rho}$ — strength rises with dislocation density.
- **Hollomon equation:** $\sigma = K \cdot \varepsilon_p^{n}$ — valid in the plastic region only; 0 < n < 1.
- **Precipitation hardening** has THREE stages: solutionizing → quenching → aging. Peak strength occurs at optimum particle size.
- **Al–Cu aging sequence:** SSSS → GP-I → GP-II (θ″) → θ′ → θ.
- **Orowan looping** governs dispersion strengthening; **cutting** governs early-stage precipitation.
- **Rule of mixtures** for composites: $\sigma_c = V_f \sigma_f + V_m \sigma_m$.
- **Combined strengthening:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \sum \Delta\sigma_i$ — contributions are additive.
- **High-temperature stability:** Only **dispersion strengthening** and **coarse grains** survive above 0.5 T_m.
- **Reversibility:** Strain hardening is removed by **recrystallization**; precipitation hardening is lost by **over-aging**; grain refinement is undone by **grain growth**.
- **Strain-hardening exponent (n)** is a measure of **formability**: high n → good deep-drawing (e.g., austenitic stainless steel, n ≈ 0.4).
- **Hall–Petch slope (k_y)** is **larger for bcc metals** than for fcc metals — grain refinement is more effective in steels than in aluminum.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cold and Hot Working
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT526 - METALLURGY AND MATERIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cold and Hot Working

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 : COLD AND HOT WORKING — SECTION 1 : FOUNDATIONS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cold Working** is defined as the plastic deformation of a metal carried out at a temperature **below its recrystallization temperature** (typically $T < 0.4\,T_m$ where $T_m$ is the absolute melting point in Kelvin). Because no new grain formation occurs during deformation, the metal exhibits **strain hardening (work hardening)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hot Working** is defined as the plastic deformation of a metal carried out at a temperature **above its recrystallization temperature** (typically $T > 0.5\,T_m$). The processes of **dynamic recovery and dynamic recrystallization** occur simultaneously with deformation, eliminating the effects of strain hardening.

> [!NOTE]
> **Recrystallization Temperature ($T_r$):** The minimum temperature at which a plastically deformed metal forms a completely new set of strain-free, equiaxed grains. Empirically, $T_r \approx 0.4\,T_m$ for pure metals, and $T_r \approx 0.5\text{–}0.6\,T_m$ for alloys.

### 1.1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**Plain-English Analogy — "Kneading Dough":**

Think of a metal as a piece of modelling clay.

- **Cold Working** is like rolling or stretching clay at **room temperature** while it is firm. The clay hardens, becomes springy, tears if pulled too far, and remembers the shape you gave it. In the same way, cold-worked metal becomes **stronger, harder, but more brittle**, and carries internal "tension" (residual stress).

- **Hot Working** is like working the clay on a **hot stove** where it is soft and pliable. You can hammer, roll, or extrude it into complex shapes **without it cracking**, because the metal continuously "heals" itself — new soft grains replace hardened ones as you deform it. The result is a **tough, ductile, refined structure** with no internal locked-in stresses.

### 1.1.3 The Three Temperature Regimes of Plastic Deformation

$$T_{\text{working}} \;\; \begin{cases} < 0.4\,T_m & \rightarrow \text{COLD WORKING (strain hardening dominant)} \\ 0.4\,T_m \leq T \leq 0.5\,T_m & \rightarrow \text{WARM WORKING (limited recovery)} \\ > 0.5\,T_m & \rightarrow \text{HOT WORKING (dynamic recrystallization dominant)} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 3):** Warm working is often examined as a bridging concept — it gives partial strain hardening benefits of cold working plus the lower forming forces of hot working. Examples: warm forging of steels, warm extrusion of aluminum.

### 1.1.4 Why the Recrystallization Temperature is the Dividing Line

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below $T_r$ — atoms lack the mobility to reorganize into new grains → strain accumulates → **dislocation density increases** → hardness/strength rises.
> Above $T_r$ — atoms are mobile enough to form new strain-free grains as fast as deformation occurs → **no net accumulation of strain** → metal stays soft and ductile.

The **dislocation density ($\rho$)** change is a direct fingerprint:

$$\rho_{\text{cold}} \uparrow \uparrow \quad (10^7 \rightarrow 10^{10}\,\text{lines/cm}^2)$$
$$\rho_{\text{hot}} \approx \text{constant} \quad (\text{due to dynamic recovery/recrystallization})$$

### 1.1.5 Common Examples (KTU Board-Favorite)

| Process | Type | Typical Temp | Product Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rolling of steel sheet | Cold | 20 °C – 200 °C | Car body panels, tin cans |
| Wire drawing | Cold | Room temp | Electrical wires, cables |
| Deep drawing | Cold | Room temp | Beverage cans, utensils |
| Forging of crankshaft | Hot | 1100 °C – 1250 °C | Engine components |
| Hot rolling of billets | Hot | 1000 °C – 1200 °C | Rails, plates, structural sections |
| Extrusion of aluminum | Hot | 400 °C – 500 °C | Window frames, tubes |
| Rolling of lead/tin | Cold (Troom > 0.4Tm) | Room temp | Foils, solder sheets |

> [!NOTE]
> Lead and tin are special — their **room temperature itself lies above 0.4 $T_m$**, so they "hot work" at room temperature. This is why lead sheets dent easily without hardening.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 : DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Cold Working — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.1.1 Mechanism of Strain Hardening

When a metal is plastically deformed below $T_r$:

1. **Dislocation multiplication** — Frank-Read sources generate new dislocations.
2. **Dislocation entanglement** — moving dislocations obstruct each other.
3. **Pile-up formation** — dislocations pile up at grain boundaries and obstacles.
4. **Increased resistance to slip** — higher stress required to continue deformation.

The metal's response is described by the **Hollomon Equation** (a KTU favorite):

$$\sigma_t = K \cdot \varepsilon_t^{\,n}$$

where:
- $\sigma_t$ = **true stress** (MPa)
- $K$ = **strength coefficient** (MPa)
- $\varepsilon_t$ = **true plastic strain** (dimensionless)
- $n$ = **strain hardening exponent** (typically $0.1$ to $0.5$ for metals)

> [!NOTE]
> Higher $n$ ⇒ greater formability (metal can be deformed more before localized necking). Annealed copper has $n \approx 0.4$, while H70 brass has $n \approx 0.5$.

### 2.1.2 Percentage Cold Work Formula

The amount of cold work is quantified as:

$$\%CW = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$$

where $A_0$ = original cross-sectional area, $A_f$ = final cross-sectional area.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently give a problem: "A 20 mm diameter rod is cold drawn to 15 mm. Find the % cold work." Use the area form (not diameter) for the standard definition.

### 2.1.3 Effects of Cold Working

| Property | Change | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Yield strength ($\sigma_y$) | ↑ Increases | Dislocation pile-ups obstruct slip |
| Tensile strength (UTS) | ↑ Increases | More stress needed for continued deformation |
| Hardness | ↑ Increases | Direct consequence of strength rise |
| Ductility (% elongation) | ↓ Decreases | Reduced ability to deform further |
| Electrical conductivity | ↓ Slightly decreases | Increased electron scattering at dislocations |
| Residual stresses | ↑ Builds up | Non-uniform plastic flow locked in |
| Density | ↓ Slightly decreases | Microvoids form between tangled dislocations |

### 2.1.4 Preferred Orientation (Texture)

After extensive cold working, grains rotate so that a particular crystallographic plane aligns with the direction of maximum flow. This produces a **deformation texture** (anisotropy).

- **BCC metals** (e.g., iron) → {100} and {111} texture
- **FCC metals** (e.g., copper) → {111} texture
- This causes **earing** in deep-drawn cups — a common practical problem.

### 2.1.5 Cold Working Processes (KTU Classification)

1. **Rolling** (sheet, strip, foil)
2. **Drawing** (wire, tube, deep drawing)
3. **Extrusion** (cold impact extrusion)
4. **Forging** (cold heading of bolts, rivets)
5. **Bending, Shearing, Stamping, Spinning, Coining, Squeezing**

### 2.1.6 Annealing of Cold-Worked Metal (3 Stages — High-Yield Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Recovery → Recrystallization → Grain Growth. This sequence is a KTU-favorite 14-mark question.

| Stage | Temperature | Microstructural Change | Property Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Recovery** | Low ($< T_r$) | Dislocation rearrangement, reduction in point defects | Electrical conductivity restored, slight softening |
| **Recrystallization** | At $T_r$ | New strain-free equiaxed grains nucleate and grow | Sharp drop in hardness, full softening |
| **Grain Growth** | Above $T_r$ | New grains grow by consuming smaller ones | Further softening, decreased strength |

## 2.2 Hot Working — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 Mechanism: Dynamic Recovery and Recrystallization

During hot deformation:

- **Dynamic Recovery (DRV):** Dislocations climb and annihilate, reducing stored strain energy.
- **Dynamic Recrystallization (DRX):** New strain-free grains nucleate at grain boundaries (especially in low stacking-fault-energy metals like Cu, Ni, austenitic steel).
- **Static Recrystallization (SRX):** After deformation stops, further grain refinement occurs during cooling.

### 2.2.2 Effects of Hot Working

| Property | Change | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Grain size | ↓ Refined | Recrystallization produces fine equiaxed grains |
| Mechanical strength | ↑ Improved (toughness) | Fine grains → Hall-Petch strengthening |
| Ductility | ↑ Improved | New strain-free grains enable more deformation |
| Porosity (castings) | ↓ Reduced | Pores and voids are welded shut |
| Residual stresses | Negligible | Continuous recovery relieves stresses |
| Surface finish | Poor (oxidation) | High temperature → scale formation |

### 2.2.3 Hot Working Processes

1. **Hot Rolling** (blooming, plate, strip mills)
2. **Hot Forging** (hammer, press, upset forging)
3. **Hot Extrusion** (direct, indirect for non-ferrous metals)
4. **Hot Drawing / Pilgering** (tube manufacturing)
5. **Hot Spinning** (large rotational symmetric parts)

### 2.2.4 Hot Working Temperature Range (Critical Concept)

$$T_{\text{hot-work, min}} > T_r \quad ; \quad T_{\text{hot-work, max}} < T_{\text{burnout}}$$

- Going **below $T_r$** → accidental cold work → strain hardening, possible cracking.
- Going **too close to melting** → **burning** (oxidation at grain boundaries) → **hot shortness** → catastrophic failure.

> [!WARNING]
> The upper limit of hot working is set by either the **solidus temperature** or the temperature at which **excessive oxidation / grain coarsening** begins. KTU examiners often ask for the safe hot-working range of steels: approximately **$900\,°\text{C} – 1250\,°\text{C}$**.

## 2.3 Cold Working vs Hot Working — Master Comparison Table

| Parameter | Cold Working | Hot Working |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | Below $T_r$ | Above $T_r$ |
| Strain hardening | Yes — significant | No (offset by recrystallization) |
| Yield strength | ↑ Increases greatly | Slight increase (refined grains) |
| Ductility | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Increases |
| Surface finish | Excellent (bright) | Poor (scaled, oxidized) |
| Dimensional accuracy | High | Low (cooling shrinkage) |
| Residual stresses | High | Negligible |
| Force required | Very high | Low (soft metal) |
| Grain structure | Distorted, elongated | Refined, equiaxed |
| Porosity | Not eliminated | Reduced/welded |
| Equipment cost | Low | High (furnaces, handling) |
| Energy efficiency | Higher (no heating) | Lower (heating cost) |
| Typical products | Sheet, foil, wire | Billets, rails, forgings |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize these formulas — they are recurring in KTU University Exam questions.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\%CW = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$ | Percentage cold work | % |
| 2 | $\varepsilon_t = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = \ln(1+\varepsilon_n)$ | True strain from area | — |
| 3 | $\sigma_t = \sigma_n(1+\varepsilon_n)$ | True stress | MPa |
| 4 | $\sigma_t = K \cdot \varepsilon_t^{\,n}$ | Hollomon (strain hardening) | MPa |
| 5 | $\sigma_y' = \sigma_y + \Delta\sigma_{\text{work}}$ | Yield strength post cold work | MPa |
| 6 | $T_r \approx 0.4\,T_m$ | Recrystallization temperature | K |
| 7 | $T_{\text{hot}} > 0.5\,T_m$ | Hot working condition | K |
| 8 | $F = K_f \cdot \sigma_f \cdot A$ | Forging / forming load | N |
| 9 | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{\sigma_0 + \sigma_f'}{2}$ | Average flow stress | MPa |
| 10 | $\sigma_f = K \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon_t^{\,n}}{1+n}$ | Mean flow stress (Hollomon) | MPa |
| 11 | $\varepsilon_{eq} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{3}\!\left(\varepsilon_1^2 + \varepsilon_2^2 + \varepsilon_3^2\right)}$ | Equivalent strain (von Mises) | — |
| 12 | $\Delta\sigma \propto \sqrt{\rho}$ | Taylor relation (dislocation strengthening) | MPa |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, all vertical separators are formatted with `\vert` or `/` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 : STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation : From Engineering Stress-Strain to True Stress-Strain

### 3.1.1 Volume Constancy Assumption

During plastic deformation, metal is **incompressible**. Therefore, the volume before and after deformation is conserved:

$$V_0 = V_f \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; A_0 \cdot L_0 = A_f \cdot L_f$$

where $A_0, A_f$ are initial and final cross-sectional areas, $L_0, L_f$ are initial and final lengths.

### 3.1.2 Definition of True Strain

True strain is the **instantaneous** strain increment integrated from initial to final length:

$$\varepsilon_t = \int_{L_0}^{L_f} \frac{dL}{L} = \ln\!\left(\frac{L_f}{L_0}\right)$$

Now applying volume constancy $\dfrac{L_f}{L_0} = \dfrac{A_0}{A_f}$:

$$\varepsilon_t = \ln\!\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - \%CW/100}\right)$$

### 3.1.3 Definition of True Stress

True stress uses the **instantaneous (actual)** cross-section, not the original:

$$\sigma_t = \frac{P}{A_f} = \frac{P}{A_0 \cdot (L_0/L_f)}$$

Since engineering stress $\sigma_n = P/A_0$ and $L_f/L_0 = 1+\varepsilon_n$:

$$\sigma_t = \sigma_n \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_n)$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Percentage Cold Work

**Problem:** A copper rod of diameter **20 mm** is cold drawn through a die to a final diameter of **15 mm**. Calculate:
(a) the percentage cold work,
(b) the true strain,
(c) the true stress if the final drawing load is 80 kN.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute initial and final areas:**

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4}(20)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 400 = 100\pi \;\text{mm}^2 \approx 314.16\;\text{mm}^2$$

$$A_f = \frac{\pi}{4}(15)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 225 = 56.25\pi \;\text{mm}^2 \approx 176.71\;\text{mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute % cold work:**

$$\%CW = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \frac{314.16 - 176.71}{314.16} \times 100$$

$$\%CW = \frac{137.45}{314.16} \times 100 \approx 43.75\,\%$$

**Step 3 — Compute true strain:**

$$\varepsilon_t = \ln\!\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{314.16}{176.71}\right) = \ln(1.7778) \approx 0.5754$$

**Step 4 — Compute true stress at final drawing load:**

$$\sigma_t = \frac{P}{A_f} = \frac{80\,000\;\text{N}}{176.71\;\text{mm}^2} = 452.7\;\text{MPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Summary:** %CW ≈ **43.75 %**, True Strain ≈ **0.575**, True Stress ≈ **452.7 MPa**. The metal has been heavily cold-worked — it would now show significant strain hardening and require annealing before further deformation.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Hollomon Equation (Mean Flow Stress)

**Problem:** A steel specimen is hot worked between $\varepsilon_1 = 0.1$ and $\varepsilon_2 = 0.5$. Given $K = 600$ MPa and $n = 0.25$, find the **mean flow stress** using the Hollomon average formula.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the mean flow stress formula (Hollomon):**

$$\sigma_f = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2 - \varepsilon_1}\int_{\varepsilon_1}^{\varepsilon_2} K \cdot \varepsilon^n \, d\varepsilon$$

**Step 2 — Perform the integration:**

$$\sigma_f = \frac{K}{\varepsilon_2 - \varepsilon_1} \cdot \left[\frac{\varepsilon^{n+1}}{n+1}\right]_{\varepsilon_1}^{\varepsilon_2}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate with given values:**

$$\sigma_f = \frac{600}{0.5 - 0.1} \cdot \left[\frac{\varepsilon^{1.25}}{1.25}\right]_{0.1}^{0.5}$$

$$\sigma_f = \frac{600}{0.4} \cdot \frac{(0.5)^{1.25} - (0.1)^{1.25}}{1.25}$$

Compute the powers:
- $(0.5)^{1.25} = 0.5^{1.25} = e^{1.25 \ln 0.5} = e^{1.25 \times (-0.6931)} = e^{-0.8664} \approx 0.4204$
- $(0.1)^{1.25} = 10^{-1.25} \approx 0.0562$

Difference: $0.4204 - 0.0562 = 0.3642$

Divide by 1.25: $0.3642 / 1.25 = 0.2914$

Multiply by $600 / 0.4 = 1500$:

$$\sigma_f = 1500 \times 0.2914 \approx 437.1\;\text{MPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Mean flow stress $\sigma_f \approx$ **437 MPa**. This is the average resistance the metal offers during hot deformation in that strain range — used to calculate forging press tonnage.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Forging Load Calculation

**Problem:** A cylindrical billet of area $A_0 = 5000$ mm² is hot forged in an open die. Final area $A_f = 2500$ mm². Using the formula $F = K_f \cdot \sigma_f \cdot A$ with $K_f = 1.5$ and $\sigma_f = 400$ MPa, find the **maximum forging force**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the appropriate area.** In open-die forging, the maximum force occurs at the **largest contact area**, which is the **initial area $A_0$** (since the area decreases as forging progresses).

**Step 2 — Apply the formula:**

$$F = K_f \cdot \sigma_f \cdot A_{\max} = 1.5 \times 400 \;\text{MPa} \times 5000\;\text{mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Convert units carefully:**

$$1\;\text{MPa} = 1\;\text{N/mm}^2$$

$$F = 1.5 \times 400 \times 5000 = 3\,000\,000\;\text{N} = 3000\;\text{kN} = 3\;\text{MN}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Forging force ≈ **3000 kN = 3 MN**. KTU exam trick — students often use $A_f$ instead of $A_{\max}$, which is the **most common error** in forging calculations.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix : Selecting Cold vs Hot Working

Use this decision logic for KTU design questions:

$$\text{Is final product thin / high-tolerance?} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{COLD WORKING}$$

$$\text{Is starting material a brittle casting with porosity?} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{HOT WORKING}$$

$$\text{Is large plastic strain (>> 50 % reduction) needed in one pass?} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{HOT WORKING}$$

$$\text{Does product need high surface finish?} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{COLD WORKING (finish pass)}$$

$$\text{Is final strength more important than ductility?} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{COLD WORKING (as-rolled)}$$

$$\text{Is fracture toughness more important?} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{HOT WORKING + controlled cooling}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Practice:** Most finished steel products undergo **both** — first **hot working** to break down the cast structure and achieve shape, then **cold working** as a final finishing pass to improve surface finish, dimensional accuracy, and strength.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 : STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Plastic Deformation Routes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Raw Material Casting or Billet] --> decision1{Required Deformation > 50 percent in one pass?}
    decision1 -- Yes --> hotA[Heat above Recrystallization Temperature T_r]
    decision1 -- No --> decision2{Final product needs high surface finish or tight tolerance?}
    decision1 -- Maybe --> warmA[Warm Working between 0.4 T_m and 0.5 T_m]
    hotA --> hotB[Hot Working - Dynamic Recovery and Recrystallization]
    hotB --> output1[Refined Grain Structure - Ductile - No Residual Stress]
    output1 --> finish1{Further finishing needed?}
    finish1 -- Yes --> coldA[Cold Working Pass]
    finish1 -- No --> final1[Final Hot Worked Product]
    decision2 -- Yes --> coldB[Cold Working - Strain Hardening Active]
    decision2 -- No --> decision3{Final strength must be very high?}
    decision3 -- Yes --> coldC[Heavy Cold Work then Partial Anneal]
    decision3 -- No --> hotC[Use Hot Working Only]
    coldB --> output2[High Strength - High Residual Stress - Distorted Grains]
    output2 --> anneal1{Annealing needed to restore ductility?}
    anneal1 -- Yes --> annealA[Recovery then Recrystallization then Grain Growth]
    anneal1 -- No --> final2[Final Cold Worked Product]
    annealA --> final3[Soft Ductile Product - Strain Free Grains]
    coldC --> final4[High Strength with some ductility]
    hotC --> final5[Hot Worked Product]
    warmA --> output3[Warm Worked Product - Partial Recovery]
```

## 4.2 Process Map: Annealing Stages of Cold Worked Metal

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startA[Stage A - Cold Worked State] -->|Low Heat| recA[Stage B - Recovery]
    recA -->|Defect annihilation, dislocation rearrangement| recB[Stage C - Recrystallization]
    recB -->|New strain free equiaxed grains| recC[Stage D - Grain Growth]
    recC -->|Time at high temperature| endA[Stage E - Coarse Grains Lower Strength]
    recA --> prop1[Electrical conductivity restored, slight softening]
    recB --> prop2[Sharp drop in hardness, full softening, ductile]
    recC --> prop3[Further softening, lower strength]
    startA --> prop0[High dislocation density, hard, brittle, residual stress]
```

## 4.3 Hot Working Process Family

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    hot[Hot Working Family - T above T_r] --> h1[Hot Rolling]
    hot --> h2[Hot Forging]
    hot --> h3[Hot Extrusion]
    hot --> h4[Hot Drawing - Tube Pilgering]
    hot --> h5[Hot Spinning]
    h1 --> prod1[Plates, Sheets, Rails, Structural Sections]
    h2 --> prod2[Crankshafts, Gears, Connecting Rods]
    h3 --> prod3[Aluminum Profiles, Brass Rods, Tubes]
    h4 --> prod4[Seamless Tubes and Pipes]
    h5 --> prod5[Large Dished Ends, Conical Shells]
```

## 4.4 Cold Working Process Family

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cold[Cold Working Family - T below T_r] --> c1[Cold Rolling]
    cold --> c2[Cold Drawing - Wire and Tube]
    cold --> c3[Cold Extrusion - Impact]
    cold --> c4[Cold Forging - Heading]
    cold --> c5[Deep Drawing, Stamping, Spinning]
    c1 --> cp1[Sheet metal, foils, car body panels]
    c2 --> cp2[Electrical wires, seamless tubes]
    c3 --> cp3[Aluminum beverage cans, toothpaste tubes]
    c4 --> cp4[Bolts, rivets, fasteners]
    c5 --> cp5[Cookware, cans, kitchen sinks]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams matter for KTU exams:** When the question asks "Classify the plastic deformation processes" or "Compare hot and cold working with examples," these block diagrams provide a complete mental map. In 14-mark questions, drawing such a tree (even schematic) and adding 2-3 supporting sentences for each branch typically scores full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A : Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define cold working. State any four effects of cold working on the properties of metals.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Cold working** is the plastic deformation of a metal carried out at a temperature **below its recrystallization temperature** ($T < 0.4\,T_m$). Because no recrystallization occurs during deformation, the strain energy accumulates in the form of increased dislocation density.

**Four effects on properties:**

1. **Yield strength and tensile strength increase** due to dislocation pile-ups obstructing further slip (strain hardening).
2. **Hardness increases** as a direct consequence of strength rise.
3. **Ductility (% elongation) decreases** because the metal can no longer deform plastically to the same extent.
4. **Residual stresses develop** because plastic flow is non-uniform and is locked in after the load is removed.
5. *(Bonus point)* Density slightly decreases due to microvoid formation between tangled dislocations.

> **Examiner's tip (3-mark answer):** Define cold work in 2 lines (1 mark), then list 4 effects with one-line reason each (2 marks = 0.5 mark each).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the recrystallization temperature? Why is it the dividing line between hot and cold working?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Recrystallization temperature ($T_r$)** is the minimum temperature at which a cold-worked metal forms a **completely new set of strain-free, equiaxed grains**, replacing the deformed structure. Empirically, $T_r \approx 0.4\,T_m$ for pure metals.

**Why it is the dividing line:**

- **Below $T_r$:** atomic mobility is insufficient to permit new grain formation. Dislocation density keeps rising, and the metal **strain-hardens** as deformation proceeds — this is **cold working**.
- **Above $T_r$:** atomic mobility is high enough that **dynamic recovery and recrystallization** occur simultaneously with deformation, continuously replacing hardened regions with new soft grains — this is **hot working**.

> Since recrystallization determines whether strain energy is retained or relieved, it is the **fundamental physical boundary** between cold and hot working regimes.

---

## 5.2 PART B : Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks with two sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**, and you must answer EITHER the OR choice. Below are two complete parallel choices.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**A. (a)** Explain the mechanism of **strain hardening** in cold working with the help of a stress-strain diagram. **(7 marks)**

**A. (b)** A copper bar of cross-section **$25 \times 25$ mm** is cold rolled to a final thickness of **15 mm** in one pass. If the original length is 200 mm and the flow curve is given by $\sigma = 520\,\varepsilon^{0.32}$ MPa, calculate:
(i) the percentage cold work,
(ii) the true strain, and
(iii) the rolling load if the contact length is 30 mm. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(a)

**Definition:** Strain hardening (work hardening) is the phenomenon in which a metal becomes **stronger and harder** as it is plastically deformed below the recrystallization temperature.

**Mechanism (Step-by-step):**

1. **Dislocation multiplication** — when a metal is plastically deformed, sources such as **Frank-Read sources** generate millions of new dislocations. Dislocation density rises from $10^7$ to $10^{10}$ lines/cm².
2. **Dislocation interaction** — dislocations moving on intersecting slip planes **entangle and obstruct** each other's motion.
3. **Pile-up formation** — dislocations pile up at grain boundaries, second-phase particles, and other obstacles. A **back-stress** develops that must be overcome for further slip.
4. **Increased critical resolved shear stress (CRSS)** — the stress required to move dislocations increases progressively with strain.
5. **Macroscopic effect** — the metal's flow stress rises with strain, seen as the upward slope of the true stress–true strain curve beyond the yield point.

**Stress-Strain Diagram Explanation:**

The diagram shows three regions: **(i) Elastic** (linear), **(ii) Yield point** (transition), and **(iii) Plastic with strain hardening** (rising curve). The **Hollomon equation** $\sigma_t = K\,\varepsilon_t^n$ describes the plastic region, where $K$ is the strength coefficient and $n$ is the strain-hardening exponent.

**Effects summary:**

| Property | Trend |
|---|---|
| Yield strength, UTS, Hardness | ↑ Increase |
| Ductility, %Elongation | ↓ Decrease |
| Residual stresses | ↑ Increase |

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(b)

**Given:**
- $h_0 = 25$ mm, $h_f = 15$ mm, $L_0 = 200$ mm, $L_c = 30$ mm
- $\sigma = 520 \cdot \varepsilon^{0.32}$ MPa, width $w = 25$ mm

**Step 1 — % Cold Work (using thickness approximation, since width is unchanged and $b_0 = b_f$):**

$$\%CW = \frac{h_0 - h_f}{h_0} \times 100 = \frac{25 - 15}{25} \times 100 = 40\,\%$$

**Step 2 — True strain (using volume constancy, $A_0 L_0 = A_f L_f$):**

$$\varepsilon_t = \ln\!\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{25}{15}\right) = \ln(1.6667)$$

$$\varepsilon_t = 0.5108$$

**Step 3 — True stress at final strain:**

$$\sigma_t = 520 \times (0.5108)^{0.32} = 520 \times e^{0.32 \times \ln(0.5108)}$$

Compute $\ln(0.5108) = -0.6716$; then $0.32 \times (-0.6716) = -0.2149$; then $e^{-0.2149} = 0.8066$

$$\sigma_t = 520 \times 0.8066 = 419.4\;\text{MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Rolling Load:**

$$F = \sigma_t \cdot L_c \cdot w = 419.4 \times 30 \times 25 = 314\,550\;\text{N} \approx 314.5\;\text{kN}$$

> **Final Answers:** (i) %CW = **40 %**, (ii) True strain = **0.5108**, (iii) Rolling load ≈ **314.5 kN**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly forget the **volume constancy** step and use $\varepsilon_t = (h_0 - h_f)/h_f$ instead of $\ln(A_0/A_f)$. Marks are lost (typically 1-2 marks) for this. Also, in rolling load formulas, use the **final (true) stress**, not the yield stress, unless the problem explicitly says otherwise.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**B. (a)** Compare **hot working and cold working** with respect to temperature, mechanism, mechanical properties, surface finish, and applications. **(7 marks)**

**B. (b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **three stages of annealing** of a cold-worked metal — recovery, recrystallization, and grain growth. State how each stage affects hardness. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(a)

**Comparative table (write this in the exam for full marks):**

| Parameter | Cold Working | Hot Working |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | Below recrystallization temp ($< 0.4\,T_m$) | Above recrystallization temp ($> 0.5\,T_m$) |
| Mechanism | Strain hardening — dislocation density rises | Dynamic recovery + dynamic recrystallization |
| Strength | Greatly increases (work hardening) | Slight increase due to grain refinement |
| Ductility | Decreases | Increases |
| Surface finish | Excellent — bright, smooth, oxide-free | Poor — oxide scale forms |
| Dimensional accuracy | High — no thermal contraction | Low — contraction on cooling |
| Residual stresses | High | Negligible |
| Forces required | High (hard metal resists deformation) | Low (soft metal flows easily) |
| Grain shape | Distorted, elongated, fibrous | Equiaxed, refined |
| Applications | Sheet rolling, wire drawing, deep drawing, cold forging | Blooming, hot rolling, hot forging, hot extrusion |
| Equipment cost | Low | High (heating furnaces, handling) |
| Energy efficiency | Higher | Lower (heat loss) |
| Material wastage | Higher (more passes) | Lower (large reduction per pass) |

**Key conceptual difference (state this in 2-3 lines):**

> The fundamental difference lies in the **competition between strain hardening and recrystallization**. In cold working, hardening dominates because the temperature is too low for new grains to form. In hot working, recrystallization occurs simultaneously with deformation, so the metal remains soft and ductile throughout the process.

**Examples:**
- Cold working: aluminum foil, car body panels, electrical wires, beverage cans.
- Hot working: rails, structural beams, crankshafts, seamless tubes.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(b)

> **Schematic Description** *(to be drawn in the exam — a hardness-vs-temperature plot with three labelled regions)*:

The plot has **Hardness (HRC or HV) on the Y-axis** and **Temperature on the X-axis**. Starting from the cold-worked state (high hardness), the curve drops in three stages:

**Stage 1 — Recovery (Low Temperature Region):**
- Temperature: well below recrystallization temperature.
- Microstructural changes: point defects (vacancies, interstitials) annihilate; dislocations **rearrange into lower-energy configurations** (e.g., cell structures); no new grains form.
- Property change: **hardness decreases only slightly**; electrical conductivity is largely restored. Internal stresses are partially relieved.

**Stage 2 — Recrystallization (At $T_r$):**
- New **strain-free, equiaxed grains** nucleate at high-energy sites (grain boundaries, deformation bands) and grow, consuming the deformed matrix.
- Property change: **hardness drops sharply** to nearly the original annealed value; ductility is fully restored.
- Driving force: stored strain energy from cold work.

**Stage 3 — Grain Growth (Above $T_r$):**
- New grains grow at the expense of smaller ones (grain boundary migration driven by reduction in grain boundary area).
- Property change: **hardness decreases further** (Hall-Petch: larger grains → lower strength); ductility remains high.

**Hardness-vs-Temperature Diagram (verbal sketch):**

```
Hardness
   |
   |* (cold worked - high)
   | *
   |  *     (Stage 1 - slight drop, Recovery)
   |   *___
   |       *      (Stage 2 - sharp drop, Recrystallization)
   |        *___________
   |                     ****____      (Stage 3 - gradual drop, Grain Growth)
   |__________________________________ Temperature
            T_recovery   T_recrystallization
```

**Summary Table for Annealing Stages:**

| Stage | Temperature | Microstructure | Hardness | Driving Force |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Recovery | $T < T_r$ | Dislocation rearrangement, no new grains | Slight drop | Stored strain energy (point defects) |
| Recrystallization | $T \approx T_r$ | New strain-free equiaxed grains | Sharp drop | Stored strain energy (dislocations) |
| Grain Growth | $T > T_r$ | Grain boundary migration, coarse grains | Gradual drop | Reduction in grain boundary area |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Many students confuse **"recovery" with "recrystallization."** Recovery does **not** produce new grains — it only rearranges existing defects. Recrystallization is the stage where new grains appear. Examiners frequently award 1-2 marks for stating this distinction clearly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cold & Hot Working — KTU Module 3 Quick Recall:**

- **Cold Working** = deformation **below** recrystallization temperature ($T < 0.4\,T_m$). No new grains form. **Strain hardening** dominates.
- **Hot Working** = deformation **above** recrystallization temperature ($T > 0.5\,T_m$). **Dynamic recovery + dynamic recrystallization** neutralize strain hardening.
- **Recrystallization temperature** is **empirically $T_r \approx 0.4\,T_m$** for pure metals, $0.5\text{–}0.6\,T_m$ for alloys. Divide by 1000 to convert $T_m$ from K to use in °C conveniently.
- **Cold work formula:** $\%CW = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$ — uses **areas**, not linear dimensions.
- **True strain** $\varepsilon_t = \ln(A_0/A_f)$. **True stress** $\sigma_t = \sigma_n(1+\varepsilon_n)$.
- **Hollomon Equation** $\sigma_t = K \cdot \varepsilon_t^{\,n}$ — describes the plastic region; $n$ is the **strain hardening exponent**.
- **Effects of cold work:** ↑ strength, ↑ hardness, ↑ residual stress, ↓ ductility, ↓ electrical conductivity, ↓ density (microvoids).
- **Annealing stages (in order):** Recovery → Recrystallization → Grain Growth. Each stage requires progressively higher temperature.
- **Hot working upper limit:** just below the **solidus** or where excessive **oxidation / grain coarsening** begins. For steels, hot working range is roughly **900 °C – 1250 °C**.
- **Hot working benefits:** refined equiaxed grains, reduced porosity (casting defects welded shut), improved toughness, no residual stresses.
- **Hot working drawbacks:** poor surface finish (scale), low dimensional accuracy, high equipment cost.
- **Forging load** $F = K_f \cdot \sigma_f \cdot A_{\max}$ — use **initial area** (largest) for open-die forging.
- **Mean flow stress (Hollomon):** $\sigma_f = \dfrac{K \cdot \varepsilon_t^{\,n}}{1+n}$ (for strain varying from 0 to $\varepsilon_t$).
- **Equiaxed vs fibrous grains:** Cold work → fibrous (elongated); Hot work → equiaxed.
- **Industry sequence:** Cast → **Hot work** (break down cast structure) → **Cold work** (finish to final dimensions/strength).
- **Special cases:** Lead, tin, zinc have $T_r$ below room temperature → they "hot work" at ambient conditions.
- **Warm working** ($0.4\,T_m \leq T \leq 0.5\,T_m$): partial recovery, intermediate force requirement, better surface finish than hot, more formability than cold.
- **Texture / Preferred Orientation:** Heavy cold work produces anisotropic properties → earing in deep-drawn cups.
- **Hall-Petch link:** Hot-worked fine grains → higher yield strength via $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k/\sqrt{d}$.
- **KTU favorites (asked repeatedly):** Compare hot vs cold working, list 4 effects of cold work, name 3 annealing stages, derive Hollomon equation, calculate %CW and rolling/forging load.

> **End of Module 3 — Cold and Hot Working Notes.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Alloy Steels: How Alloying Elements Affect Properties of Steel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT526 - METALLURGY AND MATERIAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Alloy Steels: How Alloying Elements Affect Properties of Steel # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Alloy Steel:** An **iron–carbon alloy** (base steel) to which one or more **deliberate alloying elements** (other than carbon) are added in controlled proportions—typically ranging from **1 wt% to 50 wt%**—to enhance specific mechanical, physical, or chemical properties such as hardenability, tensile strength, toughness, wear resistance, and corrosion resistance. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMRT526 – Module 3)**, alloy steels are broadly classified based on the **total alloying content**: | Classification | Total Alloying Content | Typical Examples | |---|---|---| | **Low-Alloy Steel** | **< 8 wt%** | AISI 4140, 4340, 8620, HSLA steels | | **High-Alloy Steel** | **> 8 wt%** | Stainless steels (304, 316), tool steels (HSS), Hadfield steel | > [!NOTE] > **Steel vs. Alloy Steel:** Plain carbon steel contains only Fe + C (≤ 2 wt% C) + residual Mn, Si, S, P. **Alloy steel** is distinguished by the **intentional addition** of alloying elements above the residual limit (e.g., Mn > 1.65 %, Si > 0.5 %, Cu > 0.4 %). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Spice in a Recipe" View Imagine plain carbon steel as a **plain rice dish**—nutritious but bland. Now, alloying elements act like **spices added by the chef**: - A **pinch of chromium (Cr)** is like adding **turmeric**—gives golden color and protects against "rust disease" (corrosion). - **Nickel (Ni)** is like adding **pepper**—improves toughness and ductility without making the dish "hot" (brittle). - **Molybdenum (Mo)** is like adding **cardamom**—a small amount completely transforms the aroma (high-temperature strength). - **Manganese (Mn)** acts like **salt**—removes harmful impurities (S) and refines the grain (texture). Just as too much spice ruins a dish, **excessive alloying** can make steel difficult to cast, weld, or machine. The metallurgist's job is to find the **optimal "recipe"** for the desired application. ## 1.3 Why Alloying Elements? – The Driving Forces > [!IMPORTANT] > **Primary Objectives of Alloying:** > 1. **Increase hardenability** – ability to form martensite in thick sections during quenching. > 2. **Improve mechanical strength** – via solid solution strengthening, precipitation hardening. > 3. **Enhance toughness** – grain refinement, suppression of brittle phases. > 4. **Provide corrosion/oxidation resistance** – formation of passive oxide films. > 5. **Retain properties at high temperature** – creep resistance, hot hardness. > 6. **Improve wear resistance** – carbide formers (Cr, V, W, Mo). > 7. **Stabilize specific phases** – e.g., Ni stabilizes austenite; Cr stabilizes ferrite. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics Recalled - **Density of pure iron (α-Fe):** $\rho_{Fe} = 7.87 \text{ g/cm}^3$ - **Atomic radius of Fe:** $r_{Fe} = 0.124 \text{ nm}$ (BCC at room temperature) - **Hardenability** is measured by the **Jominy End-Quench Test**, with results expressed as the **Jominy distance** (in **mm** or **inches** of **1/16 in**). - **Critical cooling rate** ($V_c$) is measured in **°C/s** or **°C/min**. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Alloying Elements by Their Effect on the Fe–Fe₃C System All alloying elements fall into **three categories** based on how they shift the **eutectoid temperature** and **γ-phase (austenite) field** in the Fe–C phase diagram: | Category | Effect on γ-field | Effect on Eutectoid | Examples | Mechanism | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Austenite Stabilizers** | **Expand γ-field** | Lower eutectoid temperature, lower eutectoid carbon content | **Ni, Mn, Co, Cu, N** | Lower the A₁ and A₃ temperatures | | **Ferrite Stabilizers** | **Contract γ-field** | Raise eutectoid temperature, raise eutectoid carbon content | **Cr, Si, Mo, W, V, Al, Ti, Nb, Zr** | Raise the A₁ and A₃ temperatures; some form stable carbides | | **Carbide Formers** | Form stable, hard carbides | Replace Fe in cementite (Fe₃C) | **Cr, Mo, W, V, Ti, Nb, Zr, Mn** | Strong thermodynamic affinity for carbon | | **Non-carbide formers** | Stay dissolved in ferrite | Dissolve in α-Fe | **Ni, Si, Co, Al, Cu, N** | Low carbide-forming tendency | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Carbide Strength Hierarchy (decreasing stability):** > $$\text{TiC} > \text{NbC} > \text{ZrC} > \text{VC} > \text{WC} > \text{Mo}_2\text{C} > \text{Cr}_{23}\text{C}_6 > \text{Cr}_7\text{C}_3 > \text{Fe}_3\text{C} > \text{Mn}_3\text{C}$$ > Stable carbides (left) resist dissolution even at high tempering temperatures → **secondary hardening** & **hot hardness**. ## 2.2 Effect of Individual Alloying Elements – The KTU Master List ### 2.2.1 Manganese (Mn) – **The Workhorse** - **Typical addition:** 0.25 – 14 wt% - **Primary roles:** 1. **Deoxidizer & desulfurizer** – forms MnS, prevents hot shortness caused by FeS. 2. **Solid-solution strengthener** of ferrite (∼ 5–10 MPa per 1 wt% Mn). 3. **Austenite stabilizer** – lowers A₁, A₃ temperatures. 4. **Enhances hardenability** – shifts TTT/CCT curves to the right. 5. **High-Mn austenitic steel (Hadfield steel, 12–14 % Mn)** – work-hardens to > 1000 BHN under impact (used in crusher jaws, railway crossings). ### 2.2.2 Silicon (Si) - **Typical addition:** 0.2 – 4 wt% (in electrical steels up to 3.5 %) - **Primary roles:** 1. **Strong deoxidizer** (kills dissolved O₂ as SiO₂). 2. **Solid-solution strengthener** of ferrite. 3. **Ferrite stabilizer** – raises A₃ temperature. 4. **Increases electrical resistivity** → used in **transformer/electrical steel** (Si 3–4 %) to reduce eddy-current losses. 5. **Promotes graphitization** in cast iron / high-Si steel. ### 2.2.3 Nickel (Ni) - **Typical addition:** 0.3 – 36 wt% - **Primary roles:** 1. **Austenite stabilizer** – the most powerful γ-stabilizer. 2. **Improves toughness**, especially **impact strength at sub-zero temperatures**
