(cryogenic steels: 9 % Ni for LNG tanks at –196 °C). 3. **Solid-solution strengthener** of ferrite. 4. **Combined with Cr** to form the classic austenitic stainless steel (18-8: 18 % Cr + 8 % Ni → AISI 304). 5. **Does not form carbides** – remains in matrix → preserves corrosion resistance. ### 2.2.4 Chromium (Cr) – **The Stainless Champion** - **Typical addition:** 0.3 – 30 wt% - **Primary roles:** 1. **Ferrite stabilizer** – raises A₃. 2. **Carbide former** – forms (Cr, Fe)₇C₃, (Cr, Fe)₂₃C₆. 3. **Corrosion resistance** – requires **minimum 10.5 wt% Cr** to form stable, self-healing **Cr₂O₃ passive layer** (stainless steel threshold). 4. **Improves wear resistance** (high-Cr tool steels, e.g., D2: 12 % Cr). 5. **Enhances high-temperature oxidation resistance** (heat-resistant steels: 25–30 % Cr). ### 2.2.5 Molybdenum (Mo) - **Typical addition:** 0.1 – 5 wt% - **Primary roles:** 1. **Strong carbide former** (Mo₂C, Mo₆C) → secondary hardening, hot hardness. 2. **Powerful hardenability booster** – most effective per wt% after Mn. 3. **Reduces temper brittleness** in Ni–Cr steels. 4. **Improves creep resistance** – used in **Cr-Mo steels** for power-plant boilers (e.g., T22, T91). 5. **Resists pitting corrosion** in stainless steels (316 has 2–3 % Mo). ### 2.2.6 Vanadium (V) - **Typical addition:** 0.05 – 5 wt% - **Primary roles:** 1. **Strongest carbide/nitride former** (VC, VN). 2. **Grain refinement** – V(C,N) particles pin austenite grain boundaries → fine grain → higher toughness (ASTM A36 with V). 3. **Secondary hardening** at tempering temperatures of 500–600 °C. 4. **Wear resistance** in high-speed tool steels (M2: 2 % V). ### 2.2.7 Tungsten (W) - **Typical addition:** 1 – 18 wt% - **Primary roles:** 1. **Forms very hard WC, W₂C** → exceptional hot hardness. 2. **Stabilizes M₆C and M₂₃C₆ carbides** in high-speed steels (T1: 18 % W). 3. **Secondary hardening** at 550–600 °C. 4. **Heavy element** – W 1 wt% ≈ Ni 2 wt% in hardenability effect. ### 2.2.8 Cobalt (Co) - **Typical addition:** 0.5 – 12 wt% - **Primary roles:** 1. **Ferrite stabilizer** at high T; raises A₃. 2. **Promotes red hardness** in HSS (M35, M42). 3. **Does not form carbides** – remains in matrix. 4. **Used in maraging steels** (18 Ni-300 contains 9 % Co for precipitation strengthening). 5. **Reduces stacking-fault energy** → enhances work-hardening rate. ### 2.2.9 Copper (Cu) - **Typical addition:** 0.1 – 0.5 wt% (in HSLA & weathering steels) - **Primary roles:** 1. **Solid-solution strengthener** of ferrite. 2. **Weathering resistance** (Cor-Ten steel: 0.25–0.55 % Cu + Cr, Ni, P → forms protective rust patina). 3. **Causes hot shortness** if not balanced with Ni (Ni ≥ ½ Cu). ### 2.2.10 Aluminum (Al), Titanium (Ti), Niobium (Nb), Boron (B) - **Al (0.02–1 %):** Strong deoxidizer; forms AlN → grain refiner. - **Ti (0.01–0.5 %):** Strong carbide former; stabilizes against intergranular corrosion (321 stainless). - **Nb (0.01–0.1 %):** NbC precipitation strengthening (HSLA-Nb steels). - **B (0.001–0.003 %):** **Powerful, cheap hardenability booster** – B 0.001 % ≈ 0.5 % Cr in hardenability effect, but only effective if Ti/Al also added to "protect" B from N. ## 2.3 Effect on the Iron–Carbon Phase Diagram – Schematic ``` Temperature (°C) | 1538 |---Pure Fe Melting------------ | 1147 |---Eutectic (4.3 % C)---------- ← Alloying shifts this LEFT or RIGHT | • 912 |---A₃ (Pure Fe)------------------ ← Cr, Si, Mo, W → shift UP | / \ ← Ni, Mn, Co → shift DOWN 727 |---Eutectoid (0.77 % C)---------- ← Alloying element X shifts this | • to different T and C % |___________________________________ Carbon (wt%) ``` - **γ-stabilizers (Ni, Mn)** **expand** the austenite region → lower A₃, lower eutectoid C %. - **α-stabilizers (Cr, Si, Mo)** **contract** the austenite region → higher A₃, higher eutectoid C %. ## 2.4 Effect on the TTT / CCT Diagram – The Hardenability Story > [!IMPORTANT] > **Alloying elements shift the TTT/CCT nose to the RIGHT** → **decrease the critical cooling rate** ($V_c$) → **deeper hardening** for the same quench severity. **Conceptual shift:** ``` Temperature | A₁ |___________________ | \ ● (Alloyed: shifted right) | \ ● (Plain C: original) | ● | / | / |____________________________ 0 10 100 1000 log(time) ↑ Longer time → slower quench OK ``` **Mathematical effect (multiplicative hardenability factor, from Grossmann & Bain):** $$\boxed{H = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \cdot H_i}$$ Where $H$ = **ideal critical diameter** (D_I) in inches, $f_i$ = multiplying factor of alloying element $i$, $H_i$ = hardenability contribution of element $i$ (tabulated, function of carbon content and grain size). ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Use `\vert` instead of `|` inside table cells to avoid markdown table breakage.** | # | Formula / Rule | Description / Units | Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $D_I = D_{I,\text{base}} \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} f_i$ | **Ideal critical diameter** ($D_I$) in **inches**; $f_i$ = Grossmann multiplying factor | Grossmann hardenability calculation | | 2 | $\text{Cr}_{eq} = \%\text{Cr} + \%\text{Mo} + 1.5 \times \%\text{Si} + 0.5 \times \%\text{Nb} + 2 \times equivalent** (wt%) – austenite-promoting power | Schaeffler diagram (stainless weld metal) | | 4 | $\text{IE} = 1.5 \cdot \%\text{Si} + 0.5 \cdot \%\text{Mn} + \%\text{Cr} + 3 \cdot \%\text{V} + 5 - 33 \cdot \%\text{Mn} - 17 \cdot \%\text{Ni} - 17 \cdot \%\text{Cr} - 21 \cdot \%\text{Mo} + 10 \cdot \%\text{Co} - 7.5 \cdot \%\text{Si} - 0 \cdot \%\text{W}$ | **Martensite start temperature** ($M_s$) in **°C** | Andrews / Kung-Kajiwara empirical equation | | 6 | $V_c = \dfrac{\Delta T_{nose}}{t_{nose}}$ | **Critical cooling rate** (°C/s) – plain C steel: ≈ 600 °C/s in water | Hardenability \Delta C, \Delta N_{\text{ss}}, \text{grain size} \downarrow$ | **Hall-Petch strengthening** (qualitative) | Alloy design for strength | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Sector | Steel Grade | Key Alloying Elements | Why It Matters | |---|---|---|---| | **Automotive chassis** | HSLA 340/590 | Nb, V, Ti (microalloyed) | High strength + good formability + weldability | | **Aerospace landing gear** | AISI 4340 | Ni, Cr, Mo | Ultra-high strength + fracture toughness | | **Nuclear reactor pressure vessel** | SA-508 Gr.3 Cl.2 | Ni, Cr, Mo, Mn | Irradiation resistance + low-temp toughness | | **LNG cryogenic tank** | 9 % Ni steel | 9 % Ni | Toughness at –196 °C | | **Cutting tools** | M2 HSS | W, Mo, V, Cr | Red hardness (cut at 600 °C) | | **Surgical implants** | AISI 316L | 18 Cr, 14 Ni, 3 Mo, low C | Biocompatibility + pitting resistance | | **Railway crossing** | Hadfield steel (X120Mn12) | 12 % Mn | Work-hardening under impact | | **Power plant boiler tubes** | T91 (9Cr-1Mo-V-Nb) | Cr, Mo, V, Nb, N | Creep resistance at 600 °C | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Effect of Alloying on Martensite Start Temperature ($M_s$) We use the **Andrews (1965) empirical linear regression** (one of the most widely used in KTU metallurgy courses). ### Step 1 – Empirical Starting Point Experiments on a wide range of alloyed steels showed that $M_s$ is **additively** affected by each alloying element, weighted by its concentration. The general linear-additive model is: $$M_s = M_s^0 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} k_i \cdot w_i$$ Where: - $M_s^0$ = $M_s$ of plain Fe–C steel (depends on %C only) - $k_i$ = weighting coefficient for element $i$ (°C per wt%) - $w_i$ = concentration of element $i$ (wt%) ### Step 2 – The $M_s^0$ Base Value From **pure binary Fe–C data**, Andrews fitted: $$M_s^0 (^\circ\text{C}) = 561 - 474 \cdot w_C$$ where $w_C$ is the carbon content in wt%. ### Step 3 – Collecting the Coefficients (Andrews / Kung-Kajiwara set) | Element (i) | Coefficient $k_i$ (°C per wt%) | Sign | |---|---|---| | C | –474 | ↓ strongly lowers $M_s$ | | Mn | –33 | ↓ | | Ni | –17 | ↓ | | Cr | –17 | ↓ | | Mo | –21 | ↓ | | Si | –7.5 | ↓ mild | | Co | +10 | ↑ raises $M_s$ | | W | 0 | ≈ no effect | | V | small negative | ↓ | | Al | small positive | ↑ | ### Step 4 – Full Andrew's Equation $$\boxed{M_s (^\circ\text{C}) = 561 - 474 \cdot \%\text{C} - 33 \cdot \%\text{Mn} - 17 \cdot \%\text{Ni} - 17 \cdot \%\text{Cr} - 21 \cdot \%\text{Mo} + 10 \cdot \%\text{Co} - 7.5 \cdot \%\text{Si}}$$ ### Step 5 – Worked Example (KTU Typical Problem) **Problem:** A steel has composition: **0.4 % C, 0.8 % Mn, 1.8 % Ni, 0.8 % Cr, 0.25 % Mo**. Estimate the $M_s$ temperature. **Solution – each substitution:** $$\begin{aligned} M_s &= 561 - 474(0.4) - 33(0.8) - 17(1.8) - 17(0.8) - 21(0.25) + 10(0) - 7.5(0) \\ M_s &= 561 - 189.6 - 26.4 - 30.6 - 13.6 - 5.25 + 0 - 0 \\ M_s &= 561 - 265.45 \\ M_s &= 295.55 \;^\circ\text{C} \end{aligned}$$ > **Result: $M_s \approx 296$ °C.** Compare to plain 0.4 % C steel: $M_s^0 = 561 - 474(0.4) = 371.4$ °C. The **75 °C depression** confirms the strong austenite-stabilizing effect of Ni + Cr + Mo. ### Step 6 – Physical Interpretation - **More retained austenite** in the as-quenched structure if $M_s$ falls below room temperature. - For AISI 4340 (0.4 % C, 0.8 % Mn, 1.8 % Ni, 0.8 % Cr, 0.25 % Mo, 0.2 % Si), $M_s$ ≈ 300 °C → mostly martensite, with small retained γ. - For AISI 304 stainless (0.08 % C, 2 % Mn, 8 % Ni, 18 % Cr), $M_s$ falls **below 0 °C** → **fully austenitic at room temperature** (no martensite formed on quenching). ## 3.2 Derivation: Grossmann Hardenability Multiplier Approach ### Step 1 – Definition of Ideal Critical Diameter ($D_I$) $D_I$ is the largest bar diameter that will form **50 % martensite at the centre** when quenched in an **ideal quench** (infinite quench severity, $H = \infty$). For a **plain carbon steel** with eutectoid composition, $D_I$ is only a few mm. Alloying raises $D_I$ to many inches. ### Step 2 – Multiplicative Hardenability Equation $$\boxed{D_I = D_{I,\text{base}} \cdot f_{\text{Mn}} \cdot f_{\text{Si}} \cdot f_{\text{Cr}} \cdot f_{\text{Ni}} \cdot f_{\text{Mo}} \cdot f_V \cdot f_{\text{Cu}} \cdot f_{\text{P}} \cdot f_{\text{S}} \cdot f_{\text{grain}}}$$ The base $D_{I,\text{base}}$ depends only on **carbon content** and is read from the standard Grossmann chart. ### Step 3 – Sample Calculation **Steel:** 0.4 % C, 1.0 % Mn, 0.5 % Cr, 0.4 % Ni, 0.2 % Mo, ASTM grain size 7. From Grossmann charts (standard tables): - $D_{I,\text{base}}$ (0.4 % C) = 0.16 in - $f_{\text{Mn}}$ = 2.95 - $f_{\text{Si}}$ = 1.18 - $f_{\text{Cr}}$ = 1.50 - $f_{\text{Ni}}$ = 1.45 - $f_{\text{Mo}}$ = 1.40 - $f_{\text{grain size 7}}$ = 1.20 $$\begin{aligned} D_I &= 0.16 \times 2.95 \times 1.18 \times 1.50 \times 1.45 \times 1.40 \times 1.20 \\ &= 0.16 \times 14.31 \\ &= 2.29 \; \text{in} \;\;(\approx 58 \; \text{mm}) \end{aligned}$$ > **Result:** The alloyed steel will harden through (≥ 50 % M) for a 58 mm bar under an **ideal quench**. A plain 0.4 % C steel would only achieve the same for a 4 mm bar — a **14× increase in hardenability**. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Summary of Effects | Alloying Element | Austenite vs. Ferrite | Carbide? | Hardenability (per wt%) | Corrosion Resistance | Hot Strength | Special Role | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Mn** | γ-stabilizer | (Mn₃C weak) | **High** | – | – | De-S, work-hardening | | **Si** | α-stabilizer | No | Low | – | – | De-O, electrical steel | | **Ni** | γ-stabilizer (strong) | No | Medium | Improves | – | Low-T toughness, cryogenic | | **Cr** | α-stabilizer | **Yes (strong)** | Medium | **Stainless (>10.5%)** | Improves | Passivation | | **Mo** | α-stabilizer | **Yes (strong)** | **Very high** | Pitting resistance | **Creep** | Reduces temper embrittlement | | **V** | α-stabilizer | **Yes (strongest)** | High | – | **Secondary hardening** | Grain refining | | **W** | α-stabilizer | **Yes (very strong)** | High (heavy) | – | **Red hardness** | HSS | | **Co** | α-stabilizer (high T) | No | Low | – | **Red hardness** | Maraging | | **Cu** | weak γ | No | Low | Weathering | – | Patina formation | | **Ti** | α-stabilizer | **Yes (strongest)** | Low | Stabilizes 321 | – | Grain refinement, fixation of C | | **Nb** | α-stabilizer | **Yes (very strong)** | Low | Stabilizes 347 | Creep | Precipitation strengthening | | **B** | – | No | **Very high (cheap)** | – | – | Need Ti protection | | **Al** | α-stabilizer | (AlN) | Low | – | – | De-O, grain refiner | | **N** | γ-stabilizer | (V, Al, Ti nitrides) | Medium | Pitting resistance | – | Used in duplex SS | --- # 4. 4.4 Schematic – Shift of TTT Diagram on Alloying ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Quench from Austenitising Temp above Acm]:::s Start --> P1[Plain Carbon Steel TTT Diagram]:::curve Start --> P2[Alloyed Steel Same C but with Ni Cr Mo TTT Diagram]:::curve P1 --> R1[Cooling Curve Must Avoid Nose Fast Quench Water]:::res P1 --> R2[Forms Martensite Only in Thin Sections]:::res P2 --> R3[Cooling Curve Slower Quench OK Oil or Even Air]:::res P2 --> R4[Forms Martensite in Thick Sections Hardenability]:::res classDef s fill:#42A5F5,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px classDef curve fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef res fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The "nose" of the TTT diagram moves to longer times when alloying elements are added → a slower cooling rate can still avoid the pearlite/bainite regions → **martensite forms even in large cross-sections**. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A – Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper – 3 Marks]** **"Why is Hadfield steel (12 % Mn) austenitic, while a 0.4 % C plain carbon steel is not? Explain the role of Mn in determining the stable phase."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Austenite stabiliser:** Manganese is a strong **γ-stabiliser** – it lowers the A₃ and A₁ temperatures and **expands the γ-phase field** of the Fe–C phase diagram. With 12 % Mn, the γ-phase is **stable down to (and below) room temperature**, suppressing the γ → α + Fe₃C eutectoid transformation. (1 mark) 2. **High carbon for strengthening:** The 1.0–1.2 % C in solution within γ provides strength; under impact loading the **ε-martensite** or mechanical twinning + carbide precipitation causes **extreme work-hardening** (→ 500 BHN from 200 BHN). (1 mark) 3. **Contrast with plain 0.4 % C steel:** It has no γ-stabiliser, so on slow cooling it transforms to **ferrite + pearlite**. Hadfield's Mn shifts the M_s below 0 °C, retaining **100 % metastable austenite** at ambient temperature. (1 mark) --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3 – 3 Marks]** **"Explain the term 'hardenability'. Why is alloy steel preferred over plain carbon steel for components requiring through-thickness hardness (e.g., large gears)?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Definition of Hardenability:** It is the ability of a steel to form **martensite at a given depth** when quenched, distinct from "hardness" (which is the final value). Measured by the **Jominy End-Quench Test**, expressed as a **Jominy curve** of hardness vs. distance. (1 mark) 2. **Plain carbon steel limitation:** The TTT "nose" of plain C steel is at very short times (~ 1 s). Only **thin sections** (a few mm) can be cooled fast enough (water quench) to avoid the pearlite nose. Large gears would be pearlitic in the centre → soft. (1 mark) 3. **Alloy steel advantage:** Elements like **Ni, Cr, Mo, Mn, B shift the nose to the right** (longer times), so a slower oil-quench still bypasses the nose in **large sections**, producing **martensite throughout** the gear thickness. (1 mark) --- ## 5.2 PART B – Essay Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Pitfall Callout:** > - ❌ Do **not** memorise element effects as a "list" without explaining the **underlying mechanism** (solid solution, carbide formation, γ-field change). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for rote answers. > - ❌ Confusing **"hardness"** (final value) with **"hardenability"** (depth of hardening) is the most common error. KTU papers contain trick questions using this. > - ❌ In $M_s$ / $D_I$ numerical problems, **forget to include all relevant elements** (especially Si) – marks are awarded per correct substitution. > - ❌ Avoid vague statements like "Cr makes steel hard" – specify **"Cr forms hard carbides (Cr₇C₃, Cr₂₃C₆) and a passive Cr₂O₃ film for corrosion resistance"**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3 Pattern]** **(a)** Discuss in detail the **classification of alloy steels** based on total alloying content. With the help of a suitable table, describe the **composition, microstructure, and at least three typical applications** of any **two** of the following: - (i) **AISI 4340** steel - (ii) **AISI 304** stainless steel - (iii) **M2 high-speed steel** - (iv) **Hadfield manganese steel (X120Mn12)** **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain, with **mechanistic reasoning**, how the following alloying elements individually modify the properties of steel: - (i) **Chromium (Cr)** – minimum 10.5 % rule and passive film formation. - (ii) **Nickel (Ni)** – austenite stabilisation and cryogenic toughness. - (iii) **Molybdenum (Mo)** – secondary hardening and creep resistance. - (iv) **Vanadium (V)** – grain refinement via V(C,N) and its role in HSLA steels. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question A** ##### **Solution to A(a):** (7 marks) **Classification (2 marks):** | Class | Total Alloying | Examples | |---|---|---| | Low-alloy steel | < 8 wt% | 4340, 8620, HSLA, 41xx, 86xx | | High-alloy steel | > 8 wt% | 304, 316, M2, D2, Hadfield | **(i) AISI 4340:** **(2 marks)** - **Composition:** 0.38–0.43 % C, 0.6–0.9 % Mn, 0.7–0.9 % Cr, 1.65–2.0 % Ni, 0.2–0.3 % Mo. - **Microstructure (after Q&T):** Tempered martensite + dispersed alloy carbides (M₂₃C₆, Mo₂C). - **Applications:** - Aircraft **landing gear**. - **Crankshafts** and **connecting rods** (heavy forgings). - High-strength **bolts**, splined shafts, gears. **(iii) M2 High-Speed Steel:** **(2 marks)** - **Composition:** 0.78–0.88 % C, 4 % Cr, 5 % Mo, 6 % W, 2 % V. - **Microstructure:** Hard tempered martensite with **primary M₆C carbides** and **secondary VC / M₂C** providing red hardness. - **Applications:** - **Drill bits, milling cutters, taps** operating at cutting temperatures up to 600 °C. - High-speed **broaches and form tools**. **(Or alternatively, present AISI 304 or Hadfield with similar 2-mark depth; student chooses any two.)** **Closing note (1 mark):** The classification helps metallurgists select steels by application – low-alloy for structural + moderate temperature, high-alloy for corrosion / wear / red-hardness / cryogenic service. --- ##### **Solution to A(b):** (7 marks) **(i) Chromium (Cr) – Passive film (1.75 marks):** - Cr has a strong affinity for oxygen. At Cr ≥ 10.5 wt%, a **continuous, self-healing Cr₂O₃ film** forms on the surface. - This passive layer is **insoluble, adherent, and 1–3 nm thick**, blocking O₂ and Cl⁻ from attacking the Fe matrix. - Cr is a **ferrite stabiliser** (raises A₃) and a **strong carbide former** – gives wear resistance in tool steels. - **Result:** *Stainlessness* in 304, pitting resistance when Mo is added (316). **(ii) Nickel (Ni) – Austenite stabiliser (1.75 marks):** - Ni lowers A₃ and A₁, expanding the γ-field. With 8 % Ni, the γ-phase is stable at room temperature → **austenitic stainless steel**. - Ni does not form carbides; remains in solid solution → **superior low-temperature toughness** (FCC structure, no ductile-to-brittle transition). - **9 % Ni steel** is used for **LNG storage tanks at –196 °C**; the Charpy impact energy remains above 27 J even at cryogenic temperatures. - Mechanism: Ni solute **raises the slip planarity** and **reduces the DBTT** of BCC ferrite. **(iii) Molybdenum (Mo) – Secondary hardening & creep (1.75 marks):** - During **tempering at 500–600 °C**, fine Mo₂C carbides precipitate coherently within martensite, **blocking dislocation motion** → **secondary hardening peak** (hardness often exceeds as-quenched value). - Mo is a **powerful hardenability booster** (high $f_{Mo}$ in Grossmann tables) – allows thick sections to be hardened in oil. - Mo is **slow to diffuse**, so its carbides **resist coarsening** at high T → **creep resistance** in 9Cr-1Mo (T91) and 2.25Cr-1Mo (T22) steels used in power plants. **(iv) Vanadium (V) – Grain refinement (1.75 marks):** - V forms **very stable V(C,N)** particles that remain undissolved even at 1100 °C. - These particles **pin austenite grain boundaries** during heating, **preventing grain growth**. - **Result: fine-grain steel** with simultaneously **higher yield strength** (Hall–Petch) and **higher toughness** (fine grain raises DBTT up). - In **HSLA-Nb-V-Ti steels**, V(C,N) provides **precipitation strengthening** of ferrite during cooling from hot-rolling. - In **M2 HSS**, VC is the hardest and most thermally stable carbide → **red hardness**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3 Pattern]** **(a)** With a **neat schematic of the TTT diagram**, explain how alloying elements improve **hardenability**. Describe the **Grossmann multiplying-factor method** for computing the **ideal critical diameter ($D_I$)** of a steel of composition **0.4 % C, 1.2 % Mn, 0.5 % Si, 0.8 % Cr, 0.3 % Mo, 0.4 % Ni**, ASTM grain size 8. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using the **Andrews empirical equation**, compute the **Martensite Start temperature ($M_s$)** of a steel containing **0.45 % C, 1.5 % Mn, 1.8 % Ni, 0.9 % Cr, 0.3 % Mo, 0.3 % Si**. State the **significance of $M_s$ depression** in alloy steels. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question B** ##### **Solution to B(a):** (7 marks) **Schematic + explanation (3 marks):** $$\begin{aligned} &\text{Alloying elements (Mn, Cr, Mo, Ni, B, V, …)}\\ &\downarrow\\ &\text{TTT "nose" shifts from } t \approx 0.5\text{–}1\text{ s (plain C)}\\ &\text{to } t \approx 10\text{–}100\text{ s (alloyed)}\\ &\downarrow\\ &\text{Critical cooling rate } V_c \text{ drops from } \sim 600 \text{ °C/s to } \sim 20 \text{ °C/s}\\ &\downarrow\\ &\text{Slower quench (oil / air) can still produce martensite at greater depths} \end{aligned}$$ **Grossmann's $D_I$ calculation (4 marks):** **Step 1 – Identify base value (1 mark):** For 0.40 % C, ASTM grain size 8: from Grossmann chart, $D_{I,\text{base}} \approx 0.18$ in. **Step 2 – Look up multiplying factors from the standard Grossmann table (1.5 marks):** | Element | Content (wt%) | $f_i$ | |---|---|---| | Mn | 1.2 | 3.50 | | Si | 0.5 | 1.18 | | Cr | 0.8 | 1.56 | | Mo | 0.3 | 1.32 | | Ni | 0.4 | 1.18 | | Grain size 8 | – | 1.30 | **Step 3 – Compute (1.5 marks):** $$\begin{aligned} D_I &= D_{I,\text{base}} \times f_{\text{Mn}} \times f_{\text{Si}} \times f_{\text{Cr}} \times f_{\text{Mo}} \times f_{\text{Ni}} \times f_{\text{grain}} \\ &= 0.18 \times 3.50 \times 1.18 \times 1.56 \times 1.32 \times 1.18 \times 1.30 \\ &= 0.18 \times 14.92 \\ &= 2.69 \; \text{inches} \;\;(\approx 68 \; \text{mm}) \end{aligned}$$ > **Result:** Under an ideal quench, this steel will form ≥ 50 % martensite in bars up to **68 mm diameter**. In a real oil quench ($H \approx 0.4$), the effective $D$ is roughly half. --- ##### **Solution to B(b):** (7 marks) **Step 1 – Write the Andrews equation (1 mark):** $$M_s (^\circ\text{C}) = 561 - 474 \cdot \%\text{C} - 33 \cdot \%\text{Mn} - 17 \cdot \%\text{Ni} - 17 \cdot \%\text{Cr} - 21 \cdot \%\text{Mo} + 10 \cdot \%\text{Co} - 7.5 \cdot \%\text{Si}$$ **Step 2 – Substitute values (2 marks):** $$\begin{aligned} M_s &= 561 - 474(0.45) - 33(1.5) - 17(1.8) - 17(0.9) - 21(0.3) - 7.5(0.3) \\ M_s &= 561 - 213.30 - 49.50 - 30.60 - 15.30 - 6.30 - 2.25 \\ M_s &= 561 - 317.25 \\ M_s &= 243.75 \;^\circ\text{C} \;\;(\approx 244 \;^\circ\text{C}) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 – Significance of $M_s$ depression (4 marks):** 1. **Retained Austenite:** As $M_s$ falls toward (or below) room temperature, **austenite that has not transformed to martensite at room T becomes "retained γ"**. This softens the as-quenched hardness and causes **dimensional instability** during service. **(1 mark)** 2. **Sub-zero treatment (cryogenic treatment):** For steels with $M_f$ (martensite finish) below 0 °C, **submerging in liquid N₂ (–196 °C)** completes the γ → M transformation → boosts hardness by 1–2 HRC and stabilises dimensions. **(1 mark)** 3. **Distortion & cracking risk:** Lower $M_s$ means the **martensitic transformation occurs over a wider temperature range** during cooling, increasing **transformation-induced stresses** → higher risk of quench cracking. **(1 mark)** 4. **Design implication:** For the present steel ($M_s \approx 244$ °C), $M_f$ would be ≈ 100 °C, so **retained γ ~ 2–4 %** is expected. To minimise this, perform **tempering immediately** after quench. **(1 mark)** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember – Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield KTU 2024 Revision Points (Module 3 – Alloy Steels):** - ☐ **Alloy steel = Fe + C + intentional alloying element(s)** (residual vs. deliberate cut-off: Mn > 1.65 %, Si > 0.5 %, Cu > 0.4 %). - ☐ **Low-alloy** (< 8 %) vs. **High-alloy** (> 8 %). - ☐ **γ-stabilisers** (Ni, Mn, Co, Cu, N) **expand** the austenite field; **α-stabilisers** (Cr, Si, Mo, W, V, Al) **contract** it. - ☐ **Carbide hierarchy (most stable first):** TiC > NbC > VC > WC > Mo₂C > Cr carbides > Fe₃C > Mn₃C. - ☐ **Hardenability ≠ Hardness.** Hardenability is a **depth** property; hardness is a **value**. - ☐ Alloying elements **shift TTT nose to the right** → **reduce $V_c$** → **increase hardenability**. - ☐ **Mn, Cr, Mo, B** are powerful hardenability boosters (in that approximate per-wt% order; B is the cheapest per unit effect). - ☐ **Cr ≥ 10.5 %** is the **stainless steel threshold** for passive Cr₂O₃ film. - ☐ **Mo + Cr** combinations dominate **creep-resistant** steels for power plants. - ☐ **V(C,N)** = grain refiner + precipitation strengthener → central to HSLA and HSS. - ☐ **18-8 (304 SS):** 18 % Cr + 8 % Ni → **austenitic**, non-magnetic, excellent corrosion resistance, no carbide hardening. - ☐ **Hadfield steel (12 % Mn):** γ-stabilised at RT → work-hardens to 500 BHN under impact. - ☐ **M2 HSS** = W-Mo-Cr-V = **red hardness**; can cut at 600 °C. - ☐ **9 % Ni steel** = cryogenic toughness (–196 °C LNG tanks). - ☐ **Boron (0.001–0.003 %)** = powerful cheap hardenability element; **must be protected from N by Ti or Al**. - ☐ **Andrews' equation** for $M_s$ is the KTU exam favourite for numericals. - ☐ **Grossmann's $D_I$** multiplicative model is the standard hardenability calculation. - ☐ **Schaeffler diagram** uses **Cr_eq** and **Ni_eq** to predict weld-metal microstructure in stainless steel. - ☐ **Temper embrittlement** in Ni–Cr steels is mitigated by adding **Mo (≥ 0.2 %)**. - ☐ **Secondary hardening** in Mo / V / W steels is due to **fine alloy-carbide precipitation** at 500–600 °C tempering. - ☐ **Excessive alloying** can harm weldability, machinability, and cost – always justify the **minimum effective addition**. ---

topics.title: Nickel Steels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT526 - METALLURGY AND MATERIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Nickel Steels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Nickel Steels** are a class of **ferrous alloys** in which **nickel (Ni)** is added as the principal alloying element to iron-carbon base metal, typically in concentrations ranging from **0.5% to 36% by weight**, to enhance mechanical strength, toughness, impact resistance, and corrosion resistance. Nickel acts as a **strong austenite stabilizer** and a **solid-solution strengthener** of ferrite, thereby shifting the eutectoid composition and depressing the critical temperature (A₃ and A₁) lines on the iron-carbon phase diagram.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> Nickel steels are categorized under the broader family of **low-alloy and high-alloy steels** depending on the nickel concentration. The 2024 Scheme specifically emphasizes **3.5% Ni steel, 5% Ni steel, 9% Ni steel, 18% Ni maraging steel, 25% Ni steel, and 36% Ni Invar steel**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **plain carbon steel** as a basic wooden plank — strong but brittle and prone to snapping under sudden impact. Now, imagine you reinforce that plank by embedding **flexible metal wires (nickel atoms)** throughout its grain structure. The plank can now bend, absorb shocks, and resist cracking.

In metallurgical terms, nickel atoms dissolve into the **ferrite (BCC) lattice** and create localized strain fields that impede dislocation motion. This is analogous to placing speed bumps on a highway — vehicles (dislocations) cannot move as freely, so the material becomes stronger and tougher.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Definition for First-Time Learners:**
> "Nickel steel is regular steel to which we add nickel — a silvery-white metal — to make it stronger, tougher, more resistant to impact, and better at handling extreme cold (cryogenic) or corrosive environments."

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| **Atomic Number of Ni** | **28** |
| **Atomic Radius of Ni** | **0.124 nm** |
| **Crystal Structure of Ni (pure)** | **FCC (A1)** |
| **Density of Ni** | **8.908 g/cm³** |
| **Melting Point of Ni** | **1455 °C** |
| **Ni Solubility in α-Fe (Room Temp)** | **~10 wt%** |
| **Young's Modulus of Ni** | **~200 GPa** |
| **Curie Temperature of Ni** | **354 °C** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Nickel Matters in Steel:**
> Nickel has an **atomic radius (0.124 nm) very close to that of iron (0.124 nm)** and the same **FCC-compatible solubility behavior**, which means it dissolves readily into both ferrite and austenite without forming brittle intermetallic phases.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Nickel Strengthening (The "Why" Behind the Effect)

Nickel influences steel through **four primary mechanisms**, each explained below:

### 2.1.1 Solid-Solution Strengthening
- Nickel atoms substitute for iron atoms in the **BCC ferrite** and **FCC austenite** lattices.
- Because of the slight atomic size mismatch and modulus difference, **elastic strain fields** form around each Ni solute atom.
- These strain fields interact with moving dislocations, **raising the critical resolved shear stress (CRSS)**.
- **Result:** Increased yield strength and tensile strength.

### 2.1.2 Austenite Stabilization
- Nickel **lowers the A₃ (γ → α) transformation temperature** and **expands the austenite (γ) phase field**.
- In high-Ni steels (e.g., 18% Ni, 25% Ni), austenite can be **retained at room temperature**, producing tough, ductile structures.

### 2.1.3 Grain Refinement
- Nickel reduces the prior austenite grain size during cooling.
- **Hall-Petch relationship** is activated: finer grains → higher yield strength.

### 2.1.4 Toughening (Impact Resistance)
- Nickel lowers the **ductile-to-brittle transition temperature (DBTT)**.
- This is the **most commercially exploited benefit** of Ni in steels, especially for low-temperature service.

## 2.2 Effect of Nickel on the Fe–C Phase Diagram

The Fe–Fe₃C diagram is shifted in the following ways by nickel addition:

| Shift Direction | Effect on Eutectoid | Result |
|---|---|---|
| **A₃ line moves down** | Eutectoid temperature decreases | More austenite at given T |
| **A₁ line moves down** | Lower critical temperature | Easier heat treatment |
| **Eutectoid carbon content decreases** | Carbon enrichment in austenite | Improved hardenability |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Relationship | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Yield Strength (Hall-Petch)** | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$ | MPa | Grain-size effect of Ni |
| **DBTT Reduction by Ni** | $\Delta T \approx -20 \text{ °C per 1 wt\% Ni}$ | °C | Cryogenic design |
| **Solid-Solution Strength Increment** | $\Delta\sigma \approx k_s \cdot X_{Ni}^{2/3}$ | MPa | Strengthening estimate |
| **Thermal Expansion (Invar, 36% Ni)** | $\alpha \approx 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$ | /°C | Precision instruments |
| **Curie Temperature (Ni content dependence)** | $T_C \approx T_{C,Fe} - 8 \cdot X_{Ni}$ (wt%) | °C | Magnetic applications |
| **Density of Ni Steel (approx.)** | $\rho \approx 7.85 + 0.011 \cdot X_{Ni}$ | g/cm³ | Weight estimation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always express nickel concentration in **weight percent (wt%)** unless specifically asked in atomic percent (at%). Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values, not the pipe symbol.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Specific Use | Ni Steel Grade |
|---|---|---|
| **Cryogenic Storage** | LNG tanks, liquid nitrogen vessels | **9% Ni steel** |
| **Marine Engineering** | Submarine hulls, propeller shafts | **3.5% Ni steel** |
| **Pressure Vessels** | High-pressure boilers, reactors | **5% Ni steel** |
| **Aerospace / Tooling** | Landing gear, rocket motor casings | **18% Ni maraging steel** |
| **Precision Instruments** | Clocks, measuring standards, bimetallic strips | **36% Ni Invar** |
| **Electrical Steels** | Transformer laminations, magnetic cores | **45–50% Ni Permalloy** |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Critical Resolved Shear Stress Increase Due to Ni Solid Solution

### Step 1 — Start with the Fleischer Solid-Solution Strengthening Model

The increment in CRSS due to a solute atom is given by:

$$
\Delta \tau = A \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon_s^{3/2} \cdot c^{1/2}
$$

where:
- $A$ = dimensionless constant (typically 0.5–1.0)
- $G$ = shear modulus of the matrix (Fe = 81.6 GPa)
- $\varepsilon_s$ = interaction parameter
- $c$ = atomic concentration of solute

### Step 2 — Define the Interaction Parameter

$$
\varepsilon_s = \vert \varepsilon_G \vert + \beta \cdot \vert \varepsilon_b \vert
$$

For nickel in iron:
- $\varepsilon_G$ = modulus mismatch = $\frac{1}{G} \cdot \frac{dG}{dc}$
- $\varepsilon_b$ = atomic size mismatch = $\frac{1}{a} \cdot \frac{da}{dc}$
- $\beta$ ≈ 3 (for screw dislocations)

### Step 3 — Numerical Evaluation for 5 wt% Ni in Steel

Compute atomic concentration:
$$
c = \frac{X_{Ni}/M_{Ni}}{X_{Ni}/M_{Ni} + X_{Fe}/M_{Fe}}
$$

Plugging $X_{Ni} = 0.05$, $X_{Fe} = 0.95$, $M_{Ni} = 58.7$ g/mol, $M_{Fe} = 55.85$ g/mol:

$$
c = \frac{0.05 / 58.7}{0.05/58.7 + 0.95/55.85}
$$

$$
c = \frac{8.518 \times 10^{-4}}{8.518 \times 10^{-4} + 1.701 \times 10^{-2}}
$$

$$
c = \frac{8.518 \times 10^{-4}}{1.787 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 0.0477
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute into the Strengthening Equation

Using literature values $\varepsilon_s \approx 0.04$, $A \approx 0.7$:

$$
\Delta \tau = 0.7 \cdot (81.6 \times 10^9) \cdot (0.04)^{3/2} \cdot (0.0477)^{1/2}
$$

$$
\Delta \tau = 0.7 \cdot 81.6 \times 10^9 \cdot 0.008 \cdot 0.2184
$$

$$
\Delta \tau \approx 99.8 \text{ MPa}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Tensile Yield Strength Increment

$$
\Delta \sigma_y = M \cdot \Delta \tau
$$

where $M \approx 2.5$ (Taylor factor for BCC polycrystals):

$$
\Delta \sigma_y = 2.5 \cdot 99.8 \approx 249.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Adding just 5 wt% Ni can raise the yield strength of ferrite by approximately **250 MPa** purely through solid-solution strengthening — without any phase transformation or precipitation.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Ni Content vs. Property Changes

| Ni Content (wt%) | Microstructure (as quenched) | Key Property Change | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0.5 – 1.0%** | Ferrite + Pearlite | Mild strengthening | Structural plates |
| **3.0 – 3.5%** | Fine Pearlite + Ferrite | Improved low-T toughness | Marine/submarine |
| **5.0%** | Bainitic/Martensitic | Better hardenability | Pressure vessels |
| **9.0%** | Martensite (retained γ) | Cryogenic toughness (-196 °C) | LNG storage |
| **18.0% (maraging)** | Martensite + Ni₃Ti precipitates | Ultra-high strength (≥1800 MPa) | Aerospace |
| **25.0%** | Austenite | Non-magnetic, high ductility | Cryogenic piping |
| **36.0% (Invar)** | Austenite | Near-zero thermal expansion | Precision instruments |
| **45 – 50% (Permalloy)** | Austenite | High magnetic permeability | Transformer cores |

## 3.3 Derivation: DBTT Reduction per Weight Percent Ni

### Empirical Relation from KTU-Standard Data

For mild-to-medium carbon steels:

$$
T_{DBTT}(Ni) = T_{DBTT}(0) - 20 \cdot X_{Ni} \quad [\text{°C}]
$$

### Worked Example for 9% Ni Steel

Given plain carbon steel DBTT = +20 °C:

$$
T_{DBTT}(9\% Ni) = 20 - 20 \cdot 9 = -160 \text{ °C}
$$

This explains why **9% Ni steel is suitable for liquid nitrogen (-196 °C) service** — its DBTT is well below the operating temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The 9% Ni steel is qualified by ASME for cryogenic service down to **-196 °C**, making it the **industry standard for LNG carriers**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture: Classification of Nickel Steels

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Nickel Steels] --> B[Low Ni Steels 0.5 to 5 percent]
    A --> C[Medium Ni Steels 5 to 10 percent]
    A --> D[High Ni Steels above 10 percent]

    B --> B1[0.5 to 1 percent Structural]
    B --> B2[3.5 percent Marine Grade]
    B --> B3[5 percent Pressure Vessel]

    C --> C1[9 percent Cryogenic Grade]
    C --> C2[Invar 36 percent Precision]

    D --> D1[18 percent Maraging Steel]
    D --> D2[25 percent Austenitic Cryogenic]
    D --> D3[45 to 50 percent Permalloy Magnetic]

    B1 --> E1[Applications: Bridges and Rails]
    B2 --> E2[Applications: Submarine Hulls]
    B3 --> E3[Applications: Boiler Plates]
    C1 --> E4[Applications: LNG Storage Tanks]
    C2 --> E5[Applications: Clocks and Bimetallic Strips]
    D1 --> E6[Applications: Rocket Casings and Landing Gear]
    D2 --> E7[Applications: Cryogenic Piping]
    D3 --> E8[Applications: Transformer Cores]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow: Strengthening Mechanism of Nickel in Steel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Pure Iron Fe] --> G1[Add Ni atoms]
    G1 --> H1[Substitutional solid solution forms]
    H1 --> I1[Elastic strain field around Ni]
    I1 --> J1[Dislocation motion impeded]
    J1 --> K1[Increased CRSS and yield strength]
    H1 --> L1[Lowered A3 and A1 temperatures]
    L1 --> M1[Expanded austenite phase field]
    M1 --> N1[Improved hardenability and toughness]
    I1 --> O1[Finer grain size on cooling]
    O1 --> P1[Hall-Petch strengthening]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison: 9% Ni Steel vs. 18% Ni Maraging Steel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[9 percent Ni Steel] --> A2[Microstructure: Tempered Martensite with retained Austenite]
    A2 --> A3[Heat Treatment: Quench and Temper at 600 C]
    A3 --> A4[UTS: 700 to 900 MPa]
    A3 --> A5[Use: LNG tanks at minus 196 C]

    B1[18 percent Ni Maraging Steel] --> B2[Microstructure: Martensite plus Ni3Ti Ni3Mo precipitates]
    B2 --> B3[Heat Treatment: Solution Treat at 820 C plus Age at 480 C]
    B3 --> B4[UTS: 1800 to 2400 MPa]
    B3 --> B5[Use: Aerospace and tooling]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Description: Fe-C Phase Diagram Shift Due to Nickel

```
       Temperature (°C)
              |
        A     | γ region EXPANDS ↘
       /|\    |  ___________________
      / | \   | /                   \
     /  |  \  |/  Eutectoid shifts   \
    /   |   \  \  left (lower C)      \
   /    |    \  \____________________\
  /     |     \                       \
 /______|______\________________________\____ Carbon (wt%)
        E
```

- **Upper dashed curve (shifted):** A₃ line displaced downward
- **Lower dashed curve (shifted):** A₁ line displaced downward
- **Eutectoid point E:** Shifts to lower carbon content and lower temperature

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The austenite (γ) phase field grows when Ni is added. This is why high-Ni steels can retain austenite at room temperature when rapidly cooled.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Q: Define "Invar" and state its typical composition and the property that makes it commercially significant.**

**Model Answer:**
Invar is a **nickel–iron alloy containing 36% Ni and 64% Fe** (balance). Its commercial significance lies in its **extremely low coefficient of thermal expansion (α ≈ 1.2 × 10⁻⁶ /°C)** near room temperature, which makes it ideal for **precision instruments, clock pendulums, bimetallic strips, and standards of length** where dimensional stability against temperature fluctuations is critical.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Q: What is the effect of nickel on the ductile-to-brittle transition temperature (DBTT) of steel? Why is this important?**

**Model Answer:**
Nickel **lowers the DBTT** of steel. Empirically, each 1 wt% Ni reduces the DBTT by approximately **20 °C**. This is critically important for **cryogenic and low-temperature applications** such as storage tanks for liquefied natural gas (LNG at -162 °C) and liquid nitrogen (LN₂ at -196 °C), where ordinary carbon steels would fail by brittle fracture. Hence, **9% Ni steel** is the industry standard for cryogenic pressure vessels.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the role of nickel as an alloying element in steel. Discuss its effect on microstructure, strength, and toughness with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 Marks)** With a neat sketch, describe the **Fe–C phase diagram shift** caused by nickel addition. How does this shift influence the **heat treatment behavior** of nickel steels?

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Introduction:**
Nickel is one of the most versatile alloying elements in ferrous metallurgy. It is added in amounts ranging from **0.5% to 50%** depending on the desired property profile.

**Step 2 — Solid-Solution Strengthening:**
Nickel atoms substitute for iron in the BCC ferrite lattice. Because the atomic radius of Ni (0.124 nm) is comparable to Fe (0.124 nm) but slightly larger, **lattice distortion** occurs. This raises the yield strength by ~50 MPa per 1 wt% Ni.

**Step 3 — Austenite Stabilization:**
Ni is a strong **γ-loop expander**. It suppresses the γ → α transformation, allowing austenite to persist at lower temperatures. Example: **25% Ni steel retains austenite at room temperature**.

**Step 4 — Toughening:**
By lowering the DBTT (~20 °C per 1 wt% Ni), nickel makes the steel suitable for sub-zero service. **9% Ni steel** is the standard for LNG tanks.

**Step 5 — Hardenability Improvement:**
Nickel increases **hardenability** by delaying pearlite transformation, allowing deeper martensitic hardening in thick sections.

**Step 6 — Example Summary Table:**

| Ni % | Property Boosted | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 3.5 | Toughness | Marine |
| 9 | Cryogenic strength | LNG |
| 18 | Ultra-strength | Aerospace |
| 36 | Low expansion | Invar instruments |

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Phase Diagram Description:**
On adding Ni, the **A₃ (γ → α) line shifts downward** and the **A₁ (eutectoid) line also shifts downward**. The **eutectoid composition shifts to lower carbon content**.

**Step 2 — Drawing Conventions:**
The Fe–C diagram is redrawn with:
- Original A₃ line as solid curve
- New (Ni-shifted) A₃ line as dashed curve below it
- Austenite field visibly expanded

**Step 3 — Heat Treatment Implications:**
- **Lower austenitizing temperature:** Steels can be fully austenitized at lower furnace temperatures → energy saving.
- **Wider austenite stability window:** Slow cooling is possible without forming coarse pearlite.
- **Improved quench response:** Martensite forms more easily in thick sections.
- **Tempering temperature flexibility:** Lower A₁ means tempering can be done at lower temperatures without risking reaustenitization.

**Step 4 — Industrial Example:**
**5% Ni steel** is normalized at 830–870 °C (vs. 900 °C for plain carbon steel) due to the lowered A₃, and the resulting structure is finer and tougher.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss **18% Ni maraging steel** — its composition, heat treatment, microstructure, and applications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the **3.5% Ni marine steel** and the **9% Ni cryogenic steel** in terms of composition, properties, and service conditions.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Composition:**
18% Ni maraging steel typically contains:
- **Ni: 18%**
- **Co: 8–12%**
- **Mo: 3–5%**
- **Ti: 0.2–1.6%**
- **C: ≤ 0.03% (ultra-low carbon)**
- **Fe: balance**

**Step 2 — Heat Treatment (Two-Stage):**

- **Stage 1 — Solution Treatment:** Heat to **820 °C** for 1 hour → produces a soft, ductile, **carbon-free martensite**.
- **Stage 2 — Aging:** Hold at **480 °C** for 3–5 hours → precipitates **Ni₃Ti, Ni₃Mo, Fe₂Mo** intermetallics within the martensitic matrix.

**Step 3 — Microstructure:**
The aged microstructure consists of:
- **Lath martensite matrix** (BCT, low-carbon, hence tough)
- **Fine, uniformly dispersed intermetallic precipitates** (Ni₃Ti, Ni₃Mo)
- Minimal or no retained austenite.

**Step 4 — Properties:**

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| **Ultimate Tensile Strength** | **1800 – 2400 MPa** |
| **Yield Strength (0.2%)** | **1700 – 2300 MPa** |
| **Elongation** | **6 – 12%** |
| **Fracture Toughness K_IC** | **80 – 120 MPa·√m** |
| **Hardness** | **50 – 60 HRC** |

**Step 5 — Applications:**
- **Aerospace:** Rocket motor casings, missile structures
- **Tooling:** Die casting dies, injection molds
- **Defense:** Armor plates
- **Nuclear:** Reactor components requiring high strength + toughness

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 3 — Comparative Analysis Table:**

| Feature | 3.5% Ni Steel | 9% Ni Steel |
|---|---|---|
| **Nickel Content** | 3.5 wt% | 9.0 wt% |
| **Carbon Content** | 0.10 – 0.18% | 0.06 – 0.10% |
| **Tensile Strength** | 450 – 600 MPa | 700 – 900 MPa |
| **Yield Strength** | 280 – 380 MPa | 580 – 700 MPa |
| **Service Temperature** | -60 °C to +200 °C | -196 °C to +100 °C |
| **Microstructure** | Fine ferrite + pearlite/bainite | Tempered martensite + retained γ |
| **Primary Use** | Submarine hulls, ship plates | LNG tanks, cryogenic piping |
| **Standard** | ASTM A203 Gr. E | ASTM A553 Type I |
| **DBTT** | ~ -50 °C | ~ -160 °C |

**Step 4 — Service-Condition Comparison:**
- **3.5% Ni steel** is used where the environment is **cold seawater** (marine) but not cryogenic. It resists **hydrogen-induced cracking** and **sulfide stress cracking**.
- **9% Ni steel** is mandatory for **cryogenic storage and transport** of liquefied gases. Its retained austenite provides **transformation-induced plasticity (TRIP)** which arrests crack propagation.

**Step 5 — Concluding Statement:**
While both steels exploit nickel's toughening effect, the **higher Ni content in 9% steel** shifts the property balance from **marine durability** to **extreme cryogenic toughness**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "maraging" with "martensitic"** — Maraging steels are NOT quenched-and-tempered in the traditional sense; they are **aged** after solution treatment. Writing "tempered martensite" alone loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to specify "wt%"** — When stating "9% Ni steel," always clarify it is **weight percent**. Atom percent values are different.
>
> 3. **Incorrect Invar composition** — Invar is **36% Ni + 64% Fe**, NOT 18% Ni. Students often confuse it with maraging steel.
>
> 4. **Phase diagram direction** — Ni shifts the A₃ line **DOWNWARD**, not upward. Drawing the shift in the wrong direction is a frequent error.
>
> 5. **Missing the DBTT connection** — The cryogenic application of 9% Ni steel is directly tied to its **low DBTT**; examiners expect this link to be stated explicitly.
>
> 6. **Skipping the precipitation mechanism in maraging** — You must mention **Ni₃Ti and Ni₃Mo precipitates**. Writing only "intermetallic compounds" is incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Nickel Steels**

- ✅ **Nickel is a strong austenite stabilizer** and a **substitutional solid-solution strengthener** of ferrite.
- ✅ Nickel **lowers A₃ and A₁** temperatures, **expands the γ-phase field**, and **decreases eutectoid carbon content**.
- ✅ Each **1 wt% Ni reduces DBTT by ~20 °C**, enabling cryogenic use.
- ✅ **3.5% Ni steel** → Marine/submarine hulls; resists marine corrosion.
- ✅ **5% Ni steel** → High-pressure boilers and pressure vessels.
- ✅ **9% Ni steel** → LNG storage at -196 °C (ASME-approved cryogenic grade).
- ✅ **18% Ni maraging steel** → Ultra-high strength (1800–2400 MPa); aged at 480 °C to form Ni₃Ti / Ni₃Mo precipitates.
- ✅ **25% Ni steel** → Fully austenitic; cryogenic piping; non-magnetic.
- ✅ **36% Ni Invar** → Near-zero thermal expansion (α ≈ 1.2 × 10⁻⁶ /°C); precision instruments.
- ✅ **45–50% Ni Permalloy** → High magnetic permeability; transformer laminations.
- ✅ **Mechanism stack:** Solid-solution + grain refinement + austenite stabilization + DBTT reduction.
- ✅ **Hall-Petch applies:** Ni refines grains → higher yield strength via $k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$.
- ✅ **Maraging = solution treat (820 °C) + age (480 °C)** — NOT conventional quench-temper.
- ✅ **Retained austenite in 9% Ni steel** contributes to TRIP effect → arrests cracks.
- ✅ **Remember atomic radius (0.124 nm) and FCC structure of Ni** for viva questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chromium Steels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT526 - METALLURGY AND MATERIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Chromium Steels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Chromium Steels** are a sub-class of **alloy steels** in which **chromium (Cr)** is deliberately added as the principal alloying element to enhance mechanical, physical, and chemical properties of plain carbon steel. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *Metallurgy and Material Engineering*, chromium steels are classified based on the chromium content as:

- **Low Chromium Steels:** Cr content between **0.5% – 2.0%**
- **Medium Chromium Steels:** Cr content between **2.0% – 10.0%**
- **High Chromium Steels:** Cr content above **10.5%** (includes the **stainless steel** family)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> Chromium is a strong **carbide former** and a **ferrite stabilizer**. The minimum chromium content required to make a steel "stainless" is **10.5%**, because at this composition a continuous, self-healing, passive oxide film of **Cr₂O₃** forms on the surface, protecting the underlying metal from corrosive attack.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a plain carbon steel bar as a **wooden fence** standing in a rainstorm. Without protection, the rain (corrosion, wear, heat) gradually erodes and weakens the wood. Now imagine coating every wooden plank with a thin layer of **waterproof varnish (chromium oxide film)**. The fence becomes weather-resistant, stronger, and lasts much longer. Similarly, chromium acts like a **molecular-level armor** added to the iron lattice.

- **Low Cr** = light varnish → better strength and hardness.
- **High Cr (>10.5%)** = full weatherproof shield → stainless behavior.
- The **chromium carbides (Cr₂₃C₆, Cr₇C₃)** that form act like **embedded gravel** in concrete — they obstruct dislocation motion, dramatically improving **hardness and wear resistance**.

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Snapshot**
> The ball bearings inside your car's wheel hub are made of **AISI 52100** steel (1% C, 1.5% Cr). The dies that stamp automotive body panels are made of **D2 / D3 tool steel** (12% Cr). The surgical scalpels used in hospitals are **AISI 420 stainless steel** (12–14% Cr). All of these are chromium steels.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Atomic number of Cr:** 24
- **Atomic weight:** **51.996 g/mol**
- **Density:** **7.19 g/cm³**
- **Melting point:** **1907 °C (2180 K)**
- **Crystal structure (pure Cr):** Body-Centered Cubic (BCC)
- **Passive film composition:** **Cr₂O₃** (Chromium sesquioxide)
- **Critical Cr threshold for passivation:** **10.5 wt%**

> [!NOTE]
> Chromium has a strong affinity for oxygen — greater than that of iron. This is *the* fundamental reason chromium steels resist corrosion and high-temperature oxidation. Whenever oxygen is encountered, chromium preferentially oxidizes first, forming a passive film that protects the iron beneath.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Chromium is Added to Steel — The Six Core Effects

The addition of chromium to steel produces **six primary metallurgical effects**, each of which is a high-frequency KTU question topic:

### Effect 1: Increased Hardenability
Chromium **shifts the Time-Temperature-Transformation (TTT) diagram** to the right, meaning that the cooling rate required to form **martensite** is dramatically reduced. This allows thicker sections to be hardened uniformly.

### Effect 2: Carbide Formation
Chromium is a **strong carbide former** — it forms hard, stable carbides:

$$\text{Cr}_{23}\text{C}_{6} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{Cr}_{7}\text{C}_{3}$$

These carbides obstruct dislocation motion, increasing **hardness, wear resistance, and high-temperature strength**.

### Effect 3: Corrosion Resistance
At concentrations above **10.5%**, Cr forms a **self-passivating Cr₂O₃ film** that prevents further oxidation — the basis of all stainless steels.

### Effect 4: High-Temperature Strength
Chromium carbides are **stable up to ~700 °C**, making chromium steels ideal for high-temperature service (boiler tubes, turbine blades, exhaust manifolds).

### Effect 5: Refinement of Grain Structure
Chromium refines the **austenite grain size**, improving **toughness and ductility** in the heat-treated condition.

### Effect 6: Wear and Abrasion Resistance
Hard chromium carbides act as **abrasion-resistant particles** in a softer martensitic matrix — the classic *D2 / D3 tool steel* microstructure.

## 2.2 Classification of Chromium Steels

| Class | Cr Content (wt%) | Typical Carbon | Representative Grade | Primary Use |
|-------|------------------|----------------|----------------------|-------------|
| Low Cr Structural | 0.5 – 2.0 | 0.15 – 0.45 | 41Cr4 (AISI 5140) | Gears, axles, crankshafts |
| Medium Cr Tool | 2.0 – 6.0 | 0.5 – 1.0 | AISI O1, AISI A2 | Cutting tools, dies |
| Ball-Bearing Steel | 1.0 – 1.5 | 0.95 – 1.10 | AISI 52100 | Ball & roller bearings |
| High Cr Tool / Die | 10.0 – 13.0 | 1.5 – 2.2 | AISI D2, D3 | Stamping dies, punches |
| Martensitic Stainless | 12.0 – 18.0 | 0.1 – 1.0 | AISI 410, 420, 440 | Cutlery, surgical tools |
| Ferritic Stainless | 11.0 – 30.0 | < 0.20 | AISI 430, 446 | Automotive trim, furnace parts |

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Memory Trick**
> Remember the three C's of chromium:
> - **C**arbide former → hardness, wear resistance
> - **C**orrosion shield → stainless behavior
> - **C**ooling tolerance → high hardenability

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All equations below are **board-examination essentials** for the topic *Chromium Steels*.

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Use |
|---|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\% \text{Cr in solid solution} = \% \text{Cr}_{total} - \% \text{Cr}_{combined\ as\ carbides}$ | Determines passivation ability |
| 2 | $\Delta t_{\text{incubation}} \propto \%\text{Cr}$ | Chromium extends TTT nose to the right |
| 3 | $\text{HRC}_{\text{bulk}} \approx f(V_{\text{carbide}})$ | Hardness depends on carbide volume fraction |
| 4 | $V_{\text{carbide}} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{steel}} \cdot \%\text{C} \cdot M_{\text{Cr}_{x}\text{C}_{y}}}{\rho_{\text{carbide}} \cdot x \cdot M_{\text{C}} \cdot 100}$ | Volume fraction of carbides in matrix |
| 5 | $E_{\text{alloy}} = E_{\text{Fe}} \cdot (1 - f_{\text{Cr}}) + E_{\text{Cr}} \cdot f_{\text{Cr}}$ | Rule of mixtures for elastic modulus |
| 6 | $\text{Pitting Resistance Equivalent} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3\,\%\text{Mo} + 16\,\%\text{N}$ | Used to rank stainless steels |
| 7 | $\sigma_{y(\text{HT})} = \sigma_{y(\text{base})} + k \cdot d^{-1/2}$ | Hall-Petch: Cr refines grain $\rightarrow$ higher yield |
| 8 | $M_{s}(\!^{\circ}\text{C}) = 539 - 423\,\%\text{C} - 30.4\,\%\text{Mn} - 12.1\,\%\text{Cr} - 7.5\,\%\text{Mo}$ | Martensite start temperature (Andrews) |

> [!NOTE]
> Equation 8 (Andrews' empirical formula) is **extremely high-yield** for KTU 2024 numerical problems. Memorize it.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Chromium Steels

| Industry | Application | Required Property |
|----------|------------|-------------------|
| Automotive | Ball bearings, gears, crankshafts | High contact fatigue life |
| Tool & Die | Punches, drawing dies, cutting tools | Wear resistance, hardness |
| Energy | Turbine blades, boiler tubes | High-temperature strength |
| Medical | Surgical instruments, implants | Corrosion resistance, biocompatibility |
| Food Processing | Cutlery, storage tanks | Hygienic, non-reactive surface |
| Construction | Rebar, structural sections | Strength, atmospheric corrosion resistance |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effect of Chromium on Martensite Start Temperature ($M_s$)

The **Martensite Start Temperature ($M_s$)** is the temperature at which austenite begins to transform into martensite on rapid cooling. Adding chromium **lowers $M_s$** because it stabilizes austenite. The **Andrews' empirical relation** accounts for this.

We start from the linear superposition of solute effects:

$$M_s = 539^{\circ}\text{C} + \sum_i (k_i \cdot C_i)$$

where $C_i$ is the weight percent of each alloying element and $k_i$ is its corresponding coefficient.

**Step 1 — List the relevant coefficients from Andrews' data:**

| Element | $k_i$ (°C per wt%) |
|---------|--------------------|
| C       | -423               |
| Mn      | -30.4              |
| Cr      | -12.1              |
| Mo      | -7.5               |
| Ni      | -17.7              |
| Si      | -7.5               |

**Step 2 — Write the full expansion for a chromium steel:**

$$M_s = 539 - 423 \cdot \%C - 30.4 \cdot \%Mn - 12.1 \cdot \%Cr - 7.5 \cdot \%Mo$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Evaluation for a 52100 Ball-Bearing Steel (1% C, 0.35% Mn, 1.5% Cr):**

$$M_s = 539 - 423(1.0) - 30.4(0.35) - 12.1(1.5)$$

$$M_s = 539 - 423 - 10.64 - 18.15$$

$$M_s = 87.21^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

The $M_s$ is approximately **87 °C**, which is *just above room temperature*. This explains why 52100 steel must be quenched vigorously — to suppress the formation of pearlite/bainite between 700 °C and 100 °C.

> [!NOTE] **KTU Exam Tip**
> A common board question: *"Compute $M_s$ for a chromium steel and comment on the resulting heat-treatment window."* Always conclude with a sentence on whether the steel can be fully hardened by oil quenching (slow quench) or needs water/brine (fast quench).

## 3.2 Derivation: Critical Chromium Concentration for Passivation

**Why 10.5%?** Let us derive the answer quantitatively.

**Step 1 — The passive film is Cr₂O₃ with a fixed composition. The Cr:Fe ratio in the film is 2:0 (pure chromium oxide).**

**Step 2 — For the film to be *continuous and self-healing*, the substrate beneath the film must supply Cr at a rate at least equal to its loss by spallation. This requires:**

$$\%\text{Cr}_{\text{bulk}} \geq \frac{M_{\text{Cr}}}{M_{\text{Cr}_{2}\text{O}_{3}}} \cdot 100 \cdot f$$

where $f$ is a geometric coverage factor ($\approx 0.5$ for a continuous monolayer).

**Step 3 — Substituting atomic masses:**

$$\frac{2 \times 51.996}{2(51.996) + 3(16.00)} = \frac{103.99}{151.99} = 0.684$$

**Step 4 — Therefore:**

$$\%\text{Cr}_{\text{bulk}} \geq 0.684 \times \frac{1}{0.5} \times 100 \approx 13.7\%$$

**Step 5 — Practically observed threshold:**

The experimentally observed threshold is approximately **10.5%**, slightly lower than the theoretical estimate because the actual passive film is not pure Cr₂O₃ but a mixed (Cr,Fe)₂O₃ spinel with lower Cr demand. The 10.5% rule is therefore an **empirical anchor** adopted by **ASTM, ISO, and BIS** standards.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Chromium Steel

Use this decision matrix to choose the correct chromium steel for a given application (a frequent KTU design-type question):

| Application | Hardness Req. | Corrosion Req. | Temp. Req. | Recommended Grade | Cr% |
|-------------|---------------|----------------|------------|-------------------|-----|
| Ball bearing | HRC 60+ | Mild | < 150 °C | AISI 52100 | 1.5 |
| Car gear | HRC 55+ | Mild | < 200 °C | AISI 5140 | 0.8 |
| Hot-work die | HRC 50+ | Mild | 500 °C | AISI H11 | 5.0 |
| Cold-work die | HRC 60+ | Mild | < 200 °C | AISI D2 | 12.0 |
| Surgical blade | HRC 50+ | High (body fluid) | < 100 °C | AISI 420 | 13.0 |
| Marine propeller shaft | HRC 30+ | Very High | < 50 °C | AISI 630 (17-4 PH) | 16.0 |
| Furnace roller | HRC 25+ | High | 900 °C | AISI 310 | 25.0 |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Heat-Treatment Logic for AISI D2 (12% Cr, 1.5% C)

**Step 1 — Austenitizing:** Heat slowly to **1010 °C** and hold until all carbides dissolve into austenite. The high Cr raises the austenitizing temperature.

**Step 2 — Quenching:** Air-cool or oil-quench. The high Cr content shifts the TTT nose far to the right, so a slow air cool is sufficient to form martensite (D2 is *air-hardening*).

**Step 3 — Tempering:** Temper at **200 °C** for 2 hours. Avoid the **475 °C embrittlement zone** characteristic of high-chromium steels.

**Step 4 — Final Hardness:** Achieves **HRC 60–62** with **secondary hardening** due to precipitation of fine alloy carbides.

> [!WARNING]
> Never temper high-Cr steels (e.g., D2, 440C) in the range **400–550 °C**. The reaction $\alpha$-ferrite $\rightarrow$ **α′ (Cr-rich BCC) + α (Fe-rich BCC)** causes severe embrittlement (called **475 °C embrittlement** or **sigma-phase embrittlement**).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flow Diagram of Chromium Steels

```mermaid
graph TD
    root1["Chromium Steels"]
    low1["Low Cr Steels 0.5 to 2.0 percent"]
    med1["Medium Cr Steels 2.0 to 10.0 percent"]
    high1["High Cr Steels above 10.5 percent"]
    root1 --> low1
    root1 --> med1
    root1 --> high1
    lowA["AISI 5140 Cr 0.8 percent"]
    lowB["AISI 5160 Cr 0.8 percent"]
    lowC["AISI 52100 Cr 1.5 percent"]
    low1 --> lowA
    low1 --> lowB
    low1 --> lowC
    medA["AISI O1 Cr 0.5 percent borderline"]
    medB["AISI A2 Cr 5.0 percent"]
    medC["AISI H11 Cr 5.0 percent hot work"]
    med1 --> medA
    med1 --> medB
    med1 --> medC
    highA["Martensitic SS 410 420 440"]
    highB["Ferritic SS 430 446"]
    highC["Austenitic SS 304 316"]
    highD["Tool and Die D2 D3 Cr 12 percent"]
    high1 --> highA
    high1 --> highB
    high1 --> highC
    high1 --> highD
    appA["Gears axles"]
    appB["Bearings springs"]
    appC["Tools dies"]
    appD["Cutlery surgical"]
    appE["Furnace parts"]
    appF["Chemical tanks"]
    lowA --> appA
    lowB --> appB
    lowC --> appB
    medB --> appC
    medC --> appC
    highA --> appD
    highB --> appE
    highC --> appF
    highD --> appC
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Microstructural Evolution During Heat Treatment of a Chromium Steel

```mermaid
graph LR
    inputA["As Rolled Cr Steel"]
    block1["Heating to 1010 C"]
    block2["Austenite plus Undissolved Cr23C6"]
    block3["Air or Oil Quench"]
    block4["Martensite plus Retained Austenite plus Cr Carbides"]
    block5["Tempering at 200 C"]
    outputA["Hardened Tool HRC 60 plus"]
    inputA --> block1
    block1 --> block2
    block2 --> block3
    block3 --> block4
    block4 --> block5
    block5 --> outputA
    sideA["Refines grain"]
    sideB["Dissolves carbides"]
    sideC["Suppresses pearlite nose"]
    sideD["Fine carbide precipitation"]
    sideE["Secondary hardening"]
    block1 -.- sideA
    block2 -.- sideB
    block3 -.- sideC
    block4 -.- sideD
    block5 -.- sideE
```

## 4.3 Property-Space Radar of a Typical Chromium Steel (e.g., AISI 52100)

| Property | Pure Iron | AISI 52100 (1.5% Cr) |
|----------|-----------|----------------------|
| Hardness (HRC) | < 5 | 60 – 64 |
| Tensile Strength (MPa) | 220 | 2100 – 2300 |
| Wear Resistance (relative) | 1× | 10× – 20× |
| Corrosion Resistance (relative) | 1× | 3× – 5× |
| Hot Hardness (at 500 °C) | Poor | Good |

> [!NOTE]
> The **trade-off** with chromium steels is between **toughness and hardness**. Higher Cr and C = higher hardness, but **lower impact toughness**. A KTU 14-mark question often asks you to justify grade selection based on this trade-off.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define chromium steels. Why is chromium considered a strong carbide former? Mention two important chromium carbides.*

**Model Answer:**

Chromium steels are alloy steels in which chromium is the principal alloying element, typically present in the range 0.5% to 30% by weight. Chromium is considered a strong carbide former because of its high affinity for carbon and its incompletely filled d-shell (3d⁵ 4s¹), which allows strong covalent bonding with carbon atoms. The two most important chromium carbides are:

- **Cr₂₃C₆** — the most chromium-rich carbide
- **Cr₇C₃** — the most commonly observed in commercial chromium steels

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is a minimum of 10.5% chromium required to make a steel stainless? Explain the role of the passive film.*

**Model Answer:**

At chromium contents above **10.5 wt%**, the steel forms a continuous, adherent, and self-healing **passive oxide film of Cr₂O₃** on the surface exposed to air or water. This film is only a few nanometers thick but acts as a barrier that prevents further diffusion of oxygen and corrosive species to the underlying iron matrix. Because the film is **self-healing**, any mechanical damage is repaired instantly by reaction with atmospheric oxygen. Below 10.5%, the Cr content is insufficient to form a *continuous* film, and the iron beneath remains vulnerable to rusting.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> For chromium-steel questions, students **commonly lose 2–3 marks** by:
> - Confusing **chromium steels** with **stainless steels** (the latter is only the *high-Cr sub-class*).
> - Forgetting to mention the **passive film** when discussing corrosion resistance.
> - Writing "Cr makes steel hard" without specifying **carbide formation** as the mechanism.
> - Omitting units in numerical answers (e.g., writing "$M_s = 87$" instead of **87 °C**).
> - Failing to mention **475 °C embrittlement** in heat-treatment questions on high-Cr steels.

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Classify chromium steels based on chromium content. For each class, give **one commercial grade** and **one application**.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with suitable diagrams the **role of chromium in shifting the TTT diagram** to the right. How does this affect hardenability? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

| Class | Cr Range | Grade | Application |
|-------|----------|-------|-------------|
| Low Cr | 0.5 – 2.0% | AISI 5140 | Automotive gears, axles |
| Medium Cr | 2.0 – 10% | AISI A2 | Cold-work dies, punches |
| High Cr (Tool) | 10 – 13% | AISI D2 | Stamping dies, shear blades |
| High Cr (Stainless) | > 10.5% | AISI 304 | Chemical tanks, kitchenware |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Chromium, being a strong carbide former, dissolves in austenite and **retards the diffusion-controlled transformation of austenite to pearlite and bainite**. This shifts the entire **C-curve (TTT nose) to longer times**, allowing slower cooling rates to still avoid the nose and form martensite.

**Practical consequence:** A plain carbon steel may require a **water quench** to harden, but a chromium steel can be hardened with a milder **oil or even air quench**. This:

- Reduces **distortion and cracking**
- Enables hardening of **thicker sections**
- Permits use of **less severe quenching media** (cost saving, safety)

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A chromium steel contains **0.4% C, 0.7% Mn, and 1.0% Cr**. Calculate the **Martensite Start Temperature ($M_s$)** using Andrews' formula. Comment on whether oil quenching will be sufficient for full hardening. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **heat treatment cycle of AISI D2 (12% Cr) tool steel**. Explain why it is called an *air-hardening steel* and what precautions must be taken during tempering. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Using Andrews' empirical relation:**

$$M_s = 539 - 423(\%\text{C}) - 30.4(\%\text{Mn}) - 12.1(\%\text{Cr})$$

**Substituting values:**

$$M_s = 539 - 423(0.4) - 30.4(0.7) - 12.1(1.0)$$

$$M_s = 539 - 169.2 - 21.28 - 12.1$$

$$M_s = 336.42^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Comment:**

With $M_s \approx 336\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$, the temperature gap between the **TTT nose (~550 °C)** and $M_s$ is wide. The rightward shift of the TTT curve due to chromium means that even an **oil quench** (cooling rate ~30 °C/s) is sufficient to bypass the pearlite nose and reach $M_s$ — therefore, **oil quenching is sufficient for full hardening**. Plain carbon steel of the same carbon content would have required a water or brine quench.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Heat Treatment Cycle of AISI D2:**

1. **Preheating:** Slow heat to 600 °C to equalize temperature and avoid thermal shock.
2. **Austenitizing:** Heat to **1010 – 1040 °C** and hold for 30 minutes. This temperature is high enough to dissolve Cr carbides into austenite.
3. **Quenching:** **Air cool** (not oil, not water). D2 is famous for being *air-hardening* because the high Cr (12%) shifts the TTT nose so far to the right that even air cooling (~3 °C/s) bypasses it.
4. **Tempering:** Temper at **200 °C** for 2 hours, twice (double temper).
5. **Final Hardness:** **HRC 60 – 62**.

**Precautions:**

- **Avoid tempering between 400 – 550 °C** — this is the **475 °C embrittlement** zone where Cr-rich α′ precipitates, causing severe loss of toughness.
- **Do not water-quench** D2 — it will crack due to high carbon and alloy content.
- **Use a protective atmosphere** during austenitizing to prevent decarburization and surface oxidation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Revision Checklist — Chromium Steels**

- ⭐ **Chromium** is the **#1 alloying element** for tool and bearing steels.
- ⭐ **Minimum 10.5% Cr** is the threshold for **stainless** behavior (passive Cr₂O₃ film).
- ⭐ **Strong carbide former** — produces **Cr₂₃C₆** and **Cr₇C₃**.
- ⭐ **Three C's:** **C**arbides, **C**orrosion shield, **C**ooling tolerance.
- ⭐ **Shifts TTT to the right** → increases **hardenability** → slower quench media acceptable.
- ⭐ **AISI 52100** (1% C, 1.5% Cr) — the standard **ball-bearing** steel.
- ⭐ **AISI D2 / D3** (12% Cr, 1.5% C) — **air-hardening** cold-work tool steel.
- ⭐ **AISI 304** (18% Cr, 8% Ni) — austenitic stainless, non-magnetic.
- ⭐ **AISI 430** (16% Cr) — ferritic stainless, magnetic, cheaper.
- ⭐ **Andrews' formula:** $M_s = 539 - 423(\%\text{C}) - 30.4(\%\text{Mn}) - 12.1(\%\text{Cr}) - 7.5(\%\text{Mo})$.
- ⭐ **Avoid tempering at 400 – 550 °C** in high-Cr steels → **475 °C embrittlement**.
- ⭐ Chromium **stabilizes ferrite** and **refines austenite grains** (Hall-Petch strengthening).
- ⭐ Chromium steels are used in **bearings, gears, dies, cutlery, turbine blades, surgical tools**.
- ⭐ **Pitting Resistance Equivalent (PRE)** = %Cr + 3.3·%Mo + 16·%N — a metric for ranking stainless corrosion performance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: High Speed Steels - Cast Irons
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT526 - METALLURGY AND MATERIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** High Speed Steels - Cast Irons

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 High Speed Steels (HSS) — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **High Speed Steels (HSS)** are a class of highly alloyed **tool steels** specifically engineered to retain their hardness, wear resistance, and cutting edge at the elevated temperatures (≈ **500–650 °C**) generated during high-speed machining of metals. This property is called **"Red Hardness"** (or *hot hardness*).

The name "High Speed" originates from the fact that these steels can machine workpiece materials at cutting speeds **2–4 times higher** than conventional carbon tool steels, without losing their hardness at the tool tip.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Race Car Engine:**
> Imagine a regular car engine that works great at low speeds but overheats and fails when pushed to 200 km/h. A high-speed steel is like a Formula 1 engine — built with special alloys (tungsten, molybdenum, vanadium, cobalt) that dissipate heat through their own microstructure and continue performing at extreme "red-hot" temperatures. The *secondary hardening* mechanism is essentially the steel "breathing" through the heat — instead of softening, it forms new nano-sized carbides that make it even harder!

### 1.1.1 Chemical Composition of HSS (Typical Ranges)

| Element | Symbol | Weight % | Function |
|---------|--------|----------|----------|
| Carbon | C | **0.70 – 1.50 %** | Forms hard carbides (M₆C, M₂₃C₆, MC) |
| Tungsten | W | **0 – 18 %** | Promotes red hardness; forms M₆C carbides |
| Molybdenum | Mo | **0 – 10 %** | Substitute for W; forms M₆C and M₂C |
| Chromium | Cr | **3.5 – 4.5 %** | Improves hardenability, wear, oxidation |
| Vanadium | V | **0.5 – 5 %** | Forms ultra-hard VC (vanadium carbide) |
| Cobalt | Co | **0 – 12 %** | Enhances red hardness dramatically |

> [!TIP]
> A useful thumb rule: **HSS contains at least 7 alloying elements in addition to iron**, making it one of the most heavily alloyed commercial steels.

---

## 1.2 Cast Irons — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Cast Iron** is a family of **Fe–C–Si** alloys containing **more than 2.14 % C** (typically **2.5 – 4.0 % C**) and a relatively high silicon content (**1 – 3 % Si**). Because of its high carbon equivalent, it is **brittle and not malleable** at room temperature, but is **excellent for casting** due to its low melting point and high fluidity.

The exceptional property of cast iron is that the carbon, which in steel is locked up as hard cementite (Fe₃C), can be made to appear in several distinct physical forms — **flakes, clusters, or spheres** — by controlling composition and cooling rate. This gives rise to different families of cast iron, each with unique properties.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Clay Pot Workshop:**
> Picture a potter shaping wet clay. The clay must be *fluid* to pour into a mold, *cheap* to make in bulk, and *strong enough* to sit on a stove. Cast iron is the metallurgical equivalent: it pours like thick honey into intricate molds, costs a fraction of forged steel, and depending on how it is cooled or treated, can be as brittle as a *biscuit* (white CI) or as tough as a *rubber tire* (ductile iron). The shape of the graphite inside (flakes, popcorn, or balls) is the "secret ingredient" that decides everything!

### 1.2.1 Why Cast Iron is Important

> [!TIP]
> - **Lowest cost** engineering material per kg of strength.
> - **Excellent fluidity** — fills complex shapes (engine blocks, pump housings).
> - **High damping capacity** — absorbs vibration (machine tool beds).
> - **Self-lubricating** (in flake graphite form) — useful for sliding contacts.

---

## 1.3 The Position of HSS and Cast Irons on the Fe–C Phase Diagram

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **High Speed Steels** are **high-alloy steels** (C < 2.14 %), located on the **steel side** of the eutectoid composition. They are hardened by martensite formation + secondary carbide precipitation.
> - **Cast Irons** are **high-carbon alloys** (C > 2.14 %), located on the **cast iron side** of the eutectic composition. Their behavior is governed by the **eutectic reaction** *Liquid → Austenite + Graphite/Cementite* at **1147 °C**.

The following schematic positions them on the iron–carbon diagram:

```
Fe        Steel Zone          Eutectoid (0.77%C)        Cast Iron Zone           C
 |---------|--------------------|------------------------|-----------------------|
 0        0.022              0.77                       2.14                   6.67
                          (A3, A1)                      (Eutectic 1147°C)
                                ↑                              ↑
                          HSS family                   Cast Iron family
                          (0.7-1.5 % C)                (2.5-4.0 % C)
```

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 High Speed Steels — Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 The AISI Classification of HSS

The American Iron and Steel Institute (AISI) classifies HSS into two principal series:

> [!NOTE]
> **T-Series (Tungsten-based):** Symbol **T1, T4, T5, T6, T15...**
> **M-Series (Molybdenum-based):** Symbol **M1, M2, M7, M10, M42...**

In modern industry, **M2 is the most widely used HSS in the world** (≈ 95 % of all cutting tools). M2 has the composition:
**Fe – 0.85 C – 6 W – 5 Mo – 4 Cr – 2 V** (wt%)

### 2.1.2 Types of Carbides in HSS

The exceptional properties of HSS arise from the alloy carbides that precipitate during heat treatment:

| Carbide | Formula | Hardness (HV) | Origin |
|---------|---------|---------------|--------|
| M₆C (η-carbide) | Fe₃W₃C / Fe₃Mo₃C | **1500 – 1800** | W- and Mo-rich, primary |
| M₂₃C₆ | Cr₂₃C₆ | **1000 – 1200** | Cr-rich, from tempering |
| MC | VC, (V,W)C | **2500 – 2800** | V-rich, hardest, finest |
| M₂C | Mo₂C, W₂C | **1500 – 1900** | Secondary, from tempering |
| M₇C₃ | Cr₇C₃ | **1200 – 1400** | Cr-rich eutectic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **vanadium carbide (VC)** is the **hardest phase** in HSS — its volume fraction directly controls wear resistance. **Higher V ⇒ better wear life, but worse grindability** (because VC is too hard to be easily abraded during tool sharpening).

### 2.1.3 Heat Treatment of HSS — The Classical Three-Stage Cycle

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 1 — Preheating (820–870 °C):** Reduces thermal shock distortion in a high-alloy steel.
> **Stage 2 — Austenitizing (1200–1300 °C):** Dissolves primary carbides into a carbon-saturated austenite.
> **Stage 3 — Quenching (oil, air, or salt bath):** Forms as-quenched martensite.
