> **Stage 4 — Tempering (triple tempering at 540–580 °C):** Precipitates fine **secondary carbides** — this is the *secondary hardening* phenomenon.

The **secondary hardening** is the *signature* of HSS. As tempering temperature rises from 200 °C to ~600 °C:

```
Hardness
   |
   |            ___________ ← Peak ~580 °C (Secondary Hardening)
   |           /           
   |          /            
   |   _____ /  (softens first from cementite precipitation)
   |  /      
   | /       
   |/__________|____________________
   0       200      400     600    Tempering Temp (°C)
```

> [!TIP]
> The peak hardness at ~580 °C is **higher than the as-quenched hardness** because the precipitated alloy carbides (M₂C, MC) are much harder than the brittle martensite they replace.

### 2.1.4 Real-World Utility of HSS

- **Cutting tools:** drills, taps, reamers, milling cutters, broaches, hobs.
- **Forming tools:** punches, dies, blanking tools.
- **Wear parts:** bearings, guides, saw blades.
- **High-temperature applications:** boiler tubes, gas turbine discs (early designs).

---

## 2.2 Cast Irons — Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Composition of Cast Iron

| Element | Weight % | Function |
|---------|----------|----------|
| Carbon (C) | **2.5 – 4.0** | Forms graphite or cementite |
| Silicon (Si) | **1.0 – 3.0** | Strong graphite stabilizer |
| Manganese (Mn) | **0.2 – 1.0** | Combines with S to form MnS |
| Sulfur (S) | **0.02 – 0.25** | Promotes cementite; kept low |
| Phosphorus (P) | **0.05 – 1.0** | Improves fluidity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Carbon Equivalent (CE)** is the single most important composition parameter:
>
> **CE = %C + 0.3 × (%Si + %P)**
>
> - **CE < 4.3 %** → Hypoeutectic cast iron (austenite dendrites + ledeburite)
> - **CE = 4.3 %** → Eutectic cast iron (pure ledeburite)
> - **CE > 4.3 %** → Hypereutectic cast iron (primary graphite + ledeburite)

### 2.2.2 The Eutectic Reaction in Cast Iron

At **1147 °C**, the following invariant reaction occurs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Liquid (4.3 \% C)} \quad &\xrightarrow{\text{cooling}} \quad \text{Austenite (2.14 \% C)} \;\; + \;\; \text{Graphite / Cementite} \\
&\text{(Eutectic: Ledeburite)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The choice between **graphite** and **cementite** as the eutectic product is governed by the **graphitization potential**, which depends on Si content, cooling rate, and the presence of inoculants.

### 2.2.3 The Five Families of Cast Iron — Master Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **1. White Cast Iron** — Carbon present as **Fe₃C (cementite)**. Hard, brittle, wear-resistant.
> **2. Grey Cast Iron** — Carbon present as **graphite flakes**. Excellent damping, machinable.
> **3. Malleable Cast Iron** — White CI heat-treated to form **tempered graphite rosettes**.
> **4. Ductile (Nodular) Cast Iron** — Graphite in **spheroidal form** (Mg-treated). High strength + ductility.
> **5. Alloy Cast Iron** — High Cr, Ni, Si for special properties (heat, corrosion, wear).

### 2.2.4 Microstructure–Property Linkage

| Type | Form of Carbon | Matrix | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Elongation (%) | Hardness (BHN) |
|------|----------------|--------|------------------------|----------------|----------------|
| White CI | Cementite (Fe₃C) | Pearlite + Ledeburite | **200 – 400** | < 1 | **400 – 600** |
| Grey CI | Flake graphite | Ferrite / Pearlite | **150 – 400** | < 1 | **120 – 250** |
| Malleable CI | Tempered graphite rosettes | Ferrite / Pearlite | **300 – 500** | **2 – 10** | **130 – 220** |
| Ductile (Nodular) CI | Spheroidal graphite | Ferrite / Pearlite | **400 – 700** | **2 – 20** | **140 – 300** |
| High-Cr Alloy CI | M₇C₃ carbides | Martensite | **400 – 800** | < 2 | **400 – 700** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that **ductile cast iron is the only cast iron that can elongate more than 2 %** because the spheroidal graphite does not act as a sharp crack-initiator (unlike flakes).

### 2.2.5 Production Processes of Different Cast Irons

> [!NOTE]
> **White CI:** Rapid cooling of low-Si melt; cementite is kinetically favoured.
> **Grey CI:** Slow cooling of high-Si melt; silicon destabilizes cementite to graphite.
> **Malleable CI:** White CI → long heat treatment (malleabilization) at 870 °C for 20–50 h.
> **Ductile CI:** Mg (or Ce) added to the ladle just before pouring; graphite grows as spheres.
> **Compacted Graphite Iron (CGI):** Intermediate treatment — short, thick graphite "worms".

### 2.2.6 Real-World Utility of Cast Iron

- **Engine blocks, cylinder liners, brake drums** — grey CI (damping, wear).
- **Pipes, fittings, manhole covers** — ductile CI (strength + toughness).
- **Crusher jaws, grinding balls** — white CI (abrasion resistance).
- **Agricultural ploughshares** — malleable CI (impact resistance).
- **Furnace trays, pump impellers for chemicals** — high-Si / high-Cr alloy CI (corrosion + heat).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (No Pipe Symbols)

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Carbon Equivalent (CE)** | $CE = \%C + 0.3 \,(\%Si + \%P)$ | Hypoeutectic if CE \textless 4.3 |
| 2 | **Eutectic composition (Fe–C)** | $C_{eutectic} = 4.3 \;wt\% \;C$ | At $T = 1147 \;°C$ |
| 3 | **Eutectoid composition** | $C_{eutectoid} = 0.77 \;wt\% \;C$ | At $T = 727 \;°C$ |
| 4 | **Eutectoid reaction** | $\gamma (0.77\%C) \rightarrow \alpha (0.02\%C) + Fe_3C (6.67\%C)$ | Pearlite formation |
| 5 | **Volume fraction (rule of mixtures)** | $V_\alpha = \dfrac{C_{Fe_3C} - C_0}{C_{Fe_3C} - C_\alpha}$ | For pearlite |
| 6 | **Hardness contribution rule** | $H_{eff} = V_{matrix} \cdot H_{matrix} + V_{carbide} \cdot H_{carbide}$ | Weighted rule |
| 7 | **Tempering temperature for HSS** | $T_{temp} = 540 - 580 \;°C$ | Triple temper for 1 h each |
| 8 | **Austenitizing temperature for HSS** | $T_{aus} = 1200 - 1300 \;°C$ | Depends on V content |
| 9 | **Co content → Red hardness** | $\Delta HRC \approx +1$ per 1 % Co (up to 12 %) | Empirical |
| 10 | **Mg addition for ductile iron** | $Mg_{residual} = 0.02 - 0.05 \;wt\%$ | Critical for spheroidization |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In numericals, always show the calculation of **CE first** — it determines whether the cast iron is hypo/hyper-eutectic, which in turn determines the **primary phase** that forms during solidification.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Numerical Problem 1 — Carbon Equivalent Calculation (Classic KTU Type)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A cast iron melt has the following composition: **C = 3.4 %, Si = 2.2 %, Mn = 0.6 %, P = 0.4 %, S = 0.08 %**.
> Determine:
> (a) The carbon equivalent (CE).
> (b) Whether the iron is hypoeutectic, eutectic, or hypereutectic.
> (c) The phases that form first during solidification.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1: Identify the given data.**

$$
\%C = 3.4, \quad \%Si = 2.2, \quad \%P = 0.4
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Carbon Equivalent formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CE &= \%C + 0.3 \times (\%Si + \%P) \\
   &= 3.4 + 0.3 \times (2.2 + 0.4) \\
   &= 3.4 + 0.3 \times 2.6 \\
   &= 3.4 + 0.78 \\
CE &= 4.18 \;wt\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compare with eutectic composition.**

$$
CE = 4.18 \; wt\% \;\; < \;\; 4.3 \; wt\%
$$

**Step 4: Conclusion on phase class.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since **CE \textless 4.3 %**, the cast iron is **HYPOEUTECTIC**.
> The **primary phase** that forms on cooling the liquid below the liquidus is **AUSTENITE (γ-iron)** dendrites. These grow until the remaining liquid reaches the eutectic composition (4.3 % C), at which point the **eutectic reaction** forms **ledeburite** (austenite + graphite/cementite mixture).

---

## 3.2 Numerical Problem 2 — Microstructure Weight % via Lever Rule (Pearlite in Steel)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A HSS sample has a **carbon content of 0.85 %** (typical of M2 grade) and has been slowly cooled to just below the **eutectoid temperature (727 °C)**.
> Calculate the weight percent of **proeutectoid carbide** and **pearlite** in the microstructure at room temperature.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1: Locate the alloy on the Fe–Fe₃C diagram.**

The alloy has $C_0 = 0.85 \;wt\% \;C$, which is **hyper-eutectoid** (above 0.77 % C).

**Step 2: Identify the phase boundaries at 727 °C.**

- Ferrite (α): $C_\alpha = 0.022 \; wt\%$
- Cementite (Fe₃C): $C_{Fe_3C} = 6.67 \; wt\%$
- Pearlite: forms at $0.77 \; wt\% \; C$

**Step 3: Apply the lever rule to find weight fraction of proeutectoid cementite.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{Fe_3C,\, pro} &= \frac{C_0 - 0.77}{6.67 - 0.77} \\
                 &= \frac{0.85 - 0.77}{5.90} \\
                 &= \frac{0.08}{5.90} \\
W_{Fe_3C,\, pro} &= 0.01356 \;\; \text{or} \;\; \mathbf{1.36 \; wt\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the lever rule to find weight fraction of pearlite.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{Pearlite} &= \frac{6.67 - C_0}{6.67 - 0.77} \\
             &= \frac{6.67 - 0.85}{5.90} \\
             &= \frac{5.82}{5.90} \\
W_{Pearlite} &= 0.9864 \;\; \text{or} \;\; \mathbf{98.64 \; wt\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Verification.**

$$
W_{Fe_3C,\, pro} + W_{Pearlite} = 1.36 + 98.64 = 100.00 \; wt\% \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!TIP]
> The microstructure therefore consists of a **continuous pearlite matrix** with a **thin network of proeutectoid cementite** decorating the prior austenite grain boundaries. This carbide network is responsible for the extreme **wear resistance** of HSS.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Problem 3 — Effect of Co Addition on Red Hardness of HSS

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> M2 HSS has a baseline hot hardness of **62 HRC** at 600 °C. Adding **8 % cobalt** is claimed to increase red hardness by **+1 HRC per 1 % Co**. Predict the hot hardness of an M2 + 8%Co variant (commercial name **M35**).

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1: Base hardness.**

$$
HRC_{base} = 62 \;\; HRC
$$

**Step 2: Increment from cobalt.**

$$
\Delta HRC = 8 \times 1 = 8 \;\; HRC
$$

**Step 3: Final hot hardness.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HRC_{M35} &= HRC_{base} + \Delta HRC \\
          &= 62 + 8 \\
HRC_{M35} &= \mathbf{70 \;\; HRC \;\; at \;\; 600 \;°C}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why **M35** (and the premium **M42** with 8 % Co + higher V) are chosen for **heavy-duty drilling and tough cutting operations** on hard alloys.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Problem 4 — Hardness by Rule of Mixtures (Grey Cast Iron)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A grey cast iron has **8 vol % graphite flakes** in a **ferrite matrix**. Given $H_{graphite} = 50 \;HV$ and $H_{ferrite} = 80 \;HV$, calculate the effective hardness of the composite.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1: Volume fractions.**

$$
V_{graphite} = 0.08, \quad V_{ferrite} = 0.92
$$

**Step 2: Apply the rule of mixtures.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{eff} &= V_{ferrite} \cdot H_{ferrite} + V_{graphite} \cdot H_{graphite} \\
        &= 0.92 \times 80 + 0.08 \times 50 \\
        &= 73.6 + 4.0 \\
H_{eff} &= \mathbf{77.6 \;\; HV}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> In reality, the effective hardness is even **lower** than this rule-of-mixtures estimate because the sharp **graphite flakes act as internal stress concentrators and crack initiators**, dramatically reducing the load-bearing cross-section. This is why grey CI is soft despite a strong matrix.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: HSS vs Cast Iron — A Comparative Study

| Property | High Speed Steel (M2) | Grey Cast Iron | Ductile Cast Iron |
|----------|-----------------------|----------------|-------------------|
| Carbon content | 0.85 % | 3.0 – 3.5 % | 3.0 – 3.7 % |
| Dominant hard phase | M₆C, MC (vanadium carbide) | Fe₃C in pearlite | Spheroidal graphite |
| Hardness (HRC) | **62 – 65** | **10 – 25** (HRB 80–110) | **15 – 30** (HRB 130–300) |
| Tensile strength (MPa) | 2000 – 2500 | 150 – 400 | 400 – 700 |
| Elongation (%) | **5 – 10** | \textless 1 | 2 – 20 |
| Damping capacity | Low | **Very high** | Moderate |
| Hot hardness at 600 °C | **58 – 62 HRC** | Softens drastically | Softens drastically |
| Cost (relative) | **High** (5–10×) | **Lowest** (baseline) | 1.5 – 2× grey |
| Typical use | Cutting tools | Machine beds, blocks | Crankshafts, pipes |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Tree of High Speed Steels (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[High Speed Steels HSS] --> B[T Series Tungsten Based]
    A --> C[M Series Molybdenum Based]
    A --> D[Specialty Grades]
    B --> B1[T1: 18W-4Cr-1V-0.75C]
    B --> B2[T4: 18W-4Cr-1V-0.75C + 5Co]
    B --> B3[T15: 12W-4Cr-5V-1.5C]
    C --> C1[M1: 8Mo-4Cr-1V]
    C --> C2[M2: 6W-5Mo-4Cr-2V]
    C --> C3[M7: 8Mo-4Cr-2V]
    C --> C4[M10: 8Mo-4Cr-2V no W]
    C --> C5[M35: M2 + 5Co]
    C --> C6[M42: 8Co + higher V]
    D --> D1[Matrix HSS]
    D --> D2[PM HSS Powder Metallurgy]
    D --> D3[Coated HSS TiN, AlTiN]
```

## 4.2 Heat Treatment Cycle for HSS — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Annealed HSS] --> P2[Stage 1: Preheat 820-870C]
    P2 --> P3[Stage 2: Austenitize 1200-1300C]
    P3 --> P4[Stage 3: Quench in oil or salt bath]
    P4 --> P5[As-quenched martensite + retained austenite]
    P5 --> P6[Stage 4: Triple Temper at 540-580C, 1h each]
    P6 --> P7[Final microstructure: tempered martensite + M2C + MC carbides]
    P7 --> P8[Hardness 62-65 HRC + Red hardness]
```

## 4.3 Classification of Cast Irons — Master Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    CI[Cast Iron Fe-C-Si, C greater than 2.14 percent] --> W[White CI]
    CI --> G[Grey CI]
    CI --> M[Malleable CI]
    CI --> D[Ductile Nodular CI]
    CI --> S[Special / Alloy CI]
    W --> W1[Cementite Fe3C structure]
    W --> W2[Hard, brittle, abrasion resistant]
    G --> G1[Flake graphite in ferrite/pearlite]
    G --> G2[Ferritic, pearlitic, ferritic-pearlitic]
    M --> M1[White CI heat treated 870C, 20-50 h]
    M --> M2[Tempered graphite rosettes]
    D --> D1[Mg or Ce added to ladle]
    D --> D2[Spheroidal graphite]
    S --> S1[High-Cr: heat resistant]
    S --> S2[High-Si: corrosion resistant]
    S --> S3[Ni-hard: wear resistant]
```

## 4.4 Microstructure Evolution During Solidification of Cast Iron

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Liquid melt T above 1300C] --> L2[Cool below liquidus]
    L2 --> L3{Composition check}
    L3 -->|CE less than 4.3 percent Hypo| L4[Primary austenite dendrites grow]
    L3 -->|CE equals 4.3 percent Eutectic| L5[Direct eutectic: austenite + graphite]
    L3 -->|CE greater than 4.3 percent Hyper| L6[Primary graphite flakes form]
    L4 --> L7[At 1147C: Eutectic reaction forms ledeburite]
    L5 --> L7
    L6 --> L7
    L7 --> L8[Cool to 738C: Eutectoid reaction]
    L8 --> L9[Final microstructure: pearlite + graphite or pearlite + cementite]
```

## 4.5 Effect of Graphite Morphology on Mechanical Behaviour

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Graphite morphology] --> B[Sharp flakes]
    A --> C[Rosette clusters]
    A --> D[Spheres nodules]
    B --> B1[Stress concentrators]
    B1 --> B2[Low strength, low ductility, high damping]
    C --> C1[Moderate stress concentration]
    C1 --> C2[Moderate strength, moderate ductility]
    D --> D1[Minimal stress concentration]
    D1 --> D2[High strength, high ductility, low damping]
```

## 4.6 Secondary Hardening Mechanism in HSS (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[As-quenched martensite at 1200C quench] --> B[Supersaturated C in BCT lattice]
    B --> C[Temper 540-580C]
    C --> D[Fine alloy carbides M2C and MC precipitate]
    D --> E[Coherent strain fields around precipitates]
    E --> F[Dislocation motion blocked]
    F --> G[Hardness increases to peak 62-65 HRC]
    G --> H[At 600C during cutting: carbides remain stable, no coarsening]
    H --> I[Result: Red hardness preserved]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q1. Define "Red Hardness" and explain its significance in High Speed Steels.**

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Red Hardness** (also called **hot hardness**) is the ability of a tool material to **retain its hardness at elevated temperatures** (typically 500 – 650 °C) encountered at the cutting edge during high-speed machining.
>
> **Significance in HSS (3 points for 3 marks):**
> 1. It allows the cutting tool to operate at **2–4 times higher cutting speeds** than carbon tool steels without losing edge integrity.
> 2. It is achieved by the **secondary hardening mechanism** — precipitation of fine alloy carbides (M₂C, MC) during tempering at 540 – 580 °C.
> 3. It is enhanced by additions of **Co, W, Mo, and V**, with cobalt being the most effective per unit weight.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Q2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between Grey Cast Iron and White Cast Iron based on (a) form of carbon, (b) one property, (c) one application.**

### Model Answer (3 Marks — Tabular Format Expected)

| Parameter | Grey Cast Iron | White Cast Iron |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| (a) Form of carbon | **Flake graphite** in ferrite/pearlite matrix | **Cementite (Fe₃C)** throughout the structure |
| (b) Property | Soft (120 – 250 BHN), excellent damping, machinable | Hard (400 – 600 BHN), brittle, abrasion-resistant |
| (c) Application | Engine blocks, machine tool beds, brake drums | Crusher jaws, grinding balls, ploughshares |

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Format)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) regulations, each module carries a **14-mark question** with **internal choice** — students attempt **one of two alternatives**. The two sub-parts typically carry **7 marks each**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted, 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **microstructure and heat treatment cycle of M2 High Speed Steel**. Discuss the role of each major alloying element. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A HSS tool made of M2 grade is to be replaced by a higher-performance grade for cutting tool steel workpieces. Compare **M2, M35, and M42** grades in terms of composition, hardness, and red hardness. Recommend the best grade with justification. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Composition of M2 HSS (2 marks):**
- 0.85 % C
- 6 % W, 5 % Mo
- 4 % Cr
- 2 % V

**2. Heat Treatment Cycle (3 marks):**

The heat treatment of M2 HSS involves **four distinct stages**:

(i) **Pre-heating at 820–870 °C** — Minimizes thermal shock in this highly alloyed steel.
(ii) **Austenitizing at 1220–1250 °C** — Carbon and alloying elements dissolve into austenite, forming a chemically homogeneous γ-phase.
(iii) **Quenching in oil, salt bath, or pressurized air** — Transforms γ to **martensite** (BCT) with a small amount of retained austenite.
(iv) **Triple tempering at 540–580 °C for 1 hour each** — Precipitates fine, coherent **M₂C and MC carbides** from the supersaturated martensite, producing the famous **secondary hardening peak** of 62 – 65 HRC.

**3. Role of Alloying Elements (2 marks):**
- **W and Mo** → form M₆C and M₂C; promote red hardness; (Mo is a cheaper substitute for W).
- **Cr** → improves hardenability and forms M₂₃C₆.
- **V** → forms extremely hard VC (up to 2800 HV); enhances wear resistance.
- **C** → ensures sufficient carbide formation.

### Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks

| Grade | C (%) | W (%) | Mo (%) | Cr (%) | V (%) | Co (%) | Hardness (HRC) | Red Hardness |
|-------|-------|-------|--------|--------|-------|--------|----------------|--------------|
| **M2** | 0.85 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 2 | 0 | 62 – 64 | Good |
| **M35** | 0.85 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 2 | **5** | 63 – 65 | **Better** |
| **M42** | 1.10 | 1.5 | 9.5 | 4 | **1.15** | **8** | **65 – 68** | **Excellent** |

**Recommendation: M42 (2 marks)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> M42 is the **best choice** for cutting tool-steel workpieces because:
> 1. Its **higher Co (8 %)** gives the highest red hardness (≈ 70 HRC at 600 °C).
> 2. Its **higher V + Mo** content forms more wear-resistant carbides.
> 3. It is the industry-standard HSS for **tough machining of hard alloys** (e.g., tool steels, high-strength steels) and for **heavy-duty drills and taps**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted, 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** Classify **cast irons** with a neat diagram. Explain the production of **ductile (nodular) cast iron** with emphasis on the role of **magnesium** and the resulting microstructure. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A cast iron has the following composition: **C = 3.2 %, Si = 2.0 %, Mn = 0.5 %, P = 0.3 %, S = 0.06 %**. Calculate the **Carbon Equivalent (CE)** and hence determine (i) the type of cast iron, (ii) the **primary phase** that forms on solidification, and (iii) the **phases present at room temperature** in a slowly cooled casting. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Classification of Cast Irons (3 marks):**

Cast irons are classified based on the **form of carbon** present in the microstructure:

- **White Cast Iron** — Cementite (Fe₃C) — produced by rapid cooling.
- **Grey Cast Iron** — Flake graphite — produced by slow cooling of high-Si melt.
- **Malleable Cast Iron** — Tempered graphite rosettes — produced by annealing white CI.
- **Ductile (Nodular) Cast Iron** — Spheroidal graphite — produced by Mg treatment.
- **Alloy Cast Iron** — High Cr, Ni, or Si for special properties.

**2. Production of Ductile Cast Iron (3 marks):**

The melt is treated in the ladle with **0.02 – 0.05 % residual magnesium** (or cerium) just before pouring. The magnesium:
- **Desulphurizes** the melt (Mg + S → MgS slag).
- **Modifies the interfacial energy** between graphite and liquid, forcing graphite to grow as **spheres (nodules)** rather than flakes.
- A small amount of **inoculant (Fe-Si)** is added to promote nucleation of graphite spheres.

**3. Resulting Microstructure (1 mark):**

A **ferritic or pearlitic matrix** with **spheroidal graphite nodules** uniformly distributed. The spheroidal shape eliminates the stress-concentration effect of flakes, giving **high strength (up to 700 MPa) and good ductility (up to 20 % elongation)**.

### Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks

**Given data:** $C = 3.2$, $Si = 2.0$, $P = 0.3$

**Step 1: Calculate the Carbon Equivalent (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CE &= \%C + 0.3 \times (\%Si + \%P) \\
   &= 3.2 + 0.3 \times (2.0 + 0.3) \\
   &= 3.2 + 0.3 \times 2.3 \\
   &= 3.2 + 0.69 \\
CE &= \mathbf{3.89 \; wt\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Type of cast iron (2 marks):**

Since $CE = 3.89 \; wt\% \; < \; 4.3 \; wt\%$, the cast iron is **HYPOEUTECTIC**.

**Step 3: Primary phase on solidification (1.5 marks):**

The primary phase that crystallizes first from the liquid is **austenite (γ-iron) dendrites**.

**Step 4: Phases at room temperature (1.5 marks):**

For a slowly cooled hypoeutectic grey cast iron, the phases at room temperature are:
- **Pearlite (α + Fe₃C)** — matrix
- **Graphite flakes** — from the eutectic reaction
- Minor **ferrite** in regions adjacent to graphite (decarburized zones)

> [!TIP]
> A schematic cooling curve and the corresponding points on the Fe–Fe₃C diagram should be drawn for full marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Cementite (Fe₃C) with Graphite** — they are entirely different phases. Cementite is a *compound*; graphite is a *free element*. Many students write "carbon is present as cementite in grey cast iron", which is **wrong** — grey iron has **graphite flakes**, not cementite.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to include P in the CE formula** — the correct formula is $CE = \%C + 0.3(\%Si + \%P)$. Marks are lost if only Si is included.
>
> 3. **Stating "M2 HSS contains 18 % W"** — that is **T1**, not M2. M2 has 6 % W. Mixing up T-series and M-series is a common KTU trap.
>
> 4. **Not explaining the term "Secondary Hardening"** — examiners expect both the *definition* (peak hardness at 540 – 580 °C) AND the *mechanism* (precipitation of fine M₂C and MC carbides).
>
> 5. **Writing "ductile iron is stronger than steel"** — this is a generalization that is **false**. Ductile iron has strength comparable to *mild* steel, not to high-strength alloy steels.
>
> 6. **Omitting the role of Mg in ductile iron** — at least 2 marks are reserved for explaining the **spheroidizing mechanism** of magnesium.
>
> 7. **Saying "white cast iron is white in colour"** — it is named for the **white, fracture surface** caused by cementite, not for any colour property. Many students write vague answers here.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "High Speed Steels – Cast Irons":**
>
> **HIGH SPEED STEELS**
> ☐ Red hardness = ability to retain hardness at 500–650 °C.
> ☐ M2 composition: 0.85C – 6W – 5Mo – 4Cr – 2V (most common).
> ☐ T-series = W-based; M-series = Mo-based.
> ☐ M42 = M2 + 8 % Co = highest red hardness (≈ 70 HRC at 600 °C).
> ☐ Heat treatment: Preheat → Austenitize (1200–1300 °C) → Quench → Triple temper (540–580 °C).
> ☐ **Secondary hardening** = precipitation of M₂C + MC carbides during tempering.
> ☐ V forms the hardest carbide (VC, 2800 HV) → controls wear resistance.
> ☐ Applications: drills, taps, milling cutters, broaches, punches.
>
> **CAST IRONS**
> ☐ C = 2.5 – 4.0 %; Si = 1 – 3 %; total Fe–C–Si family.
> ☐ **CE = %C + 0.3(%Si + %P)**; eutectic CE = 4.3 %.
> ☐ Eutectic at 1147 °C: $L \rightarrow \gamma + \text{Graphite (or } Fe_3C\text{)}$.
> ☐ Eutectoid at 727 °C: $\gamma \rightarrow \alpha + Fe_3C$ (pearlite).
> ☐ **White CI** = Fe₃C structure, hard + brittle + abrasion-resistant.
> ☐ **Grey CI** = graphite flakes, soft + damping + machinable.
> ☐ **Malleable CI** = tempered graphite rosettes, ductile from white CI.
> ☐ **Ductile CI** = spheroidal graphite via Mg (0.02–0.05 %) treatment.
> ☐ **Alloy CI** = high Cr, Ni, Si for heat / corrosion / wear resistance.
> ☐ Applications: engine blocks (grey), pipes (ductile), crusher jaws (white).
>
> **KEY NUMBERS TO MEMORIZE**
> ☐ Eutectic: **4.3 % C @ 1147 °C**
> ☐ Eutectoid: **0.77 % C @ 727 °C**
> ☐ Solubility of C in α-Fe: **0.022 % @ 727 °C**
> ☐ Solubility of C in γ-Fe: **2.14 % @ 1147 °C**
> ☐ M2 tempering peak: **~580 °C → 62–65 HRC**
> ☐ Mg in ductile iron: **0.02–0.05 % residual**

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principal Non-Ferrous Alloys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT526 - METALLURGY AND MATERIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Principal Non-Ferrous Alloys

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principal Non-Ferrous Alloys** are engineering metallic materials in which the **base metal is not iron (Fe)**, and the alloying elements are deliberately added in controlled proportions to obtain specific combinations of mechanical, physical, chemical, and tribological properties such as high strength-to-weight ratio, corrosion resistance, electrical/thermal conductivity, and superior machinability.

Unlike ferrous alloys (steels and cast irons), non-ferrous alloys are generally **non-magnetic**, possess **superior corrosion resistance** in most environments, and are characterized by **lower density** and **higher cost per unit mass**. They are indispensable where ferrous metallurgy fails — for example, in marine atmospheres, aerospace structures, electrical transmission, and chemical process equipment.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus:** The syllabus specifically demands study of *principal* (commercially dominant) alloy systems based on **Copper (Cu), Aluminium (Al), Nickel (Ni), and a brief overview of Lead (Pb), Tin (Sn), Zinc (Zn), Titanium (Ti), and bearing alloys**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **Swiss Army knife** — the same handle (base metal) is fitted with different blades (alloying elements) for different jobs. A pocket knife for cutting rope (pure Al, soft & ductile), a saw for wood (Al-Cu alloy, hard), a corkscrew for wine (Cu-Zn alloy, decorative). 

Similarly, the **base metal** (Cu, Al, Ni) acts as the "handle" providing the dominant character, and **alloying additions** act as the "blades" tailoring properties:
- Adding **Zn to Cu** → makes brass (tough, ductile, used in cartridges)
- Adding **Sn to Cu** → makes bronze (corrosion resistant, used in marine propellers)
- Adding **Cu + Mg + Si to Al** → makes Duralumin (high strength, used in aircraft skins)
- Adding **Cu + Ni to Ni-base** → makes Monel (resists seawater corrosion)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric to Remember:**
> - Density of Al ≈ **2.7 g/cm³** (1/3 that of steel)
> - Density of Ti ≈ **4.5 g/cm³** (about 60% of steel, but twice as strong per kg)
> - Electrical conductivity of pure Cu ≈ **100% IACS** (International Annealed Copper Standard) — the benchmark for all conductors.

## 1.3 Classification of Principal Non-Ferrous Alloys

Non-ferrous alloys are classified primarily on the **basis of the base metal**, and secondarily on the **major alloying addition**.

| S.No. | Base Metal | Principal Alloy Systems | Typical Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Copper (Cu)** | Brasses, Bronzes, Cupronickels | Cartridge brass, Admiralty brass, Phosphor bronze, Gun metal |
| 2 | **Aluminium (Al)** | Wrought & Cast Al alloys | Duralumin, Y-alloy, Hiduminium, Al-Si (LM series) |
| 3 | **Nickel (Ni)** | Ni-Cu, Ni-Cr, Ni-Cr-Fe | Monel, Inconel, Nichrome, Invar |
| 4 | **Lead (Pb)** | Pb-Sb, Pb-Sn | Type metal, Solder, Lead-acid battery grids |
| 5 | **Tin (Sn)** | Sn-Sb-Cu, Sn-Pb | Babbitt metal, Pewter, Solders |
| 6 | **Zinc (Zn)** | Zn-Cu, Zn-Al | Die-casting alloys (Mazak), Brass substitutes |
| 7 | **Titanium (Ti)** | Ti-Al-V, Ti-Al-Sn | α-alloys, α-β alloys (Ti-6Al-4V) |
| 8 | **Magnesium (Mg)** | Mg-Al-Zn, Mg-Zn-Zr | Elektron, AZ91, ZK60 |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Copper and Its Alloys

### 2.1.1 Pure Copper (Commercially Pure, C11000 / C12200)

Pure copper is the benchmark electrical conductor. Two grades dominate the market:
- **ETP Copper (Electrolytic Tough Pitch):** Contains **0.02–0.04% O₂**, has **≥ 100% IACS** conductivity. Cannot be used in reducing atmospheres above 400°C due to *hydrogen embrittlement*.
- **OFHC Copper (Oxygen-Free High Conductivity):** Contains **< 0.001% O₂**, used in high-vacuum electronics and waveguides.

**Key Properties:**
- FCC crystal structure (ductile at all temperatures)
- Density: **8.96 g/cm³**
- Melting point: **1083°C**
- Electrical resistivity: **1.724 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m** at 20°C
- Cold-worked strength: UTS ≈ **220–250 MPa**; annealed: UTS ≈ **220 MPa**

### 2.1.2 Brasses (Cu-Zn Alloys)

Brasses are alloys of copper with **zinc as the principal alloying element** (5–45% Zn). The zinc atoms occupy substitutional positions in the Cu FCC lattice. As Zn content increases:

- **α-Brass** (up to 39% Zn): Single-phase FCC, ductile, cold-workable. Used for deep drawing, cartridge cases (70/30 brass).
- **α+β Brass** (39–45% Zn): Two-phase (FCC α + BCC β), harder, hot-workable. Used for hot forging, plumbing fittings.
- **β-Brass** (> 45% Zn): Single-phase BCC, brittle at room temperature, hot-workable only.

**Special Brasses (KTU High-Yield):**

| Alloy | Composition (wt%) | Distinctive Feature | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cartridge Brass** | 70 Cu – 30 Zn | Excellent deep-drawing | Bullet cartridges, shells |
| **Muntz Metal** | 60 Cu – 40 Zn | Hot-workable, contains β-phase | Ship sheathing, condenser tubes |
| **Admiralty Brass** | 69 Cu – 30 Zn – 1 Sn | Sn improves corrosion resistance in seawater | Marine condenser tubes |
| **Naval Brass** | 60 Cu – 39 Zn – 1 Sn | Higher strength than Admiralty | Marine hardware, propeller shafts |
| **Manganese Brass** | 70 Cu – 29 Zn – 1 Mn | High tensile strength | Valve stems, pump parts |
| **Nickel Silver** | 60 Cu – 20 Zn – 20 Ni | Silvery appearance, no Ag | Cutlery, decorative items |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Zinc?** Zinc lowers the melting point (Cu mp = 1083°C, Zn mp = 420°C), increases strength via solid solution strengthening, and imparts a golden colour proportional to Zn content.

### 2.1.3 Bronzes (Cu-Sn / Cu-Al / Cu-Si / Cu-Be)

Historically, "bronze" meant **Cu-Sn** alloys, but the term is now extended to **all Cu-based alloys EXCEPT brasses and cupronickels**.

- **Tin Bronze:** 5–12% Sn. The hardest and most corrosion-resistant among bronzes. Used for marine propellers, bearings, bells (bell metal = 20% Sn). Toughness decreases above 5% Sn due to brittle δ-phase.
- **Phosphor Bronze:** Cu + Sn + 0.1–0.5% P. P acts as a deoxidizer. Excellent fatigue resistance, used for springs, electrical contacts, gears.
- **Aluminium Bronze:** 5–12% Al. Highest strength among Cu alloys (UTS up to 700 MPa). Excellent seawater corrosion resistance. Used for ship propellers, pump impellers.
- **Silicon Bronze:** 3% Si + 1% Mn. Good weldability, used for chemical plant equipment.
- **Beryllium Bronze:** 2% Be. Can be **age-hardened** to UTS ≈ 1400 MPa — strongest Cu alloy. Non-sparking — used for tools in explosive environments. Toxic during processing.

### 2.1.4 Cupronickels (Cu-Ni Alloys)

- **Constantan (60 Cu – 40 Ni):** Near-zero temperature coefficient of resistance; used for strain gauges, thermocouples (Type J with Fe).
- **Monel (Cupronickel ≠ Monel — Monel is Ni-base, discussed later)**
- **75 Cu – 25 Ni + 0.5 Fe + 0.5 Mn:** Used for **coinage** (US 5-cent "nickel" coin).

## 2.2 Aluminium and Its Alloys

### 2.2.1 Why Aluminium?

Aluminium has a **density of 2.7 g/cm³** (about 1/3 that of steel) and an FCC structure, giving excellent formability. Its corrosion resistance comes from a thin, self-healing **Al₂O₃** layer (≈ 10 nm thick) that forms instantly on exposure to air.

- **Limitation:** Pure Al has very low strength (UTS ≈ 90 MPa). To be useful in structural applications, it must be alloyed.
- **Strengthening Mechanisms Used:** Solid solution, cold working, precipitation (age) hardening, dispersion strengthening.

### 2.2.2 Classification of Al Alloys

Al alloys are classified by the **Aluminum Association** using a 4-digit designation:

| Series | Major Alloying Element | Heat-Treatable? | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1xxx** | Pure Al (≥ 99%) | No | 1100 (foil, electrical busbars) |
| **2xxx** | Cu | Yes (age-hardenable) | 2014, 2024 (Duralumin) |
| **3xxx** | Mn | No | 3003 (cookware, beverage cans) |
| **4xxx** | Si | Yes (some) | 4043 (welding filler) |
| **5xxx** | Mg | No | 5083 (marine structures) |
| **6xxx** | Mg + Si | Yes | 6061, 6063 (architectural extrusions) |
| **7xxx** | Zn | Yes | 7075 (aircraft, high-strength) |
| **8xxx** | Others (Li, Fe) | — | 8090 (Al-Li aerospace) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Aluminium Alloys:**

### 2.2.3 Duralumin (Al-Cu-Mg-Mn System)

**Composition:** Al + 4% Cu + 0.5% Mg + 0.5% Mn (+ Si, Fe as impurities).

**Heat Treatment (Age Hardening, three stages):**
1. **Solution Treatment:** Heat to **495–505°C**, soak until all Cu dissolves into α-Al FCC solid solution.
2. **Quenching:** Rapidly cool in water → supersaturated solid solution (SSSS) of Cu in Al at room temperature. Soft and ductile.
3. **Ageing (Precipitation):** Hold at **150–200°C** for several hours → fine, coherent **θ″ (Al₂Cu)** precipitates form, blocking dislocation motion → **UTS jumps from ~200 MPa to ~400 MPa**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Duralumin is the workhorse of aircraft structures (Wrights brothers used it). The famous **"Duralumin controversy"** of 1906 involved Alfred Wilm discovering age hardening accidentally at the Royal Prussian Metallurgical Institute.

### 2.2.4 Y-Alloy (Al-Cu-Ni-Mg System)

**Composition:** Al + 4% Cu + 2% Ni + 1.5% Mg.

- Retains strength at **elevated temperatures up to 300°C** (better than Duralumin).
- Used for **pistons, cylinder heads, and crankcases** of IC engines (e.g., Rolls-Royce Merlin engine).
- Heat treatment: Solution treat at 520°C, quench, age at 200°C.

### 2.2.5 Other Important Al Alloys (KTU Favourites)

| Alloy | Composition (wt%) | Distinguishing Feature | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Hiduminium (R.R. alloys)** | Al-Cu-Ni-Mg-(Fe/Si) variants | R.R.50 = piston alloy, R.R.72 = cylinder head alloy | Aero-engine pistons |
| **LM-4 (Al-Si-Cu)** | Al + 5% Si + 3% Cu | Sand cast, general purpose | Crankcases, gear housings |
| **LM-6 (Al-Si)** | Al + 12% Si | Excellent fluidity, near-eutectic | Intricate castings, instrument cases |
| **LM-25 (Al-Si-Mg)** | Al + 7% Si + 0.3% Mg | Heat-treatable, weldable | Marine castings, aircraft fittings |
| **Magnalium** | Al + 5–30% Mg | Light + corrosion resistant + machinable | Aircraft parts, optical instruments |
| **Aluminium Bronzes** | Already covered under Cu | (See Cu-Al bronzes above) | Marine propellers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Modification of Al-Si Alloys:** Adding **0.05% Sodium (or Na-salt)** to hypereutectic Al-Si refines the coarse Si needles into fine spheroids → dramatically improves ductility and strength. Discovered by **Aladar Pacz in 1920**.

## 2.3 Nickel and Its Alloys

Pure nickel is FCC, ferromagnetic, with melting point **1453°C**. It forms the basis of **superalloys** for high-temperature service.

### 2.3.1 Monel (Ni-Cu System)

**Composition:** ~67% Ni + 30% Cu + small Fe, Mn, Si.

- **Properties:** Tough, ductile, **excellent resistance to seawater and HF acid**, non-magnetic.
- **Cannot be hardened by heat treatment** (Ni-Cu is a single-phase solid solution across the whole range).
- **Applications:** Propeller shafts, pump impellers, condenser tubes, chemical plant, musical instrument valves (e.g., French horns).

### 2.3.2 Inconel (Ni-Cr-Fe System)

**Composition:** ~76% Ni + 16% Cr + 8% Fe (Inconel 600); modern versions add Mo, Nb, Ti.

- **Properties:** Exceptional **oxidation resistance at temperatures up to 1000°C** due to Cr₂O₃ film. Retains strength in aggressive chemical environments.
- **Applications:** Gas turbine blades, rocket engine components, nuclear reactors, exhaust manifolds.

### 2.3.3 Nichrome (Ni-Cr System)

**Composition:** 80% Ni + 20% Cr.

- **Properties:** Highest electrical resistivity among common alloys (~**1.10 × 10⁻⁶ Ω·m**), forms stable Cr₂O₃ layer, withstands up to 1100°C in air.
- **Applications:** Heating elements in toasters, industrial furnaces, electric irons, hair dryers.

### 2.3.4 Invar (Ni-Fe System)

**Composition:** 64% Fe + 36% Ni.

- **Property:** Near-zero thermal expansion coefficient (~**1.2 × 10⁻⁶ /K**) up to 200°C.
- **Application:** Precision instruments, clock pendulums, bimetallic strips reference standards, LNG cargo tanks.

## 2.4 Other Important Non-Ferrous Alloys

### 2.4.1 Babbitt Metal (Bearing Alloys)

**White Babbitt (Tin-base):** 89% Sn + 7% Sb + 4% Cu.
**Lead-Babbitt (Lead-base):** 80% Pb + 15% Sb + 5% Sn.

- **Microstructure:** Soft matrix (Sn or Pb) with hard cuboids of **SbSn (or Cu₆Sn₅)** intermetallics.
- **Why?** The hard phase carries the load; the soft phase allows the bearing to conform to shaft misalignment and embed dust particles — a *self-lubricating* combination.
- **Application:** Plain bearings in automotive, marine, and turbine engines.

### 2.4.2 Solders

| Type | Composition | Melting Point | Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Soft solder (50/50) | 50% Sn + 50% Pb | 212°C | General plumbing, electronics |
| Soft solder (60/40) | 60% Sn + 40% Pb | 188°C | Electronics (eutectic, sharp mp) |
| Lead-free solder | 96.5% Sn + 3% Ag + 0.5% Cu | 217°C | RoHS-compliant electronics |
| Brazing solder (Brass) | 60% Cu + 40% Zn | ~900°C | Joining Cu, steel parts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Eutectic Significance:** The 60/40 Sn-Pb is a *eutectic composition* — it has the **lowest melting point (183°C eutectic vs 188°C for 60/40)** and solidifies at a single temperature, giving superior joint quality for electronic PCBs.

### 2.4.3 Titanium Alloys

- **CP Titanium (Grade 1–4):** Unalloyed, excellent corrosion resistance, used in chemical plants, biomedical implants.
- **Ti-6Al-4V (Grade 5):** The "workhorse" of Ti alloys. α-β microstructure. Used in **aircraft engine fans, compressor blades, airframes (Boeing 787 has 15% Ti), medical implants**.
- **Ti-10V-2Fe-3Al:** Near-β alloy, ultra-high strength, used in landing gear.

### 2.4.4 Magnesium Alloys

- **AZ91 (Mg-9Al-1Zn):** Most common cast Mg alloy, used in automotive steering wheels, laptop cases.
- **ZK60 (Mg-6Zn-0.5Zr):** High-strength wrought alloy.
- **Density: 1.74 g/cm³** — lightest structural metal. Limitation: highly flammable in chip form, requires inert-gas welding.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Density & Weight Saving Calculations**

$$\rho_{alloy} = \frac{1}{\sum_i \frac{w_i}{\rho_i}}$$

where $w_i$ is the mass fraction and $\rho_i$ the density of each constituent.

**Weight saving (%) compared to steel ($\rho_{steel} = 7.85$ g/cm³):**

$$\text{Weight Saving (\%)} = \left(1 - \frac{\rho_{alloy}}{7.85}\right) \times 100$$

**Electrical conductivity (% IACS):**

$$\% \text{ IACS} = \frac{\rho_{Cu}}{\rho_{alloy}} \times 100 = \frac{1.724 \times 10^{-8}}{\rho_{alloy}} \times 100$$

**Resistivity increase with alloying (Nordheim's rule, dilute limit):**

$$\rho_{alloy} = \rho_{pure} + C \cdot x(1-x)$$

where $x$ is the solute atom fraction and $C$ a constant. This explains why even small amounts of impurity drastically reduce Cu's conductivity.

**Young's Modulus & Specific Stiffness (key for aerospace selection):**

$$E_{specific} = \frac{E_{alloy}}{\rho_{alloy}}$$

| Alloy | Density $\rho$ (g/cm³) | Young's Modulus $E$ (GPa) | Specific Modulus $E/\rho$ (GPa·cm³/g) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Steel | 7.85 | 210 | 26.8 |
| Al-alloy (7075) | 2.81 | 72 | 25.6 |
| Ti-6Al-4V | 4.43 | 114 | 25.7 |
| Mg-alloy (AZ91) | 1.81 | 45 | 24.9 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Although aluminium, titanium, and magnesium have vastly different absolute stiffness, their **specific stiffness is roughly equal**. This is why aerospace engineers choose between them based on **strength, temperature, corrosion, and cost**, not on raw $E$.

**Brass Zn content vs colour (KTU board favourite):**

| Zn content | Colour | Phase |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0% | Red (pure Cu) | α |
| 5% | Reddish | α |
| 20% | Yellowish-red | α |
| 30% | Yellow (Cartridge brass) | α |
| 35% | Full yellow | α |
| 40% | Light yellow (Muntz) | α + β |
| 45% | Pale yellow | α + β |
| 50%+ | White | β (brittle) |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Density of Cartridge Brass (70 Cu – 30 Zn)

> **Problem:** Calculate the theoretical density of a 70 wt% Cu – 30 wt% Zn brass alloy. Given: $\rho_{Cu} = 8.96$ g/cm³, $\rho_{Zn} = 7.13$ g/cm³.

**Step 1 — Write the mixture rule for density (mass-weighted reciprocal):**

$$\frac{1}{\rho_{alloy}} = \frac{w_{Cu}}{\rho_{Cu}} + \frac{w_{Zn}}{\rho_{Zn}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the weight fractions:**

$$\frac{1}{\rho_{alloy}} = \frac{0.70}{8.96} + \frac{0.30}{7.13}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each term with full numerical accuracy:**

$$\frac{0.70}{8.96} = 0.078125 \;\; \text{g}^{-1}\!\cdot\!\text{cm}^{3}$$

$$\frac{0.30}{7.13} = 0.042076 \;\; \text{g}^{-1}\!\cdot\!\text{cm}^{3}$$

**Step 4 — Sum the contributions:**

$$\frac{1}{\rho_{alloy}} = 0.078125 + 0.042076 = 0.120201 \;\; \text{g}^{-1}\!\cdot\!\text{cm}^{3}$$

**Step 5 — Invert to obtain the alloy density:**

$$\rho_{alloy} = \frac{1}{0.120201} = 8.319 \;\; \text{g/cm}^{3}$$

**Step 6 — Compare with experimental value:**

Experimental density of 70/30 cartridge brass ≈ **8.53 g/cm³** (slightly higher because of lattice contraction when Zn dissolves in Cu — Vegard's-law deviation). The mixture rule is a *first-order approximation* valid for an ideal solution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the deviation?** The atomic radius of Zn (0.133 nm) is slightly larger than Cu (0.128 nm), and the FCC lattice parameter of the solid solution does not scale linearly with composition — a classic **Vegard's law** failure.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Weight Saving of Duralumin vs Mild Steel

> **Problem:** A wing component currently made of mild steel ($\rho = 7.85$ g/cm³) is to be redesigned in Duralumin ($\rho = 2.80$ g/cm³). If the component must maintain the same stiffness, calculate the **percentage weight saving** and the **percentage volume increase** required.

**Step 1 — Define the design constraint (constant stiffness, $k$):**

For a fixed geometry, $k = \frac{AE}{L}$. If we redesign with the same shape, we have to use **thicker sections** in Al to compensate for its lower $E$.

But for a simpler comparison assuming **equal volume of material replaced** (i.e., 1 cm³ of steel replaced by 1 cm³ of Duralumin):

$$\text{Weight Ratio} = \frac{\rho_{Al}}{\rho_{Fe}} = \frac{2.80}{7.85}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the weight ratio:**

$$\text{Weight Ratio} = 0.3567$$

**Step 3 — Compute percentage weight saving:**

$$\text{Weight Saving (\%)} = (1 - 0.3567) \times 100 = 64.33\%$$

So 1 cm³ of Duralumin weighs only 35.67% of the same volume of steel — a **64.33% weight saving**.

**Step 4 — For constant stiffness, the volume must increase inversely with $E$:**

$$\frac{V_{Al}}{V_{Fe}} = \frac{E_{Fe}}{E_{Al}} = \frac{210}{72} = 2.917$$

So the Al component must be **2.92× the volume** of the steel one (e.g., thicker ribs).

**Step 5 — Net weight saved even with increased volume:**

$$\frac{W_{Al}}{W_{Fe}} = \frac{\rho_{Al} \cdot V_{Al}}{\rho_{Fe} \cdot V_{Fe}} = \frac{2.80 \times 2.917}{7.85 \times 1} = \frac{8.167}{7.85} = 1.040$$

Hmm, in this simplified case, the Al redesign is actually **4% heavier** than the steel one! This shows that **stiffness-driven design is a poor fit for Al vs Steel**. However, for **strength-driven design** (yield strength) the comparison favours Al:

$$\frac{W_{Al}}{W_{Fe}} = \frac{\rho_{Al} \cdot (\sigma_{y,Fe}/\sigma_{y,Al})}{\rho_{Fe}} = \frac{2.80 \times (250/503)}{7.85} = 0.178$$

→ **82% weight saving for equal load capacity.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Aluminium is selected for **strength-critical, not stiffness-critical, applications**. This is why aircraft wings (stiffness-driven) use increasing amounts of **carbon-fibre composites** rather than Al, but fuselage skins (strength-driven) remain predominantly Al.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Age-Hardening of Duralumin (Quantitative Strength Contribution)

The yield strength contribution from precipitation hardening can be estimated by the **Orowan looping** mechanism:

$$\tau_{Orowan} = \frac{Gb}{L - 2r}$$

where $G$ is shear modulus, $b$ is Burgers vector, $L$ is inter-precipitate spacing, $r$ is precipitate radius.

For a coherent θ″ platelet in Duralumin:
- $G_{Al} \approx 26$ GPa
- $b = 0.286$ nm
- Typical spacing $L \approx 30$ nm
- $r \approx 3$ nm

$$\tau_{Orowan} = \frac{26 \times 10^{9} \cdot 0.286 \times 10^{-9}}{30 \times 10^{-9} - 6 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$= \frac{7.436 \times 10^{-9}}{24 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.310 \;\; \text{GPa} = 310 \;\; \text{MPa}$$

Adding the matrix contribution (~50 MPa) gives a yield strength ≈ **360 MPa**, matching the experimentally observed value of ~400 MPa for peak-aged Duralumin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Over-ageing:** When precipitates grow too large (Ostwald ripening), $L$ increases rapidly, $\tau_{Orowan}$ drops, and the alloy *softens*. This is why **service temperature control** is critical for age-hardened Al alloys in aerospace (e.g., Concorde's airframe had thermal limits).

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix: When to Choose Which Alloy?

| Property Required | Best Choice | Reasoning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Maximum electrical conductivity** | Pure Cu (ETP / OFHC) | 100% IACS, no solute scattering |
| **Cheapest corrosion-resistant structural alloy** | Admiralty brass (Cu-Zn-Sn) | Tin addition resists dezincification in seawater |
| **Maximum strength in Cu-alloy** | Beryllium bronze (Cu-2% Be, age-hardened) | UTS ≈ 1400 MPa, non-sparking |
| **Lightest strong structural part** | 7075 Al alloy (Al-Zn-Mg-Cu) | UTS ≈ 570 MPa at 2.81 g/cm³ |
| **High-temperature strength > 500°C** | Inconel / Nimonic (Ni-base) | γ′ precipitation, Cr₂O₃ film |
| **Heating element wire** | Nichrome (80 Ni – 20 Cr) | High ρ, oxidation resistance to 1100°C |
| **Zero thermal expansion (instruments)** | Invar (64 Fe – 36 Ni) | $\alpha \approx 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$/K |
| **Seawater pump, propeller shaft** | Monel (Ni-Cu) | Resists HF, chlorides, biofouling |
| **Seawater propeller (high load)** | Aluminium bronze (Cu-Al-Fe-Ni) | UTS ≈ 700 MPa, resists cavitation |
| **Aerospace structural skin** | Duralumin / 2024 Al | Age-hardenable, fatigue-resistant |
| **IC engine piston** | Y-alloy / LM13 (Al-Cu-Ni-Mg) | Retains strength to 300°C |
| **Plain bearing (high load)** | White Babbitt (Sn-Sb-Cu) | Soft matrix + hard intermetallics |
| **Lead-acid battery grid** | Pb-Ca-Sn or Pb-Sb | Ca addition reduces gassing |
| **Aerospace fan blade (high-temp, low-density)** | Ti-6Al-4V | $\rho = 4.43$ g/cm³, UTS = 950 MPa, mp ≈ 1660°C |
| **Lowest density structural part** | Mg-alloy AZ91 / ZK60 | $\rho = 1.74$ g/cm³ |

## 3.5 Selection Logic Flow for Non-Ferrous Alloys (Engineering Decision Tree)

```
Q1: Is corrosion resistance the PRIMARY requirement?
   ├── YES → Marine/chemical environment?
   │         ├── YES → Cu-Ni / Monel / Aluminium bronze
   │         └── NO (chemical plant) → Inconel / Hastelloy / Ti
   └── NO  → Q2

Q2: Is HIGH strength-to-weight the priority?
   ├── YES → Service temperature?
   │         ├── < 200°C → 7075 Al / Duralumin
   │         ├── 200–400°C → Ti-6Al-4V
   │         └── > 600°C → Ni-base superalloys
   └── NO  → Q3

Q3: Is it an ELECTRICAL or THERMAL application?
   ├── Electrical conductor → Pure Cu (ETP) or Al (busbars, weight-sensitive)
   ├── Heating element → Nichrome (Ni-20Cr)
   └── Low thermal expansion → Invar (Fe-36Ni)

Q4: Is it a TRIBOLOGICAL application (bearing)?
   └── YES → Soft Babbitt on hardened steel shell

Q5: Is the cost the deciding factor?
   └── YES → Zn die-casting alloy (Mazak) or leaded brass
```
