```mermaid
    q1 --> q3[High Conductivity]
    q1 --> q4[High Temperature]
    q1 --> q5[Corrosion Resistance]
    q1 --> q6[High Strength]
    q2 --> q2a[Mg AZ91 1.74 g per cm3]
    q2 --> q2b[Al 7075 2.81 g per cm3]
    q3 --> q3a[Cu ETP 100 percent IACS]
    q3 --> q3b[Al 1100 61 percent IACS]
    q4 --> q4a[Nichrome to 1100 C]
    q4 --> q4b[Inconel to 1000 C]
    q4 --> q4c[Ti to 600 C]
    q5 --> q5a[Monel seawater]
    q5 --> q5b[Titanium aggressive chemicals]
    q5 --> q5c[Al Al2O3 passive film]
    q6 --> q6a[Be Cu 1400 MPa]
    q6 --> q6b[Ti 6Al 4V 950 MPa]
    q6 --> q6c[7075 Al 570 MPa]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**"List the composition, properties, and TWO typical applications of Duralumin."**

**Model Answer (Concise, 3-mark depth):**

**Composition:** Al + 4% Cu + 0.5% Mg + 0.5% Mn (+ minor Si, Fe, Zn).

**Properties:**
- Density ≈ **2.78 g/cm³** (about 1/3 that of steel)
- UTS after age-hardening ≈ **400–500 MPa** (vs. 90 MPa for pure Al)
- Good fatigue resistance, moderate corrosion resistance
- Excellent machinability and formability in the solution-treated (T4) condition

**Two Applications:**
1. **Aircraft structural skins, ribs, and rivets** (Duralumin is the original "aircraft alloy" used in Junkers J 1 and later airframes).
2. **Automotive and truck body panels, propellers, and high-speed rotating components** where high strength-to-weight ratio is required.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write only "aircraft parts" without naming a specific component (e.g., "wing ribs" instead of "aircraft"). Examiners expect specificity — **name at least one sub-component**.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
**"Differentiate between brass and bronze with two points of distinction."**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis of Distinction | Brass | Bronze |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Principal alloying element** | Copper + **Zinc** (Cu-Zn) | Copper + **Tin** (Cu-Sn), or Cu-Al, Cu-Si, Cu-Be |
| **Colour** | Yellow (proportional to Zn content) | Reddish-brown (originally Cu-Sn) |
| **Cost** | Cheaper (Zn is less costly than Sn) | More expensive |
| **Melting point** | Lower (920–950°C for 70/30 brass) | Higher (≈ 1000°C for 5% Sn bronze) |
| **Corrosion resistance** | Moderate; susceptible to **dezincification** in seawater | Excellent, especially in marine environments |
| **Typical uses** | Cartridge cases, plumbing fittings, decorative items | Bearings, marine propellers, bell metal, springs |

**Two crisp distinctions to write in the exam:**
1. Brass = Cu-Zn; Bronze = Cu-Sn (or other non-Zn alloying).
2. Brass is cheaper and has lower melting point; bronze is more corrosion resistant and has higher strength.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question is 14 marks, with sub-parts typically (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. *Answer ANY ONE* of the two full-question choices.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat Cu-Zn phase diagram, explain the different types of brasses formed at various zinc compositions. List the composition and two applications of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the composition, heat treatment, and properties of **Y-alloy**. Mention its specific applications in the aircraft and automobile industry. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Brasses & Cu-Zn Phase Diagram (7 marks)

The **Cu-Zn phase diagram** shows five invariant reactions but the technologically relevant region is 0–50% Zn. The diagram can be sketched as:

```
   Temperature (°C)
    1083 ┤● Cu (mp)
         │ \
         │  \  α (FCC)
         │   \____________
         │   α/(α+β)     \
     902 ┤   ●  α/(α+β′)  \
         │   / \           \____ β (BCC) → α
     750 ┤  /   \               /
         │ /     α+β region    /
     500 ┤/                   /
         │                   /
         ●  β'              /
         │                 /
     420 ┤● Zn (mp)
         └───────────────────────────────
         0      20      39     45    50
                    % Zn
```

**Phase regions:**

| Zn Content | Phase | Name | Property | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0–39% | α (FCC) | **α-Brass** | Ductile, cold-workable, single-phase | Cartridge brass (30% Zn) — shells, deep drawing |
| 39–45% | α + β′ | **α-β Brass** (Muntz) | Two-phase, harder, hot-workable | Muntz metal (40% Zn) — ship sheathing, condenser plates |
| 45–50% | β (BCC) | **β-Brass** | Brittle at RT, hot-workable | Limited — special brazing alloys |

**Special brasses (composition & applications):**
- **Cartridge brass (70 Cu – 30 Zn):** Cartridge cases, radiator cores, lamp fixtures.
- **Muntz metal (60 Cu – 40 Zn):** Marine sheathing, condenser tubes, valve stems.
- **Admiralty brass (69 Cu – 30 Zn – 1 Sn):** Condenser tubes handling seawater.
- **Naval brass (60 Cu – 39 Zn – 1 Sn):** Marine hardware, propeller shafts.

**Marking Distribution (7 m):** Phase diagram sketch (3 m) + classification table with examples (2 m) + special brasses (2 m).

---

#### Part (b) — Y-Alloy (7 marks)

**Composition:** Al + 4% Cu + 2% Ni + 1.5% Mg (with possible minor additions of Fe, Si, Mn, Ti).

**Heat Treatment Sequence:**

1. **Solution Treatment:** Heat to **525–540°C**, hold for several hours. All Cu and Ni₂Al intermetallics dissolve into the FCC α-Al matrix.
2. **Quenching:** Hot water quench (not cold — to avoid distortion) → **Supersaturated solid solution (SSSS)**.
3. **Ageing / Precipitation:** Hold at **200°C for 16 hours** → fine precipitates of **CuAl₂ (θ) and NiAl₃ (β′)** form → peak hardness.

**Properties:**
- Density: **2.76 g/cm³** (light)
- UTS: **350–400 MPa** in peak-aged (T6) condition
- **Retains strength at elevated temperatures up to 300°C** (key advantage over Duralumin)
- Good fatigue resistance and low thermal expansion
- Lower ductility than Duralumin

**Applications:**
- **Aircraft pistons** (e.g., Rolls-Royce Merlin engine pistons in WWII Spitfires)
- **Diesel engine pistons, cylinder heads, and crankcases**
- **High-temperature structural components in aero-engines**
- **R.R. 50 and R.R. 72** (Hiduminium variants) used in Rolls-Royce cylinder heads and pistons.

**Marking Distribution (7 m):** Composition (1 m) + heat treatment (3 m) + properties & applications (3 m).

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam - May 2022]**

**(a)** Explain **age hardening** (precipitation hardening) with reference to Duralumin. Sketch and label the characteristic strength-vs-time ageing curve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the composition, distinctive properties, and applications of **Monel metal, Nichrome, and Invar**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Age Hardening of Duralumin (7 marks)

**Principle:** Age hardening (precipitation hardening) is a heat-treatment process that increases the strength of certain alloys by the controlled formation of extremely fine, uniformly dispersed second-phase particles within the original phase matrix.

**Three-Step Process (in Duralumin — Al-4% Cu):**

1. **Solution Treatment:** Heat the alloy to **495–505°C** (just below the eutectic temperature of 548°C) and hold for ~1 hour. All Cu dissolves to form a uniform α-solid solution.

2. **Quenching:** Rapidly cool in water to room temperature. Cu atoms are *frozen* in solution, producing a **supersaturated solid solution (SSSS)**. The alloy is now soft and ductile.

3. **Ageing:** Reheat to **150–200°C** for 4–16 hours OR hold at room temperature for several days (*natural ageing*). Fine, coherent precipitates of **CuAl₂ (θ″ → θ′ → θ)** form.

**Strength vs Time Curve:**

```
   Strength (MPa)
       │            ← over-aged (coarsened)
   450 │           /\
       │          /  \
   400 │         /    \  ← peak hardness
       │        /      \
   300 │       /        \
       │      /          \  ← softening
   200 │     /            \
       │    /  ← under-aged
   100 │___/_____________________ 
       0   2h  6h  12h  24h   Time
            ↑
        Quench + Ageing Time
```

**Key Stages:**
- **Under-ageing** (early): GP zones form (Guinier-Preston zones — clusters of Cu atoms on {100} planes).
- **Peak-ageing:** Coherent **θ″ platelets** form — maximum strengthening (UTS ≈ 470 MPa for 2024 Al).
- **Over-ageing:** θ″ → θ′ (semi-coherent) → θ CuAl₂ (incoherent). Strength drops as inter-particle spacing increases.

**Mechanism of Strengthening:** The fine precipitates block dislocation motion via:
- **Particle shearing** (small, coherent precipitates)
- **Orowan looping** (larger, incoherent precipitates)

**Marking Distribution (7 m):** Process description (3 m) + curve sketch with labels (2 m) + mechanism/strengthening explanation (2 m).

---

#### Part (b) — Monel, Nichrome, Invar (7 marks)

**1. Monel Metal:**
- **Composition:** ~67% Ni + 30% Cu + 1.5% Fe + 1% Mn (small Si, C, S).
- **Properties:** Tough, ductile, **excellent resistance to corrosion in seawater, hydrofluoric acid, and alkalis**. Non-magnetic. Cannot be hardened by heat treatment (single-phase solid solution).
- **Applications:** Propeller shafts, pump impellers, condenser tubes, valve trim, musical instrument valves (e.g., French horns, tubas), chemical plant equipment.

**2. Nichrome:**
- **Composition:** 80% Ni + 20% Cr (with traces of Fe, Mn).
- **Properties:** **High electrical resistivity** (~110 μΩ·cm) — about **65× that of pure Cu**. Forms a stable, adherent **Cr₂O₃** layer that protects against oxidation up to **1100°C** in air. Does not burn or scale at high temperatures.
- **Applications:** **Heating elements** in toasters, electric irons, hair dryers, industrial resistance furnaces, and rheostats.

**3. Invar:**
- **Composition:** 64% Fe + 36% Ni.
- **Properties:** **Coefficient of thermal expansion ≈ 1.2 × 10⁻⁶ /K** between 0–200°C — among the **lowest of any metallic alloy** (about 1/10 that of steel). Magnetostrictive behaviour.
- **Applications:** Precision measuring instruments, **bimetallic thermostat strips** (passive element), clock pendulums, **LNG cargo tanks** (where dimensional stability is critical at cryogenic temperatures), optical and laser instrument mounts.

**Marking Distribution (7 m):** Each alloy: Composition (0.5 m) + Properties (1 m) + Applications (1 m) — allocate 1 mark for diagram/comparative summary table.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Places Students Lose Marks in Non-Ferrous Alloy Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Monel with Cupronickel.** Monel is a *Ni-base* alloy (67 Ni – 30 Cu); cupronickel is a *Cu-base* alloy (75 Cu – 25 Ni). Examiners *immediately* deduct 1 mark.
> 2. **Stating "Duralumin contains 4% copper" without units or context.** Write "**4 wt% Cu**" and mention the **ageing temperature (~180°C)** for full credit.
> 3. **Drawing a Cu-Zn diagram but not labelling the α/(α+β) boundary near 39% Zn.** This is the most tested point; forget it, lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Omitting the heat-treatment step (Solution → Quench → Age)** in age-hardening answers. Examiners allocate 3 marks just to the *process* — never write only "it is heated and cooled".
> 5. **Writing "Inconel is corrosion resistant" without specifying the temperature or mechanism.** Add: "forms **Cr₂O₃ passive film**; stable up to **1000°C**."
> 6. **Forgetting density in the answer.** Many "list properties" questions expect density. A quick bullet "**ρ = 8.96 g/cm³**" is worth 0.5 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Principal Non-Ferrous Alloys (Module 3)**

**Brasses (Cu-Zn):**
- α-brass: < 39% Zn, FCC, cold-workable, ductile, golden-yellow
- α-β brass: 39–45% Zn, hot-workable (Muntz metal at 40% Zn)
- Cartridge brass: 70/30, deep-drawing
- Admiralty brass: 1% Sn for seawater condenser tubes
- Naval brass: 60/39/1, high-strength marine hardware
- Nickel silver: 60-20-20, decorative, no Ag

**Bronzes (Cu-Sn / Cu-Al / Cu-Be):**
- Tin bronze: 5–12% Sn, marine, bearings
- Phosphor bronze: + 0.1–0.5% P, springs, contacts
- Aluminium bronze: 5–12% Al, UTS up to 700 MPa, propellers
- Beryllium bronze: 2% Be, age-hardenable to UTS ≈ 1400 MPa, non-sparking tools

**Cupronickels (Cu-Ni):**
- Constantan (60 Cu – 40 Ni), near-zero resistance-temperature coefficient, strain gauges
- 75-25 cupronickel, coinage

**Aluminium Alloys:**
- 1xxx pure, 2xxx Al-Cu (Duralumin, age-hardenable), 5xxx Al-Mg (marine), 6xxx Al-Mg-Si (extrusions), 7xxx Al-Zn (highest strength, 7075)
- **Duralumin:** 4% Cu, 0.5% Mg, 0.5% Mn; solution @ 500°C, quench, age @ 180°C
- **Y-alloy:** Al-Cu-Ni-Mg, retains strength to 300°C, **pistons**
- **LM-6:** Al-12Si, eutectic modification with **Na** for cast instrument cases
- **Modification:** Na added to Al-Si refines coarse Si plates → eutectic shift improves ductility

**Nickel Alloys:**
- **Monel:** 67 Ni – 30 Cu, seawater resistance, propellers, non-magnetic
- **Inconel:** Ni-Cr-Fe, Cr₂O₃ film, high-T (1000°C), turbines
- **Nichrome:** 80 Ni – 20 Cr, heating elements, ρ ≈ 110 μΩ·cm
- **Invar:** 64 Fe – 36 Ni, **lowest thermal expansion (1.2 × 10⁻⁶/K)**, precision instruments

**Titanium:**
- CP-Ti (Gr 1–4), Ti-6Al-4V (Gr 5, workhorse), α + β microstructure
- **Aerospace skins, compressor blades, biomedical implants**

**Magnesium:**
- AZ91 (cast), ZK60 (wrought), density 1.74 g/cm³, **lightest structural**
- Flammable, inert-gas welding required

**Bearing Alloys:**
- White Babbitt: 89 Sn – 7 Sb – 4 Cu, soft matrix + hard SbSn intermetallics
- Microstructure essential for self-lubricating behaviour

**Solders:**
- 60/40 Sn-Pb eutectic (mp 183°C), 96.5/3/0.5 Sn-Ag-Cu lead-free

**Key Numbers to Memorize:**
- Density of pure Cu: **8.96 g/cm³**
- Density of pure Al: **2.71 g/cm³**
- Density of Ti-6Al-4V: **4.43 g/cm³**
- Melting point of Cu: **1083°C**
- Melting point of Al: **660°C**
- 100% IACS resistivity: **1.724 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m**
- Max UTS Be-bronze: **~1400 MPa**
- Max UTS Ti-6Al-4V: **~950 MPa**
- Max UTS 7075-T6 Al: **~570 MPa**
- Invar expansion coefficient: **1.2 × 10⁻⁶/K**
- Nichrome resistivity: **~110 μΩ·cm**
- Solution treatment of Duralumin: **~500°C**
- Ageing of Duralumin: **150–200°C**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fatigue - Creep - DBTT - Super Plasticity - Need
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT526 - METALLURGY AND MATERIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fatigue - Creep - DBTT - Super Plasticity - Need

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fatigue (Cyclic Loading Failure)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024):**
Fatigue is the progressive, localized structural damage and failure of a material subjected to fluctuating stresses and strains — typically **below the yield strength** of the material — over a sufficient number of cycles. The phenomenon is governed by the initiation and sub-critical propagation of a crack until final fracture occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition:** Fatigue failure is a *brittle-appearing* failure that occurs **without warning** at a stress level significantly below the **ultimate tensile strength (UTS)** and often below the yield strength.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine bending a paper clip back and forth. The first few bends are easy. After 10–20 bends, you hear a tiny "tick" — that is a micro-crack forming. After 30 bends, the clip snaps suddenly. The paper clip never experienced a single high load, yet it fractured. This is **fatigue** — *death by a thousand small cycles*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **Endurance Limit (Se):** Maximum stress below which the material can survive an *infinite* number of cycles (≈ **10⁶ to 10⁷ cycles**).
> - **Fatigue Life (Nf):** Total number of cycles to failure at a given stress amplitude.
> - **Stress Range (Δσ), Stress Amplitude (σa), Mean Stress (σm).**

---

## 1.2 Creep (Time-Dependent Deformation)

**Formal Definition:**
Creep is the **time-dependent permanent (plastic) deformation** of a material when subjected to a **constant load or stress** at a **constant elevated temperature** (typically T > **0.4 Tm**, where Tm is the melting point in Kelvin).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a candle left on a hot summer day. Slowly, the candle leans and sags — even though nothing physical is pushing it down beyond its own weight. The wax is *creeping* under the constant force of gravity. The same happens to jet engine turbine blades, boiler tubes, and nuclear reactor components operating at high temperatures for thousands of hours.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Point:** Creep is *time-dependent*, *temperature-dependent*, and *diffusion-controlled*. It does **not** occur at room temperature for most metals (with the exception of lead, solder, and polymers).

---

## 1.3 DBTT — Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature

**Formal Definition:**
DBTT is the **temperature below which a material (typically BCC metals like ferritic steels) transitions from ductile (high-energy absorbing) fracture to brittle (low-energy absorbing, cleavage-type) fracture** when subjected to impact or dynamic loading. It is a **critical design parameter** for components operating in cold environments.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A chocolate bar behaves very differently when frozen vs. at room temperature. When frozen, it snaps like glass (brittle). When warm, it bends and deforms (ductile). BCC steels behave similarly — they become brittle when the temperature drops below a critical threshold.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Test:** Charpy V-Notch Impact Test (ASTM E23).
> **Key Temperatures Marked on the Transition Curve:**
> - **USE** (Upper Shelf Energy)
> - **LSE** (Lower Shelf Energy)
> - **NDT** (Nil Ductility Temperature)
> - **FATT** (Fracture Appearance Transition Temperature — at 50% cleavage)

---

## 1.4 Superplasticity

**Formal Definition:**
Superplasticity is the exceptional ability of certain fine-grained polycrystalline materials to undergo **extremely large uniform tensile elongations (typically > 200%, often > 1000%)** prior to failure, under specific conditions of temperature, strain rate, and microstructure.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine chewing gum. You can stretch it slowly to many times its original length before it breaks. A superplastic metal behaves like a very slow, hot, fine-grained version of chewing gum — it can be deformed into incredibly complex shapes without cracking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Mandatory Conditions for Superplasticity:**
> 1. **Grain size < 10 μm** (preferably < 5 μm, equiaxed).
> 2. **Temperature T > 0.5 Tm** (above half the absolute melting point).
> 3. **Strain rate 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻³ s⁻¹** (slow forming).

---

## 1.5 The "Need" — Why Study These Phenomena?

| Phenomenon | Engineering Need | Real-World Consequence |
|------------|------------------|------------------------|
| **Fatigue** | Rotating shafts, aircraft wings, bridges experience millions of cycles | Air France Flight 447 (2011), Aloha Airlines Flight 243 (1988) |
| **Creep** | Gas turbines, boiler tubes, nuclear reactors operate at high T for years | Power plant boiler tube rupture, turbine blade elongation |
| **DBTT** | Arctic pipelines, ship hulls, LNG tanks operate in cold | Liberty Ships (WWII) hull fractures in North Atlantic |
| **Superplasticity** | Need for complex-shaped aerospace & automotive parts with low cost | Ti-6Al-4V superplastic forming of aircraft panels |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 FATIGUE — Theory & Failure Mechanism

### Stages of Fatigue Failure (3 Stages):
1. **Crack Initiation** (~90% of total life in high-cycle fatigue)
   - Slip bands, intrusions, extrusions on persistent slip bands (PSBs).
   - Usually at surface defects, inclusions, or stress concentrators.
2. **Crack Propagation** (Stage II — stable growth)
   - Crack advances incrementally per cycle (Paris Law).
   - Striations visible on fracture surface.
3. **Final Fracture** (Stage III — unstable)
   - When crack reaches critical size (K → KIC), rapid brittle failure.

### S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve):

```
σ (Stress) |\
           | \
           |  \   σe = Endurance Limit
           |   ●─────────── (S-N becomes horizontal for steels)
           |    \____
           |         \_____
           |               ●
           |________________●__________→ N (Cycles, log scale)
                  10⁶        10⁷       10⁸
```

### The S-N Equation (Basquin's Law):
$$\sigma_{a} = \sigma'_{f} \,(2 N_{f})^{b}$$

where:
- $\sigma_{a}$ = stress amplitude
- $\sigma'_{f}$ = fatigue strength coefficient
- $N_{f}$ = cycles to failure
- $b$ = fatigue strength exponent (typically −0.05 to −0.12)

### Coffin-Manson (Low-Cycle Fatigue — Strain-Based):
$$\frac{\Delta \varepsilon_{p}}{2} = \varepsilon'_{f} \,(2 N_{f})^{c}$$

where $c$ ≈ −0.5 to −0.7.

### Paris Law (Crack Growth):
$$\frac{da}{dN} = C (\Delta K)^{m}$$

where $\Delta K = Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$

---

## 2.2 CREEP — Theory & Stages

### Three Stages of Creep Curve:

| Stage | Name | Strain Rate Behavior |
|-------|------|----------------------|
| I | Primary (Transient) Creep | Decreasing strain rate — work hardening |
| II | Secondary (Steady-State) Creep | **Constant minimum strain rate** — longest stage, design-relevant |
| III | Tertiary Creep | Accelerating strain rate — necking, void formation → rupture |

### Primary Creep Equation (Andrade):
$$\varepsilon = \beta \, t^{1/3}$$

### Steady-State (Norton-Bailey) Creep:
$$\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = A \, \sigma^{n} \, \exp\left(\frac{-Q}{RT}\right)$$

where:
- $A$ = material constant
- $\sigma$ = applied stress
- $n$ = stress exponent (3–8 for dislocation creep, 1–2 for diffusional creep)
- $Q$ = activation energy for self-diffusion
- $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K) — **Universal Gas Constant**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

### Larson-Miller Parameter (for rupture life):
$$LMP = T \, (\log t_{r} + C)$$

where $C$ ≈ 20 (for steels), $t_{r}$ in hours, $T$ in Kelvin.

---

## 2.3 DBTT — Theory & Influencing Factors

### Factors Increasing DBTT (Brittle Tendency):
- **Lower temperature**
- **Higher strain rate** (impact loading)
- **Triaxial stress state** (notch)
- **Larger grain size**
- **Higher carbon content** (in steels)
- **Neutron irradiation** (reactor pressure vessels)

### Factors Decreasing DBTT (Ductile Improvement):
- **Refining grain size** (Hall-Petch effect)
- **Lower carbon content**
- **Adding Ni, Mn** (austenite stabilizers)
- **Lower strain rate**

### Ductile-to-Brittle Transition for Different Crystal Structures:
- **BCC metals:** Exhibit strong DBTT (e.g., α-Fe, Cr, Mo, W)
- **FCC metals:** No sharp DBTT (remain ductile even at very low T) — e.g., Cu, Al, Ni, austenitic SS
- **HCP metals:** Low-temperature brittleness due to limited slip systems — e.g., Mg, Zn

### Charpy Energy-Temperature Curve:

```
Energy (J) |         ● USE (Upper Shelf)
           |        /│
           |       / │
           |      /  │   Transition Zone
           |     /   │
           |    /    │
           |   /     │
           |  /      │
           | /       │
           |/        │● LSE (Lower Shelf)
           |_________●_____________→ T (°C)
                NDT  FATT(50%)
```

---

## 2.4 SUPERPLASTICITY — Theory

### Strain Rate Sensitivity Index (m):
The key parameter — when **m > 0.3**, superplasticity occurs.
$$\sigma = K \, \dot{\varepsilon}^{m}$$

$$\log \sigma = \log K + m \log \dot{\varepsilon}$$

### Constitutive Equation:
$$\dot{\varepsilon} = A \, \frac{D \cdot G \cdot b}{kT} \left(\frac{b}{d}\right)^{p} \left(\frac{\sigma}{G}\right)^{n}$$

where d = grain size, p = grain size exponent (≈ 2 for grain boundary sliding).

### Mechanism: **Grain Boundary Sliding (GBS)**
- Grains slide past each other like a deck of cards.
- Accommodated by diffusion (Coble creep) or dislocation movement.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Name / Use | Variables |
|---|---------|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | $\sigma_{a} = \sigma'_{f}(2N_f)^{b}$ | Basquin's Law (HCF) | Stress amplitude vs life |
| 2 | $\dfrac{\Delta\varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon'_f(2N_f)^c$ | Coffin-Manson (LCF) | Plastic strain vs life |
| 3 | $\dfrac{da}{dN} = C(\Delta K)^{m}$ | Paris Law | Crack growth rate |
| 4 | $\Delta K = Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Stress Intensity Range | Y = geometry factor |
| 5 | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = A\sigma^n \exp(-Q/RT)$ | Norton-Bailey (Steady Creep) | Steady-state creep rate |
| 6 | $LMP = T(\log t_r + C)$ | Larson-Miller Parameter | Rupture life prediction |
| 7 | $\sigma = K \dot{\varepsilon}^{m}$ | Superplastic flow | m > 0.3 → superplastic |
| 8 | $\varepsilon_f \propto d^{-1/2}$ | Hall-Petch (DBTT) | Grain size effect |
| 9 | $\Delta\sigma = \sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}$ | Stress Range | Fatigue loading |
| 10 | $R = \sigma_{min}/\sigma_{max}$ | Stress Ratio | R = −1 fully reversed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When solving numerical problems on creep, always convert temperature to **Kelvin** (add 273 to °C) and use **R = 8.314 J/mol·K**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem #1: Basquin's Law Application

**Problem (KTU Pattern):**
A steel shaft has fatigue strength coefficient $\sigma'_f = 1000$ MPa and exponent $b = -0.1$. The component is subjected to fully-reversed loading. Calculate the fatigue life for a stress amplitude of $\sigma_a = 350$ MPa.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Given:**
- $\sigma'_f = 1000$ MPa
- $b = -0.1$
- $\sigma_a = 350$ MPa

**Equation:**
$$\sigma_{a} = \sigma'_{f} \,(2 N_{f})^{b}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the known values:
$$350 = 1000 \times (2 N_{f})^{-0.1}$$

**Step 2:** Divide both sides by 1000:
$$\frac{350}{1000} = (2 N_{f})^{-0.1}$$
$$0.35 = (2 N_{f})^{-0.1}$$

**Step 3:** Take natural log of both sides:
$$\ln(0.35) = -0.1 \times \ln(2 N_{f})$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate:
$$-1.0498 = -0.1 \times \ln(2 N_{f})$$
$$\ln(2 N_{f}) = \frac{-1.0498}{-0.1} = 10.498$$

**Step 5:** Exponentiate:
$$2 N_{f} = e^{10.498} = 36{,}326$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $N_f$:
$$N_{f} = \frac{36{,}326}{2} \approx 18{,}163 \text{ cycles}$$

**Final Answer:** $N_f \approx 1.82 \times 10^4$ cycles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This is **low-cycle fatigue** regime (< 10⁵ cycles). For high-cycle fatigue, the stress amplitude must be below the endurance limit.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem #2: Steady-State Creep Rate

**Problem:**
A nickel-based superalloy turbine blade operates at $T = 900°C$ under stress $\sigma = 100$ MPa. The Norton-Bailey constants are:
$A = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$ (MPa units), $n = 5$, $Q = 280$ kJ/mol.
Calculate the steady-state creep rate.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Given:**
- $T = 900 + 273 = 1173$ K
- $\sigma = 100$ MPa
- $A = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$
- $n = 5$
- $Q = 280{,}000$ J/mol
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

**Equation:**
$$\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = A \, \sigma^{n} \, \exp\left(\frac{-Q}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 1:** Compute the exponential term:
$$\frac{-Q}{RT} = \frac{-280{,}000}{8.314 \times 1173} = \frac{-280{,}000}{9752.3} = -28.715$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate exp(−28.715):
$$\exp(-28.715) = 3.40 \times 10^{-13}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\sigma^n$:
$$\sigma^{n} = 100^{5} = 10^{10}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply all terms:
$$\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = 1.2 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{10} \times 3.40 \times 10^{-13}$$
$$\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = 1.2 \times 3.40 \times 10^{-6+10-13}$$
$$\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = 4.08 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Final Answer:** $\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} \approx 4.08 \times 10^{-9}$ s⁻¹ (very low → good creep resistance).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem #3: Larson-Miller Parameter

**Problem:**
For a 1Cr-0.5Mo steel, the Larson-Miller constant $C = 20$. If a component must last $t_r = 100{,}000$ hours at $T = 600°C$, calculate the LMP. If at a higher temperature $T = 650°C$, the LMP is the same, what is the new rupture life?

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Part (a):** Calculate LMP at 600°C

**Step 1:** Convert temperature:
$$T_1 = 600 + 273 = 873 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the formula:
$$LMP_1 = 873 \times (\log(100{,}000) + 20)$$
$$LMP_1 = 873 \times (5 + 20) = 873 \times 25$$
$$\boxed{LMP_1 = 21{,}825 \text{ K·h}}$$

**Part (b):** Find new rupture life at 650°C

**Step 1:** Convert temperature:
$$T_2 = 650 + 273 = 923 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2:** Set $LMP_1 = LMP_2$:
$$21{,}825 = 923 \times (\log t_{r2} + 20)$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $\log t_{r2}$:
$$\log t_{r2} + 20 = \frac{21{,}825}{923} = 23.643$$
$$\log t_{r2} = 3.643$$
$$t_{r2} = 10^{3.643} \approx 4393 \text{ hours}$$

**Final Answer:** Rupture life drops to **~4393 hours** (a 96% reduction!) when temperature increases by just 50°C.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 50°C increase in operating temperature can **drastically reduce creep life** — this is why superalloys with high-T capability are essential for jet engines.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table

| Property | Fatigue | Creep | DBTT | Superplasticity |
|----------|---------|-------|------|-----------------|
| **Driver** | Cyclic stress | Constant stress at high T | Low temperature | Special microstructure |
| **Time dependency** | Cycle-dependent | Time-dependent | Instantaneous | Strain-rate dependent |
| **Key parameter** | $N_f$ (cycles) | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss}$ (s⁻¹) | $T_{DBTT}$ (°C) | m (strain rate sensitivity) |
| **Critical temperature** | None | T > 0.4 Tm | Below threshold | T > 0.5 Tm |
| **Failure mode** | Brittle crack | Rupture/elongation | Cleavage fracture | Extreme elongation |
| **Test method** | Rotating beam, axial | Constant load creep test | Charpy impact | Tensile at controlled $\dot{\varepsilon}$ |
| **Affected metals** | All | All at high T | BCC primarily | Fine-grained alloys |
| **Key equation** | Basquin/Paris | Norton-Bailey | USE/LSE/FATT | $\sigma = K\dot{\varepsilon}^m$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Failure Analysis Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Component Subjected to Load] --> B{Loading Type?}
    B -- Cyclic Fluctuating --> C[Fatigue Analysis]
    B -- Constant at High T --> D[Creep Analysis]
    B -- Impact at Low T --> E[DBTT Analysis]
    B -- Slow Forming High T --> F[Superplasticity Analysis]
    C --> C1[Basquin: S-N Curve]
    C --> C2[Paris Law: Crack Growth]
    C --> C3[Coffin-Manson: LCF]
    D --> D1[Primary Stage - Hardening]
    D --> D2[Secondary Stage - Steady State]
    D --> D3[Tertiary Stage - Rupture]
    D --> D4[Norton-Bailey Equation]
    E --> E1[Charpy Impact Test]
    E --> E2[USE / FATT / NDT / LSE]
    E --> E3[BCC Metals Affected]
    F --> F1[Grain Size less than 10 micrometer]
    F --> F2[T greater than 0.5 Tm]
    F --> F3[Strain Rate 10 power minus5 to 10 power minus3]
    F --> F4[m greater than 0.3]
```

## 4.2 S-N Curve — Block Schematic of Regions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[High Stress Region - LCF] --> B[Transition Zone 10 power3 to 10 power6]
    B --> C[Endurance Limit Region - HCF]
    C --> D[Infinite Life - below Se]
    A1[Plastic Strain Dominant] --> A
    B1[Both Elastic and Plastic] --> B
    C1[Elastic Strain Dominant] --> C
    D1[No Failure Expected] --> D
```

## 4.3 Creep Curve — Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Loading Applied at t=0] --> B[Primary Creep - Decreasing Slope]
    B --> C[Secondary Creep - Constant Minimum Slope]
    C --> D[Tertiary Creep - Increasing Slope]
    D --> E[Rupture - Final Failure]
    B1[Work Hardening Dominant] --> B
    C1[Balanced Hardening and Recovery] --> C
    D1[Necking and Void Formation] --> D
```

## 4.4 DBTT Charpy Transition Curve Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Charpy V-Notch Test at Various Temperatures] --> B[Plot Energy versus T]
    B --> C[Upper Shelf Energy Region]
    B --> D[Transition Zone]
    B --> E[Lower Shelf Energy Region]
    C --> C1[Ductile Fracture 100 percent Shear]
    D --> D1[Mix of Ductile and Cleavage]
    D --> D2[Mark FATT at 50 percent Cleavage]
    E --> E1[100 percent Cleavage Brittle]
```

## 4.5 Superplastic Forming — Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fine Grained Sheet d less than 10 micrometer] --> B[Heat to T greater than 0.5 Tm]
    B --> C[Apply Argon Gas Pressure Slowly]
    C --> D[Strain Rate Controlled 10 power minus4 s power minus1]
    D --> E[Material Deforms like Viscous Fluid]
    E --> F[Elongation greater than 500 percent]
    F --> G[Complex Shape Formed in Single Operation]
    G --> H[Cool to Room Temperature - Shape Retained]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visual Tip:** Always label axes clearly in the S-N curve. The **endurance limit** is found only for **ferrous metals and Ti alloys** — aluminum and most non-ferrous metals do NOT have a true endurance limit; their S-N curve continues to drop.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]
**Define creep. List the three stages of creep with a neat sketch of the creep curve.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Creep is the time-dependent permanent deformation of materials under constant stress at elevated temperature (T > 0.4 Tm).
- **Three Stages (2 marks):**
  - **Stage I (Primary/Transient):** Decreasing strain rate due to work hardening.
  - **Stage II (Secondary/Steady-State):** Constant minimum strain rate — balance of hardening and recovery. **Most important for design.**
  - **Stage III (Tertiary):** Accelerating strain rate due to necking, void formation, leading to rupture.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]
**What is DBTT? Why does it occur in BCC metals but not in FCC metals?**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**
- **DBTT (1 mark):** The Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature is the temperature below which a material's fracture mode changes from ductile to brittle under impact loading.
- **BCC vs FCC (2 marks):** In BCC metals, the **Peierls-Nabarro stress** is high, and slip is difficult at low temperatures. The yield strength rises sharply as T decreases, eventually exceeding the cleavage stress → brittle fracture. FCC metals have many close-packed slip systems that remain active even at low T, so no sharp transition occurs.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - KTU Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the S-N curve in detail. Discuss the differences between high-cycle fatigue (HCF) and low-cycle fatigue (LCF) with relevant equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steel component has $\sigma'_f = 900$ MPa and $b = -0.08$. It experiences fully-reversed stress amplitude of 400 MPa. Using Basquin's law, calculate the fatigue life $N_f$. State the regime of operation. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — S-N Curve, HCF, LCF (7 marks):

**S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve) — 3 marks:**
- The S-N curve plots **stress amplitude (S) vs cycles to failure (N)** on a semi-log scale.
- For steels: curve becomes horizontal at Se = **endurance limit** (typically 0.4–0.5 × UTS).
- For non-ferrous metals (Al, Cu): no true endurance limit — curve continues to drop.
- Three regions: (i) Static failure region, (ii) HCF region (10³–10⁶ cycles), (iii) Endurance/infinite life region.

**HCF vs LCF Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | HCF | LCF |
|---------|-----|-----|
| Cycles | > 10⁴–10⁵ | < 10⁴ |
| Stress | Below yield (elastic) | Above yield (plastic) |
| Strain | Elastic strain dominant | Plastic strain dominant |
| Equation | Basquin: $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f(2N_f)^b$ | Coffin-Manson: $\dfrac{\Delta\varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon'_f(2N_f)^c$ |
| Initiation site | Surface defects, inclusions | Slip band intrusions/extrusions |
| Examples | Rotating shafts, bridges | Turbine disks on startup/shutdown |

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):

**Given:** $\sigma'_f = 900$ MPa, $b = -0.08$, $\sigma_a = 400$ MPa

**Step 1:** Apply Basquin's Law:
$$\sigma_{a} = \sigma'_{f} \,(2 N_{f})^{b}$$
$$400 = 900 \times (2 N_{f})^{-0.08}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange:
$$(2 N_{f})^{-0.08} = \frac{400}{900} = 0.4444$$

**Step 3:** Take logarithm:
$$-0.08 \times \ln(2 N_{f}) = \ln(0.4444) = -0.8109$$
$$\ln(2 N_{f}) = 10.137$$

**Step 4:** Solve:
$$2 N_{f} = e^{10.137} \approx 25{,}222$$
$$\boxed{N_f \approx 12{,}611 \text{ cycles}}$$

**Regime:** Since $N_f \approx 1.26 \times 10^4$ < 10⁵, this is **Low-Cycle Fatigue (LCF)** regime. Plastic strain is significant.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** What is superplasticity? List and explain the three essential conditions required for a material to exhibit superplastic behavior. Discuss the dominant deformation mechanism with a suitable diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a superplastic Zn-22%Al alloy, the strain rate sensitivity index is $m = 0.5$ and the strength coefficient is $K = 800$ MPa·s^0.5. Calculate the flow stress at a strain rate of $10^{-3}$ s⁻¹. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) — Superplasticity Theory (7 marks):

**Definition (1 mark):** Superplasticity is the ability of certain fine-grained materials to undergo **uniform tensile elongations > 200% (often > 1000%)** under specific conditions.

**Three Conditions (3 marks):**
1. **Ultra-fine, equiaxed, stable grain size < 10 μm** (typically 1–5 μm). Smaller grain = more grain boundaries for sliding.
2. **Deformation temperature T > 0.5 Tm** (Tm = absolute melting point). Required for grain boundary diffusion.
3. **Strain rate 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻³ s⁻¹** — slow forming. Higher rates cause dislocation creep; lower rates cause diffusion cavitation.

**Mechanism — Grain Boundary Sliding (3 marks):**
- Grains slide past each other like a deck of playing cards.
- Accommodated by **Coble creep** (grain boundary diffusion) and **dislocation glide**.
- No significant change in grain shape — grains remain equiaxed even after large strain.
- Resistance to necking due to high m value → uniform deformation.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):

**Given:** $m = 0.5$, $K = 800$ MPa·s^0.5, $\dot{\varepsilon} = 10^{-3}$ s⁻¹

**Step 1:** Apply superplastic flow equation:
$$\sigma = K \, \dot{\varepsilon}^{m}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:
$$\sigma = 800 \times (10^{-3})^{0.5}$$
$$\sigma = 800 \times 10^{-1.5}$$
$$\sigma = 800 \times 0.03162$$
$$\sigma \approx 25.3 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3:** Comment:
The flow stress is very low (**~25 MPa**), which is typical of superplastic materials. Since $m = 0.5 > 0.3$, the material is in the **superplastic regime** and can undergo large uniform elongations without localized necking. This low flow stress is advantageous for **superplastic forming (SPF)** of complex aerospace and automotive components.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Read Carefully!):**
>
> 1. **Creep Problems — Temperature in Kelvin:** Students frequently forget to convert °C to K. Always use **T in Kelvin** with $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K. **(−2 marks typical deduction)**
>
> 2. **Basquin's Law — Factor of 2:** Some textbooks use $(N_f)^b$ instead of $(2N_f)^b$. Read the question carefully and stick to the version given in the KTU module. **(−1 mark)**
>
> 3. **DBTT — Only BCC Metals:** Do not state that all metals have a DBTT. FCC metals (Cu, Al, austenitic SS) do **not** show a sharp transition. **(−1 mark)**
>
> 4. **Superplasticity — Three Conditions:** Writing "fine grain" alone is not enough. Always quantify: **d < 10 μm, T > 0.5 Tm, $\dot{\varepsilon}$ in 10⁻⁵–10⁻³ s⁻¹**. **(−2 marks)**
>
> 5. **S-N Curve — Endless Endurance Limit for Non-Ferrous:** Don't draw a horizontal line for Al/Cu alloys — they have no true endurance limit. **(−1 mark)**
>
> 6. **Units of Creep Rate:** Always express in **s⁻¹** or **%/hr**. Mixing units in a single calculation loses marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ⚡ High-Density Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Fatigue** = failure under **cyclic** stress below yield strength; 3 stages: **initiation → propagation → final fracture**.
- ✅ **S-N Curve (Wöhler):** Stress amplitude vs cycles; **Endurance Limit (Se)** exists only for steels and Ti.
- ✅ **Basquin's Law** (HCF): $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f(2N_f)^b$
- ✅ **Coffin-Manson** (LCF): $\Delta\varepsilon_p/2 = \varepsilon'_f(2N_f)^c$
- ✅ **Paris Law:** $da/dN = C(\Delta K)^m$
- ✅ **Creep** = time-dependent deformation at **T > 0.4 Tm** under constant stress.
- ✅ **3 Stages:** Primary (decreasing $\dot{\varepsilon}$) → **Secondary (steady-state, design-relevant)** → Tertiary (rupture).
- ✅ **Norton-Bailey:** $\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = A\sigma^n \exp(-Q/RT)$
- ✅ **Larson-Miller:** $LMP = T(\log t_r + C)$, with $C \approx 20$.
- ✅ **DBTT** = transition from ductile to brittle; **only prominent in BCC** metals.
- ✅ **Charpy Test:** USE, FATT (50%), NDT, LSE are key points on the curve.
- ✅ **Refining grain size → lowers DBTT** (Hall-Petch).
- ✅ **Superplasticity** requires: **d < 10 μm, T > 0.5 Tm, $\dot{\varepsilon}$ 10⁻⁵–10⁻³ s⁻¹**.
- ✅ **Mechanism:** Grain Boundary Sliding (GBS); $m > 0.3$ → superplastic.
- ✅ **Need for Study:** Failures cost lives, money, and reputation. Each phenomenon has caused **historical engineering disasters** (Liberty ships → DBTT, Aloha Airlines → fatigue, turbine failures → creep).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"Cyclic = Fatigue. Constant + Hot = Creep. Cold + Impact + BCC = DBTT. Fine Grain + Hot + Slow = Superplasticity."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties and Applications of Composites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT526 - METALLURGY AND MATERIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Properties and Applications of Composites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **composite material** is an engineered, heterogeneous material composed of two or more distinct constituent phases (a **matrix** and a **reinforcement**) combined at a macroscopic level to produce a material with properties superior to those of its individual components. The matrix binds and transfers load to the reinforcement, while the reinforcement carries the primary load and provides stiffness and strength.

$$ \text{Composite} = \text{Matrix (binder)} + \text{Reinforcement (load-bearing)} $$

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 4), composites are classified primarily by **matrix type**:

| Composite Family | Matrix Material | Common Reinforcement | KTU Abbreviation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Polymer Matrix Composites** | Thermoset/Thermoplastic | Glass, Carbon, Aramid fibers | **PMC** |
| **Metal Matrix Composites** | Aluminum, Titanium, Magnesium | SiC, Al₂O₃, Carbon | **MMC** |
| **Ceramic Matrix Composites** | Al₂O₃, SiC, Si₃N₄ | Carbon, SiC fibers | **CMC** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme specifically emphasizes the link between the **type of reinforcement** (particulate, fiber, or laminar) and the resulting **engineering properties**. Marks are frequently awarded for correctly identifying the matrix–reinforcement pairing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **reinforced concrete pillar** supporting a building. The **concrete** alone is strong in compression but brittle and weak in tension. The **steel rebar** inside is ductile and excellent in tension. Together, they form a **composite** that handles both compression and tension.

Similarly, in an engineering composite:
- The **matrix** is like the **concrete** — it holds everything together, protects the reinforcement, and distributes the load.
- The **reinforcement** is like the **steel rebar** — it provides the strength and stiffness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Neither the matrix nor the reinforcement alone would perform as well as the combination. This is called the **"synergistic effect"** — a hallmark feature of composites frequently tested in KTU exams.

## 1.3 Why Composites Matter in Engineering

Modern engineering demands materials that combine properties that are normally mutually exclusive:
- **Lightweight** + **High Strength** (aerospace)
- **Stiff** + **Tough** (automotive crash structures)
- **Corrosion-Resistant** + **Heat-Resistant** (rocket nozzles)

Traditional monolithic materials (single-phase metals, polymers, or ceramics) cannot satisfy all these requirements simultaneously. Composites fill this gap.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Composite Properties — "SADLTC":** **S**trength, **A**nisotropy, **D**urability, **L**ightweight, **T**oughness, **C**orrosion-resistance.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following metrics are considered **standard engineering benchmarks** in KTU board exams for composite evaluation:

- **Specific Strength** (Strength / Density) — measured in **kN·m/kg** or **MPa/(g/cm³)**
- **Specific Modulus** (Modulus / Density) — measured in **MN·m/kg** or **GPa/(g/cm³)**
- **Fiber Volume Fraction (Vf)** — dimensionless, typically **0.3 to 0.7**
- **Critical Fiber Length (lc)** — the minimum fiber length required for effective load transfer
- **Aspect Ratio (l/d)** — fiber length to diameter ratio, must be **> 100** for high performance

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Constituents of a Composite

A composite material is architecturally composed of two phases:

### A. The Matrix (Binder Phase)
The matrix is the **continuous phase** that:
- Holds the reinforcement in place
- Transfers external load to the reinforcement via **shear stresses** at the interface
- Protects reinforcement from environmental damage (oxidation, moisture, abrasion)
- Provides shape and surface finish

**Common Matrix Materials:**
- **PMC:** Epoxy, Polyester, Vinyl ester, Phenolic, PEEK
- **MMC:** Aluminum alloys (6061, 7075), Titanium (Ti-6Al-4V), Magnesium
- **CMC:** Alumina (Al₂O₃), Silicon Carbide (SiC), Boron Carbide (B₄C)

### B. The Reinforcement (Dispersed Phase)
The reinforcement is the **discontinuous (or aligned continuous) phase** that:
- Carries the majority of the applied load
- Provides high strength and stiffness
- Defines the directional (anisotropic) properties

**Common Reinforcement Forms:**
- **Fibers:** Glass, Carbon, Aramid (Kevlar), Boron
- **Whiskers:** SiC, Al₂O₃ (single-crystal short fibers)
- **Particles:** SiC, Al₂O₃, TiC, WC
- **Laminae/Layers:** Plywood, laminated metal sheets

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favorite Question:** *"Distinguish between fibers, whiskers, and particles as reinforcements."* The answer must emphasize that fibers are long and continuous, whiskers are short single crystals, and particles are equiaxed.

## 2.2 Classification of Composites

### Classification by Reinforcement Geometry

**1. Fiber-Reinforced Composites (FRC)**
- Fibers carry the load along their length
- Two sub-types:
  - **Continuous fiber composites:** Fibers run the entire length of the part
  - **Discontinuous (short) fiber composites:** Fibers are short and randomly oriented

**2. Particulate Composites**
- Equiaxed particles dispersed in the matrix
- Examples: **Cermets** (WC-Co cutting tools), concrete with gravel, **Al-SiC** pistons
- Reinforcement improves **isotropic** strength, wear resistance, and machinability

**3. Laminar (Layered) Composites**
- Stacked layers of different materials
- Examples: Plywood, **CFRP-CFRP** laminates with varying fiber orientation, **GLARE** (Glass Laminate Aluminum Reinforced Epoxy) used in Airbus A380 fuselage

### Classification by Matrix Type (Already covered in §1.1)

## 2.3 Mechanical Properties of Composites — The Engineering Perspective

### A. High Strength-to-Weight Ratio (Specific Strength)
Composites offer superior specific strength compared to metals:

| Material | Density (g/cm³) | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Specific Strength (kN·m/kg) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Mild Steel | 7.87 | 400 | 50.8 |
| Aluminum 6061 | 2.70 | 310 | 114.8 |
| **Epoxy + 60% Carbon Fiber** | 1.60 | 1500 | **937.5** |
| **Epoxy + 60% Glass Fiber** | 2.00 | 1000 | 500.0 |

### B. Anisotropy
- Composites are **anisotropic** (properties vary with direction).
- **Carbon-fiber reinforced polymer (CFRP)** is far stiffer along the fiber direction than perpendicular to it.
- This is a **design feature**, not a defect — engineers can tailor fiber orientation to match load paths.

### C. Fatigue Resistance
- Composites, especially **CFRP**, exhibit excellent fatigue resistance.
- Failure occurs gradually through matrix cracking, fiber-matrix debonding, and finally fiber fracture — providing **fail-safe** behavior.

### D. Corrosion & Chemical Resistance
- Polymer matrices resist corrosion, acids, and alkalis.
- Used in **chemical storage tanks, marine hulls, and pipelines**.

### E. Thermal Stability
- CMCs (e.g., SiC/SiC) retain strength above **1000 °C**, making them ideal for **turbine blades and rocket nozzles**.

### F. Damping & Vibration Resistance
- The matrix-reinforcement interface absorbs vibrational energy, giving composites higher **damping capacity** than metals.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Equations)

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **frequently tested** in KTU End Semester Examinations. Memorize the rule of mixtures (Voigt and Reuss models) and the critical fiber length equation.

| # | Property | Equation | Model / Assumption | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Composite Density | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | Rule of mixtures (density) | g/cm³ |
| 2 | Volume Fraction Constraint | $V_f + V_m = 1$ | Conservation of volume | dimensionless |
| 3 | Modulus (Parallel / Voigt) | $E_c^{\parallel} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Iso-strain (fibers aligned with load) | GPa |
| 4 | Modulus (Series / Reuss) | $E_c^{\perp} = \left(\dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}\right)^{-1}$ | Iso-stress (fibers perpendicular to load) | GPa |
| 5 | Tensile Strength (Parallel) | $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | Fiber-dominated failure | MPa |
| 6 | Mass-Volume Conversion | $V_f = \dfrac{\rho_m W_f}{\rho_m W_f + \rho_f W_m}$ | Convert weight fraction $W$ to volume fraction $V$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Critical Fiber Length | $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \, d}{2 \, \tau_i}$ | Minimum length for full load transfer | m or mm |
| 8 | Effective Fiber Length Factor | $\eta_l = 1 - \dfrac{l_c}{2l}$ | Efficiency factor for short fibers | dimensionless |
| 9 | Composite Tensile Strength (Short Fiber) | $\sigma_c = \eta_l \, \sigma_f \, V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | Modified rule of mixtures | MPa |
| 10 | Longitudinal Shear Modulus (PMC) | $G_{12} = \dfrac{G_m}{1 - V_f}$ | Halpin–Tsai approximation | GPa |

> [!WARNING]
> **Notation Trap:** The fiber volume fraction $V_f$ is always taken along the **loading direction**. Mixing $V_f$ for both parallel and perpendicular models in the same calculation is a common error and costs marks.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace (Boeing 787, Airbus A350):** ~50% of the airframe by weight is composite material, primarily CFRP, reducing fuel consumption by ~20%.
- **Wind Turbine Blades:** Glass-fiber reinforced polymer (GFRP) blades can exceed 80 m in length, exploiting the stiffness-to-weight advantage.
- **Automotive (Formula 1, Tesla Model S):** CFRP monocoque chassis — combining crashworthiness with low mass.
- **Civil Engineering:** FRP rebars replace steel in marine bridges, eliminating corrosion.
- **Biomedical:** Carbon-fiber reinforced PEEK is used for orthopedic implants because its stiffness can be tuned to match bone, preventing "stress shielding."

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Rule of Mixtures for Density

### Problem Setup
A composite consists of fiber (f) and matrix (m). The total volume of the composite is the sum of the individual volumes, and similarly for mass.

**Given:**
- Mass of fibers: $M_f$
- Mass of matrix: $M_m$
- Mass of composite: $M_c = M_f + M_m$
- Volumes: $V_c = V_f + V_m$
- Densities: $\rho_f$, $\rho_m$, $\rho_c$

**To Prove:**

$$ \rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m $$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Write the definition of density for each phase and the composite.

The density of the fibers is given by the ratio of their mass to their volume:

$$ \rho_f = \frac{M_f}{V_f} \quad \Rightarrow \quad M_f = \rho_f V_f $$

Similarly, the density of the matrix relates its mass to its volume:

$$ \rho_m = \frac{M_m}{V_m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad M_m = \rho_m V_m $$

The density of the composite is defined as the total mass divided by the total volume:

$$ \rho_c = \frac{M_c}{V_c} $$

**Step 2:** Apply conservation of mass and volume.

The total mass of the composite is the sum of the masses of the fiber and matrix phases:

$$ M_c = M_f + M_m $$

The total volume of the composite is the sum of the volumes of the fiber and matrix phases:

$$ V_c = V_f + V_m $$

**Step 3:** Substitute the mass expressions into the composite density equation.

We substitute the expressions for $M_f$ and $M_m$ derived in Step 1 into the composite density equation:

$$ \rho_c = \frac{M_f + M_m}{V_c} = \frac{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}{V_c} $$

**Step 4:** Normalize by the total volume using volume fractions.

Volume fractions are defined as the ratio of each phase's volume to the total volume of the composite:

$$ V_f^{(f)} = \frac{V_f}{V_c}, \quad V_f^{(m)} = \frac{V_m}{V_c} $$

Dividing both numerator and denominator of the equation in Step 3 by $V_c$, we obtain:

$$ \rho_c = \rho_f \left(\frac{V_f}{V_c}\right) + \rho_m \left(\frac{V_m}{V_c}\right) $$

Therefore, the final expression for the density of the composite is:

$$ \boxed{\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m} $$

where $V_f$ and $V_m$ now denote the volume fractions. ∎

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The Rule of Mixtures for Elastic Modulus (Voigt / Parallel / Iso-strain Model)

### Assumption
The fibers and matrix experience the **same strain** when loaded parallel to the fiber direction. This is the "iso-strain" or "parallel" model.

**Given:**
- Fiber modulus: $E_f$
- Matrix modulus: $E_m$
- Composite modulus: $E_c$
- Volume fractions: $V_f + V_m = 1$

**To Derive:**

$$ E_c^{\parallel} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m $$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** State the iso-strain assumption.

Under the parallel model, both phases experience the same strain $\varepsilon$ when the composite is loaded along the fiber direction:

$$ \varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon $$

**Step 2:** Express the load carried by each phase.

The load carried by the fibers is the product of stress in the fibers and their cross-sectional area:

$$ P_f = \sigma_f A_f = E_f \varepsilon \, A_f $$

The load carried by the matrix is similarly the product of stress in the matrix and its cross-sectional area:

$$ P_m = \sigma_m A_m = E_m \varepsilon \, A_m $$

The total load on the composite is the sum of the loads carried by the fiber and matrix phases:

$$ P_c = P_f + P_m = \varepsilon \left( E_f A_f + E_m A_m \right) $$

**Step 3:** Convert areas to area fractions (equal to volume fractions for uniform prisms).

For a uniform cross-section along the length, the area fractions equal the volume fractions:

$$ \frac{A_f}{A_c} = V_f, \quad \frac{A_m}{A_c} = V_m $$

Therefore, the fiber area is $A_f = V_f A_c$ and the matrix area is $A_m = V_m A_c$.

**Step 4:** Express the composite modulus.

The composite modulus relates the composite stress to the composite strain. The composite stress is the total load divided by the total cross-sectional area:

$$ \sigma_c = \frac{P_c}{A_c} = \frac{\varepsilon \left( E_f A_f + E_m A_m \right)}{A_c} $$

Substituting $A_f = V_f A_c$ and $A_m = V_m A_c$:

$$ \sigma_c = \varepsilon \left( E_f V_f + E_m V_m \right) $$

The composite modulus is the ratio of composite stress to composite strain. Since $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon$:

$$ E_c = \frac{\sigma_c}{\varepsilon} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m $$

Therefore, the rule of mixtures for the composite modulus under iso-strain is:

$$ \boxed{E_c^{\parallel} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m} \qquad \text{(Voigt Model)} \quad \blacksquare $$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Inverse Rule of Mixtures for Elastic Modulus (Reuss / Series / Iso-stress Model)

### Assumption
The fibers and matrix experience the **same stress** when loaded perpendicular to the fiber direction.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** State the iso-stress assumption.

Under the series (perpendicular) model, both phases carry the same stress when load is applied perpendicular to the fibers:

$$ \sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m = \sigma $$

**Step 2:** Express the strain in each phase.

The strain in the fibers is the stress divided by the fiber modulus:

$$ \varepsilon_f = \frac{\sigma}{E_f} $$

The strain in the matrix is the stress divided by the matrix modulus:

$$ \varepsilon_m = \frac{\sigma}{E_m} $$

**Step 3:** Express the total elongation.

For a series arrangement, the total elongation of the composite is the sum of the elongations of the fiber and matrix phases:

$$ \Delta L_c = \Delta L_f + \Delta L_m $$

In terms of strains and original lengths, $\Delta L = \varepsilon L$. Therefore:

$$ \varepsilon_c L_c = \varepsilon_f L_f + \varepsilon_m L_m $$

**Step 4:** Convert lengths to volume fractions.

For a uniform cross-section, the length fractions equal the volume fractions:

$$ \frac{L_f}{L_c} = V_f, \quad \frac{L_m}{L_c} = V_m $$

Dividing both sides of the elongation equation by $L_c$:

$$ \varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f V_f + \varepsilon_m V_m $$

**Step 5:** Substitute the strain expressions and solve for the composite modulus.

Substituting the strain expressions from Step 2 into the equation from Step 4:

$$ \varepsilon_c = \frac{\sigma}{E_f} V_f + \frac{\sigma}{E_m} V_m = \sigma \left( \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} \right) $$

Since $E_c = \sigma / \varepsilon_c$:

$$ \frac{1}{E_c} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} $$

Therefore, the inverse rule of mixtures for the composite modulus under iso-stress is:

$$ \boxed{E_c^{\perp} = \left(\frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}\right)^{-1}} \qquad \text{(Reuss Model)} \quad \blacksquare $$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A glass-fiber reinforced epoxy composite has 40% volume fraction of glass fibers. Given $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³, $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa. Calculate the **density** and the **elastic modulus (parallel and perpendicular)** of the composite.

### Solution

**Given Data:**
- $V_f = 0.40$, $V_m = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60$
- $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³
- $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa

**Step 1: Calculate the density.**

Substitute the given values into the rule of mixtures for density:

$$ \rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m = (2.55)(0.40) + (1.20)(0.60) $$

Compute the contribution from the fiber phase:

$$ \rho_f V_f = 2.55 \times 0.40 = 1.02 \text{ g/cm}^3 $$

Compute the contribution from the matrix phase:

$$ \rho_m V_m = 1.20 \times 0.60 = 0.72 \text{ g/cm}^3 $$

Adding both contributions gives the composite density:

$$ \rho_c = 1.02 + 0.72 = 1.74 \text{ g/cm}^3 $$

**Step 2: Calculate the modulus (Parallel / Voigt).**

Substitute the given values into the iso-strain rule of mixtures:

$$ E_c^{\parallel} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m = (72)(0.40) + (3.5)(0.60) $$

Compute the contribution from the fiber phase:

$$ E_f V_f = 72 \times 0.40 = 28.8 \text{ GPa} $$

Compute the contribution from the matrix phase:

$$ E_m V_m = 3.5 \times 0.60 = 2.1 \text{ GPa} $$

Adding both contributions gives the composite modulus in the parallel direction:

$$ E_c^{\parallel} = 28.8 + 2.1 = 30.9 \text{ GPa} $$

**Step 3: Calculate the modulus (Perpendicular / Reuss).**

Substitute the given values into the iso-stress inverse rule of mixtures:

$$ E_c^{\perp} = \left( \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} \right)^{-1} = \left( \frac{0.40}{72} + \frac{0.60}{3.5} \right)^{-1} $$

Compute the contribution from the fiber phase:

$$ \frac{V_f}{E_f} = \frac{0.40}{72} = 0.005556 \text{ GPa}^{-1} $$

Compute the contribution from the matrix phase:

$$ \frac{V_m}{E_m} = \frac{0.60}{3.5} = 0.17143 \text{ GPa}^{-1} $$

Adding both contributions gives the total compliance:

$$ \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} = 0.005556 + 0.17143 = 0.17698 \text{ GPa}^{-1} $$

Taking the inverse gives the composite modulus in the perpendicular direction:

$$ E_c^{\perp} = \frac{1}{0.17698} = 5.65 \text{ GPa} $$

### Final Answers

| Property | Calculated Value | Units |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Composite Density** $\rho_c$ | **1.74** | g/cm³ |
| **Modulus (Parallel)** $E_c^{\parallel}$ | **30.9** | GPa |
| **Modulus (Perpendicular)** $E_c^{\perp}$ | **5.65** | GPa |

> [!TIP]
> **Observations:** The parallel modulus is **5.5× higher** than the perpendicular modulus. This dramatic anisotropy is the defining engineering feature of fiber composites — the reason designers orient fibers along the load path.
