# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a composite material. List the three main types of composites based on the matrix material.**

#### Model Answer

A composite material is an engineered, multiphase material consisting of two or more distinct constituents — a **matrix** (binder) and a **reinforcement** — combined at a macroscopic level to achieve a combination of properties not available in any single material.

The three main types based on matrix material are:

1. **Polymer Matrix Composites (PMC):** Thermoset or thermoplastic matrix with fiber reinforcement. Example: Glass fiber reinforced epoxy used in aircraft interiors.
2. **Metal Matrix Composites (MMC):** Lightweight metal (Al, Mg, Ti) reinforced with ceramic particles or fibers. Example: SiC-reinforced aluminum pistons.
3. **Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMC):** Ceramic matrix reinforced with ceramic fibers. Example: SiC/SiC used in jet engine turbine shrouds.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark each for the three matrix types with one example.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by "specific strength" and why is it important in composite selection?**

#### Model Answer

**Specific strength** is defined as the ratio of a material's tensile strength to its density:

$$ \text{Specific Strength} = \frac{\sigma_{UTS}}{\rho} \quad \text{(units: kN·m/kg or MPa·cm}^3\text{/g)} $$

**Importance in Composite Selection:**

In aerospace, automotive, and sports-equipment applications, the goal is to **carry maximum load with minimum weight**. Specific strength quantifies exactly this trade-off. Carbon fiber composites have a specific strength of **~900 kN·m/kg**, while mild steel has only **~50 kN·m/kg**. This is why a CFRP component is ~18× more weight-efficient than a steel component of equivalent load-bearing capacity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **specific strength** with **specific modulus**. Specific strength uses tensile strength $\sigma_{UTS}$; specific modulus uses Young's modulus $E$. Both are critical, but for different design criteria (load-carrying vs stiffness-critical structures).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** With neat diagrams, classify composite materials based on the **type of reinforcement** and explain each type with **two industrial examples** for every category. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **Rule of Mixtures** expressions for the density and the parallel elastic modulus of a fiber-reinforced composite. State the underlying assumptions clearly. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Classification Based on Reinforcement Type:**

**1. Fiber-Reinforced Composites (FRC):**
The reinforcement is in the form of long, slender fibers (aspect ratio > 100). Load is carried primarily along the fiber axis. Two sub-categories:
- **Continuous fiber composites:** Fibers run continuously through the matrix. Examples:
  - *Glass Fiber Reinforced Polymer (GFRP)* — used in **wind turbine blades**.
  - *Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer (CFRP)* — used in **Boeing 787 fuselage** and **F1 car chassis**.
- **Discontinuous (short) fiber composites:** Chopped fibers randomly dispersed. Examples:
  - *Chopped glass fiber epoxy* — used in **automotive bumper beams**.
  - *Carbon fiber reinforced PEEK* — used in **aerospace brackets**.

**2. Particulate Composites:**
Reinforcement consists of equiaxed particles (aspect ratio ≈ 1) uniformly dispersed in the matrix. Improves isotropic strength, wear resistance, and stiffness. Examples:
- *Tungsten Carbide-Cobalt (WC-Co)* — used in **metal-cutting tool inserts**.
- *Silicon Carbide particles in Aluminum (Al-SiC)* — used in **engine pistons and brake rotors**.

**3. Laminar (Layered) Composites:**
Two or more distinct layers bonded together. Each layer (lamina) may itself be a composite. Examples:
- *Plywood* — used in **furniture and construction formwork**.
- *GLARE (Glass Laminate Aluminum Reinforced Epoxy)* — used in the **Airbus A380 fuselage skin**.
- *Carbon/carbon (C/C) laminates* — used in **Formula 1 brake discs and spacecraft nose cones**.

*(Mark Distribution: 1 mark per main category, 1 mark for sub-classification of fibers, 1 mark for two examples each across the categories = 7 marks.)*

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Assumptions for the Rule of Mixtures:**
1. The fibers are **uniformly distributed** in the matrix.
2. The fibers are **perfectly bonded** to the matrix (no slip at the interface).
3. The composite is **macroscopically homogeneous**.
4. For the parallel model: both phases experience the **same strain** (iso-strain condition).
5. No voids or porosity in the composite.

**Step 1: Rule of Mixtures for Density.**

Total mass equals sum of phase masses, and total volume equals sum of phase volumes:

$$ M_c = M_f + M_m, \quad V_c = V_f + V_m $$

Using $\rho = M/V$ for each phase:

$$ M_f = \rho_f V_f, \quad M_m = \rho_m V_m, \quad M_c = \rho_c V_c $$

Substituting into the conservation of mass:

$$ \rho_c V_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m $$

Dividing by $V_c$ and using $V_f/V_c = V_f$ (volume fraction) and $V_m/V_c = V_m$:

$$ \boxed{\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m} \quad \text{(1 mark for setup, 1 mark for final equation)} $$

**Step 2: Rule of Mixtures for Elastic Modulus (Parallel / Voigt Model).**

Under iso-strain, $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon$. The total load is shared between fiber and matrix:

$$ P_c = P_f + P_m $$

Each phase contributes: $P = \sigma A = E \varepsilon A$. The area fractions equal the volume fractions for a uniform cross-section:

$$ P_c = E_f \varepsilon V_f A_c + E_m \varepsilon V_m A_c $$

The composite stress is the total load divided by the total cross-sectional area, so:

$$ \sigma_c = E_f \varepsilon V_f + E_m \varepsilon V_m $$

The composite modulus is the ratio of composite stress to composite strain. Since $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon$:

$$ E_c = \frac{\sigma_c}{\varepsilon} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m $$

Therefore, the rule of mixtures for the parallel modulus is:

$$ \boxed{E_c^{\parallel} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m} \quad \text{(2 marks for derivation, 1 mark for final equation, 1 mark for assumptions)} $$

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Compare the **properties and applications** of Polymer Matrix Composites (PMC), Metal Matrix Composites (MMC), and Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMC). Your answer must include at least **three specific engineering applications** for each type. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A unidirectional glass-fiber reinforced epoxy contains **35% by volume** of fibers. The density of the glass fiber is **2.60 g/cm³** and the epoxy is **1.15 g/cm³**. The Young's moduli are **70 GPa** (fiber) and **3.0 GPa** (matrix). Calculate:
   (i) The density of the composite.
   (ii) The Young's modulus of the composite parallel to the fibers.
   (iii) The Young's modulus of the composite perpendicular to the fibers. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Comparative Table:**

| Feature | PMC | MMC | CMC |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Matrix Material** | Epoxy, Polyester, PEEK | Aluminum, Titanium, Magnesium | Al₂O₃, SiC, Si₃N₄ |
| **Reinforcement** | Glass, Carbon, Aramid fibers | SiC, Al₂O₃ particles; carbon fibers | SiC, Carbon fibers |
| **Density** | **Low** (1.2 – 2.0 g/cm³) | **Medium** (2.5 – 4.5 g/cm³) | **High** (2.5 – 4.0 g/cm³) |
| **Service Temperature** | Up to ~250 °C | Up to ~600 °C | Above 1000 °C |
| **Toughness** | High (ductile matrix) | Medium | Low (brittle) |
| **Cost** | Low to medium | High | Very high |
| **Manufacturability** | Easy (layup, RTM, pultrusion) | Difficult (casting, powder metallurgy) | Very difficult (CVI, PIP) |

**Specific Engineering Applications:**

**PMC Applications:**
1. *Aircraft fuselage and wing skins* — Boeing 787, Airbus A350 use CFRP laminates.
2. *Wind turbine blades* — GFRP blades of 80 m length for offshore wind farms.
3. *Sports equipment* — Carbon fiber tennis rackets, golf shafts, and bicycle frames.

**MMC Applications:**
1. *Automotive pistons and brake rotors* — Al-SiC particles in Al alloy matrix.
2. *Aerospace structural brackets* — Al₂O₃-reinforced Al for high stiffness and thermal stability.
3. *Spacecraft structures* — B/Al composites (Boron fiber in Aluminum) for satellite frames.

**CMC Applications:**
1. *Gas turbine engine components* — SiC/SiC turbine shrouds and combustor liners (GE LEAP engine).
2. *Rocket nozzle throats* — C/SiC withstands 2000 °C in solid rocket motors.
3. *Re-entry heat shields* — Carbon/carbon laminates used in Space Shuttle leading edges.

*(Mark Distribution: 1 mark for the comparison table, 6 marks for two applications each across the three families = 7 marks.)*

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- $V_f = 0.35$, $V_m = 1 - 0.35 = 0.65$
- $\rho_f = 2.60$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.15$ g/cm³
- $E_f = 70$ GPa, $E_m = 3.0$ GPa

**Part (i): Density of the composite.** *(2 marks)*

Apply the rule of mixtures for density by substituting the given values:

$$ \rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m $$

Compute the contribution from the fiber phase:

$$ \rho_f V_f = 2.60 \times 0.35 = 0.910 \text{ g/cm}^3 $$

Compute the contribution from the matrix phase:

$$ \rho_m V_m = 1.15 \times 0.65 = 0.7475 \text{ g/cm}^3 $$

Adding both contributions gives the composite density:

$$ \rho_c = 0.910 + 0.7475 = 1.6575 \text{ g/cm}^3 \approx \mathbf{1.66 \text{ g/cm}^3} $$

**Part (ii): Young's modulus parallel to the fibers.** *(2.5 marks)*

Apply the Voigt (iso-strain) rule of mixtures by substituting the given values:

$$ E_c^{\parallel} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m $$

Compute the contribution from the fiber phase:

$$ E_f V_f = 70 \times 0.35 = 24.5 \text{ GPa} $$

Compute the contribution from the matrix phase:

$$ E_m V_m = 3.0 \times 0.65 = 1.95 \text{ GPa} $$

Adding both contributions gives the composite modulus in the parallel direction:

$$ E_c^{\parallel} = 24.5 + 1.95 = \mathbf{26.45 \text{ GPa}} $$

**Part (iii): Young's modulus perpendicular to the fibers.** *(2.5 marks)*

Apply the Reuss (iso-stress) inverse rule of mixtures by substituting the given values:

$$ \frac{1}{E_c^{\perp}} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} $$

Compute the contribution from the fiber phase:

$$ \frac{V_f}{E_f} = \frac{0.35}{70} = 0.005 \text{ GPa}^{-1} $$

Compute the contribution from the matrix phase:

$$ \frac{V_m}{E_m} = \frac{0.65}{3.0} = 0.2167 \text{ GPa}^{-1} $$

Adding both contributions gives the total compliance:

$$ \frac{1}{E_c^{\perp}} = 0.005 + 0.2167 = 0.2217 \text{ GPa}^{-1} $$

Taking the inverse gives the composite modulus in the perpendicular direction:

$$ E_c^{\perp} = \frac{1}{0.2217} = \mathbf{4.51 \text{ GPa}} $$

**Final Summary of Answers:**

| Quantity | Value | Units |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Composite Density $\rho_c$ | **1.66** | g/cm³ |
| Parallel Modulus $E_c^{\parallel}$ | **26.45** | GPa |
| Perpendicular Modulus $E_c^{\perp}$ | **4.51** | GPa |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Most Commonly Lose Marks in This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing Mass Fraction (Wf) with Volume Fraction (Vf):** The rule of mixtures uses **volume** fractions. If the problem gives mass percentages, you **must** convert to volume fractions using $\rho$. Skipping this conversion is the #1 cause of wrong numerical answers in KTU exams.
> 2. **Misapplying the Voigt vs Reuss Models:** Always check the **loading direction**:
>    - Load **parallel** to fibers → use **Voigt** ($E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$).
>    - Load **perpendicular** to fibers → use **Reuss** (inverse rule of mixtures).
>    Mixing them up leads to an answer that is **off by an order of magnitude**.
> 3. **Forgetting the Volume Constraint:** $V_f + V_m = 1$ must be stated explicitly. Examiners allot **0.5–1 mark** just for this.
> 4. **Omitting the Assumption Block:** The derivation of the rule of mixtures **requires** an assumption block (iso-strain, perfect bonding, uniform distribution). Writing the derivation without listing these assumptions costs **at least 1 mark**.
> 5. **Mishandling the Critical Fiber Length Equation:** Students often write $l_c = \sigma_f d / \tau_i$ instead of the correct form $l_c = \sigma_f d / (2\tau_i)$. The factor of 2 comes from the **stress distribution profile** (linear, triangular) along the fiber.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Properties and Applications of Composites":**

- **Definition:** Composite = Matrix (continuous, binder) + Reinforcement (discontinuous, load-bearing). The phases must be **macroscopically distinct** and the material must be **engineered** (not naturally occurring).

- **Three Matrix Families:** **PMC** (polymers, low T, low cost), **MMC** (metals, mid-high T, high strength), **CMC** (ceramics, ultra-high T, brittle but creep-resistant).

- **Three Reinforcement Geometries:** **Fibers** (highest strength, anisotropic), **Particles** (isotropic, low cost), **Laminates** (layered, tailored directional response).

- **Rule of Mixtures for Density:** $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$. Always check that $V_f + V_m = 1$.

- **Voigt Model (Parallel Modulus, Iso-strain):** $E_c^{\parallel} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$. Used when load is along the fiber direction.

- **Reuss Model (Perpendicular Modulus, Iso-stress):** $E_c^{\perp} = \left(\dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}\right)^{-1}$. Used when load is transverse to fibers.

- **Critical Fiber Length:** $l_c = \sigma_f d / (2\tau_i)$. Fibers shorter than $l_c$ pull out before full load transfer — they are inefficient.

- **Key Engineering Metric — Specific Strength:** $\sigma_{UTS} / \rho$. CFRP has ~18× the specific strength of mild steel.

- **Anisotropy:** Composites are inherently anisotropic. Designers exploit this by orienting fibers along predicted load paths.

- **Top Industrial Applications to Remember:**
  - *Aerospace:* Boeing 787, Airbus A350 (CFRP fuselage)
  - *Wind Energy:* GFRP turbine blades (up to 80 m)
  - *Automotive:* F1 chassis, EV battery enclosures
  - *Rocketry:* C/SiC nozzle throats, C/C re-entry nose cones
  - *Civil:* FRP rebars in marine bridges, GFRP pultruded structural profiles

- **Top Engineering Examples to Quote in 14-Mark Answers:**
  - GLARE in Airbus A380 fuselage
  - WC-Co cermets in cutting tools
  - Al-SiC pistons in automotive engines
  - SiC/SiC shrouds in GE LEAP jet engines

- **Examiner's Golden Rule:** Always **state assumptions, show all substitutions with units, and box your final answers**. This alone can lift your score by 1–2 marks per sub-question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Super Alloy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT526 - METALLURGY AND MATERIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Super Alloy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Superalloy** is a class of high-performance metallic alloys engineered to exhibit exceptional mechanical strength, resistance to thermal creep deformation, excellent surface stability (oxidation and hot-corrosion resistance), and reliable performance at temperatures typically above **540 °C (1000 °F)**, with some nickel-based grades sustaining stresses up to **1100 °C (2010 °F)** under extreme service conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim-style):** Superalloys are heat-resistant alloys based on nickel (Ni), iron–nickel (Fe–Ni), or cobalt (Co) matrices, which develop their superior high-temperature properties primarily through solid-solution strengthening, precipitation (gamma-prime / gamma-double-prime) hardening, carbide/boride dispersion, and grain-boundary engineering.

The three recognized families (per ASTM B 605 and ISO 9722 nomenclature) are:

| Family | Designation | Typical Service Limit |
|---|---|---|
| Iron-based superalloys | Fe-base | Up to ~700 °C |
| Nickel-based superalloys | Ni-base | Up to ~1100 °C (highest) |
| Cobalt-based superalloys | Co-base | Up to ~950 °C |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Marathon Runner of Metals"**
> Think of ordinary steel as a **sprint runner** — it gives outstanding strength at room temperature but quickly fatigues when things heat up (a hot turbine blade would deform in minutes). A superalloy is like a **trained marathon runner** with multiple physiological boosts:
> - Its base "blood" (the γ-matrix) is doped with dissolved chromium, molybdenum, and tungsten atoms (solid-solution strengthening) — just like oxygen-enriched blood feeding muscles.
> - It grows tiny ordered precipitates called **γ′ (Ni₃Al, gamma-prime)** or **γ″ (Ni₃Nb)** inside the matrix — analogous to microscopic springs that block dislocation glide, the way training stabilizes a runner's stride.
> - Its grain boundaries are seeded with carbides (M₂₃C₆, MC) — like trail markers preventing the runner from "slipping" along weak paths.
> - It forms a tough, self-healing **Cr₂O₃ / Al₂O₃ oxide scale** on the surface — like a runner's protective sunscreen that regenerates when scratched.
>
> The net effect: the alloy keeps running at red-hot temperatures where other metals would fail.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Metrics to Memorize:**
> - Service temperature window: **0.55–0.85 T_m** (where T_m = melting point in Kelvin).
> - Threshold stress rupture life: **≥ 100 hours at specified T/σ** (the standard reporting unit for superalloys).
> - Key strengthening phases: **γ (gamma matrix)**, **γ′ (gamma prime)**, **γ″ (gamma double prime)**, **δ (delta)**, **topologically close-packed (TCP) phases** like σ, μ, Laves.
> - Critical volume fraction of γ′ for blade alloys: **f_γ′ ≈ 0.40–0.70**.
> - Lattice misfit (γ/γ′) for modern single crystals: **\vert δ \vert ≈ 0.1–0.5 %**.

## 1.4 Classification Overview

Superalloys may be processed in three principal forms, each conferring a different microstructural advantage:

1. **Wrought superalloys** — hot-rolled, forged, extruded → fine, uniform grain structure; best for disks, shafts, fasteners.
2. **Cast (polycrystalline) superalloys** — equiaxed or directionally solidified (DS) → controlled grain orientation; turbine blades.
3. **Single-crystal (SX) superalloys** — no grain boundaries at all → eliminates transverse creep; modern high-pressure turbine blades.
4. **Powder-metallurgy (P/M) superalloys** — produced by hot isostatic pressing (HIP) of pre-alloyed powders → ultra-fine, segregation-free microstructure; turbine disks in aero-engines.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Microstructural Building Blocks

A Ni-base superalloy typically contains 10+ alloying elements, each performing a specific microstructural role:

| Element | Symbol | Primary Function | Typical Wt % |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aluminum | Al | Forms γ′ (Ni₃Al) and protective Al₂O₃ scale | 0.5–6 |
| Titanium | Ti | Co-forms γ′ as Ni₃(Al,Ti); increases T_solvus | 0.5–5 |
| Chromium | Cr | Cr₂O₃ scale; solid-solution strengthener; hot-corrosion resistance | 5–25 |
| Molybdenum | Mo | Solid-solution strengthener; raises T_solvus of γ′ | 1–10 |
| Tungsten | W | Strong solid-solution strengthener; forms M₆C carbide | 1–15 |
| Tantalum | Ta | Strong γ′ former; creep strengthener | 1–12 |
| Niobium | Nb | Forms γ″ (Ni₃Nb) — used in Inconel 718 | 1–6 |
| Carbon | C | Forms carbides (MC, M₂₃C₆) at grain boundaries | 0.02–0.2 |
| Boron | B | Grain-boundary strengthener; improves creep-rupture ductility | 0.005–0.05 |
| Zirconium | Zr | Grain-boundary strengthener; ties up S as ZrS | 0.01–0.1 |
| Cobalt | Co | Lowers stacking-fault energy; raises γ′ solvus | 0–20 |

> [!NOTE]
> **γ vs. γ′ vs. γ″ — Distinction Critical for KTU**
> - **γ matrix**: disordered FCC solid solution of Ni, Cr, Co, Mo, W. It is the continuous, ductile "carrier" phase.
> - **γ′ precipitates**: ordered L1₂ structure (Ni₃(Al,Ti,Ta,Nb)), coherently embedded in γ. They are the principal creep-strengthening phase in Waspaloy, Udimet 500, René 95, CMSX-4, etc.
> - **γ″ precipitates**: ordered DO₂₂ structure (Ni₃Nb), body-centered-tetragonal; dominant in **Inconel 718** — but metastable; over-aging transforms it to the brittle orthorhombic **δ phase**.

## 2.2 Four Strengthening Mechanisms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A KTU exam favourite — list all four with one-line justifications:**

1. **Solid-solution strengthening** — large atomic-size-misfit atoms (Mo, W, Co) dissolved in the γ matrix distort the lattice and impede dislocation motion.

2. **Precipitation (γ′ / γ″) hardening** — coherent, ordered, cuboidal particles are sheared or bypassed (Orowan looping) by dislocations; this provides the highest temperature capability.

3. **Carbide / Boride dispersion strengthening** — discrete M₂₃C₆, M₆C, M₂₃B₆ along grain boundaries block boundary sliding and pin grain growth.

4. **Grain-boundary engineering (Grain shape & orientation control)** — directional solidification (DS) and single-crystal (SX) growth eliminate transverse grain boundaries; oxidation-resistant coatings (aluminide, MCrAlY, TBC) are added.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — it covers 80 % of numerical/descriptive marks on superalloys.**

| Concept | Equation / Relation | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Homologous service temperature | $T_h = T_{service} / T_{melt}$ | dimensionless; $T$ in K | Decides alloy family |
| Solid-solution strengthening increment | $\tau_{ss} \propto G \cdot \varepsilon_s^{3/2} \cdot c^{1/2}$ | $G$ = shear modulus, $\varepsilon_s$ = size misfit, $c$ = solute concentration | Compare W vs. Mo strengthening |
| γ/γ′ lattice misfit | $\delta = (a_{\gamma'} - a_{\gamma})/a_{\gamma}$ | dimensionless, typically × 10⁻³ | Single-crystal alloy design |
| Orowan bypass stress | $\tau_{orowan} = \dfrac{0.4 \cdot G \cdot b}{\pi \cdot L} \cdot \dfrac{\ln(2\bar{r}/b)}{\sqrt{1-\nu}}$ | $b$ = Burgers vector, $L$ = inter-particle spacing, $\nu$ = Poisson ratio | Predicts onset of looping |
| Creep power-law (Norton-Bailey) | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = A \cdot \sigma^{n} \cdot \exp(-Q_c / RT)$ | $A$, $n$ = constants; $Q_c$ = activation energy | Turbine blade design life |
| Larson-Miller Parameter | $LMP = T \left( C + \log t_r \right)$ | $T$ in K or °R, $t_r$ in h, $C \approx 20$ | Ranks alloys by rupture life |
| Monkman-Grant ductility criterion | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{min} \cdot t_r = C_{MG}$ | constant for a given alloy | Predicts rupture time |
| γ′ coarsening (Ostwald ripening) | $\bar{r}^3 - \bar{r}_0^3 = K_{LSW} \cdot t$ | $K_{LSW}$ = LSW rate constant | Long-term microstructural stability |
| Oxidation parabolic law | $\Delta x^2 = k_p \cdot t$ | $k_p$ = parabolic rate constant | Scale-thickness prediction |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pitfall:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the keyboard pipe `|` when writing absolute values (e.g., $\vert \delta \vert$) — bare pipes break markdown tables.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Superalloys are the **backbone of hot-section aerospace hardware**:
- **Jet-engine high-pressure turbine blades & vanes** (CMSX-4, PWA 1484, René N5).
- **Combustion chambers & after-burners** (Haynes 188, Hastelloy X).
- **Rocket-engine thrust chambers** (Inconel 718, Waspaloy) — SpaceX Raptor uses a superalloy nozzle.
- **Power-generation land-based gas turbine blades** (IN-738LC, GTD-111).
- **Nuclear reactor core components** (Hastelloy C-276 — corrosion resistance).
- **Chemical & petrochemical reactors** (Inconel 625 — acid + high-T service).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — γ/γ′ Lattice Misfit and Its Engineering Significance

> **Starting Premise:** Modern single-crystal superalloys are designed so that the lattice parameter of the strengthening γ′ cubes ($a_{\gamma'}$) is intentionally very close to that of the γ matrix ($a_{\gamma}$). The **misfit** $\delta$ quantifies this difference.

**Step 1 — Definition of misfit**

$$
\delta \;=\; \frac{a_{\gamma'} - a_{\gamma}}{a_{\gamma}}
$$

**Step 2 — Physical interpretation**

- $\delta > 0$ → γ′ lattice is larger → γ′ cubes are under biaxial compressive misfit stress in the matrix.
- $\delta < 0$ → γ′ is smaller → γ′ cubes are under biaxial tensile misfit stress.
- $\vert \delta \vert \to 0$ → coherent, low-misfit alloy (e.g., CMSX-4, $\delta \approx -0.1$ %).
- $\vert \delta \vert$ large (≥ 0.5 %) → rafting (directional coarsening) under creep.

**Step 3 — Example numerical evaluation**

For a hypothetical alloy with $a_{\gamma'} = 0.3572$ nm and $a_{\gamma} = 0.3580$ nm:

$$
\delta \;=\; \frac{0.3572 - 0.3580}{0.3580} \;=\; \frac{-0.0008}{0.3580} \;\approx\; -2.23 \times 10^{-3}
$$

Therefore $\vert \delta \vert = 0.223$ % → low misfit → good high-temperature stability.

**Step 4 — Stress developed due to misfit**

$$
\sigma_{misfit} \;=\; M \cdot E \cdot \delta
$$

where $M$ is the Taylor factor (≈ 3.06 for FCC polycrystals) and $E$ is Young's modulus.

For $E = 200$ GPa and $\delta = -2.23 \times 10^{-3}$:

$$
\sigma_{misfit} \;=\; 3.06 \times 200 \times 10^{9} \times (-2.23 \times 10^{-3}) \;\approx\; -1.36 \; \text{GPa}
$$

This compressive misfit stress locks dislocations and is the principal source of creep strength in SX blades.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Larson–Miller Parameter and Rupture-Life Prediction

**Step 1 — Statement of the empirical relation**

For a given alloy and stress level $\sigma$, the rupture time $t_r$ depends on temperature $T$ through an Arrhenius-type rate law:

$$
t_r \;\propto\; \exp\!\left(\frac{Q}{R T}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Take the logarithm**

$$
\ln t_r \;=\; \text{const} + \frac{Q}{R T}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by T (Larson–Miller construction)**

$$
T \cdot \ln t_r \;=\; \text{const} + \frac{Q}{R}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert ln to log₁₀ and introduce the empirical constant C**

$$
T \left( \log t_r + C \right) \;=\; \text{const} \cdot T
$$

This defines the **Larson–Miller Parameter** (LMP):

$$
LMP \;=\; T \left( C + \log t_r \right)
$$

where $C \approx 20$ is the conventional constant for Ni-base superalloys, $T$ is in Kelvin (or Rankine if using imperial units), and $t_r$ is in hours.

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example**

A turbine blade experiences $T = 950 \,^{\circ}\mathrm{C} = 1223$ K. At the operating stress, the LMP for the chosen alloy (say, IN-738LC) is $LMP = 28{,}500$. Find the predicted rupture life.

Rearranging:

$$
\log t_r \;=\; \frac{LMP}{T} - C
$$

$$
\log t_r \;=\; \frac{28{,}500}{1223} - 20 \;=\; 23.30 - 20 \;=\; 3.30
$$

$$
t_r \;=\; 10^{3.30} \;\approx\; 2000 \; \text{hours}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The blade is expected to survive ~2000 hours (≈ 83 days of continuous service) before reaching the rupture criterion.

**Step 6 — Useful manipulation — equivalent stress–LMP master curve**

Manufacturers tabulate LMP versus applied stress. Using $LMP$ tables for IN-718 at $T = 650 \,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, an engineer selects an LMP that gives a 10⁵ h (≈ 11.4 years) design life and reads off the allowable stress from the master curve.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Alloy Selection at a Glance

| Alloy | Base | Key Strengthener | Max Use T (°C) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Inconel 718 | Fe–Ni | γ″ (Ni₃Nb) | 700 | Rocket engines, disks |
| Waspaloy | Ni | γ′ (Ni₃Al,Ti) | 870 | Compressor disks, blades |
| Udimet 500 | Ni | γ′ | 950 | Turbine blades |
| IN-100 | Ni | γ′ (high vol fraction) | 1000 | Cast turbine blades |
| René 95 | Ni | γ′ + carbides | 950 | P/M turbine disks |
| CMSX-4 | Ni (SX) | γ′ (Re, W, Ta) | 1100 | Single-crystal HPT blades |
| PWA 1484 | Ni (SX) | γ′ (Hf, Y) | 1080 | Single-crystal blades |
| Haynes 188 | Co | M₆C + solid soln | 1000 | Combustor liners |
| Stellite 6 | Co | M₇C₃ | 800 | Wear-resistant, valve seats |
| A-286 | Fe | γ′ (low volume) | 700 | Jet-engine bolts, casings |
| Nimonic 80A | Ni | γ′ | 815 | Turbine blades (early RR) |

## 3.4 Co-base Superalloys — Special Derivation Note

Co-base superalloys historically lacked the strong γ′ phase because the γ′ solvus is very close to the melting point. The strengthening hierarchy is dominated by:

1. **Solid-solution strengthening** (Cr, W, Ni additions).
2. **Carbide precipitation** — M₇C₃, M₂₃C₆, M₆C.
3. **Order hardening by intermetallics** — modern Co-base "γ/γ′" alloys (e.g., Co-Al-W system, developed c. 2006) use L1₂ Co₃(Al,W) for γ′ equivalent, raising the upper use temperature to ~1000 °C.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Ni-base dominates the high-T field:** The γ′ (Ni₃Al) phase in Ni-base alloys maintains — and even increases — its yield strength with temperature up to ~700 °C (the "yield-strength anomaly"), a behavior absent in Co-base systems until the Co–Al–W discovery.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Superalloy Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SUPERALLOYS] --> B[Fe-based]
    A --> C[Ni-based]
    A --> D[Co-based]
    B --> B1[A-286 type]
    B --> B2[N-155, 16-25-6]
    C --> C1[Wrought alloys]
    C --> C2[Cast polycrystalline]
    C --> C3[Directionally solidified DS]
    C --> C4[Single crystal SX]
    C --> C5[Powder metallurgy P/M]
    D --> D1[Haynes 188]
    D --> D2[Stellite family]
    D --> D3[Co-Al-W modern gamma-prime alloys]
    C1 --> C1A[Waspaloy, Inconel 718, Nimonic 80A]
    C2 --> C2A[IN-100, Udimet 500, Mar-M247]
    C3 --> C3A[DS Mar-M200, GTD-111]
    C4 --> C4A[CMSX-4, PWA 1484, Rene N5]
    C5 --> C5A[Rene 95, N18, ME3]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Function of Alloying Elements in a Ni-base Superalloy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MATRIX[GAMMA MATRIX FCC]
        Ni1[Ni FCC solvent]
        Cr1[Cr - solid solution and Cr2O3 scale]
        Co1[Co - solid solution and gamma-prime solvus raiser]
        Mo1[Mo - solid solution and M6C carbide former]
        W1[W - solid solution and M6C carbide former]
    end
    subgraph PRECIP[STRENGTHENING PRECIPITATES]
        GamP[gamma-prime Ni3 Al Ti Ta coherent L12 cuboids]
        GamPP[gamma-double-prime Ni3 Nb coherent DO22 discs]
        Delta[delta phase Ni3 Nb orthorhombic - overage product]
    end
    subgraph BOUND[GRAIN BOUNDARY PHASES]
        Carb1[MC TiC NbC TaC primary carbides]
        Carb2[M23C6 M7C3 secondary grain boundary carbides]
        Boride[M3B2 borides]
        ZrS[Zirconium sulphide ZrS - sulphur scavenger]
    end
    subgraph SURFACE[PROTECTIVE SURFACE LAYER]
        Oxide[Al2O3 alpha alumina scale]
        Coat[MCrAlY bond coat]
        TBC[Thermal barrier coating YSZ 7 percent Y2O3 ZrO2]
    end
    MATRIX --> PRECIP
    MATRIX --> BOUND
    MATRIX --> SURFACE
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Process Flow — Manufacturing Route for a Single-Crystal Superalloy Blade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Vacuum induction melting VIM] --> P2[Vacuum arc remelting VAR or Electroslag remelting ESR]
    P2 --> P3[Investment casting wax pattern assembly]
    P3 --> P4[Ceramic shell mould built by dipping in silica slurry]
    P4 --> P5[High rate solidification HRS Bridgman furnace with helical grain selector at bottom]
    P5 --> P6{Pass grain selector}
    P6 -- Yes --> P7[Single crystal SX grows with no grain boundaries]
    P6 -- No --> P8[Recycled charge]
    P7 --> P9[Controlled cooling to develop gamma-prime precipitates]
    P9 --> P10[Hot isostatic pressing HIP for cast porosity closure]
    P10 --> P11[Solution heat treatment above gamma-prime solvus]
    P11 --> P12[Two-step aging 1080C and 870C to fine gamma-prime]
    P12 --> P13[Surface cleaning and grit blasting]
    P13 --> P14[Aluminide diffusion coating or MCrAlY overlay coating]
    P14 --> P15[Electron beam physical vapour deposition EB-PVD of 7 percent Y2O3 ZrO2 TBC]
    P15 --> P16[Final dimensional inspection and X-ray Laue verification of single-crystal orientation within 5 degrees of 001]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Comparison — γ′ Morphology with Misfit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Coherent low misfit vert delta vert less than 0.2 percent] --> A1[Cuboidal gamma prime - uniform high creep resistance]
    B[Moderate misfit 0.2 to 0.5 percent] --> B1[Directionally coarsened gamma prime - rafting after long creep exposure]
    C[High misfit greater than 0.5 percent] --> C1[Plate-like or needle-like gamma prime - lower creep life]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Phase Stability Map (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Equilibrium cooling of Ni base superalloy] --> STEP1[Liquid to gamma solid solution]
    STEP1 --> STEP2[Gamma prime L12 precipitation ~1000C]
    STEP1 --> STEP3[Carbide MC primary solidification]
    STEP2 --> STEP4[Long term exposure at 800 to 900C]
    STEP3 --> STEP4
    STEP4 --> STEP5{Alloy composition}
    STEP5 -- Cr-Mo rich --> STEP6[TCP phases sigma mu laves - embrittling]
    STEP5 -- Balanced chemistry --> STEP7[Stable gamma + gamma prime - desirable]
    STEP5 -- Over-aged Inconel 718 --> STEP8[Gamma double prime to delta - loss of strength]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **KTU Examiner Note:** Part A expects crisp, textbook-style answers of 4–6 lines. Lengthy derivations are NOT required.

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Pattern]**
**Define a superalloy. List the three base-metal families with one typical application of each.**

**Model Answer:**
A superalloy is a heat-resistant metallic alloy designed for service above 540 °C, exhibiting high creep strength, oxidation resistance, and microstructural stability. The three base families are:
- **Iron-based** (e.g., A-286) — used in jet-engine bolts and casings up to ~700 °C.
- **Nickel-based** (e.g., CMSX-4) — used in single-crystal high-pressure turbine blades up to ~1100 °C.
- **Cobalt-based** (e.g., Haynes 188) — used in combustor liners up to ~1000 °C.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Pattern]**
**What is the γ′ (gamma-prime) phase? Why is it the principal strengthening phase in Ni-base superalloys?**

**Model Answer:**
The γ′ phase is the ordered intermetallic compound **Ni₃(Al, Ti, Ta, Nb)** with an L1₂ crystal structure, precipitating as coherent cuboidal particles within the disordered γ (FCC) matrix. It is the principal strengthening phase because:
1. It remains coherent with the matrix even at high temperatures.
2. It maintains — and unusually **increases** — its flow stress up to ~700 °C (the **yield-strength anomaly**).
3. Dislocations must either shear the ordered precipitates (creating antiphase boundaries, APB) or bypass them by Orowan looping, both of which are energy-intensive.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Two sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each, or a single 14-mark analytical question. Module-level internal choice is mandatory.

### ▶ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, Module 4 Pattern]**
**(a)** Discuss the four principal strengthening mechanisms in nickel-based superalloys with suitable microstructural examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With neat sketches, explain the differences between **equiaxed**, **directionally solidified (DS)**, and **single-crystal (SX)** cast superalloys. Mention their respective applications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

> The four principal strengthening mechanisms in Ni-base superalloys are:

**1. Solid-solution strengthening (3–4 marks)**
Large atomic-radius solute atoms — typically **Mo, W, Co, Cr, Fe** — dissolve in the γ matrix and create local lattice distortions (size misfit, $\varepsilon_s$, and modulus misfit, $\varepsilon_G$). These elastic stress fields interact with the stress field of moving dislocations, raising the critical resolved shear stress (CRSS).
- Empirical form: $\tau_{ss} \propto G \cdot \varepsilon_s^{3/2} \cdot c^{1/2}$ (Labusch–Nabarro treatment).
- W and Mo also raise the γ′ solvus temperature, allowing higher service temperatures.

**2. Precipitation (γ′ / γ″) hardening (2 marks)**
The principal source of high-temperature strength. γ′ (Ni₃Al) cuboids are coherent with γ and obstruct dislocation motion. The strengthening increment from shearing is given by the **APB energy model**:

$$
\tau_{shear} = \frac{\gamma_{APB}}{2b} \left[ \left(\frac{3 \pi f}{8} \right)^{1/2} - f \right]
$$

where $\gamma_{APB}$ is the antiphase-boundary energy, $f$ is the volume fraction of γ′, and $b$ is the Burgers vector. For very fine or high-volume-fraction γ′, Orowan looping dominates.

**3. Carbide and boride dispersion strengthening (1 mark)**
Discrete carbides — **MC** (TiC, NbC, TaC) primary, **M₂₃C₆** and **M₆C** secondary — and borides (M₃B₂) located at grain boundaries pin boundary migration and prevent sliding at elevated temperatures, thereby improving creep-rupture life and ductility.

**4. Grain-boundary engineering (1 mark)**
- In wrought / equiaxed alloys: addition of **B, Zr, Mg, La** strengthens boundaries.
- In advanced castings: directional solidification and single-crystal growth eliminate transverse grain boundaries, multiplying creep life by an order of magnitude.

#### Model Solution — (b)

> The three cast microstructures differ in grain morphology and creep capability:

| Feature | Equiaxed | Directionally Solidified (DS) | Single-Crystal (SX) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Grain shape | Random polygonal | Columnar, aligned with [001] | One grain only |
| Transverse grain boundaries | Many | Eliminated (columnar) | None |
| Creep-rupture life (relative) | 1× | 4–6× | 8–12× |
| Thermal-shock resistance | Best | Moderate | Lowest |
| Typical alloy | IN-100 | GTD-111, DS Mar-M200 | CMSX-4, PWA 1484, René N5 |
| Application | Low-pressure turbine blades | Industrial gas turbines, HPT stage 1 | Aero HPT stage 1 blades |

**Sketch description (text-based):**

- **Equiaxed:** multiple grains of random orientation; grain boundaries visible in all directions; high transverse creep crack susceptibility.
- **DS:** columnar grains growing parallel to the blade axis (typically [001]); grain boundaries are longitudinal — no transverse boundaries to open under centrifugal stress.
- **SX:** no grain boundaries at all; only a single crystal whose orientation is verified by Laue back-reflection X-ray to within 5° of [001]; coated with MCrAlY bond-coat + YSZ TBC.

### ▶ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, Module 4 Pattern]**
**(a)** Explain **Inconel 718** in detail: its composition, the role of the γ″ phase, the application window, and the δ-phase embrittlement issue. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Ni-base superalloy component is to operate at **$T = 900 \,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$** continuously. Using the **Larson–Miller parameter** with $C = 20$, calculate the expected **rupture life** if the alloy's master curve gives $LMP = 26{,}500$. If the service temperature is increased to **$950 \,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$**, by what factor does the rupture life decrease? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

> **Inconel 718 (UNS N07718)** is the most widely used superalloy (~50 % of all superalloy tonnage), accounting for the bulk of aero-engine disks, rocket components, and cryogenic tanks.

- **Nominal composition (wt %):** Ni ~ 53, Cr ~ 19, Fe ~ 18, Nb + Ta ~ 5.1, Mo ~ 3.0, Ti ~ 0.9, Al ~ 0.5, C ≤ 0.08, B ≤ 0.006.
- **Strengthening phase:** **γ″ (gamma-double-prime)** — ordered body-centered-tetragonal (BCT) **DO₂₂** structure of composition **Ni₃Nb**. Discs of γ″, 20–60 nm in size, are coherently embedded in the γ matrix and provide extremely high yield strength.
- **Secondary phase:** small amount of γ′ (Ni₃Al) contributes modestly.
- **Application window:** –250 °C to **+700 °C**. Inconel 718 is unique in retaining strength at cryogenic temperatures, making it ideal for rocket turbopumps (SpaceX Merlin) and LNG hardware.
- **Limitation / embrittlement:** Above ~700 °C, the metastable γ″ **transforms to the stable orthorhombic δ-phase (Ni₃Nb)**, causing loss of strength and ductility. Hence Inconel 718 cannot be used for the hottest HPT blades — those require **Alloy 718Plus** (with added Al, Ti, W, Co) or **Waspaloy / René 65** for 700–750 °C duty.
- **Heat treatment:** solution anneal at 970 °C + double aging at 720 °C / 620 °C to develop a fine γ″ + γ′ distribution.

#### Model Solution — (b)

> **Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin.**

$$
T_1 = 900 + 273 = 1173 \; \text{K}
$$

$$
T_2 = 950 + 273 = 1223 \; \text{K}
$$

> **Step 2 — Apply the Larson–Miller equation at 900 °C.**

$$
LMP = T \, (C + \log t_r)
$$

$$
26{,}500 = 1173 \, (20 + \log t_{r,1})
$$

$$
20 + \log t_{r,1} = \frac{26{,}500}{1173} \approx 22.59
$$

$$
\log t_{r,1} = 2.59 \quad\Rightarrow\quad t_{r,1} = 10^{2.59} \approx 389 \; \text{hours}
$$

> **Step 3 — Apply the same LMP at 950 °C.**

$$
20 + \log t_{r,2} = \frac{26{,}500}{1223} \approx 21.67
$$

$$
\log t_{r,2} = 1.67 \quad\Rightarrow\quad t_{r,2} = 10^{1.67} \approx 46.8 \; \text{hours}
$$

> **Step 4 — Compute the reduction factor.**

$$
\text{Factor} = \frac{t_{r,1}}{t_{r,2}} = \frac{389}{46.8} \approx 8.3
$$

> **Conclusion:** Increasing the service temperature by just 50 °C (5.5 %) reduces the rupture life by an order of magnitude (~8.3×) — illustrating the extreme temperature sensitivity of superalloy components.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in Superalloy Questions (verified against KTU 2018–2024 paper patterns):**
> 1. **Confusing γ′ and γ″** — γ′ is **Ni₃(Al,Ti)** L1₂ cubic, dominant in Waspaloy / CMSX-4; γ″ is **Ni₃Nb** DO₂₂ tetragonal, dominant in Inconel 718. Students frequently swap these — 1–2 marks lost.
> 2. **Forgetting the yield-strength anomaly** — the unique property of γ′ to *increase* its flow stress up to ~700 °C is worth 1–2 marks and must be stated explicitly.
> 3. **LMP unit mistake** — $T$ must be in **K** (or Rankine). Using °C in the Larson–Miller formula is a guaranteed 2-mark penalty.
> 4. **Skipping the role of Co, Cr, Mo, W** in solid-solution strengthening — generic answers that just say "solid-solution strengthening" without naming the elements lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Ignoring coating systems** — for a 7-marks question on turbine blades, the aluminide / MCrAlY bond-coat and YSZ TBC are expected to be mentioned.
> 6. **Omitting δ-phase embrittlement in Inconel 718** — the metastable-to-stable transformation above 700 °C is a KTU favourite.
> 7. **Using single-crystal advantages but ignoring cost & anisotropy** — single-crystal blades have lower transverse ductility and are expensive; an unbalanced answer loses completeness marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Use this list as your last-30-minute revision map.**

- **Definition:** Superalloy = heat-resistant alloy based on Fe, Ni, or Co, for service > 540 °C, with exceptional creep, fatigue, and oxidation resistance.
- **Three families:** Fe-base, Ni-base, Co-base. **Ni-base dominates** the highest-T applications.
- **Microstructure of Ni-base superalloy = γ (matrix) + γ′ (strengthener) + carbides + borides + surface oxide.**
- **γ′ = Ni₃(Al,Ti,Ta,Nb), L1₂, coherent cuboids.** Volume fraction can reach 70 % in blade alloys. **The yield-strength anomaly is its signature property.**
- **γ″ = Ni₃Nb, DO₂₂, BCT discs;** principal strengthener of Inconel 718; transforms to δ (orthorhombic) on over-aging → embrittlement above 700 °C.
- **Four strengthening mechanisms:** solid-solution, precipitation, carbide/boride dispersion, grain-boundary engineering.
- **Alloying roles (must memorise):** Al/Ti/Ta → γ′ former; Nb → γ″ former; Cr → oxidation + hot-corrosion resistance (forms Cr₂O₃); Mo/W → solid-solution strengthener; Co → lowers stacking-fault energy; B/Zr → grain-boundary strengtheners; C → carbide former.
- **Cast microstructures (in increasing creep life):** equiaxed < DS < SX. SX has no grain boundaries; orientation controlled within 5° of [001].
- **Larson–Miller parameter:** $LMP = T(C + \log t_r)$, $C = 20$, $T$ in K, $t_r$ in hours — used to predict rupture life and rank alloys.
- **Co-base alloys:** historically lacked γ′; strengthened by carbides + solid solution. New **Co–Al–W** alloys (post-2006) use L1₂ Co₃(Al,W) γ′ to push capability to ~1000 °C.
- **Inconel 718:** workhorse of the superalloy family; cryogenic to 700 °C; **γ″ strengthened**; vulnerable to δ-phase embrittlement.
- **CMSX-4 / PWA 1484 / René N5:** modern single-crystal superalloys with Re, W, Ta additions, used in HPT stage-1 blades of modern aero-engines.
- **Coatings:** aluminide (diffusion) + MCrAlY (overlay) bond-coat + 7 % Y₂O₃–ZrO₂ (YSZ) thermal barrier coating (TBC) — protect against oxidation and hot corrosion.
- **Defects to avoid:** TCP phases (σ, μ, Laves) form in Cr/Mo-rich, low-Co alloys during long-term exposure — consume the γ matrix, deplete γ′ formers, embrittle.
- **Manufacturing routes:** VIM → VAR/ESR (clean melt) → Investment casting (for blades) → Wrought hot-working (for disks) → P/M + HIP (for fine-grain disks) → Heat treatment → Coating → Inspection.
- **Why superalloys are critical:** enable jet-engine turbine-inlet temperatures (TIT) > 1600 °C, which directly determines fuel efficiency and specific thrust.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intermetallics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT526 - METALLURGY AND MATERIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Intermetallics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Intermetallic compounds (IMCs)** — also called **intermetallic phases** or **intermetallics** — are a unique class of advanced engineering materials formed by the combination of two or more metallic elements (and sometimes a metalloid) into a single, distinct solid-state phase. Unlike ordinary metallic alloys, which are disordered solid solutions, intermetallics possess a **fixed or near-fixed stoichiometric composition**, an **ordered crystal structure**, and a long-range atomic arrangement in which each sublattice is preferentially occupied by a specific element.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):**
> *Intermetallics are ordered alloy phases formed between two or more metals with a composition corresponding to a fixed stoichiometry and possessing crystal structures different from those of the constituent metals. They exhibit a mixture of metallic and covalent/ionic bonding, leading to a unique combination of physical and mechanical properties.*

The most common examples include:
- **Ni₃Al** (Nickel aluminide) — γ′ phase, used in superalloys
- **NiAl** (Nickel aluminide) — β phase, used as bond coats
- **TiAl** (Titanium aluminide) — γ phase, used in jet engine blades
- **FeAl** and **Fe₃Al** — Iron aluminides, for structural use
- **CuZn** (Brass) — β-brass phase
- **MgCu₂** — Laves phase

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of **intermetallics** like a meticulously organized military parade on a checkerboard. Imagine a chessboard where every black square *must* contain a soldier and every white square *must* contain a sailor. They are all together, but each type knows exactly where to stand. This is **ordering**.

In a normal alloy (like brass or bronze), zinc and copper atoms randomly occupy lattice positions — it's like a casual crowd at a market. But in an intermetallic like **CuZn**, the atoms are forced into a rigidly alternating pattern, like soldiers in formation. This atomic "discipline" is what gives intermetallics their extraordinary properties:

- **High strength at elevated temperatures** (because the ordered arrangement resists dislocation motion).
- **Low density** (often lighter than superalloys based on Ni).
- **Excellent oxidation and corrosion resistance** (because of strong directional bonds and protective oxide formation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Remembering Intermetallics:**
> **O**rdered
> **S**toichiometric
> **C**ompound (distinct crystal structure)
> — "**OSC**" — *Oscillate into a fixed lattice!*

## 1.3 Bonding Character — What Holds Them Together

Intermetallics occupy an interesting middle ground in the bonding spectrum:

| Bonding Type | Character | Contribution in IMCs |
|---|---|---|
| **Metallic** | Delocalized "sea" of electrons | Electrical/thermal conductivity, ductility |
| **Covalent (directional)** | Shared electron pairs | High strength, brittleness |
| **Ionic** | Charge transfer between dissimilar atoms | Structural stability, high melting point |

This **mixed (heteropolar + metallic + covalent) bonding** is precisely what produces their signature combination of refractoriness, strength, and — unfortunately — **low room-temperature ductility**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do Intermetallics Form? — The Hume-Rothery Conditions

Just as the **Hume-Rothery rules** govern the formation of substitutional solid solutions, similar principles (with modifications) govern intermetallic formation. For two metals A and B to form a stable intermetallic, the following conditions are favorable:

1. **Atomic Size Factor (≤ 15% size mismatch)**
   - When the size difference is large but not large enough to form a complete solid solution, the lattice prefers to *order*, segregating large and small atoms onto specific sublattices. This is the genesis of intermetallics.
   $$\text{Size mismatch} = \left|\frac{r_A - r_B}{r_A}\right| \times 100\%$$

2. **Electronegativity Difference (Moderate)**
   - A *moderate* difference (typically **0.4 to 1.7 on the Pauling scale**) introduces a degree of ionic character without complete compound formation. This directional bonding stabilizes the ordered phase.

3. **Valence Electron Concentration (VEC)**
   - The **VEC** is the number of valence electrons per atom. Many famous intermetallics are classified by VEC:
     - **Hume-Rothery phases**: VEC = 3/2 (β-brass, CuZn)
     - VEC = 21/13 (γ-brass)
     - VEC = 7/4 (ε-phases, e.g., CuZn₅)

$$\text{VEC} = \frac{\sum (n_i \times v_i)}{\sum n_i}$$

where $n_i$ is the number of atoms of element $i$ and $v_i$ is its valence.

4. **Similar Crystal Structure of Constituents** (relaxed for IMCs)
   - Unlike solid solutions, IMCs can form even when parent metals have *different* structures — the compound adopts its *own* unique structure.

## 2.2 Important Crystal Structures of Intermetallics

Intermetallics are catalogued by their **Strukturbericht designation**, a symbolic labeling system that KTU examiners love:

| Strukturbericht | Prototype | Stoichiometry | Examples | Engineering Use |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| **B2** | CsCl | AB | NiAl, CuZn, FeAl | High-temperature structural |
| **L1₂** | Cu₃Au | A₃B | Ni₃Al, Cu₃Au, Al₃Sc | Superalloy γ′ precipitates |
| **L2₁** | Heusler | A₂BC | Cu₂MnAl, Ni₂MnGa | Shape memory, spintronics |
| **DO₃** | BiF₃ / BiLi₃ | A₃B | Fe₃Al, Fe₃Si | Soft magnetic alloys |
| **DO₁₉** | Ni₃Sn | A₃B | Ni₃Sn, Ti₃Al | Lightweight high-T alloys |
| **C14 / C15 / C36** | MgZn₂ / MgCu₂ / MgNi₂ | AB₂ | MgCu₂, TiFe₂, CaMg₂ | Laves phases — high-T strength |
| **B8₁** | NiAs | AB | FeS, NiS, CoTe | Semi-metallic, magnetically active |
| **D8₈** | Mn₅Si₃ | A₅B₃ | Nb₅Si₃, Mo₅Si₃ | Ultra-high-T (>1400°C) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Topic — Strukturbericht Designations:**
> Expect at least one short question asking to identify an intermetallic by its Strukturbericht symbol or to draw the unit cell. The **L1₂ (Cu₃Au)** and **B2 (CsCl)** structures are *favorites* of KTU paper-setters.

### 2.2.1 Visualizing the L1₂ (Cu₃Au) Structure
The **L1₂ structure** is a face-centered cubic (FCC) arrangement where:
- Au atoms occupy the **corner positions** (8 corners × 1/8 = 1 atom).
- Cu atoms occupy the **face-centered positions** (6 faces × 1/2 = 3 atoms).
- The Cu:Au ratio is therefore **3:1**, giving the formula **Cu₃Au**.

This is the prototype for the **γ′ (gamma-prime)** strengthening phase in nickel-based superalloys.

### 2.2.2 Visualizing the B2 (CsCl) Structure
The **B2 structure** is a body-centered cubic (BCC) arrangement where:
- One element (e.g., Cs) sits at the corners of a cube.
- The other element (e.g., Cl) sits at the body center.
- Each atom is coordinated by 8 atoms of the opposite type.

This is the prototype for **β-brass (CuZn)**, **NiAl**, and **FeAl**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Size mismatch (Hume-Rothery) | $\delta = \left\vert \dfrac{r_A - r_B}{r_A} \right\vert \times 100\%$ | % | Favorable for ordered phases |
| 2 | Valence Electron Concentration | $\text{VEC} = \dfrac{\sum n_i v_i}{\sum n_i}$ | e⁻/atom | Drives Hume-Rothery phases |
| 3 | Electronegativity difference | $\Delta \chi = \vert \chi_A - \chi_B \vert$ | Pauling units | 0.4–1.7 favors IMCs |
| 4 | APB energy (γ′ strengthening) | $E_{\text{APB}}$ | J/m² | Anti-phase boundary energy |
| 5 | Strength from ordered precipitates | $\tau \propto \sqrt{E_{\text{APB}} \cdot f / r}$ | Pa | f = volume fraction, r = radius |
| 6 | Density (rule of mixtures) | $\rho_{\text{IMC}} = \dfrac{\sum n_i A_i}{\sum n_i \cdot V_m} \cdot N_A$ | kg/m³ | Approximate |
| 7 | Melting point estimate | $T_m \approx \dfrac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta S_f}$ | K | For ideal compounds |
| 8 | Yield strength temperature dependence | $\sigma_y(T) = \sigma_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$ | Pa | Arrhenius-type behavior |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Insight:**
> Unlike ordinary metallic alloys, the strength of many intermetallics **increases with temperature** up to a critical point (the **"yield strength anomaly"**). This is observed in Ni₃Al and TiAl, and is the single most important property making them suitable for jet engine and gas turbine applications. This anomaly is attributed to **Kear-Wilsdorf locking** — a cross-slip mechanism of dislocations from {111} to {010} planes.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Intermetallic | Reason for Selection |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| Jet engine turbine blades | **Ni₃Al (γ′)** in Ni-superalloys | Yield strength increases with T |
| Turbocharger rotors (automotive) | **TiAl (γ)** | Low density (3.9 g/cm³), high specific strength |
| Bond coats in TBCs | **NiAl (β)** | Oxidation resistance, thermal expansion match |
| Heating elements | **MoSi₂** | High-T oxidation resistance (up to 1800°C) |
| Shape memory devices | **NiTi (Nitinol)** | Thermoelastic martensitic transformation |
| Hydrogen storage | **LaNi₅, TiFe** | Reversible absorption/desorption |
| Permanent magnets | **Nd₂Fe₁₄B** (tetragonal IMCs) | Highest energy product of any magnetic material |

## 2.5 Strengthening Mechanisms in Intermetallics

1. **Solid solution strengthening** — foreign atoms on the "wrong" sublattice create local disorder that pins dislocations.
2. **Precipitation hardening** — second-phase particles (e.g., γ′ in Ni-superalloys) obstruct dislocation motion.
3. **Anti-phase boundary (APB) strengthening** — dislocations moving through an ordered lattice create APBs; the APB energy acts as a drag force.
4. **Hall-Petch strengthening** — grain refinement; particularly effective because of limited slip systems.
5. **Kear-Wilsdorf lock formation** — cross-slip of screw dislocations onto {010} cube planes, leading to the anomalous yield behavior.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Atomic Packing Factor (APF) of the L1₂ (Cu₃Au) Structure

The APF is the ratio of the volume of atoms in a unit cell to the total unit cell volume. This is a frequent KTU derivation problem.

### Step 1 — Identify Atoms per Unit Cell
The L1₂ unit cell is FCC, with 4 atoms per unit cell (8 corners × 1/8 + 6 faces × 1/2 = 4). Of these:
- **1 atom** is Au (at corners) — placed on the **A sublattice**.
- **3 atoms** are Cu (on face centers) — placed on the **B sublattice**.

### Step 2 — Relate the Atomic Radius to the Lattice Parameter
In an FCC arrangement, atoms touch along the **face diagonal**. The face diagonal length is $a\sqrt{2}$, and contains 4 atomic radii (2 radii from each of the two face-centered atoms, plus 2 radii to the corner atoms — actually, the diagonal has 4r: ½ atom on one corner → 1r → face atom → 1r → ½ atom on opposite corner, wait, that's 2r; let me redo carefully).

Actually, in pure FCC, the face diagonal is $a\sqrt{2} = 4r$ (one full atom diameter at the center, plus 2 half-diameters at the corners). Therefore:

$$a\sqrt{2} = 4r \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \frac{4r}{\sqrt{2}} = 2\sqrt{2}\,r$$

### Step 3 — Compute the APF
The volume of 4 atoms (each of volume $\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$):

$$V_{\text{atoms}} = 4 \times \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 = \frac{16}{3}\pi r^3$$

The volume of the unit cell:

$$V_{\text{cell}} = a^3 = (2\sqrt{2}\,r)^3 = 16\sqrt{2}\,r^3$$

Therefore:

$$\text{APF}_{\text{L1}_2} = \frac{V_{\text{atoms}}}{V_{\text{cell}}} = \frac{\frac{16}{3}\pi r^3}{16\sqrt{2}\,r^3} = \frac{\pi}{3\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7405$$

> **This is the same APF as pure FCC** — a *key result*. The L1₂ structure is essentially an ordered FCC, and ordering does *not* change the packing efficiency. The KTU "twist" is that students often forget this fact.

## 3.2 Derivation: APF of the B2 (CsCl) Structure

### Step 1 — Atoms per Unit Cell
- 1 atom at corners (8 × 1/8 = 1).
- 1 atom at body center (1 × 1 = 1).
- **Total: 2 atoms.**

### Step 2 — Lattice Parameter
Atoms touch along the **body diagonal** $a\sqrt{3}$:

$$a\sqrt{3} = 2r_A + 2r_B$$

For equal atoms ($r_A = r_B = r$):

$$a\sqrt{3} = 4r \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \frac{4r}{\sqrt{3}}$$

### Step 3 — APF
$$V_{\text{atoms}} = 2 \times \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 = \frac{8}{3}\pi r^3$$

$$V_{\text{cell}} = a^3 = \left(\frac{4r}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^3 = \frac{64r^3}{3\sqrt{3}}$$

$$\text{APF}_{\text{B2}} = \frac{\frac{8}{3}\pi r^3}{\frac{64r^3}{3\sqrt{3}}} = \frac{8\pi \sqrt{3}}{64} = \frac{\pi\sqrt{3}}{8} \approx 0.6802$$

> **Result:** The B2 structure has APF ≈ 0.68, which equals that of pure BCC. This makes sense because B2 is an *ordered* BCC.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing L1₂, B2, and Laves Phases

| Property | **L1₂ (Ni₃Al)** | **B2 (NiAl)** | **C15 Laves (MgCu₂)** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Coordination number | 12 | 8 | 12 (Mg), 16 (Cu) |
| Slip systems | Many (12) | Few (~5) | Very few (~3) |
| Ductility at RT | **Ductile** | Brittle | Very brittle |
| Strength anomaly? | **Yes** (strong) | Mild | No |
| Density (g/cm³) | ~7.5 | ~5.9 | ~5.7–8.0 |
| Max use temperature (°C) | ~1100 | ~1600 | ~1200 |
| Key engineering use | Superalloy γ′ | Bond coats | High-T structural |

## 3.4 Derivation: Strength Anomaly in Ni₃Al (Yield Strength vs. Temperature)

For **Ni₃Al**, the yield strength $\sigma_y$ exhibits a peak around **600–800°C** before dropping. The Kear-Wilsdorf (1962) model explains this by thermally-activated cross-slip:

The energy barrier for cross-slip of a screw dislocation from {111} to {010} is given by:

$$\Delta E_{\text{KW}} = E_{\text{APB}}^{(111)} - E_{\text{APB}}^{(010)} > 0$$

Because $E_{\text{APB}}^{(010)} < E_{\text{APB}}^{(111)}$, cross-slip is energetically favorable once thermal activation provides the necessary energy. The locking probability is:

$$P_{\text{KW}} = \frac{b \cdot \Delta E_{\text{KW}}}{k_B T} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E_{\text{KW}}}{k_B T}\right)$$

This produces the empirical relationship:

$$\sigma_y(T) = \sigma_0 + A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E_{\text{KW}}}{k_B T}\right)$$

where:
- $\sigma_0$ is the athermal component.
- $A$ is a constant.
- $\Delta E_{\text{KW}}$ is the Kear-Wilsdorf lock energy.
- $k_B$ is the Boltzmann constant.

**Physical interpretation:** As $T$ increases, more dislocations become locked (sessile) on the {010} plane, *increasing* the macroscopic yield strength. This continues until very high temperatures where diffusional creep and climb take over, causing the strength to drop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Hook:**
> A common 7-mark question is: *"Explain the yield strength anomaly in Ni₃Al with reference to the Kear-Wilsdorf mechanism."* Memorize the equation above, sketch a $\sigma_y$ vs. $T$ curve, and label the Kear-Wilsdorf lock formation region.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map — Classification of Intermetallics

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Intermetallic Compounds] --> B[By Stoichiometry]
    A --> C[By Bonding]
    A --> D[By Engineering Class]
    B --> B1[Line Compounds e.g. NiAl]

    D4 --> E4[High-T creep resistant]

```

```mermaid
    stepA[Start: Two metallic melts A and B] --> stepB[Mix stoichiometric proportions]
    stepB --> stepC[Cast or melt under inert atmosphere]
    stepC --> stepD[Control cooling rate]

    stepE --> stepF[Ordered crystal structure forms e.g. L12 or B2]
    stepF --> stepG[Optional heat treatment annealing homogenization]

```

## 4.3 Comparative Decision Tree — Which Intermetallic to Choose?

```mermaid

    start{Application Requirement?}
    start --> p1[Max use temperature?]
    p1 --> p1a[Less than 800C: Ti3Al alpha-2]

    p1 --> p1c[Above 1100C: NiAl beta or MoSi2]

    p1b --> crit1
    p1c --> crit1
    crit1 --> crit1a[Yes: TiAl gamma phase]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Intermetallic Compounds. Mention two examples.
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**Model Answer:**

> Intermetallic compounds are **ordered solid-state phases** formed between two or more metals (or a metal and a metalloid) with a fixed or near-fixed stoichiometric ratio and a crystal structure that differs from those of the constituent elements. They are characterized by mixed metallic-covalent-ionic bonding and a long-range ordered arrangement of atoms on distinct sublattices.

**Examples:**
1. **Ni₃Al** — γ′ phase in Ni-based superalloys.
2. **TiAl** — γ phase used in jet engine low-pressure turbine blades.
3. **CuZn** — β-brass (any two of the above).

*(3 marks: definition 1.5 marks + 2 examples 0.75 each)*

---

### Question 2: What is meant by "Yield Strength Anomaly" in intermetallics?
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]**

**Model Answer:**

> The **yield strength anomaly** refers to the unusual phenomenon in certain intermetallics (most famously **Ni₃Al**) where the yield strength **increases with temperature** up to a critical temperature (typically 600–800°C) before eventually decreasing. This contradicts the typical behavior of metals, where strength always decreases with increasing temperature.

> The phenomenon is explained by the **Kear-Wilsdorf (1962) mechanism**: thermally activated cross-slip of $\frac{a}{2}\langle 110\rangle$ screw dislocations from the octahedral {111} plane to the cube {010} plane, where they become **sessile (locked)** and unable to glide, thereby raising the macroscopic flow stress.

*(3 marks: definition 1.5 marks + mechanism 1.5 marks)*

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Question A (a) [7 Marks]:**
Describe the **Hume-Rothery rules** governing the formation of intermetallics. Explain how the **valence electron concentration (VEC)** determines the stability of β-brass (CuZn), γ-brass, and ε-phases.

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Regular Exam]**

**Model Answer:**

The formation of intermetallic compounds is governed by a set of empirical conditions, many of which are extensions of the **Hume-Rothery rules** originally formulated for solid solutions. For intermetallic formation, the following are key:

**1. Atomic Size Factor:**
When atomic radii differ by **more than 15%**, complete solid solubility is hindered. The lattice often responds by *ordering* — segregating large and small atoms onto specific sublattices, thereby lowering the strain energy.

$$\delta = \left\vert \frac{r_A - r_B}{r_A} \right\vert \times 100\%$$

**2. Electronegativity:**
A moderate electronegativity difference (Δχ between 0.4 and 1.7 on the Pauling scale) introduces partial ionic character, stabilizing the ordered compound.

**3. Valence Electron Concentration (VEC):**
VEC is the **most decisive** factor for many intermetallics, particularly the **Hume-Rothery electron compounds**:

$$\text{VEC} = \frac{\sum n_i v_i}{\sum n_i}$$

For a binary compound A_xB_y with $x + y = 1$:

- If A has valence $V_A$ and B has valence $V_B$:

$$\text{VEC} = x V_A + y V_B$$

**Examples of Hume-Rothery Phases:**

| Phase | VEC | Examples | Crystal Structure |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| **β (β-brass)** | 3/2 = 1.50 | CuZn, CuBe, AgZn | B2 (CsCl) / BCC |
| **β′ (β-manganese)** | 3/2 = 1.50 | Cu₅Si, Ag₃Al | Complex cubic |
| **γ (γ-brass)** | 21/13 ≈ 1.615 | Cu₅Zn₈, Fe₅Zn₂₁ | Complex cubic (52 atoms/cell) |
| **ε (ε-phase)** | 7/4 = 1.75 | CuZn₅, AgZn₅ | Close-packed hexagonal |

**Physical origin:** The VEC determines the **Fermi surface-Brillouin zone interactions**. When the Fermi surface just touches certain Brillouin zone boundaries (e.g., {110} for β, {330}/{411} for γ), the energy is minimized and that structure becomes stable. This is called the **Hume-Rothery mechanism** or **Jones mechanism**.

**Conclusion:** Intermetallics form when atomic size mismatch, electronegativity difference, and especially VEC combine to favor a *new ordered structure* over a disordered solution.

---

**Question A (b) [7 Marks]:**
With a neat sketch, describe the **L1₂ (Cu₃Au) structure**. Discuss its role in **Ni-based superalloys** and explain why it imparts exceptional high-temperature strength.

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Regular Exam]**

**Model Answer:**

**L1₂ (Cu₃Au) Structure:**

The L1₂ structure is an **ordered face-centered cubic (FCC)** arrangement, with space group $Pm\bar{3}m$. The unit cell contains **4 atoms**:
- **1 Au-type atom** at the corner positions (8 × 1/8 = 1).
- **3 Cu-type atoms** at the face-centered positions (6 × 1/2 = 3).
- Stoichiometry: **Cu₃Au** (or A₃B in general).

**Lattice parameter and APF:**

$$a = 2\sqrt{2}\,r \quad ; \quad \text{APF} = \frac{\pi}{3\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7405$$

*(This is identical to pure FCC — ordering does not change packing efficiency.)*

**Coordination:**
- Each Cu atom has **4 Au + 8 Cu** nearest neighbors (12-fold coordination).
- Each Au atom has **12 Cu** nearest neighbors.

**Role in Ni-Based Superalloys:**

In Ni-superalloys, the **γ′ (gamma-prime) phase** has the L1₂ structure with composition **(Ni,Co,Cr)₃(Al,Ti)**. The γ′ precipitates are:
- **Coherent** with the γ (FCC Ni-rich) matrix.
- **Cuboidal** in shape, with low lattice misfit (~0.3% for modern alloys).
- Present at volume fractions of **50–70%** in advanced single-crystal superalloys.

**Why Exceptional High-Temperature Strength?**

1. **Anomalous yield behavior:** Through the **Kear-Wilsdorf mechanism**, the strength of γ′ *increases* with temperature up to ~800°C.
2. **Anti-phase boundary (APB) energy:** $\frac{a}{2}\langle 110\rangle$ dislocations shearing γ′ create APBs, which cost energy proportional to the APB energy. This raises the critical resolved shear stress (CRSS).
3. **Coherency strains:** The slight lattice misfit between γ and γ′ produces coherency strain fields that impede dislocation motion.
4. **Cutting and bypass mechanisms:** At small precipitate sizes, dislocations *cut* the precipitates (strengthening $\propto \sqrt{r}$). At large sizes, they *bypass* them by Orowan looping (strengthening $\propto 1/r$). The peak occurs at the critical size.
5. **Low diffusivity at high T:** The ordered structure suppresses diffusion-controlled creep.

**Engineering applications:** γ′ is the principal strengthening phase in **first-generation** single-crystal superalloys (e.g., CMSX-4, PWA 1480) used in jet engine high-pressure turbine blades operating at temperatures up to **1100°C**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**Question B (a) [7 Marks]:**
Compare the **L1₂ (Cu₃Au)** and **B2 (CsCl)** structures of intermetallics. Discuss the **slip systems**, **ductility**, and **temperature dependence of yield strength** in each.

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | **L1₂ (Cu₃Au type)** | **B2 (CsCl type)** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Prototype | Cu₃Au | CsCl |
| Underlying Bravais lattice | FCC | BCC (ordered) |
| Space group | $Pm\bar{3}m$ | $Pm\bar{3}m$ |
| Atoms per unit cell | 4 (1 A + 3 B) | 2 (1 A + 1 B) |
| Stoichiometry | A₃B | AB |
| Coordination number | 12 | 8 |
| APF | $\frac{\pi}{3\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.74$ | $\frac{\pi\sqrt{3}}{8} \approx 0.68$ |
| Slip system at RT | {111}⟨110⟩ (12 systems) | {110}⟨111⟩ (12 systems, but limited in ordered form) |
| Ductility at RT | **Ductile to moderately ductile** | **Brittle** |
| Yield strength vs. T | **Anomalous increase** with T (Kear-Wilsdorf) | Mild anomaly; generally decreases with T |
| Examples | Ni₃Al, Cu₃Au, Al₃Sc | NiAl, CuZn (β-brass), FeAl |

**Key Engineering Insights:**

- **L1₂ is more ductile at RT** because FCC offers more independent slip systems (5) and the Burgers vector of partial dislocations in the ordered lattice preserves long-range order.
- **B2 is more brittle** because only a few independent slip systems are available in the ordered lattice, and {110}⟨111⟩ dislocations are not always easy to activate in compounds like NiAl.
- **L1₂ exhibits the yield strength anomaly**; B2 typically does not (or only mildly).
- **B2 has higher melting points** and better oxidation resistance — making it suitable for **bond coats** in thermal barrier coatings (TBCs) on turbine blades.

---

**Question B (b) [7 Marks]:**
Discuss the **Kear-Wilsdorf mechanism** for the yield strength anomaly in **Ni₃Al**. Sketch the $\sigma_y$ vs. T curve and identify the temperature regime of maximum strength.

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]**

**Model Answer:**

**The Kear-Wilsdorf Mechanism (1962):**

In disordered FCC metals, the dislocation Burgers vector is $\frac{a}{2}\langle 110\rangle$ and the slip plane is the close-packed {111}. In ordered L1₂ Ni₃Al, the Burgers vector remains $\frac{a}{2}\langle 110\rangle$, but the **slip behavior is more complex**.

A pair of $\frac{a}{2}\langle 110\rangle$ superpartial dislocations separated by an **anti-phase boundary (APB)** is required to shear the ordered lattice. The lead superpartial creates the APB; the trailing superpartial restores order.

**Mechanism Steps:**

1. At low temperatures, the dislocations glide on {111} planes.
2. As temperature increases, **thermal activation** enables the **leading screw superpartial** to cross-slip from the {111} octahedral plane onto the **non-close-packed {010} cube plane**.
3. The {010} plane has a **lower APB energy** than {111}, so this cross-slip is energetically favorable.
4. Once on {010}, the superpartial becomes **sessile (locked)** — it can no longer glide.
5. The trailing superpartial is also dragged into a locked configuration, forming a **Kear-Wilsdorf (KW) lock**.
6. KW locks **obstruct further dislocation motion**, raising the macroscopic flow stress.
7. With increasing temperature, the **density of KW locks increases**, hence the strength increases.
8. At very high temperatures, **diffusional creep** and **dislocation climb** dominate, and the strength finally decreases.

**Yield Strength vs. Temperature Curve:**

```
σ_y
  ↑
  │            ╭──────╮  ← Peak strength
  │           ╱        ╲     (KW lock saturation)
  │          ╱          ╲
  │         ╱            ╲
  │        ╱              ╲
  │       ╱                ╲  ← Creep-dominated
  │      ╱                  ╲
  │     ╱                    ╲
  │____╱______________________╲______→
  0    200   400   600   800  1000  T (°C)
                       ↑
                Maximum strength
                (Kear-Wilsdorf peak)
```

**Temperature of Peak Strength:** Typically **600–800°C** for stoichiometric Ni₃Al.

**Engineering Significance:** This anomalous behavior is *the* reason Ni₃Al-based superalloys are the workhorse material for **jet engine hot-end components**. Conventional superalloys would have already lost most of their strength at these temperatures, but Ni₃Al becomes *stronger*.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Common Mistakes in 14-Mark Answers:**
> 1. **Confusing the APF of L1₂ with B2.** L1₂ has APF = 0.74 (FCC); B2 has APF = 0.68 (BCC). Examiners deduct 1 mark if you mix them up.
> 2. **Forgetting the stoichiometry.** L1₂ is A₃B (4 atoms/cell); B2 is AB (2 atoms/cell). This is a *favourite* 1-mark trick question.
> 3. **Mishandling the Kear-Wilsdorf explanation.** Many students write "dislocations cross-slip" without specifying *which planes* ({111} → {010}) and *why* (lower APB energy on {010}). Always include both.
> 4. **Drawing the σ–T curve incorrectly.** The curve must *rise* before peaking, not just be a straight declining line. If the peak is missing, lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Not citing Strukturbericht notation.** KTU examiners expect terms like **B2, L1₂, DO₃, DO₁₉** by name, not just "FCC-ordered" or "BCC-ordered."

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Intermetallics (KTU PEMRT526 Module 4):**

### ✅ Core Definitions
- ☐ Intermetallic = **ordered, stoichiometric, distinct crystal structure** — *not* a solid solution.
- ☐ Mixed bonding: **metallic + covalent + ionic**.
- ☐ **Strukturbericht notation**: B2, L1₂, L2₁, DO₃, DO₁₉, C14/C15/C36.

### ✅ Key Crystal Structures
- ☐ **L1₂ (Cu₃Au)**: FCC, 4 atoms/cell, A₃B, APF = 0.74.
- ☐ **B2 (CsCl)**: BCC-ordered, 2 atoms/cell, AB, APF = 0.68.
- ☐ **C15 (MgCu₂)**: Laves phase, AB₂, FCC, complex (24 atoms/cell).

### ✅ Formation Rules
- ☐ Size mismatch ≤ 15% favors ordering (not solid solution).
- ☐ VEC = 3/2 (β), 21/13 (γ), 7/4 (ε) — **Hume-Rothery electron compounds**.
- ☐ Moderate Δχ (0.4–1.7) stabilizes ordered phases.

### ✅ Properties
- ☐ **Yield strength anomaly** in Ni₃Al (peak at 600–800°C).
- ☐ **Kear-Wilsdorf mechanism**: {111} → {010} cross-slip; APB energy.
- ☐ Generally **brittle at RT** (except L1₂, e.g., Ni₃Al).
- ☐ **High melting point, low density, oxidation resistant**.

### ✅ Engineering Examples (Memorize At Least 5)
- ☐ **Ni₃Al** (γ′) — turbine blades.
- ☐ **NiAl** (β) — TBC bond coats.
- ☐ **TiAl** (γ) — turbocharger rotors, LPT blades.
- ☐ **Fe₃Al** (DO₃) — structural, sulfidation-resistant.
- ☐ **MgCu₂** (C15 Laves) — high-T creep-resistant.
- ☐ **MoSi₂** — heating elements (up to 1800°C).
- ☐ **NiTi (Nitinol)** — shape memory alloys.
- ☐ **Nd₂Fe₁₄B** — permanent magnets (highest energy product).

### ✅ Equations to Memorize
- ☐ $\text{APF}_{\text{L1}_2} = \dfrac{\pi}{3\sqrt{2}}$
- ☐ $\text{APF}_{\text{B2}} = \dfrac{\pi\sqrt{3}}{8}$
- ☐ $\delta = \left\vert \dfrac{r_A - r_B}{r_A} \right\vert \times 100\%$
- ☐ $\text{VEC} = \dfrac{\sum n_i v_i}{\sum n_i}$

### ✅ Frequently Asked Concepts
- ☐ Why are IMCs brittle at room temperature? *(Limited slip systems, APBs, mixed bonding.)*
- ☐ Why is Ni₃Al strong at high T? *(Yield strength anomaly + KW locking.)*
- ☐ What is an APB? *(Anti-phase boundary — a region of disorder in an ordered lattice.)*
- ☐ Why is the C15 Laves phase brittle? *(Complex crystal structure with few slip systems.)*

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Intermetallics**

topics.title: Maraging Steel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT526 - METALLURGY AND MATERIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Maraging Steel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maraging Steel** is a class of **ultra-high-strength, low-carbon (typically C < 0.03 wt%) martensitic steel** that derives its exceptional mechanical properties not from carbon strengthening, but from the precipitation of finely dispersed **intermetallic compounds** during a low-temperature aging (precipitation hardening) treatment applied to a nearly carbon-free, soft, metastable martensitic matrix.

The term **"maraging"** is a portmanteau of **MAR**tensite + **AGING**, reflecting the two-stage strengthening philosophy: (1) formation of a tough, ductile **lath martensite** on cooling, followed by (2) age-hardening by **coherent/semi-coherent intermetallic precipitates** (e.g., Ni₃Ti, Ni₃Mo, Fe₂Mo, NiAl).

## 1.2 Standard Chemical Composition (18Ni-300 Grade)

| Element | Wt % | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Ni** | **18%** | Stabilizes martensite; lowers Ms temperature |
| **Co** | **8.5%** | Strengthens matrix, enhances precipitate formation |
| **Mo** | **5.0%** | Forms Ni₃Mo / Fe₂Mo precipitates |
| **Ti** | **0.75%** | Forms Ni₃Ti — primary strengthener |
| **Al** | **0.10%** | Forms Ni₃Al; refines precipitates |
| **C** | **< 0.03%** | Intentionally minimal to avoid carbide brittleness |
| **Fe** | Balance | Parent matrix element |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Metric:** Maraging steel **18Ni-300** has a nominal **ultimate tensile strength (UTS) ≈ 300 ksi (≈ 2068 MPa)** and yield strength ≈ 290 ksi (≈ 2000 MPa), while still maintaining **fracture toughness (K_IC) of 80–110 MPa·√m** — a combination unattainable in conventional quenched-and-tempered carbon steels.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Karate Master Trainee":**
> Imagine a young martial artist (the **soft martensite matrix**) with superb reflexes and flexibility (high ductility/toughness), but lacking raw striking power. Through a strict training regimen (**aging heat treatment**), the master develops tiny, well-coordinated "muscle knots" — analogous to **intermetallic precipitates** distributed uniformly throughout the body. These knots do **not** make the master brittle (as would be the case with massive carbides in high-carbon steel), but rather give **focused, uniform, elastic strength**. The art of maraging is precisely this: the precipitates are **sub-nanometre to nanometre scale**, coherent with the matrix, distributing stress evenly without creating crack-initiation sites.

## 1.4 Why Maraging Steel Exists — The Engineering Problem

Conventional high-strength steels (AISI 4340, H13, etc.) achieve strength through **carbon + martensite**, but pay a heavy price:
- High dislocation density → **embrittlement**
- Carbide precipitation at grain boundaries → **stress corrosion cracking**
- Weldability is poor due to **hardenability** and **hydrogen-induced cracking**

Maraging steel **decouples** strength from carbon: it gets its **lath martensite from Ni (not from C)**, and its **strength from intermetallic precipitates (not carbides)**. This yields:
- **Excellent weldability** (no hard martensite zone in HAZ)
- **Superior dimensional stability** during aging (minimal distortion)
- **High fracture toughness** even at 300 ksi strength level
- **Easy machinability in the solution-annealed (soft) state**
