# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Stage Processing Route

### Stage 1 — Solution Annealing (Softening Treatment)
- **Temperature:** 820 °C – 850 °C (austenite single-phase region for 18Ni steels).
- **Hold time:** 1 hour per 25 mm of section thickness (homogenisation).
- **Cooling:** Air cool to room temperature.
- **Result:** Fully retained **lath martensite (BCT, but with very low tetragonality due to near-zero C)**, soft (~30–35 HRC), ductile, easily machinable.

> [!NOTE]
> The martensite start temperature (M_s) in 18Ni-300 is approximately **155–200 °C**, well above room temperature — so **100% martensite** forms simply on air cooling. No quenching medium is required, which is a major industrial advantage.

### Stage 2 — Aging (Precipitation Hardening)
- **Temperature:** 480 °C – 510 °C (typical: **485 °C ± 5 °C** for 18Ni-300).
- **Hold time:** **3 to 12 hours** (typically 5 h for aerospace grade).
- **Cooling:** Air cool to RT.
- **Result:** Formation of nanometre-scale intermetallic precipitates. Hardness rises to **~52–55 HRC**, UTS to **~300 ksi**.

### Stage 3 — Optional Sub-zero / Stress Relief
- For critical dimensional tolerance components (e.g., tooling for die-casting), a **sub-zero soak in liquid nitrogen (−196 °C)** is sometimes applied to transform any retained austenite that may have formed upon re-heating during aging.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Precipitates

During aging, **Co acts as a catalyst** by reducing the solubility of Mo and Ti in the martensitic matrix, **increasing the volume fraction of precipitates** without coarsening them. The principal strengthening precipitates are:

| Precipitate | Crystal Structure | Diameter | Strengthening Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ni₃Ti (η-phase)** | Hexagonal (D0₂₄) | 10–30 nm | Coherent → semi-coherent; Orowan looping past critical size |
| **Ni₃Mo** | Orthorhombic | 5–15 nm | Coherency strain hardening |
| **Fe₂Mo (μ-phase)** | Rhombohedral | Coarse; forms on over-aging | Loss of coherency → softening |
| **NiAl (B2)** | BCC (CsCl-type) | 5–20 nm | Coherency + modulus mismatch |

## 2.3 Strengthening Contributions (Orowan + Shear Mechanism)

The yield strength of maraging steel can be approximated as:

$$\sigma_{y} = \sigma_{0} + \sigma_{ss} + \sigma_{ppt} + \sigma_{disl} + \sigma_{gb}$$

where each term represents a different strengthening contribution:
- $\sigma_{0}$ — lattice friction stress of Fe (~50 MPa)
- $\sigma_{ss}$ — solid solution strengthening from Ni, Co, Mo
- $\sigma_{ppt}$ — **precipitation strengthening (dominant)**
- $\sigma_{disl}$ — dislocation strengthening
- $\sigma_{gb}$ — Hall-Petch grain boundary contribution

For **fine, coherent, shearable precipitates** (radius $r$ < critical), the strengthening is governed by:

$$\sigma_{ppt} = \frac{0.13 \, G \, b}{\lambda} \ln\left(\frac{r}{b}\right)$$

For **non-shearable precipitates** (Orowan looping, $r$ > critical):

$$\sigma_{Orowan} = \frac{0.4 \, M \, G \, b}{\pi \sqrt{1-\nu}} \cdot \frac{\ln(2r/b)}{\lambda}$$

where:
- $G$ = shear modulus of Fe matrix (~80 GPa)
- $b$ = Burgers vector magnitude (0.248 nm for α-Fe)
- $M$ = Taylor factor (~2.75 for BCC)
- $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio (0.33)
- $\lambda$ = inter-precipitate spacing
- $r$ = mean precipitate radius

## 2.4 Critical Aging Phenomena (Examiner's Favourite!)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Under-aging:** Insufficient time/temperature → low volume fraction of precipitates → low strength.
> **Peak aging:** Optimum precipitate size + distribution → maximum hardness.
> **Over-aging:** Coarsening of precipitates, loss of coherency, possible formation of equilibrium phases (Fe₂Mo, Laves phase) → strength loss and embrittlement.
>
> The activation energy for coarsening follows the **Ostwald ripening (LSW) equation**:
> $$r^3 - r_0^3 = K \cdot t$$
> where $K \propto D \gamma_{int} V_m / (RT)$, $D$ = diffusivity, $\gamma_{int}$ = interfacial energy, $V_m$ = molar volume.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_{UTS} \approx 300$ ksi (18Ni-300) | Industry benchmark strength | MPa, ksi |
| 2 | $M_s = 550 - 350(\%C) - 35(\%Mn) - 17(\%Ni) - 21(\%Mo)$ | Martensite start temperature | °C |
| 3 | $r^3 - r_0^3 = K t$ (LSW) | Particle coarsening kinetics | nm³, s |
| 4 | $\sigma_{Orowan} \propto \frac{Gb}{\lambda}\ln\left(\frac{2r}{b}\right)$ | Precipitate strengthening | MPa |
| 5 | $K_{IC} = Y \sigma_f \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Fracture toughness (Irwin) | MPa·√m |
| 6 | $HRC \approx \frac{UTS (MPa)}{9.81 \times 0.344}$ | Approx. hardness–strength conversion | HRC, MPa |
| 7 | $V_f^{ppt} \approx \frac{0.01 \cdot \rho_{precipitate}}{\rho_{matrix}}$ | Volume fraction of precipitates | % |
| 8 | $t_{opt} \approx \frac{\pi r^2 N_{Av}}{4 D \, C_0}$ | Optimum aging time (Avrami-type) | s |
| 9 | $\Delta T_{aging} \approx 0.4 T_m$ | Typical aging temperature (K) | K |
| 10 | $\varepsilon_{f} \approx 8 - 12\%$ (peak aged) | Total elongation at failure | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical rule of thumb for design:** Each **1 wt% increase in Ti** adds ~**50–70 MPa** in yield strength, but reduces fracture toughness. Each **1 wt% Co** adds ~**30–50 MPa** and improves toughness up to ~10% Co.

## 2.6 Engineering Applications

- **Aerospace:** Rocket motor casings, landing-gear components, helicopter rotor shafts.
- **Tooling:** Die-casting dies, injection moulds (for aluminium/zinc alloys), extrusion tooling, forging dies — where dimensional stability is critical.
- **Defence:** Armour-piercing core material, missile bodies, gun barrels.
- **Nuclear:** Reactor pressure-vessel internals (because of low Co-induced radioactivity when made with **Co-free** grades).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimum Aging Time (Avrami-Type Approach)

The fraction transformed $X$ during aging follows the **Johnson–Mehl–Avrami–Kolmogorov (JMAK)** equation:

$$X(t) = 1 - \exp(-k t^{n})$$

where $k$ is a temperature-dependent rate constant, $n$ is the Avrami exponent (typically $n = 1.5$ for diffusion-controlled growth of precipitates from a supersaturated martensitic matrix).

For **peak aging** at the temperature where the **strength vs. time** curve reaches its maximum:

$$\frac{d\sigma_{ppt}}{dt} = 0$$

Assuming $\sigma_{ppt} \propto X(t)$ and combining with the Orowan relation, the optimum time $t_{opt}$ is:

$$t_{opt} = \left(\frac{\ln 2}{k}\right)^{1/n}$$

### Numerical Example — 18Ni-300 aged at 485 °C

Given:
- $k = 1.05 \times 10^{-4} \; s^{-1.5}$ (empirical, for 485 °C)
- $n = 1.5$

$$t_{opt} = \left(\frac{0.693}{1.05 \times 10^{-4}}\right)^{1/1.5}$$

$$t_{opt} = \left(6{,}600 \; s^{1.5}\right)^{2/3}$$

$$t_{opt} = 6{,}600^{0.6667} \approx 351 \; s \approx 5.85 \; \text{hours}$$

This matches the industry standard of **5–6 h aging at 485 °C** for peak hardness in 18Ni-300 — confirming the model.

## 3.2 Derivation: Strength–Toughness Trade-off (Ashby Plot Logic)

Fracture toughness $K_{IC}$ in maraging steel generally follows a **linear inverse relationship** with yield strength in the peak-aged condition:

$$K_{IC} = K_0 - \alpha \cdot \sigma_{y}$$

where:
- $K_0 \approx 220 \; \text{MPa}\sqrt{m}$ (intercept for pure Fe-Ni martensite)
- $\alpha \approx 0.07 \; \text{MPa}\sqrt{m}/\text{MPa}$ (slope; depends on cleanliness, S/P content)

**Sample calculation:** For $\sigma_y = 2000 \; \text{MPa}$,

$$K_{IC} = 220 - 0.07 \times 2000 = 220 - 140 = 80 \; \text{MPa}\sqrt{m}$$

which matches the typical quoted value of **80–110 MPa·√m** for 18Ni-300.

## 3.3 Derivation: Effect of Cobalt on Precipitate Volume Fraction

Cobalt does not form its own precipitates in maraging steel. Instead, it acts by:
1. Lowering the **solubility of Mo and Ti in α-Fe**.
2. Increasing the **chemical driving force** for nucleation.

The increased volume fraction $V_f$ can be approximated by the **Gibbs-Thomson modified driving force**:

$$\Delta G_{v}^{eff} = \Delta G_{v}^{0} - \frac{2 \gamma_{int} V_m}{r}$$

For each **1 wt% Co** addition, empirical data shows:
- $V_f$ of Ni₃Ti increases by ~**0.6%**
- $V_f$ of Ni₃Mo increases by ~**0.3%**

Combined yield-strength increment per 1% Co:

$$\Delta \sigma_y \approx 0.6 \times 1500 + 0.3 \times 1200 \approx 1260 \; \text{MPa/wt\%}$$

After normalising by typical **1.6 GPa total solid-solution strength**, the practical observed increment is ~**30–50 MPa per 1% Co**, as noted earlier. The discrepancy is because the simple additivity above ignores **overlap of stress fields** between adjacent precipitates and the law-of-mixtures saturation effect.

## 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example — Selecting a Maraging Grade

**Problem:** A landing-gear actuator housing requires:
- Minimum UTS = **275 ksi (1895 MPa)**
- Minimum $K_{IC}$ = **70 MPa·√m**
- Must be welded
- Maximum weight

**Step 1 — Eliminate carbon steels** (4340, 300M):
Carbon steels have $K_{IC} < 50$ MPa·√m at 1895 MPa → **disqualified**.

**Step 2 — Select maraging grade:**
- 18Ni-250: UTS = 250 ksi, $K_{IC}$ = 110 MPa·√m → UTS insufficient
- **18Ni-300: UTS = 300 ksi, $K_{IC}$ = 80–110 MPa·√m → SATISFACTORY**
- 18Ni-350: UTS = 350 ksi, $K_{IC}$ = 35–50 MPa·√m → toughness too low

**Step 3 — Select over-aged or peak-aged?**
Since the requirement is only 275 ksi, a slight **over-aging at 510 °C for 8 h** would reduce UTS to ~285 ksi and increase $K_{IC}$ to ~120 MPa·√m — a more robust choice. **Decision: 18Ni-300, slightly over-aged.**

**Step 4 — Confirm weldability:** Maraging steel can be welded by **TIG with matching filler (18Ni-300 wire)** followed by **post-weld aging at 485 °C/5 h** with no preheat required — **CONFIRMED**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define maraging steel. Why is it called "mar-aging"? Explain the role of cobalt in its strengthening.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Maraging steel is a class of ultra-high-strength martensitic steel with very low carbon (< 0.03 wt%) and 18–25% Ni, which derives its strength from the precipitation of intermetallic compounds during aging. (1 mark)

The term "mar-aging" = **MAR**tensite + **AGING**, because the steel is first converted to soft, ductile lath martensite (on air cooling) and then hardened by aging at ~485 °C. (1 mark)

Cobalt does not form its own precipitates but **lowers the solubility of Mo and Ti in the matrix**, thereby increasing the **volume fraction of Ni₃Ti and Ni₃Mo** precipitates and enhancing strength by ~30–50 MPa per wt% Co. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any three intermetallic precipitates responsible for strengthening in 18Ni-300 maraging steel. What happens during over-aging?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Three strengthening intermetallic precipitates: **Ni₃Ti (η-phase)**, **Ni₃Mo**, and **NiAl (B2 phase)**. (1.5 marks — 0.5 each)

During over-aging, the precipitates **coarsen (Ostwald ripening)**, lose coherency with the matrix, and transform into equilibrium phases such as **Fe₂Mo (Laves phase)**. This results in **loss of strength**, but the **fracture toughness may improve** because coarse, incoherent particles are less efficient obstacles to dislocation motion and do not nucleate microvoids as readily. (1.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat flow diagram, describe the **heat treatment sequence** for 18Ni-300 maraging steel. Explain the metallurgical changes occurring at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **role of Ni, Co, Mo, and Ti** in the design of maraging steels. Compare the properties of maraging steel with **AISI 4340 steel** of equivalent strength. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Heat Treatment Sequence for 18Ni-300:**

| Stage | Temperature | Time | Cooling | Microstructure / Hardness | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Solution Annealing | 820–850 °C | 1 h per 25 mm | Air cool | Single-phase austenite → soft lath martensite (BCT, low C); **~30–35 HRC** | 2 |
| 2. Aging | 480–510 °C (typ. 485 °C) | 3–12 h (typ. 5 h) | Air cool | Precipitation of Ni₃Ti, Ni₃Mo, NiAl in martensite; **~52–55 HRC** | 3 |
| 3. (Optional) Sub-zero | −196 °C (LN₂) | 1 h | Slow warm-up | Converts retained austenite → martensite | 1 |
| 4. (Optional) Stress Relief | 400 °C | 1 h | Air cool | Reduces residual stresses from machining | 1 |

**Metallurgical changes:**
- **Solution annealing:** All alloying elements (Ni, Co, Mo, Ti) dissolve in γ-austenite; on air cooling, the γ → α' transformation occurs below M_s (~180 °C), forming soft, ductile, low-tetragonality lath martensite — the "ready-to-machine" state.
- **Aging:** A diffusion-controlled precipitation process. Supersaturated martensite rejects Mo, Ti atoms, forming nanometre-scale coherent/semi-coherent precipitates. The increase in hardness is due to **coherency strain fields** and **dislocation–precipitate interaction (Orowan mechanism)**.
- **Sub-zero:** Eliminates any retained γ-austenite that may have re-formed during aging (reversion), improving dimensional stability.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Roles of Alloying Elements:**

- **Ni (18%):** Provides the martensitic structure without carbon. **Lowers M_s** to ~180 °C (so 100% martensite forms on air cooling), increases **toughness** by suppressing brittle carbide networks, and acts as the **primary matrix former** in precipitates (Ni₃Ti, Ni₃Mo, NiAl).
- **Co (8.5%):** A **"precipitation catalyst"** — does not form its own precipitates but **reduces the solubility of Mo and Ti in the α-Fe matrix**, increasing the **driving force for nucleation** and hence the **volume fraction of strengthening precipitates**. Also raises the M_s, allowing more complete martensite transformation.
- **Mo (5%):** Forms **Ni₃Mo** and **Fe₂Mo (Laves)** — both effective strengtheners. Provides **secondary hardening** (i.e., resistance to over-aging / temper resistance). Improves **corrosion resistance** and **pitting resistance**.
- **Ti (0.75%):** Forms **Ni₃Ti (η-phase)** — the **most potent strengthener** in 18Ni maraging steel, contributing the largest single increment in yield strength.

**Comparison with AISI 4340 (UTS ≈ 300 ksi, quenched & tempered):**

| Property | 18Ni-300 Maraging | AISI 4340 (Q&T) |
|---|---|---|
| **Carbon content** | < 0.03% | 0.40% |
| **Hardening mechanism** | Intermetallic precipitation | Martensite + carbide dispersion |
| **Weldability** | Excellent (no preheat) | Poor (requires preheat + PWHT) |
| **Distortion after aging** | Negligible | Significant |
| **Fracture toughness at 300 ksi** | 80–110 MPa·√m | 30–55 MPa·√m |
| **Stress-corrosion cracking** | Low susceptibility | High susceptibility |
| **Machinability (soft state)** | Excellent | Difficult (already hardened) |
| **Cost** | ~5–8× higher | Lower |
| **Service temperature** | Limited to ~400 °C (over-aging) | Up to ~250 °C |

(2 marks for element roles, 3 marks for comparison table, 2 marks for conclusion)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Internal Choice]

**(a)** Explain the **Orowan looping mechanism** and how it contributes to the yield strength of maraging steel. Derive the Orowan stress expression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **effect of aging temperature and time** on the hardness, strength, and fracture toughness of 18Ni-300. Sketch the relevant curves. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Orowan Looping Mechanism:**

When a precipitate particle is **non-shearable** (i.e., its critical resolved shear stress to cut the particle is greater than the stress required to bow a dislocation between particles), the dislocation bypasses the particle by **bowing around it** and leaving behind an **Orowan dislocation loop**. (1 mark)

Each bypass event:
1. Increases the **dislocation line length** → raises the elastic energy.
2. Creates a **back-stress** on subsequent dislocations → macroscopic increase in yield stress. (1 mark)

**Derivation of Orowan Stress:**

Consider a dislocation of line tension $T \approx 0.5 G b^2$ moving past a row of particles of radius $r$ separated by spacing $\lambda$.

The dislocation bows into a circular arc of radius $R$. At the **critical bowing condition**, the radius equals the half-spacing:

$$R = \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

The force balance on a segment of length $L$ gives the **critical resolved shear stress (CRSS)**:

$$\tau_{Orowan} = \frac{T}{b R} = \frac{0.5 G b^2}{b \cdot \lambda/2} = \frac{G b}{\lambda}$$

Including the Taylor factor $M$ (for converting shear stress to tensile yield stress) and the logarithmic correction for finite dislocation core radius:

$$\sigma_{Orowan} = \frac{0.4 \, M \, G \, b}{\pi \sqrt{1 - \nu}} \cdot \frac{\ln(2 r / b)}{\lambda}$$

where:
- $G$ = shear modulus (80 GPa for Fe)
- $b$ = Burgers vector (0.248 nm)
- $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio (0.33)
- $M$ = Taylor factor (≈ 2.75)
- $\lambda$ = inter-precipitate spacing (≈ 30–100 nm in maraging steel)
- $r$ = precipitate radius (≈ 10 nm)

**Numerical evaluation for 18Ni-300 (peak aged):**

With $\lambda = 50 \; \text{nm}$, $r = 10 \; \text{nm}$:

$$\frac{\ln(2 \times 10 / 0.248)}{\sqrt{1 - 0.33}} = \frac{\ln(80.6)}{0.819} = \frac{4.39}{0.819} = 5.36$$

$$\sigma_{Orowan} = \frac{0.4 \times 2.75 \times 80{,}000 \times 0.248 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi} \times \frac{5.36}{50 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\sigma_{Orowan} = 8.69 \times 10^{3} \times 1.07 \times 10^{5} = 930 \; \text{MPa}$$

Adding $\sigma_0 + \sigma_{ss} + \sigma_{disl} \approx 1070 \; \text{MPa}$ gives a **total yield strength of ~2000 MPa**, which matches the experimental 18Ni-300 value. (2 marks for derivation, 1 mark for plugging in numbers, 2 marks for physical interpretation)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Effect of Aging Temperature:**

At **low aging temperatures (< 400 °C):** Diffusion is too slow; insufficient precipitates nucleate. Hardness remains at ~35 HRC (under-aged). (1 mark)

At **optimum temperature (480–510 °C):** Peak nucleation rate + moderate growth → fine, coherent precipitates (5–20 nm) → **maximum hardness (~55 HRC)** and **maximum yield strength (~2000 MPa)**. (1.5 marks)

At **high temperatures (> 540 °C):** Coarsening (Ostwald ripening) dominates. Precipitates grow to >50 nm, lose coherency, transform to equilibrium Laves phase (Fe₂Mo). **Hardness drops**, but **toughness may increase** because coarse particles are less effective at nucleating microvoids. (1.5 marks)

**Effect of Aging Time (at 485 °C):**

```
Hardness (HRC)
55 |          ___________
   |         /           \
50 |        /             \
   |       /               \  <-- over-age
45 |      /                 \
   |     /                   \____
40 |____/                          (peak-aged plateau)
   |____/________________________
   0    2    4    6   8   10  12   Time (h)
        ↑       ↑
    under-age   peak-age
```

(1.5 marks for the curve description)

**Fracture toughness behaviour:** $K_{IC}$ is **highest in the under-aged and over-aged conditions**, **lowest at peak aging** (because the high density of fine coherent particles is efficient at nucleating microvoids that coalesce into cracks). The trade-off curve is a **mirror image** of the hardness curve. (1.5 marks)

**Practical implication:** For components requiring **fracture-critical performance** (e.g., aerospace primary structure), designers often specify a **slight over-aging** to gain 10–20% toughness at a small (5–10%) strength cost. (1 mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "maraging" with "martensitic stainless steel"** (e.g., 17-4 PH). Maraging steels have **near-zero C** and are strengthened by **intermetallics**, NOT carbides. (–2 marks if confused)
> 2. **Do NOT list Fe₃C as a precipitate** in maraging steel — it is essentially absent.
> 3. **For the Orowan derivation, students often forget the $M$ (Taylor) factor** and the logarithmic core-radius correction. Both are mandatory in the KTU marking scheme.
> 4. **Aging curve is a hardness-vs-time plot at CONSTANT temperature** (NOT a TTT diagram). Many students draw a TTT curve and lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Remember to specify units** in numerical substitutions (MPa·√m for $K_{IC}$, GPa for modulus, nm for precipitate size).
> 6. **In the 4340 comparison**, students often omit the **weldability** advantage of maraging steel — this is a high-yield 2-mark point.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Maraging Steel (Module 4):**
>
> - **Definition:** Ultra-high-strength, **low-carbon (< 0.03%)**, Ni–Co–Mo–Ti martensitic steel, strengthened by **intermetallic precipitation** during aging.
> - **Naming:** **MAR**tensite + **AGING** (two-stage process).
> - **Key grades:** 18Ni-250, **18Ni-300** (most common), 18Ni-350 (highest strength, lowest toughness).
> - **Composition fingerprint:** 18% Ni, 8.5% Co, 5% Mo, 0.75% Ti, 0.1% Al, < 0.03% C.
> - **Solution annealing:** 820–850 °C, 1 h/25 mm, **air cool** → soft lath martensite (~30–35 HRC, easily machinable).
> - **Aging:** **485 °C / 5 h** (peak-aged) → ~52–55 HRC, UTS ≈ 300 ksi.
> - **Strengthening precipitates:** **Ni₃Ti (η)** — primary; **Ni₃Mo**; **NiAl (B2)**; **Fe₂Mo (Laves)** — only on over-aging.
> - **Co's role:** Catalyses precipitation by lowering Mo/Ti solubility; does NOT form its own precipitates.
> - **Orowan mechanism:** Dominant at peak aging; $\sigma_{Orowan} \propto (Gb/\lambda) \ln(2r/b)$.
> - **Coarsening (LSW) law:** $r^3 - r_0^3 = K t$ — governs over-aging.
> - **Properties:** UTS = 300 ksi, $K_{IC}$ = 80–110 MPa·√m, ~8–12% elongation.
> - **Advantages over 4340:** Better weldability, higher toughness, lower distortion, simpler heat treatment (no quench), no preheat.
> - **Disadvantages:** **5–8× costlier** (vacuum induction melting, high Ni/Co content), limited service temperature (~400 °C max).
> - **Applications:** Rocket motor casings, aircraft landing gear, die-casting dies, armour-piercing cores, missile bodies, nuclear reactor internals.
> - **Critical "Don'ts":** Do not quench (no carbon to require it); do not weld without post-weld aging; do not over-age unless toughness is critical.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Titanium - Ceramics - Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT526 - METALLURGY AND MATERIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Titanium - Ceramics - Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🏗️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Titanium — Definition & The "Aerospace Metal" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Titanium (Ti)* is a transition metal (Atomic Number **22**, Group IVB, Period 4) characterized by two allotropic crystal forms — **α-Titanium (HCP, stable below 882.5 °C)** and **β-Titanium (BCC, stable above 882.5 °C)**. It possesses an exceptional strength-to-density ratio, outstanding corrosion resistance (due to a passive TiO₂ oxide film), and excellent biocompatibility.

### 🍎 Intuitive Analogy — "The Aeroplane's Skeleton"
Imagine you are building an aeroplane. You need a material that is:
- **Light as aluminium** (so it can fly),
- **Strong as steel** (so it doesn't snap),
- **Immune to rust** like a gold ring (so it survives rain, salt, and jet fuel).

Titanium is precisely that "**best of three worlds**" material. Its density is **4.5 g/cm³** (about 60% of steel's 7.87 g/cm³) yet it can match steel's strength when alloyed. The only "cost" is that it is **expensive and difficult to machine**, much like how a diamond is hard but expensive to cut.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Titanium exists in two allotropic forms, and the ratio of α/β phase is **engineered by alloying** to obtain specific mechanical properties. This is the foundation of the **α, β, and α+β alloy classifications**.

---

## 1.2 Ceramics — Definition & The "Brick Wall" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Ceramics* are inorganic, non-metallic solids composed of **metallic and non-metallic elements** (e.g., oxides, nitrides, carbides, borides, silicates) held together by **ionic and/or covalent bonds**. They are typically hard, brittle, refractory (high melting point), and electrical/thermal insulators.

### 🍎 Intuitive Analogy — "The Brick Wall vs. The Steel Frame"
Think of a **brick wall** versus a **steel frame**:
- A **brick wall (ceramic)** is hard, resists compression, and insulates heat/electricity — but throw a stone at it, and it **shatters** (brittle).
- A **steel frame (metal)** dents and bends — it does not shatter — but it conducts heat and electricity.

In a ceramic, atoms are locked in **rigid ionic/covalent bonds** in fixed geometric positions (no free electrons, no slip planes). Hence, ceramics cannot deform plastically the way metals do — they simply **fracture** when overloaded.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The brittleness of ceramics is a direct consequence of their **bonding type (mixed ionic + covalent)** and **lack of free electrons**. This must be linked to their **crystal structures (AX, AmXn types)** when answering 14-mark questions.

---

## 1.3 Ceramic Crystal Structures — The "Packing of Spheres" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> Ceramic crystal structures are described by the **radius ratio rules** for ionic solids and the **Pauling rules** for coordination. The most common ceramic structure types are **AX (e.g., NaCl rock-salt, CsCl, ZnS zinc-blende)**, **AmXn (e.g., CaF₂ fluorite)**, and **more complex structures (e.g., perovskite ABO₃, spinel AB₂O₄)**.

### 🍎 Intuitive Analogy — "Marbles in a Box"
Imagine **two different sized marbles** in a box:
- The **larger marble = anion (O²⁻, Cl⁻, F⁻)** — it does most of the packing.
- The **smaller marble = cation (Na⁺, Ti⁴⁺, Ca²⁺)** — it fits into the *holes* (interstices) between the large marbles.

The size of the cation that fits inside the hole determines the **coordination number (CN)** and the resulting structure — exactly as **radius ratio rules** dictate.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants (Box-Out)

| Parameter | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| Ti atomic number | **22** |
| Ti density (α-phase) | **4.507 g/cm³** |
| α → β transformation temperature | **882.5 °C (1155.5 K)** |
| Ti Young's modulus | **~115 GPa** |
| TiO₂ passive film thickness | **~5–10 nm** |
| Cation/anion radius ratio range | **0.155 → 1.0** (determines CN) |
| Typical ceramic bond energy | **~1000 kJ/mol** (very strong) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Titanium — Extraction, Properties, Alloying

### 2.1.1 Why is Titanium Difficult to Extract?

Titanium is the **9th most abundant element in Earth's crust**, but its extraction is notoriously difficult because:

1. At high temperatures, Ti is **highly reactive** with O₂, N₂, H₂, and C → it forms stable oxides, nitrides, hydrides, and carbides.
2. Direct reduction of TiO₂ with carbon gives **TiC**, not pure Ti.
3. Hence, the **Kroll Process** (1940) is used industrially, involving **chlorination followed by reduction with Mg**.

### 2.1.2 The Kroll Process — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Chlorination (Carbo-chlorination of rutile ore, TiO₂):**

$$ \text{TiO}_2 \, (s) + 2\text{Cl}_2 \, (g) + 2\text{C} \, (s) \xrightarrow{950^\circ C} \text{TiCl}_4 \, (g) + 2\text{CO} \, (g) $$

**Why this step?** — It converts the unreactive solid oxide into a volatile, easily purified chloride (TiCl₄ boils at 136 °C). Distillation purifies it.

**Step 2 — Reduction by magnesium in an argon atmosphere:**

$$ \text{TiCl}_4 \, (g) + 2\text{Mg} \, (l) \xrightarrow{800\text{–}850^\circ C} \text{Ti} \, (s) + 2\text{MgCl}_2 \, (l) $$

**Step 3 — Vacuum distillation** to remove residual Mg and MgCl₂ → yields **Titanium sponge**, which is then crushed, pressed, and **arc-melted** into ingots.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative Process:** The **Hunter Process** uses Na instead of Mg but produces finer, less pure sponge. The **FFC Cambridge Process** (electro-deoxidation of TiO₂ in molten CaCl₂) is an emerging, lower-temperature alternative.

### 2.1.3 Allotropic Transformation & Alloying

Pure Ti transforms from **HCP (α) → BCC (β)** at 882.5 °C. This is the *engineering handle* used to develop alloy classes:

| Alloy Class | Phase Stabilizer | Result | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **α-alloys** | α-stabilizers: Al, O, N, C | HCP retained at all temps → weldable, strong at high T | Ti-5Al-2.5Sn |
| **β-alloys** | β-stabilizers: V, Mo, Nb, Ta, Fe | BCC retained at room T → formable, heat-treatable | Ti-15V-3Cr-3Al-3Sn |
| **α+β alloys** | Mix of both | Two-phase → balance of strength & ductility | Ti-6Al-4V (most used!) |
| **Near-α alloys** | Mostly α + small β | High creep resistance at high T | Ti-6Al-2Sn-4Zr-6Mo |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *Alphabets → Al* (alpha stabilizer) and *Vanadium → V* (beta stabilizer) and the most famous alloy is **Ti-6Al-4V** (the "workhorse" of aerospace, ~50% of all Ti used globally).

### 2.1.4 Properties of Titanium — The "Why Engineers Love It" List

- **Density:** 4.507 g/cm³ — 60% lighter than steel.
- **Strength:** Tensile strength 240–1200 MPa (depending on alloy/temper).
- **Corrosion resistance:** A stable, adherent **TiO₂ passive film** self-heals instantly in the presence of oxygen. Resistant to chlorides (where stainless steel fails).
- **Biocompatibility:** Non-toxic, integrates with bone (osseointegration) — used in implants.
- **Thermal expansion:** ~9 × 10⁻⁶ /K — lower than aluminium and steel.
- **Non-magnetic** and **non-toxic**.

### 2.1.5 Engineering Applications of Titanium

| Sector | Component / Application |
|---|---|
| Aerospace | Jet engine compressor blades, fuselage, landing gear, rocket parts |
| Biomedical | Hip & knee implants, dental implants, surgical instruments |
| Chemical | Heat exchangers, valves, pumps for chlor-alkali plants |
| Marine | Submarine hulls, propeller shafts, offshore rigs |
| Automotive | Connecting rods, valves, springs (high-end cars) |
| Power generation | Steam turbine blades, condenser tubes |

---

## 2.2 Ceramics — Classification, Bonding, and Properties

### 2.2.1 Classification of Ceramics

**A. Traditional Ceramics** (made from natural minerals like clay, silica, feldspar):
- Pottery, bricks, tiles, porcelain, glass, cement, refractories.

**B. Advanced / Engineering Ceramics** (synthetic, high-purity):
- **Oxides:** Al₂O₃ (alumina), MgO (magnesia), SiO₂ (silica), ZrO₂ (zirconia)
- **Carbides:** SiC, TiC, WC, B₄C
- **Nitrides:** Si₃N₄, TiN, AlN, BN
- **Borides:** TiB₂, ZrB₂
- **Glasses & Glass-ceramics**

### 2.2.2 Bonding in Ceramics

Ceramics exhibit a **mix of ionic and covalent bonding**:
- **Ionic character** dominates in oxides and halides (electronegativity difference > 1.7).
- **Covalent character** dominates in carbides and nitrides (small electronegativity difference, directional bonds).
- The **partial ionic character (%)** can be estimated by **Pauling's equation** (a common exam formula):

$$ \% \text{ Ionic Character} = \left(1 - e^{-\frac{(\Delta \chi)^2}{4}} \right) \times 100 $$

where $\Delta \chi$ is the **electronegativity difference** between the two atoms.

> [!TIP]
> **Example:** For MgO, $\Delta \chi = 3.44 - 1.31 = 2.13$. The % ionic character ≈ 70%. Hence MgO is predominantly ionic.

### 2.2.3 Properties of Ceramics — "The Trade-off Table"

| Property | Behaviour | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Hardness** | Very high (Mohs 9 for Al₂O₃) | Strong directional bonds |
| **Compressive strength** | High | Rigid atomic network |
| **Tensile strength / toughness** | **Low / brittle** | No slip systems, no plastic deformation |
| **Melting point** | Very high (2000–4000 °C) | Strong ionic/covalent bonds |
| **Electrical conductivity** | Insulator (mostly) | No free electrons (band gap > 4 eV) |
| **Thermal conductivity** | Low (except BeO, SiC) | Phonon scattering, no free electrons |
| **Thermal expansion** | Low (except ZrO₂) | Strong bonds restrict vibration |
| **Chemical resistance** | Excellent (oxides) | Already oxidised; stable |

### 2.2.4 Why Are Ceramics Brittle? (A 14-Mark Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Reasons for Brittleness in Ceramics:**
> 1. **No free electrons** → no metallic bond → no ductile deformation mechanism.
> 2. **Ionic bonds are non-directional** but **covalent bonds are highly directional** → few independent slip systems.
> 3. **Presence of microcracks and pores** (Griffith flaws) → stress concentrations lead to catastrophic crack propagation.
> 4. **Dislocation movement is restricted** in ionic/covalent lattices due to **charge neutrality** requirements during slip.
> 5. Crack propagation obeys the **Griffith criterion**:

$$ \sigma_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E\gamma_s}{\pi a}} $$

where $\sigma_c$ = critical fracture stress, $E$ = Young's modulus, $\gamma_s$ = surface energy, $a$ = half crack length.

---

## 2.3 Ceramic Crystal Structures — The Radius Ratio Rules

The **radius ratio** $r_c / r_a$ (cation radius / anion radius) determines the **coordination number (CN)** and hence the crystal structure:

| Radius Ratio $r_c / r_a$ | Coordination Number (CN) | Geometry | Example Structure |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.000 – 0.155 | 2 | Linear | — |
| 0.155 – 0.225 | 3 | Triangular | B₂O₃ |
| 0.225 – 0.414 | 4 | Tetrahedral | **ZnS (zinc blende), SiO₂ (cristobalite)** |
| 0.414 – 0.732 | 6 | Octahedral | **NaCl (rock salt), MgO, FeO** |
| 0.732 – 1.000 | 8 | Cubic | **CsCl, fluorite-related** |

> [!TIP]
> **Worked Example:** For MgO, $r_{\text{Mg}^{2+}} = 0.072$ nm, $r_{\text{O}^{2-}} = 0.140$ nm, so $r_c / r_a = 0.072 / 0.140 = 0.514$. This falls in the 0.414–0.732 range → **CN = 6 → Octahedral → Rock-salt (NaCl) structure**. ✓

---

## 2.4 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | % Ionic character (Pauling) | $\% = (1 - e^{-(\Delta \chi)^2 / 4}) \times 100$ | % | Bonding analysis |
| 2 | Kroll process reduction | $\text{TiCl}_4 + 2\text{Mg} \rightarrow \text{Ti} + 2\text{MgCl}_2$ | — | Extraction of Ti |
| 3 | α→β transformation | at **882.5 °C** | °C / K | Phase identity of Ti alloys |
| 4 | Density (HCP) | $\rho = \dfrac{n \cdot A}{V_c \cdot N_A}$ | g/cm³ | Theoretical density of Ti (n=6, A=47.87) |
| 5 | Radius ratio | $r = r_c / r_a$ | dimensionless | Predicting CN & structure |
| 6 | Coordination number | CN = number of nearest anion neighbours of cation | — | Structural classification |
| 7 | Packing factor (rock salt) | 0.74 (FCC) for both sublattices | — | Density calculation |
| 8 | Griffith fracture criterion | $\sigma_c = \sqrt{2E\gamma_s / (\pi a)}$ | Pa | Brittle fracture |
| 9 | Young's modulus (rule of mixtures) | $E_c = E_a V_a + E_m V_m$ (composite) | GPa | Cermet/composite design |
| 10 | Thermal shock resistance | $R = \sigma_f (1 - \nu) / (E \alpha)$ | — | Ceramic selection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** Remember the three most-tested ceramic structures in 14-mark questions: **NaCl (rock salt)**, **CsCl**, and **ZnS (zinc blende)**. Practice drawing them with the cation/anion position vectors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Density of α-Titanium (HCP)

> This derivation is a KTU-board-favourite — it tests whether the student knows the **HCP unit cell geometry** and can apply the density formula.

**Step 1 — Identify the HCP basis for α-Titanium:**

α-Ti is HCP with lattice parameters $a = 0.2950$ nm, $c = 0.4683$ nm, and the ideal $c/a$ ratio is 1.633.

**Step 2 — Volume of the HCP unit cell:**

The HCP unit cell (containing 3 primitive cells, i.e., n = 6 atoms) has volume:

$$ V_{HCP} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} a^2 \cdot c $$

Substituting values:

$$ V_{HCP} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} (0.2950 \times 10^{-9})^2 \times (0.4683 \times 10^{-9}) $$

**Step 3 — Compute the area of the hexagonal base:**

$$ A_{hex} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} a^2 = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} (0.2950 \times 10^{-9})^2 $$

$$ A_{hex} = 0.8660 \times (8.7025 \times 10^{-20}) = 7.534 \times 10^{-20} \text{ m}^2 $$

**Step 4 — Multiply by height c:**

$$ V_{HCP} = 7.534 \times 10^{-20} \times 4.683 \times 10^{-10} $$

$$ V_{HCP} = 3.529 \times 10^{-29} \text{ m}^3 = 3.529 \times 10^{-23} \text{ cm}^3 $$

**Step 5 — Apply the density formula:**

$$ \rho = \frac{n \cdot A}{V_{HCP} \cdot N_A} $$

where $n = 6$ atoms/HCP cell, $A = 47.87$ g/mol (Ti atomic mass), $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ /mol.

$$ \rho = \frac{6 \times 47.87}{3.529 \times 10^{-23} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}} $$

$$ \rho = \frac{287.22}{21.25} \approx 4.51 \text{ g/cm}^3 $$

> ✅ **Result matches the textbook value of 4.507 g/cm³ — derivation is verified.**

---

## 3.2 Analytical Example: % Ionic Character of TiO₂ (Bond Analysis)

**Step 1 — Electronegativity values (Pauling scale):**
- $\chi_{Ti} = 1.54$
- $\chi_{O} = 3.44$

**Step 2 — Electronegativity difference:**

$$ \Delta \chi = 3.44 - 1.54 = 1.90 $$

**Step 3 — Apply Pauling's equation:**

$$ \% \text{ Ionic} = \left(1 - e^{-(\Delta \chi)^2 / 4}\right) \times 100 $$

$$ = \left(1 - e^{-(1.90)^2 / 4}\right) \times 100 $$

$$ = \left(1 - e^{-3.61/4}\right) \times 100 $$

$$ = \left(1 - e^{-0.9025}\right) \times 100 $$

$$ = (1 - 0.4055) \times 100 = 59.45\% $$

**Interpretation:** TiO₂ has ~59% ionic character → **mixed ionic + covalent bond**, which is the reason TiO₂ is so chemically stable (a great passive layer on Ti metal).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Example: Radius Ratio & Structure of CaF₂ (Fluorite)

**Step 1 — Ionic radii:**
- $r_{Ca^{2+}} = 0.100$ nm
- $r_{F^-} = 0.133$ nm

**Step 2 — Compute radius ratio:**

$$ r = \frac{r_{Ca^{2+}}}{r_{F^-}} = \frac{0.100}{0.133} = 0.752 $$

**Step 3 — Apply rule:**
0.732 < 0.752 < 1.0 → **CN = 8 → Cubic coordination**.

**Step 4 — Identify the structure:**
In fluorite (CaF₂), $Ca^{2+}$ ions occupy **FCC positions** and $F^-$ ions occupy **all 8 tetrahedral holes**. The stoichiometry is 1 Ca : 2 F → **AmXn type with m=1, n=2**.

> ✅ Consistent with the radius ratio prediction.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Example: Theoretical Density of NaCl (Rock-Salt)

**Step 1 — Lattice parameter:** $a = 0.564$ nm
**Step 2 — Atoms per unit cell:** n = 4 formula units = 4 Na⁺ + 4 Cl⁻
**Step 3 — Molar mass:** $A_{NaCl} = 22.99 + 35.45 = 58.44$ g/mol
**Step 4 — Volume:** $V = a^3 = (5.64 \times 10^{-8})^3 = 1.794 \times 10^{-22}$ cm³
**Step 5 — Density formula:**

$$ \rho = \frac{n \cdot A}{V \cdot N_A} = \frac{4 \times 58.44}{1.794 \times 10^{-22} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}} $$

$$ \rho = \frac{233.76}{108.06} \approx 2.164 \text{ g/cm}^3 $$

> ✅ Matches experimental density of NaCl (~2.165 g/cm³). Excellent cross-check.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Ceramic Structure Comparison (Exam Quick-Reference)

| Property | Rock Salt (NaCl) | CsCl | Zinc Blende (ZnS) | Fluorite (CaF₂) | Perovskite (CaTiO₃) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cation CN** | 6 | 8 | 4 | 8 | 12 (for Ca) |
| **Anion CN** | 6 | 8 | 4 | 4 | 6 (for O) |
| **Cation site** | Octahedral hole | Cubic hole | Tetrahedral hole | Cubic (FCC) | Cubic (corner) |
| **Anion site** | FCC positions | Simple cubic | FCC positions | All Td holes | Edge + face centre |
| **Atoms per cell** | 4 (formula units) | 1 | 4 | 4 | 1 |
| **Packing** | Cubic close | Simple cubic | Cubic close | Cubic close | Cubic close |
| **Example** | MgO, FeO, NiO | CsBr, CsI | ZnS, SiC, GaAs | UO₂, ThO₂ | BaTiO₃, SrTiO₃ |
| **Engineering use** | Refractory linings | — | Semiconductors, LED | Nuclear fuel | Capacitors, piezoelectrics |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick:** If a question gives the radius ratio and asks for the structure, *first identify CN*, *then map to the 5 standard structures above*. That's a guaranteed 7 marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📊 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow: Kroll Process for Titanium Extraction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ore1["Rutile Ore TiO2"] --> grind["Crushing and Grinding"]
    grind --> chlor["Chlorination with Cl2 and C at 950 C"]
    chlor --> tiCl4["TiCl4 Gas BP 136 C"]
    tiCl4 --> dist["Purification by Fractional Distillation"]
    dist --> red["Reduction with Molten Mg at 800 C in Argon"]
    red --> spong["Titanium Sponge Crude"]
    spong --> vac["Vacuum Distillation Removes Mg and MgCl2"]
    vac --> crush["Crushing and Pressing"]
    crush --> melt["Vacuum Arc Melting to Ingot"]
    melt --> final["Wrought Titanium Ready for Alloying"]
    final --> alloy1["Alpha Alloy e.g. Ti 5Al 2.5Sn"]
    final --> alloy2["Beta Alloy e.g. Ti 15V 3Cr 3Al 3Sn"]
    final --> alloy3["Alpha plus Beta Alloy e.g. Ti 6Al 4V"]
    byprod["Byproduct MgCl2 Electrolysed back to Mg and Cl2 Recycled"]
    red -.-> byprod
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Ceramic Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ceramics["Ceramics Inorganic Non Metallic"]
    ceramics --> trad["Traditional Ceramics"]
    ceramics --> adv["Advanced Engineering Ceramics"]
    trad --> t1["Clay Based Pottery Bricks Tiles"]
    trad --> t2["Silica Based Glass and Porcelain"]
    trad --> t3["Cement and Concrete"]
    trad --> t4["Refractories Firebricks"]
    adv --> oxides["Oxides Al2O3 MgO ZrO2 SiO2"]
    adv --> carbides["Carbides SiC TiC WC B4C"]
    adv --> nitrides["Nitrides Si3N4 TiN AlN BN"]
    adv --> borides["Borides TiB2 ZrB2 HfB2"]
    adv --> glasses["Glasses and Glass Ceramics"]
    oxides --> app1["Cutting Tools Insulators Abrasives"]
    carbides --> app2["Armor Abrasives High Temp"]
    nitrides --> app3["Engine Components Bearings"]
    borides --> app4["Hypersonic Vehicles Nuclear"]
    glasses --> app5["Cooktops Telescope Mirrors"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Radius Ratio Logic & Structure Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rr["Compute r cation over r anion"] --> q1{"r less than 0.155"}
    rr --> q2{"0.155 to 0.225"}
    rr --> q3{"0.225 to 0.414"}
    rr --> q4{"0.414 to 0.732"}
    rr --> q5{"0.732 to 1.000"}
    q1 --> cn2["CN 2 Linear"]
    q2 --> cn3["CN 3 Triangular B2O3"]
    q3 --> cn4["CN 4 Tetrahedral ZnS SiO2"]
    q4 --> cn6["CN 6 Octahedral NaCl MgO"]
    q5 --> cn8["CN 8 Cubic CsCl CaF2"]
    cn4 --> struct1["Zinc Blende or Wurtzite"]
    cn6 --> struct2["Rock Salt"]
    cn8 --> struct3["CsCl or Fluorite"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block: Why Are Ceramics Brittle — Cause-Effect Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    b1["Mixed Ionic plus Covalent Bonds"] --> b2["No Free Electrons in Lattice"]
    b2 --> b3["No Metallic Slip Mechanism"]
    b3 --> b4["No Dislocation Glide Across Charge Imbalance"]
    b4 --> b5["Microcracks from Griffith Flaws"]
    b5 --> b6["Stress Concentration at Crack Tip"]
    b6 --> b7["Catastrophic Brittle Fracture"]
    p1["No Ductility Low Toughness K1c 1 to 5 MPa sqrt m"] --> b7
    s1["Low Thermal Shock Resistance R low"] --> b7
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Block: Ti Allotropic Transformation & Alloying Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    lowT["Below 882.5 C"] --> alpha["Alpha Phase HCP"]
    highT["Above 882.5 C"] --> beta["Beta Phase BCC"]
    alphaAl["Add Al O N C"] --> alphaBoost["Alpha Stabilized HCP Retained"]
    betaStab["Add V Mo Nb Fe Ta"] --> betaBoost["Beta Stabilized BCC Retained"]
    mixAlloy["Add Mix of Stabilizers"] --> dualP["Alpha plus Beta Dual Phase"]
    alphaBoost --> exA["Example Ti 5Al 2.5Sn"]
    betaBoost --> exB["Example Ti 15V 3Cr 3Al 3Sn"]
    dualP --> exC["Example Ti 6Al 4V 50 percent of Ti used"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any three characteristic properties of titanium that make it suitable for aerospace applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **High strength-to-weight ratio** — Ti has density 4.51 g/cm³, which is 60% of steel, but tensile strength matches steel when alloyed → reduces airframe weight.
2. **Excellent corrosion resistance** — Forms a self-healing passive TiO₂ film, resistant to salt water, jet fuel, and atmospheric corrosion.
3. **High melting point (1668 °C) and retain strength at elevated temperatures** — Suitable for jet engine compressor blades and rocket nozzles.

*(Any 3 properties with one-line justification — full 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define ceramics. Why are ceramics brittle in nature?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Ceramics are inorganic, non-metallic solids composed of metallic and non-metallic elements held by **ionic and/or covalent bonds**, e.g., Al₂O₃, SiC, ZrO₂.
Ceramics are brittle because:
- They possess **strong directional bonds** (mixed ionic + covalent) with **no free electrons** and **no metallic slip mechanism**.
- **Dislocation movement is restricted** due to charge-neutrality requirements across the slip plane.
- **Pre-existing microcracks (Griffith flaws)** act as stress raisers and propagate catastrophically (Griffith criterion: $\sigma_c = \sqrt{2E\gamma_s / \pi a}$).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **As per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, students must answer ONE full 14-mark question with internal choice. Both choices are provided below.**

---

### 🔷 QUESTION A — (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat flow diagram, explain the **Kroll process for the extraction of titanium** from rutile ore. State the limitations of this process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **allotropic transformation in titanium** and classify titanium alloys with suitable examples. Highlight the role of **α and β stabilizers**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Step 1 — Why Kroll process is needed (1 mark):**
Titanium has very high affinity for oxygen, nitrogen, hydrogen, and carbon at high temperatures. Direct reduction of TiO₂ with carbon yields TiC, not pure Ti. Hence an indirect route via TiCl₄ is used.

**Step 2 — Step 1 of process — Chlorination (2 marks):**

$$ \text{TiO}_2 + 2\text{Cl}_2 + 2\text{C} \xrightarrow{950^\circ C} \text{TiCl}_4 \uparrow + 2\text{CO} \uparrow $$

TiCl₄ is a volatile liquid (b.p. 136 °C) → easily separated and purified by fractional distillation.

**Step 3 — Step 2 of process — Reduction (2 marks):**

$$ \text{TiCl}_4 + 2\text{Mg} \xrightarrow{800\text{–}850^\circ C, \text{Ar}} \text{Ti (sponge)} + 2\text{MgCl}_2 $$

**Step 4 — Purification and consolidation (1 mark):**
Vacuum distillation removes residual Mg and MgCl₂. The titanium sponge is then crushed, pressed into compacts, and **vacuum-arc melted** to form ingots.

**Step 5 — Limitations (1 mark):**
- **Batch process** (not continuous) → low productivity, high cost.
- **Energy-intensive** (high temperature, inert atmosphere).
- Ti powder/ingot is **brittle at room temperature** due to dissolved H₂ and O₂.
- Mg and Cl₂ must be **recycled** to be economical.

> ✅ **Flow Diagram (Mermaid):**
```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["TiO2 Rutile"] --> B["Chlorination Cl2 plus C 950 C"]
    B --> C["TiCl4 Gas Distill"]
    C --> D["Reduce with Mg 850 C Ar"]
    D --> E["Ti Sponge plus MgCl2"]
    E --> F["Vacuum Distillation"]
    F --> G["Arc Melting Ingot"]
```

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Step 1 — Allotropic transformation (2 marks):**
Pure Ti exists as **α-Ti (HCP)** below **882.5 °C** and transforms to **β-Ti (BCC)** above this temperature.

**Step 2 — α-stabilizers (1.5 marks):**
- Elements that **raise** the α→β transformation temperature.
- Examples: **Al, O, N, C, B**.
- Function: Solute atoms stabilize HCP structure → single-phase α alloys.

**Step 3 — β-stabilizers (1.5 marks):**
- Elements that **lower** the α→β transformation temperature.
- Two sub-classes:
  - *Isomorphous β-stabilizers* (form solid solution with β-Ti): **V, Mo, Nb, Ta**.
  - *Eutectoid β-stabilizers*: **Fe, Mn, Cr, Ni, Cu, Si, H**.

**Step 4 — Alloy classification (1.5 marks):**

| Class | Stabilizer | Example | Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| α | Al, O | Ti-5Al-2.5Sn | Weldable, creep resistant |
| Near-α | Mostly α | Ti-6Al-2Sn-4Zr-6Mo | High T strength |
| α+β | Both | **Ti-6Al-4V** | Balanced (most used) |
| β | V, Mo | Ti-15V-3Cr-3Al-3Sn | Heat-treatable, formable |

**Step 5 — Role summary (0.5 mark):**
Alloying modifies phase stability, enabling tailored mechanical and corrosion properties for aerospace, biomedical, and marine applications.

---

### 🔷 QUESTION B — (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Define ceramics. Classify ceramics with suitable examples and explain the **bonding mechanisms** in ceramics using Pauling's equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With neat diagrams, explain the **crystal structures** of (i) **Rock salt (NaCl)**, (ii) **CsCl**, and (iii) **Zinc blende (ZnS)**. Use **radius ratio rules** to justify the coordination number. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
Ceramics are **inorganic, non-metallic solids** composed of metallic and non-metallic elements (e.g., Al, Mg, Si + O, N, C) held together by **ionic and/or covalent bonds**, typically hard, brittle, refractory, and electrically insulating.

**Step 2 — Classification with examples (3 marks):**

| Class | Examples |
|---|---|
| Traditional | Bricks, tiles, pottery, glass, cement, refractories |
| Oxides | Al₂O₃, MgO, SiO₂, ZrO₂ |
| Carbides | SiC, TiC, WC, B₄C |
| Nitrides | Si₃N₄, TiN, AlN, BN |
| Borides | TiB₂, ZrB₂ |
| Glasses & glass-ceramics | Borosilicate, Zerodur, Pyrex |

**Step 3 — Bonding — Pauling's equation (2 marks):**

$$ \% \text{ Ionic Character} = \left(1 - e^{-(\Delta \chi)^2 / 4}\right) \times 100 $$

where $\Delta \chi$ = difference in Pauling electronegativity between the cation and anion.

If $\Delta \chi \geq 1.7$ → bond is > 50% ionic.
If $\Delta \chi \leq 1.7$ → bond is predominantly covalent.

**Step 4 — Worked example (1 mark):**
For **Al₂O₃**: $\Delta \chi = 3.44 - 1.61 = 1.83$.
$\% \text{ ionic} = (1 - e^{-0.838}) \times 100 = (1 - 0.432) \times 100 = 56.8\%$.
Hence Al₂O₃ is **~57% ionic, ~43% covalent** — typical mixed-bond ceramic.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Step 1 — Radius ratio rule (1 mark):**
The radius ratio $r = r_c / r_a$ determines CN. Critical limits:
- $0.414 \leq r < 0.732$ → CN = 6 (Octahedral) → **Rock salt**.
- $0.732 \leq r \leq 1.0$ → CN = 8 (Cubic) → **CsCl**.
- $0.225 \leq r < 0.414$ → CN = 4 (Tetrahedral) → **Zinc blende**.

**Step 2 — Rock salt (NaCl) structure (2 marks):**
- Both Na⁺ and Cl⁻ form **interpenetrating FCC sublattices**.
- Each Na⁺ is octahedrally coordinated by 6 Cl⁻ and vice versa.
- **CN = 6**, **n = 4 formula units per cell**, **packing factor = 0.74 (FCC) for each sublattice**.
- Example: MgO, FeO, NiO.
- **Na⁺ radius** = 0.102 nm, **Cl⁻ radius** = 0.181 nm → $r = 0.102 / 0.181 = 0.564$ → falls in 0.414–0.732 range → **CN = 6 confirmed** ✓.

**Step 3 — CsCl structure (2 marks):**
- Cl⁻ ions at **corners of a cube**, Cs⁺ at **body centre** (or vice versa).
- Each ion is **cubically coordinated by 8** counter-ions.
- **CN = 8**, **n = 1 formula unit per cell**, **packing factor = 0.68**.
- Example: CsBr, CsI.
- **Cs⁺ radius** = 0.170 nm, **Cl⁻ radius** = 0.181 nm → $r = 0.170 / 0.181 = 0.939$ → falls in 0.732–1.0 range → **CN = 8 confirmed** ✓.

**Step 4 — Zinc blende (ZnS) structure (2 marks):**
- S²⁻ ions at **FCC positions**, Zn²⁺ ions at **alternate tetrahedral holes (½ of them)**.
- Each Zn²⁺ is tetrahedrally coordinated by 4 S²⁻ and vice versa.
- **CN = 4**, **n = 4 formula units per cell**, **packing factor = 0.74**.
- Example: SiC (β-SiC), GaAs, diamond cubic.
- **Zn²⁺ radius** = 0.074 nm, **S²⁻ radius** = 0.184 nm → $r = 0.074 / 0.184 = 0.402$ → falls in 0.225–0.414 range → **CN = 4 confirmed** ✓.

> ✅ **Schematic Block (Mermaid):**
```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Three Ceramic Structures"] --> B["NaCl Rock Salt CN 6 Octahedral"]
    A --> C["CsCl Cubic CN 8 Cubic"]
    A --> D["ZnS Zinc Blende CN 4 Tetrahedral"]
    B --> B1["FCC Anion plus Cation at Octa Holes"]
    C --> C1["SC Anion plus Cation at Cube Centre"]
    D --> D1["FCC Anion plus Cation at Tetra Holes"]
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Don't confuse CsCl with fluorite (CaF₂):** CsCl has CN = 8:8 with one formula unit per cell. Fluorite has CN = 8:4 with **4 formula units per cell** (F⁻ at Td holes, Ca²⁺ at FCC nodes).
> 2. **Don't state "Kroll process is continuous":** It is a **batch process** — a frequently asked correction. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Don't say "ceramics have no slip system"** — they have **insufficient independent slip systems** (only one for HCP-like ionic structures).
> 4. **Don't write just "Ti is light"** — always quantify: **density 4.51 g/cm³, 60% of steel**.
> 5. **For radius-ratio questions, always show the arithmetic**, not just the conclusion.
> 6. **Don't omit the role of TiO₂ passive film** in corrosion resistance — it is a 2-mark fetcher in most 14-mark answers.
> 7. **Aluminium is the most commonly confused element** — students write it as β-stabilizer. Correct: Al is an **α-stabilizer** (raises transformation temperature).

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Titanium is the **9th most abundant element** but the **4th most used structural metal** (after Fe, Al, Cu).
- ✅ **Kroll process** = TiO₂ → TiCl₄ → Ti sponge (via Mg reduction in Ar).
- ✅ α-Ti = **HCP, < 882.5 °C**; β-Ti = **BCC, > 882.5 °C**.
- ✅ **α-stabilizers: Al, O, N, C, B**; **β-stabilizers: V, Mo, Nb, Ta, Fe**.
- ✅ **Ti-6Al-4V** = the most used titanium alloy (~50% of all Ti applications).
- ✅ Ti's **corrosion resistance** comes from the **passive TiO₂ film** (~5–10 nm thick, self-healing).
- ✅ Ceramics = **inorganic, non-metallic**, ionic + covalent bonds, hard, brittle, insulating.
- ✅ **% ionic character (Pauling)** = $(1 - e^{-(\Delta\chi)^2/4}) \times 100$.
- ✅ **Radius ratio rules** determine CN: 0.155, 0.225, 0.414, 0.732.
- ✅ **Three key ceramic structures**: NaCl (CN=6), CsCl (CN=8), ZnS zinc blende (CN=4).
- ✅ Ceramics are **brittle** because of strong directional bonds, no free electrons, restricted dislocation motion, and Griffith microcracks.
- ✅ **Griffith criterion:** $\sigma_c = \sqrt{2E\gamma_s / (\pi a)}$.
- ✅ **Thermal shock resistance** $R = \sigma_f (1 - \nu) / (E \alpha)$ is low for ceramics → cracking on rapid heating/cooling.
- ✅ **Cermets** (e.g., WC-Co) combine ceramic hardness with metal toughness — used in cutting tools.
- ✅ **Advanced ceramic applications**: SiC in bulletproof vests, ZrO₂ in hip implants, Si₃N₄ in engine bearings, Al₂O₃ in substrates, BaTiO₃ in capacitors.
- ✅ **Engineering ceramic sectors**: aerospace, nuclear (UO₂ fuel), electronics (substrates), biomedical (zirconia implants), automotive (SiC brake discs).

> 🎯 **KTU Final Tip:** In 14-mark answers, **always pair every property with a number (density, temperature, strength)** and **every structure with a radius-ratio calculation** — that single habit alone lifts your score by 3–4 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT526 - METALLURGY AND MATERIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Engineering Applications of Metallic Materials

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
*Engineering applications of metallic materials* refers to the systematic selection, processing, and deployment of ferrous metals (steels, cast irons), non-ferrous metals (aluminum, copper, titanium, magnesium, nickel, and their alloys), and advanced materials (composites, powder metallurgy parts, smart materials) in load-bearing, structural, tribological, corrosion-resistant, and high-temperature service environments based on their mechanical, physical, and chemical property profiles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Emphasis:** The "Applications" module is **not** about memorising lists. The Board Examiner expects you to correlate *property → application*. Always answer in the format: **"Material X has property Y (e.g., high strength-to-weight ratio), therefore it is used for Z (e.g., aircraft structures)."**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **carpentry toolkit**. A hammer is for driving nails, a saw is for cutting wood, and a screwdriver is for tightening screws. No single tool does everything well. Similarly, **no single metal is the best for every engineering job**.

- **Steel** is the "all-purpose hammer" — strong, cheap, versatile.
- **Aluminium** is the "lightweight screwdriver" — easy to handle, but softer.
- **Titanium** is the "premium precision tool" — excellent strength and corrosion resistance, but expensive.
- **Cast Iron** is the "heavy-duty clamp" — hard and wear-resistant, but brittle.

A good engineer (like a good carpenter) knows **which material to pick for which job**, and *why*. This is the heart of Module 4.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Material Selection

The following metrics are **bolded** as they appear verbatim in KTU question papers:

- **Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$)** — measured in **MPa**
- **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS, $\sigma_{uts}$)** — measured in **MPa**
- **Density ($\rho$)** — measured in **g/cm³** or **kg/m³**
- **Specific Strength** = $\sigma_y / \rho$ — measured in **kNm/kg**
- **Young's Modulus ($E$)** — measured in **GPa**
- **Hardness (HRC, HB, HV)** — dimensionless or kgf/mm²
- **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$)** — measured in **°C**
- **Melting Point ($T_m$)** — measured in **°C**
- **Fatigue Limit ($\sigma_e$)** — measured in **MPa**
- **Creep Rate ($\dot{\epsilon}$)** — measured in **s⁻¹**

> [!NOTE]
> **Specific Strength** is the single most important metric KTU examiners use to test whether you can *justify* a material choice in aerospace, automotive, and structural applications.

---

## 1.4 Broad Classification of Applications

| **Class** | **Examples of Service Requirements** | **Typical Materials Used** |
|---|---|---|
| Structural | Bridges, buildings, beams | Mild steel, structural steel |
| High-strength | Gears, shafts, crankshafts | Alloy steels (AISI 4140, 4340) |
| Wear-resistant | Mining, crushing, dies | Tool steels, Hadfield steel |
| Corrosion-resistant | Marine, chemical | Stainless steel, Ti, Monel |
| High-temperature | Turbine blades, exhaust | Ni-superalloys, Inconel |
| Lightweight | Aircraft, automobiles | Al-alloys (2024, 7075), Mg, Ti |
| Electrical | Conductors, contacts | Pure Cu, brass, bronze |
| Magnetic | Motors, transformers | Silicon steel, permalloy |

---

## 1.5 Why "Applications" is a High-Yield KTU Module

Module 4 carries **typical weightage of 15–20%** in the End Semester Exam (ESE) and frequently features direct 3-mark questions like *"List any four applications of Hadfield steel"* or 14-mark analytical questions like *"Compare the applications of ferrous and non-ferrous alloys in the automotive industry."* Mastering the *property-to-application mapping* is mandatory.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Property-Application Mapping Framework

Every material selection decision in engineering follows a **four-step logic chain**:

1. **Identify Service Conditions** → Load type (static/dynamic), temperature, environment, wear, fatigue.
2. **Determine Critical Property** → Which property governs failure? (Strength, toughness, corrosion, weight).
3. **Screen Candidate Materials** → Use Ashby charts / property databases.
4. **Validate & Select** → Consider cost, manufacturability, availability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners reward answers that explicitly state the **property linkage**. Saying *"Stainless steel is used in chemical plants"* scores less than *"Stainless steel contains ≥10.5% Cr, forming a passive Cr₂O₃ film, hence it is used in chemical plants for corrosion resistance."*

---

## 2.2 Applications of Carbon Steels

Carbon steels are classified by **carbon content**, which directly controls hardness, strength, and weldability.

| **Type** | **% C** | **Key Properties** | **Engineering Applications** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low-carbon (Mild) | < 0.25% | Ductile, weldable, machinable | Sheets, wires, pipes, car bodies, structural beams |
| Medium-carbon | 0.25–0.6% | Balance of strength & ductility | Railway tracks, axles, gears, crankshafts |
| High-carbon | 0.6–1.0% | Hard, wear-resistant, less ductile | Springs, chisels, cutting tools, blades |
| Ultra-high-carbon | > 1.0% | Very hard, brittle | Knives, punches, shear blades |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Rule of Thumb:** *As carbon content increases → strength and hardness increase, but ductility and weldability decrease.*

---

## 2.3 Applications of Alloy Steels

Alloying elements are added to enhance specific properties:

### 2.3.1 Stainless Steels (Corrosion Resistance)
- **Austenitic (304, 316):** Kitchen utensils, chemical tanks, surgical implants, marine hardware.
- **Ferritic (430):** Automotive trims, exhaust manifolds, washing machine drums.
- **Martensitic (410, 420):** Cutlery, surgical instruments, turbine blades, ball bearings.
- **Duplex (2205):** Offshore oil platforms, desalination plants, pressure vessels.

### 2.3.2 Tool Steels (Wear & Hardness)
- **T-series (T1):** Tungsten high-speed steel → cutting tools, drills, taps.
- **M-series (M2, M42):** Molybdenum HSS → drills, end mills, reamers.
- **D-series (D2, D3):** Cold-work die steel → blanking dies, forming dies, punches.
- **H-series (H11, H13):** Hot-work die steel → die-casting dies, forging dies, extrusions.
- **P-series (P20):** Plastic mould steel → injection moulds, die-casting moulds.

### 2.3.3 High-Strength Low-Alloy (HSLA) Steels
- Microalloyed with **Nb, V, Ti** for grain refinement.
- **Applications:** Offshore platforms, bridges, ship hulls, line-pipe for oil/gas transmission.

### 2.3.4 Hadfield Steel (Austenitic Manganese Steel)
- Composition: **12% Mn, 1.2% C**.
- Property: **Work-hardening** under impact — surface hardness rises from ~200 HB to ~550 HB.
- **Applications:** Rock crusher jaws, railway crossings (frogs), shot-blasting equipment, bulldozer blades, tank tracks.

---

## 2.4 Applications of Cast Irons

| **Type** | **Microstructure** | **Key Property** | **Applications** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Grey cast iron | Flake graphite | Damping, machinability | Engine blocks, machine tool beds, pipes |
| White cast iron | Fe₃C (cementite) | Hard, brittle, abrasion-resistant | Mill liners, slurry pumps, crushing rolls |
| Malleable cast iron | Tempered graphite clusters | Ductile relative to grey iron | Connecting rods, gears, farm equipment |
| Ductile (Nodular) iron | Spheroidal graphite | High strength + ductility | Crankshafts, cam shafts, pipes, wind turbine hubs |
| High-alloy (Ni-Hard) | Carbides in martensite | Wear + corrosion resistance | Slurry pumps, grinding balls, coal pulverisers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** *"Why is grey cast iron used for machine tool beds?"*
> **Answer:** Flake graphite provides **excellent vibration damping** and **self-lubrication**, which is critical for absorbing chatter during machining — a property steel cannot match.

---

## 2.5 Applications of Aluminium and Its Alloys

Aluminium has **density = 2.7 g/cm³** (one-third of steel) and forms a protective Al₂O₃ layer.

| **Alloy Series** | **Main Alloying Element** | **Applications** |
|---|---|---|
| 1xxx (pure) | ≥99% Al | Electrical busbars, chemical equipment, foil |
| 2xxx | Cu | Aircraft structures (2024-T3), rivets |
| 3xxx | Mn | Beverage cans, roofing, heat exchangers |
| 5xxx | Mg | Marine hulls, ship superstructures, pressure vessels |
| 6xxx | Mg + Si | Architectural extrusions, automotive panels, bicycle frames |
| 7xxx | Zn | Aircraft wing spars, missile structures, high-stress parts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Heat-Treatable series (2xxx, 6xxx, 7xxx)** gain strength via precipitation hardening.
> **Non-heat-treatable series (1xxx, 3xxx, 5xxx)** gain strength only via cold work (strain hardening).

---

## 2.6 Applications of Copper and Its Alloys

- **Pure Copper:** Electrical wiring, busbars, heat exchangers (highest conductivity among non-precious metals, ~100% IACS).
- **Brass (Cu + Zn):** Cartridge cases, plumbing fittings, musical instruments, decorative items.
- **Bronze (Cu + Sn):** Bearings, bushings, ship propellers (historically), sculptures.
- **Aluminium Bronze (Cu + Al):** Marine propellers, pump impellers, gears — high strength + corrosion resistance.
- **Beryllium Copper:** Non-sparking tools for explosive environments, springs, electrical contacts.

---

## 2.7 Applications of Titanium Alloys

- **Density = 4.5 g/cm³**, strength up to **1100 MPa**, excellent corrosion resistance.
- **Ti-6Al-4V (Grade 5):** Aircraft compressor blades, fuselage frames, rocket casings, biomedical implants (hip joints, dental).
- **Commercially Pure (CP) Ti:** Chemical reactors, desalination, medical implants, architecture.

---

## 2.8 Applications of Magnesium Alloys

- **Density = 1.74 g/cm³** (lightest structural metal).
- **AZ91D (Mg-Al-Zn):** Steering wheels, instrument panels, laptop cases, gearbox housings.
- **WE43, ZE41:** Aerospace gearbox housings, helicopter transmission casings (high-temperature Mg-rare earth alloys).

---

## 2.9 Applications of Nickel and Superalloys

- **Pure Ni:** Coinage, batteries (Ni-Cd, Ni-MH), plating.
- **Monel (Ni-Cu):** Marine propeller shafts, chemical processing equipment, musical instrument valves.
- **Inconel (Ni-Cr-Fe):** Gas turbine blades, rocket engines, nuclear reactors — exceptional high-temperature strength.
- **Nimonic:** Turbine discs, combustion chambers (older generation).
- **Hastelloy (Ni-Mo-Cr):** Chemical reactors handling HCl, H₂SO₄.

---

## 2.10 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> These are the **only quantitative relations** KTU expects you to know in the Applications module. All are *derived* in Section 3.

| **Formula / Metric** | **Expression** | **Unit** | **Application Context** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specific Strength | $SS = \sigma_y / \rho$ | kNm/kg | Aerospace material selection |
| Specific Modulus | $SE = E / \rho$ | MNm/kg | Stiffness-critical lightweight design |
| Strength-to-Weight Ratio | $R_{sw} = \sigma_{uts} / (\rho \cdot g)$ | m | Lightweight structural design |
| Ashby Performance Index (tie-rod) | $P = \sigma_y / \rho$ | kNm/kg | Tension members |
| Ashby Performance Index (beam) | $P = E^{1/3} / \rho$ | GN^(1/3) m²/kg | Stiff beams |
| Ashby Performance Index (plate) | $P = E^{1/3} / \rho$ | GN^(1/3) m²/kg | Stiff plates |
| Fatigue Life (Basquin) | $\sigma_a = \sigma_f (2N_f)^b$ | MPa | Cyclic loading design |
| Creep Power Law | $\dot{\epsilon} = A \sigma^n \exp(-Q_c/RT)$ | s⁻¹ | High-temperature service |
| Hardness-Tensile Correlation | $\sigma_{uts} \approx 3.45 \times HB$ | MPa | Quick property estimation |
| Rule of Mixtures (composites) | $E_c = V_f E_f + V_m E_m$ | GPa | Composite material design |

> [!WARNING]
> The Ashby Performance Indices are **frequently asked** as 3-mark conceptual questions. Memorise the *form* (which property divided by density to what power) for each loading geometry.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Specific Strength (Strength-to-Weight Ratio)

**Problem Setup:** A structural component of mass $m$ must support a tensile load $F$. Show that the lightest material for a given load-carrying capacity is the one with the highest **specific strength**.

### Step 1: Define the geometric and loading parameters

Let the cross-sectional area of the member be $A$ and length be $L$. The mass of the member is:

$$
m = \rho \cdot A \cdot L
$$

where $\rho$ is the density of the material.

### Step 2: Apply the strength constraint (yield criterion)

For safe design under a tensile load $F$, the induced stress must not exceed the yield strength:

$$
\sigma = \frac{F}{A} \leq \sigma_y
$$

Therefore, the **minimum required area** is:

$$
A = \frac{F}{\sigma_y}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute the minimum area into the mass equation

$$
m_{min} = \rho \cdot L \cdot \frac{F}{\sigma_y}
$$

Since $L$ and $F$ are fixed by the design, minimising mass is equivalent to **minimising** the factor:

$$
\frac{\rho}{\sigma_y}
$$

which is the **inverse of specific strength**.

### Step 4: Final result

To minimise the mass of a tensile load-bearing member, **maximise** the specific strength:

$$
SS = \frac{\sigma_y}{\rho}
$$

This is why aluminium alloys (SS ≈ 150–250 kNm/kg) and titanium alloys (SS ≈ 250–300 kNm/kg) are preferred over mild steel (SS ≈ 50–60 kNm/kg) in aerospace structures.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Ashby Performance Index for a Stiff Lightweight Beam

**Problem Setup:** A cantilever beam of length $L$ and rectangular cross-section $b \times h$ carries a tip load $F$. The tip deflection $\delta$ must not exceed a given value. Derive the material index that minimises the beam's mass.

### Step 1: Write the tip deflection formula

For a cantilever beam with end load:

$$
\delta = \frac{F L^3}{3 E I}
$$

where $I$ is the second moment of area of the cross-section.

### Step 2: Express $I$ for a rectangular cross-section

$$
I = \frac{b h^3}{12}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute $I$ and solve for the minimum $h$

The deflection constraint $\delta = \delta_{max}$ gives:

$$
\delta_{max} = \frac{F L^3}{3 E} \cdot \frac{12}{b h^3} = \frac{4 F L^3}{E b h^3}
$$

Solving for $h$:

$$
h = \left( \frac{4 F L^3}{E b \, \delta_{max}} \right)^{1/3}
$$

### Step 4: Express the mass of the beam

The volume is $V = b \cdot h \cdot L$, and mass $m = \rho \cdot V$. Using the $h$ from Step 3:

$$
m = \rho \cdot b \cdot L \cdot \left( \frac{4 F L^3}{E b \, \delta_{max}} \right)^{1/3}
$$

### Step 5: Isolate the material-dependent term

Group the terms that depend on material properties:

$$
m = \text{(geometric constants)} \times \rho \cdot \left( \frac{1}{E} \right)^{1/3}
$$

To minimise $m$, we must **minimise**:

$$
\frac{\rho}{E^{1/3}}
$$

or equivalently **maximise** the **Ashby Performance Index** for a stiff beam:

$$
P_{beam} = \frac{E^{1/3}}{\rho}
$$

### Step 6: Real-world verification

For a given stiffness, materials with high $E^{1/3}/\rho$ include CFRP composites, magnesium alloys, and aluminium alloys — explaining their dominance in aerospace beams.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Hardness-Tensile Strength Correlation

**Problem Setup:** For a ductile metal, show that $\sigma_{uts} \approx 3.45 \times HB$.

### Step 1: Recall the Brinell hardness definition

HB is the ratio of applied load to surface area of indentation:

$$
HB = \frac{F}{A_{indentation}}
$$

where $A_{indentation} \approx \pi \cdot D \cdot t$, with $D$ = ball diameter, $t$ = indentation depth.

### Step 2: Empirical correlation

For most ductile metals (steels, brasses, aluminium alloys), the empirical relationship established by Tabor and others is:

$$
\sigma_{uts} \approx 3.45 \times HB
$$

### Step 3: Practical significance

This lets engineers **estimate tensile strength from a non-destructive hardness test**, which is critical in quality control and material selection. Example: A steel with HB = 200 → $\sigma_{uts} \approx 690$ MPa, suitable for **medium-strength applications like gears and axles**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Material Selection Decision Tree

| **Service Requirement** | **Dominant Property Needed** | **First-Choice Material** | **Reasoning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lightweight + high strength | Specific strength | Ti-6Al-4V, 7075-T6 | High $\sigma_y/\rho$ |
| Corrosion resistance in HCl | Chemical inertness | Hastelloy C-276 | Resists Cl⁻ attack |
| High-temp (≥800°C) strength | Creep resistance | Inconel 718 | $\gamma'$ precipitation hardening |
| Vibration damping | Internal friction | Grey cast iron | Flake graphite absorbs vibration |
| Impact wear resistance | Work hardening | Hadfield Mn steel | 12% Mn → surface hardens under impact |
| Electrical conduction | Low resistivity | Pure Cu (OFHC) | 100% IACS conductivity |
| Non-magnetic + corrosion | Austenitic structure | SS 304, 316 | FCC → non-magnetic |
| Magnetic core | High permeability | Si-steel (3% Si) | Low eddy current loss |
| Biomedical implant | Biocompatibility + fatigue | CP-Ti, Ti-6Al-4V | Osseointegration, fatigue life |
| Cryogenic storage | Toughness at low T | Austenitic SS 304 | No ductile-to-brittle transition |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Selecting Material for an Aircraft Wing Spar

**Given:** An aircraft wing spar must have:
- $\sigma_y \geq 500$ MPa
- $\rho \leq 2800$ kg/m³
- Fatigue resistance at 10⁷ cycles
- Corrosion resistance in humid atmosphere

**Step 1: Compute specific strength constraint**

$$
SS = \frac{\sigma_y}{\rho} \geq \frac{500 \times 10^6}{2800} = 1.79 \times 10^5 \text{ Nm/kg} = 179 \text{ kNm/kg}
$$

**Step 2: Screen candidate materials**

- Mild steel: SS ≈ 53 kNm/kg → **rejected** (too heavy).
- Aluminium 2024-T3: SS ≈ 185 kNm/kg, density 2780 kg/m³, $\sigma_y \approx 345$ MPa → **fails strength**.
- Aluminium 7075-T6: SS ≈ 200 kNm/kg, density 2810 kg/m³, $\sigma_y \approx 503$ MPa → **passes all criteria**.
- Ti-6Al-4V: SS ≈ 285 kNm/kg → passes, but cost ~10× Al → **selective use**.

**Step 3: Final selection**

**Aluminium 7075-T6** is the optimal cost-to-performance choice for the wing spar, while **Ti-6Al-4V** is reserved for high-stress joints.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Q: List any four applications of Hadfield manganese steel. Why is it preferred for railway crossings?**

**Model Answer:**

Hadfield steel (12% Mn, 1.2% C) is used in:
1. **Railway crossings (frogs and switches)** — surface work-hardens under wheel impact.
2. **Rock crusher jaws and cones** — resists impact abrasion.
3. **Shot-blasting and peening equipment** — withstands repeated impact.
4. **Bulldozer blades and excavator buckets** — high wear resistance in abrasive soil.
5. **Tanks and military track shoes** — high impact strength.

**Reason for railway crossings:** Under repeated impact from train wheels, the austenitic structure work-hardens at the surface, raising hardness from ~200 HB to ~550 HB, providing **exceptional wear resistance while retaining a tough core** to resist fracture.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Q: Why is grey cast iron preferred over steel for machine tool beds? Justify with microstructure.**

**Model Answer:**

Grey cast iron contains **flake graphite** distributed in a ferritic/pearlitic matrix. This microstructure gives:

1. **High vibration damping** — flake graphite interfaces absorb and dissipate vibrational energy, suppressing chatter during machining.
2. **Self-lubrication** — graphite acts as a solid lubricant, reducing wear on mating surfaces.
3. **High compressive strength** with good machinability.
4. **Low cost** and excellent castability into complex bed geometries.

Steel, with no graphite, has **poor damping capacity** and would transmit vibrations, reducing machining accuracy and tool life.
