## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

**Q: (a)** Compare the composition, microstructure, properties, and applications of **ferrous** and **non-ferrous** alloys with specific reference to the **automotive industry**. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Discuss the **property-to-application mapping** for **aluminium alloys** in **aerospace structures**. Use specific alloy designations and justify your answer using the **specific strength criterion**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

| **Aspect** | **Ferrous Alloys** | **Non-Ferrous Alloys** |
|---|---|---|
| Base metal | Iron (Fe) | Al, Cu, Ti, Mg, Ni, Zn |
| Density | 7.8–8.0 g/cm³ (high) | 1.7–8.9 g/cm³ (variable) |
| Cost | Generally lower | Higher (especially Ti, Ni) |
| Strength | Up to 2000 MPa (alloy steels) | Up to 1100 MPa (Ti alloys) |
| Corrosion | Prone to rust (except SS) | Al and Ti form protective oxide |
| Magnetic | Yes (except austenitic SS) | Generally non-magnetic |

**Automotive Applications:**

**Ferrous:**
- **Body panels:** Mild steel (DC04) — formability, weldability, low cost.
- **Engine block:** Grey cast iron — damping, machinability, wear resistance.
- **Crankshaft, camshaft:** Ductile (nodular) cast iron or medium-carbon alloy steel (4140) — fatigue strength, toughness.
- **Gears:** Case-hardened low-carbon alloy steel (16MnCr5) — wear resistance.
- **Springs:** Spring steel (51CrV4) — high yield strength, fatigue resistance.
- **Exhaust manifold:** Ferritic SS (409) — heat + corrosion resistance.

**Non-Ferrous:**
- **Pistons:** Al-Si alloy (A390 / LM13) — lightweight, low thermal expansion.
- **Cylinder head:** Al-Si-Cu (A380) — lightweight, good thermal conductivity.
- **Radiator:** Al alloys / brass — high thermal conductivity.
- **Wheels:** Al alloys (A356-T6) or Mg alloys (AZ91) — weight reduction.
- **Bearings:** Cu-Pb-Sn bronze (leaded bronze) — wear resistance, conformability.
- **Electrical wiring:** Pure Cu — highest conductivity.
- **Plating:** Chrome, nickel — corrosion protection and aesthetics.

> **Key Insight:** The automotive industry uses ferrous alloys where **cost, strength, and durability dominate**, and non-ferrous alloys where **weight, thermal, or corrosion properties are critical**.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Aluminium Alloys in Aerospace — Property-Application Mapping**

The aerospace industry demands materials with **high specific strength** to maximise payload and fuel efficiency.

**Quantitative criterion:**

$$
SS = \frac{\sigma_y}{\rho} \geq 150 \text{ kNm/kg}
$$

| **Alloy** | **Composition** | **$\sigma_y$ (MPa)** | **$\rho$ (kg/m³)** | **SS (kNm/kg)** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2024-T3 | Al-Cu-Mg | 345 | 2780 | 124 | Lower wing skins, fuselage |
| 7075-T6 | Al-Zn-Mg-Cu | 503 | 2810 | 179 | Upper wing skins, spars |
| 6061-T6 | Al-Mg-Si | 276 | 2700 | 102 | Aircraft tubing, fittings |
| Al-Li 8090 | Al-Li-Cu-Mg | 450 | 2540 | 177 | Wing leading edges, floor beams |

**Reasoning:**

- **2024-T3** has good fatigue resistance and damage tolerance → used for **fuselage skins and lower wing skins** that experience cyclic pressurisation.
- **7075-T6** has the highest specific strength in the aluminium family → used for **highly loaded wing spars and ribs**.
- **Al-Li alloys** reduce density by 3% and increase stiffness by 6% per 1% Li → used in **modern Airbus A350 and F-16 components** to reduce mass.
- **6061-T6** has good weldability and corrosion resistance → used in **secondary structures, hydraulic lines, and helicopter rotor masts**.

**Property-to-application link:**
- High $\sigma_y$ → load-bearing wing structure.
- Low $\rho$ → reduced fuel consumption.
- Good fatigue life → safe cyclic service.
- Corrosion resistance → humidity at altitude.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2022, Modified]

**Q: (a)** With suitable examples, explain the role of **alloying elements** in enhancing the properties of steel for specific applications. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Discuss the **applications of nickel-based superalloys** in **gas turbine engines**. Justify the choice using the **creep resistance criterion**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Role of Alloying Elements in Steel:**

| **Element** | **Effect on Properties** | **Resulting Application** |
|---|---|---|
| **Cr (>10.5%)** | Forms passive Cr₂O₃ film | Stainless steels (304, 316) for chemical/marine use |
| **Ni (8–20%)** | Stabilises austenite, increases toughness | Cryogenic tanks, aircraft structural SS |
| **Mo (2–5%)** | Improves high-temp strength, pitting resistance | Boiler steel, HSLA for pressure vessels |
| **Mn (12%)** | Austenite stabiliser, work hardening | Hadfield steel for railway crossings |
| **V (0.1–0.5%)** | Forms V(C,N), grain refinement | High-speed tool steel, springs |
| **W (18%)** | Forms hard W-carbides, hot hardness | T1 high-speed steel for cutting tools |
| **Co (5–10%)** | Retains hardness at red heat | M42 HSS, high-temp tool steel |
| **Si (2–3%)** | Increases electrical resistivity, magnetic permeability | Transformer core (electrical steel) |
| **Ti, Nb** | Forms carbides, prevents grain growth | Microalloyed HSLA for line pipe |

**Synthesis:** Each alloying element targets a specific microstructural feature (carbide, grain size, phase stability) to optimise a specific service property. No single alloying element can satisfy all requirements — this is why **multi-alloy steels** dominate engineering.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Nickel-Based Superalloys in Gas Turbines — Creep Resistance Justification**

Gas turbine blades operate at **temperatures up to 1100°C** under **centrifugal stress** for thousands of hours. The dominant failure mode is **creep rupture**.

**Quantitative criterion (Larson-Miller parameter):**

$$
P_{LM} = T \left( C + \log t_r \right)
$$

where $T$ is in Kelvin, $t_r$ is rupture time in hours, $C \approx 20$ for superalloys.

**Creep rate model:**

$$
\dot{\epsilon} = A \sigma^n \exp\left( -\frac{Q_c}{RT} \right)
$$

Superalloys are designed to **minimise** $\dot{\epsilon}$ by:
- Maximising $Q_c$ (activation energy) through **solid-solution strengthening** with Co, Cr, Mo, W.
- Maximising $n$ (stress exponent) through **precipitation strengthening** with $\gamma'$ phase (Ni₃Al, Ni₃Ti).
- Adding **grain boundary strengtheners** like B, Zr, Hf.

**Common superalloys and applications:**

| **Alloy** | **Composition Highlights** | **Application in Turbine** |
|---|---|---|
| Inconel 718 | Ni-19Fe-18Cr-5Nb-3Mo | Compressor discs, low-pressure turbine blades |
| Inconel 100 | Ni-10Cr-15Co-5Ti-5Al | High-pressure turbine blades |
| Waspaloy | Ni-19Cr-13Co-4Mo-3Ti | Turbine discs, shafts |
| Rene 95 | Ni-14Cr-8Co-4Al-3Ti | High-stress discs |
| CMSX-4 | Ni-6.5Cr-10Co-6W-3Re | Single-crystal HPT blades (F-class engines) |

**Property-to-application link:**
- **Single-crystal blades** (CMSX-4) — eliminate grain boundaries (weakest link for creep).
- **Thermal barrier coatings (TBCs)** of yttria-stabilised zirconia (YSZ) — reduce surface temperature by ~150°C.
- **Internal cooling channels** — convection cooling with compressed air.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Listing without justification:** Writing "Inconel is used in turbine blades" without mentioning creep resistance, $\gamma'$ strengthening, or high-temperature strength.
> 2. **Confusing alloy series:** Mixing up 2xxx, 6xxx, and 7xxx aluminium alloys. Memorise: **2 = Cu, 3 = Mn, 5 = Mg, 6 = Mg+Si, 7 = Zn**.
> 3. **Forgetting units:** Specific strength must be in **kNm/kg or kPa·m³/kg**, not bare MPa.
> 4. **Not linking microstructure to property:** Always tie composition → microstructure → property → application.
> 5. **Neglecting cost factor:** Examiners appreciate a brief mention of cost-availability trade-offs in the final selection.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### High-Density Revision Checklist

- **Carbon Steel Rule:** ↑ C → ↑ strength, ↑ hardness, ↓ ductility, ↓ weldability.
- **Stainless Steel Threshold:** ≥10.5% Cr → corrosion resistance (passive film).
- **Hadfield Steel:** 12% Mn + 1.2% C → **work-hardening** under impact.
- **Grey Cast Iron:** Flake graphite → **vibration damping** (machine tool beds).
- **Ductile Cast Iron:** Spheroidal graphite → strength + ductility (crankshafts).
- **Aluminium Alloys:**
  - 2xxx (Al-Cu) → aircraft skins (2024).
  - 7xxx (Al-Zn) → highest strength (7075-T6, wing spars).
  - 6xxx (Al-Mg-Si) → extrusions, automotive.
- **Titanium 6Al-4V:** Best **specific strength** + biocompatibility → aerospace + implants.
- **Nickel Superalloys:** $\gamma'$ precipitation → **creep resistance** → turbine blades.
- **Specific Strength = $\sigma_y / \rho$** → the **master metric** for lightweight design.
- **Ashby Index for Stiff Beams:** $E^{1/3}/\rho$ → maximise for minimum-weight stiff structures.
- **Hardness-Tensile:** $\sigma_{uts} \approx 3.45 \times HB$ → quick property estimate.
- **Monel (Ni-Cu):** Marine propeller shafts (HCl, seawater).
- **Hastelloy:** Chemical reactors with HCl, H₂SO₄ (Cl⁻-rich media).
- **Rule of Mixtures:** $E_c = V_f E_f + V_m E_m$ → composite stiffness prediction.

### Key Alloy Designation Memory Aid

- **AISI-SAE 4-digit steel:** First 2 digits = alloying type; last 2 = carbon % × 100.
- **Aluminium 7075:** Zn-rich; **2024:** Cu-rich; **6061:** Mg-Si-rich.
- **Inconel 718:** Fe-bearing superalloy; **Waspaloy:** Co-bearing; **CMSX-4:** Re-bearing single crystal.

### Final Mantra for KTU Answers

> **"Composition → Microstructure → Property → Application"** — Always use this four-link chain in your answers. Examiners award full marks only when all four links are explicitly stated.
